actions.thought: Preflop UTG in 6-handed play with A9s suited. This is a strong hand for opening range: suited Ace with good playability, flush potential, and ace blocker. Standard play is to raise for value and position control. Limping is weak and invites multiway pots; folding too tight. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is standard sizing to build pot and thin the field. Minraise too small and could induce squeezes.
A2o is a weak hand facing a 3x UTG raise. UTG opening ranges are typically tight, and A2 offsuit plays poorly multiway and out of position. Easy fold.

I hold 8c 7c, a suited connector, in the Cutoff. The UTG player opened for 3 BB. With effective stacks of 100 BB, this hand has excellent implied odds to crack strong UTG ranges (like overpairs) by making straights or flushes. Being in position on the opener is a significant advantage. While a 3-bet is possible to balance my bluffing range, flat calling is preferable here to keep the pot manageable and realize equity, although I must be wary of a squeeze from the Button. Folding is too tight given the stack depth and playability of the hand.

Ad4h is a weak hand to play against a raise from UTG and a caller in the CO. UTG's opening range typically dominates A4o, and the hand has poor playability post-flop. While I have the button, the risk of being squeezed by the blinds or being dominated outweighs the potential reward of calling. Folding is the most disciplined play to start the tournament.

UTG’s 3bb open is a strong range and it’s already gone multiway with a caller. K9s is often dominated when we make top pair (KQ/KJ/AK from UTG), and from the small blind we’ll realize equity poorly being out of position to both raiser and caller, with the BB still able to squeeze. Even though the pot odds look tempting, this is a marginal defend that can bleed chips over time; I’ll pass and wait for better spots.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 and a caller, 2♣5♠ is the absolute bottom of the range. I’m out of position against at least the raiser (and likely Pro if we go multiway), have almost no showdown equity, and there’s no realistic fold equity for a raise. Pot is 750 and a call costs 200 (≈21% pot odds) but my actual equity vs an UTG raising range plus a caller is far lower than that. Better to preserve chips for spots where I have position or a real hand. Fold.

Preflop UTG raise with A9hh was standard. Flop Ks5c7s completely misses my hand: ace-high, no pair, no straight/flush draw (wrong suit for spades, backdoor hearts at best). OOP heads-up vs Pro's CO calling range, which includes many Kx, pairs, sets, and spade draws. Board is drawy with Ks7s. No value to bet, weak showdown, low equity (~25-35% vs range if called). Bluffing OOP risky as Pro can have sticky calling range or check-raise value. Best play is check-fold to any reasonable bet, preserving stack early in tournament (hand 1, deep stacks ~97BB). Avoid spew.

I hold middle pair (7s) on a Ks 5c 7s board. The UTG opener has checked to me. This board texture hits a UTG opening range fairly well (AK, KQ, AA, KK), so his check could be a trap or pot control, though it often indicates a missed hand like AQ/AJ or a small pair. Betting here has little value as I primarily fold out hands I am already beating (ace-highs) and isolate myself against better hands (KX). Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keeps the pot manageable, and protects my checking range. I also have backdoor club and straight possibilities to improve on later streets.

Turn brings Qh, which adds a heart to the board. I now have Ah9h, giving me ace-high with backdoor flush draw (two running hearts needed) and gutshot to Broadway (but no real straight draw). Board: Ks5c7sQh – dynamic with spade flush draw, possible KQ, QTs, sets, two-pair combos in villain's CO calling range (pairs, suited connectors, broadway, draws). My equity remains low (~20-30% vs range), no showdown value improvement, still OOP. Preflop raise got thinned field but OOP vs Pro's wide calling range. Early tournament (hand 1, 97BB deep), no need to bluff or force action with marginal air. Checking preserves stack, induces bluffs or allows free river showdown if villain checks behind weaker hands/draws. Betting polarizes too thin, risks check-raise from value/draws. Folding irrelevant as unchecked. Plan: check, evaluate bet sizing if any.

On the Turn, the board is Ks 5c 7s Qh. I hold a pair of 7s (8c 7c). The opponent (UTG) has checked to me again. The Queen on the turn hits the UTG range (AQ, QJs) but his double check suggests he likely doesn't have a strong King or a set, or he is pot controlling. My hand has showdown value against Ace-highs and smaller pairs. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, as worse hands will fold and better hands (Kx, Qx, JJ-99) will call. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, potentially improve on the river (trips or two pair), and keeps the pot small which suits my marginal holding.

Runner-runner miracle: the Qh on turn and 8h on river complete the nut flush with Ah9h (A♥9♥Q♥8♥). This is the stone nuts—no higher flush possible (Ace-high flush), and board has no straight (Ks5c7sQh8h pairs no straight draws). Villain (Pro, CO caller) checked behind flop (Ks5c7s, drawy) and turn (Qh, adds backdoor heart), suggesting a weaker range: possibly Kx (top pair, but slowplayed?), mid pairs (88-QQ? but QQ unlikely pre-call), missed spade draws (JsTs etc.), or weak ace-high/Ax. Unlikely villain has the nut flush themselves (would need Ah + another heart, but we block Ah; runner-runner less likely in their range). Pot 750, deep stacks (97BB), hand 1 of 150—plenty of ICM life, but maximize EV by value betting. OOP, checked to us all streets, so bet for value vs. bluff-catchers, worse flushes (JhTh etc.), two-pair (K7/KQ unlikely), sets (too thin). Sizing: ~half-pot (375→400 rounded) extracts max from calling range without pricing in folds or scaring off pairs; pot-sized too big OOP risks check-raise (though we snap-call with nuts). Avoid check (misses value from showdown hands). Plan: bet 400, call reasonable raise, shove over big jam.

I have improved to two pair (Eights and Sevens) on the river, which is a very strong hand given the passive action on the flop and turn (Check-Check). The opponent (UTG) opened pre-flop but checked two streets, suggesting pot control with a hand like AK/AA or a trap. His river bet of 400 into a pot of 750 looks like thin value with Top Pair or an Overpair, or possibly a set of 8s that just arrived. My hand beats the majority of his value range (AK, AA, AQ, KQ if he checked turn, though KQ is unlikely to check turn). However, raising is thin because he likely folds one-pair hands that I beat, and only calls or re-raises with sets or better two pairs (KQ, 88) which crush me. Calling ensures I realize my equity against his entire range and win a healthy pot without risking a blow-up in the first hand of the tournament.
72 suited is one of the weakest hands in poker. From UTG with 5 players behind, I need a much stronger hand to open. Easy fold.

I hold Jack of Hearts and Ten of Hearts (JTs) in Middle Position. This is a premium suited connector with excellent playability and the potential to make straights and flushes. With the UTG player folding, I have the opportunity to open the pot. Limping would be passive and invite multi-way pots where my equity drops. Raising exerts pressure on the remaining players and builds the pot for when I hit a strong hand. A standard open size of 2.5 big blinds (250 chips) is appropriate here to balance value and risk.

I am holding Ace-Jack suited in the Cutoff, a premium hand. A player in Middle Position has opened for 2.5x the big blind. To take control of the hand and capitalize on my positional advantage, I will 3-bet to 800. This puts pressure on the original raiser and makes it less likely for the players in the blinds or the button to enter the pot with marginal hands.

KQo on the Button is too strong to simply fold versus a single 3-bet, especially with position for the rest of the hand. A 4-bet here would be largely as a bluff/merge and risks running into a strong range (and/or getting jammed) without enough fold equity given the opener still behind and deep stacks. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity in position, and I can play cautiously postflop on A-high or heavily pressured boards where I’m often dominated.

I’m in the small blind with T8o facing a 3-bet to 800 from the cutoff after an MP open to 250 and the button called. Pot is already 2,000 and it costs me 750 to continue. This is a weak, unsuited hand that will be out of position against multiple opponents—calling turns this into a bloated multiway pot with poor equity and lots of dominated situations (and the BB still has to act). A min 4-bet to 1,350 would be a thin bluff and I don’t want to bloat my stack with a marginal hand. Fold and wait for a better spot or positionally advantaged hand.

Preflop in BB with A4h suited, facing a 3-bet from Flash in CO after Pro's open (who likely folded), and a flat-call from FiveTwo on BTN. Pot is 2000, to call 700 more (total 800 from me). Great squeeze opportunity: dead money from blinds, Pro's raise, and both opponents may fold weaker 3-bets/flats to a 4-bet. A4h is an excellent semi-bluff with nut flush potential, ace blocker reducing villain's AA/AKs, and decent equity (~30%+ H2H vs strong ranges, still playable multiway). Position is poor if calling multiway OOP, so raising applies fold equity (~50%+ chance to take down pot pre) while maintaining playability deepstacked (~100BB). Raising to 2000 (~1x pot total bet size) balances value (sets, two-pair potential) and bluffs. If Flash 4-bets strong range, can fold; if called, proceed cautiously postflop with initiative.

I am facing a cold 4-bet from the Big Blind to 2000 after I opened to 250, the Cutoff 3-bet to 800, and the Button cold-called. While JTs is a strong playable hand, it performs poorly against a tight 4-bet range (likely QQ+, AK) and the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) will be very low (~1) if I call, destroying my implied odds. Furthermore, the original 3-bettor (Flash) is still behind me and could 5-bet shove, forcing me out after putting in chips. Being sandwiched with a drawing hand in a bloated pot is a losing strategy. The prudent play is to fold.

I am holding Ace-Jack suited, which is a strong hand in most situations, but I am facing significant strength here. After my 3-bet to 800, the Button cold-called, and then the Big Blind (Elon) 4-bet to 2000. A 4-bet from the blinds into two active players usually represents a very narrow, premium range like QQ+, or AK. Even though the pot odds are tempting (1200 to call for a potential pot of over 5000), calling leaves me out of position against the original 4-bettor and potentially the Button. Furthermore, AJs is often dominated by the hands Elon would 4-bet with. To preserve my stack early in the tournament and avoid a high-variance spot where I am likely behind, I will fold.

Facing a big 4-bet from the BB to 2000 after a raise/3-bet/call sequence, KQo is in rough shape versus a likely value-heavy range (QQ+/AK, maybe some AQs). We’re frequently dominated (especially by AK), and if we call the extra 1200 the pot becomes ~5100 with only ~5300 effective behind, creating an SPR around 1 where we’ll be pressured into stacking off too often with a hand that doesn’t realize equity well. The pot odds look tempting, but the reverse implied odds and low postflop maneuverability make this a poor continue. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack for better spots.

I am sitting Under the Gun (UTG) with Jack-Deuce offsuit (Jd 2s). This is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em, being unsuited, unconnected, and containing a low card. From early position in a 6-handed game, I should be playing a tight range of hands. There is absolutely no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand given the deep stacks (100+ BB) and early tournament stage. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o) is a marginal hand in middle position in a 6-max game. While it can be an opening hand in some loose strategies, it often performs poorly when facing a 3-bet or when called by stronger Ace-X hands from the blinds or button. Given the early stage of the tournament and the stack depths, I choose to fold and wait for a more premium hand to avoid being dominated out of position.

Folds to me in the cutoff with Q9o. This is a reasonable late-position open to pick up the blinds and play in position versus the button; the offsuit nature makes it less attractive to limp/call and realize equity multiway. I’ll apply pressure with a standard small open and plan to fold versus significant 3-bet action from the button/blinds.

Cutoff opened to 250 (pot 400). I have T2o on the button — a very weak, often-dominated offsuit hand with poor equity against a CO opening range. To call 250 into a 400 pot I need ~38.5% equity, which T2o does not reliably have vs a standard CO range. A min 3‑bet to 400 as a light steal is possible, but 3‑betting this hand is a poor spot: it’s easily dominated, will be hard to play post‑flop if called, and exposes me to 4‑bets or multiway action from the blinds. With deep stacks and many profitable spots ahead, conserve chips and fold this marginal holding.

3c5s is a trash hand offsuit with poor playability postflop. Facing a cutoff raise, likely wide but still ahead of my hand. SB position is tough OOP. Pot odds to call 200 into ~600 (3:1) are marginal but hand realizes poorly, especially multiway potential with BB behind. No reason to 3-bet bluff as it blocks nothing useful and folds out better. Preserve stack for premium spots after aggressive play in prior hands.

JTo in BB facing a min-raise from cutoff. Getting 400:150 pot odds (2.67:1). This is a solid defending hand - connected broadway cards play well postflop. I'll call and see a flop in position against a single raiser.

I'm first to act on A66 board with JTo. I completely missed this flop - no pair, no draws. This board favors the preflop raiser's range (FiveTwo likely has many Ax hands). I should check and see what FiveTwo does. If they bet, I'll likely fold with zero showdown value and minimal equity.

Heads-up in position as the preflop raiser on A66, I retain a range advantage (lots of Ax and overpairs) while BB has many whiffs and medium pairs that dislike continuing. With Q9o I have little showdown value, so checking back just invites turn pressure and gives up the chance to win immediately. I’ll c-bet small to leverage fold equity on this dry, paired board, and if I’m called I can mostly shut down unless I pick up equity or a very favorable turn.

I have JTo on A66 board with no pair and no draws. FiveTwo bet 200 into 500. Even with 3.75:1 pot odds, I have almost no equity - just runner-runner straight draws at best. This board heavily favors villain's range with Ax hands and pocket pairs. Easy fold.

I am UTG with AhTd. While this is at the bottom of a standard opening range in 6-max, I will open for 3bb to represent strength and take control of the pot. If faced with a significant 3-bet, I will likely fold as AT offsuit doesn't play well against a strong range, especially out of position. I'm keeping an eye on Elon, who has already shown significant aggression in earlier hands.

Facing a 3bb UTG open with 6♠4♥ offsuit from middle position, this hand is too weak and plays poorly postflop (dominated, low connectivity, no suitedness). With several players still to act behind and deep stacks, calling would be -EV and 3-betting is unnecessary spew. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Flash (UTG) opened to 300 in a 6-max game. JdTh is a playable but marginal hand — it performs well in position and has good flop playability, but is not a value 3-bet candidate vs an UTG open. The pot is 450 and calling 300 gives reasonable pot odds to see a flop while keeping my options open. A min-raise to 500 would be too small to generate reliable fold equity and a larger 3-bet commits chips without a strong hand; I prefer to leverage my positional advantage postflop. Plan: call to see the flop, c-bet when I have favorable texture or equity (top pair/draws/bluff-catchers), and fold to heavy aggression when I have no equity or clear domination.

86o is a weak offsuit hand with limited playability. Facing an UTG raise from Flash (3x BB) and a cutoff call from Minni, this is multiway potentially with blinds to act. Defending from the button requires better equity or suited/connectors for implied odds. Pot odds are ~2.5:1 (300 to 750), but postflop OOP risks (actually IP vs raiser/flatter, but blinds act) and poor equity vs UTG range make calling marginal at best. No strong player reads justify a hero call or squeeze. 3-betting as a bluff is risky with deeper stacks and decent players. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament.
4c9s is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing an UTG raise and a cold caller, I'm out of position with no playability. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Q6o facing an UTG open to 3bb and a Cutoff call. Even though I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 950, requiring ~21% equity), my hand performs very poorly multi-way and out of position. The UTG opening range is strong, and the caller likely has better broadways or pairs that dominate my Queen. Q6o has serious reverse implied odds here. Folding is the prudent decision to preserve my stack.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a dry Ace-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor from UTG, I should lead with a continuation bet to extract value from Minni's calling range, which likely includes middle pocket pairs or weaker Aces. A bet of 400 into a 750 pot is a standard size to maintain pressure and build the pot while my hand is likely ahead.

I called preflop as planned, but the A‑7‑5 rainbow flop completely misses JdTh — no pair and no real draw (only remote runner‑runner outs). Flash opened UTG and now c‑bets 400 into a 1,150 pot; calling 400 to win 1,550 requires ~25.8% equity. Against a sensible UTG open/c‑bet range (many A‑x, top pairs and strong broadways) my realistic equity is well below that threshold, and my pair-outs (J or T) are often still behind Ax. Raising to 800 would be an expensive bluff with poor blockers and high risk of a 3‑bet or call where I’m dominated. Preserve my ~9,550 stack and fold to this aggression.

6-handed UTG with J3o is far too weak to open. Limping creates a bloated multiway pot out of position with a dominated jack and poor playability, and raising just burns chips against opponents who have shown willingness to 3-bet and apply pressure. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for stronger UTG holdings.

Unopened pot in middle position with K♥J♦ — a playable, well-connected broadway hand worth opening. With ~95BB effective I want a standard 2.5–3x open to take the initiative, build fold equity, and avoid giving cheap multiway spots. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300 total). Plan: take a stab at the pot preflop and C-bet favorable flops; be prepared to fold to a large 3-bet but consider calling a small 3-bet to play postflop given stack depth.

Qh3d is a weak offsuit hand with poor high-card strength, connectivity, and playability postflop. Facing a standard 3x open-raise from middle position (Minni), in cutoff with three players behind (button, SB, BB), all deep-stacked. No strong reads suggest Minni is weak; folding preserves stack for better spots. Calling or 3-betting this hand risks difficult postflop situations OOP or dominated. Standard discipline: fold preflop trash.

T8s on the button facing a 3x open from MP. This is a solid suited connector with good playability in position. Too good to fold but not strong enough to 3-bet for value. Calling to see a flop in position is the standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd Th. Minni opened to 3bb from Middle Position and Claude called on the Button. QTo is a marginal hand that is easily dominated by the opening and calling ranges (e.g., AQ, KQ, AJ, AT). Furthermore, I would be playing the entire post-flop out of position against two opponents. Although the pot odds are decent, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a fold. Calling invites the Big Blind into the pot as well, creating a difficult multi-way scenario.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce of hearts. Minni opened for 300 from middle position and Claude called from the button. The small blind folded. It costs me 200 more to call into a total pot of 750, meaning I am getting nearly 4-to-1 odds (21% required equity). A2 suited plays well in multi-way pots due to its nut-flush potential. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds and the strength of a suited Ace justify a call to see the flop.

I have Ace-high on a paired board (6c-6s-Qc). I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni) and the button (Claude). Since I missed the flop completely and have no draws, the standard play is to check and see how the other players react. Betting here into two opponents would be a low-frequency bluff that is unlikely to succeed given the board texture and the presence of a Queen or a pocket pair in their ranges.

I was the preflop raiser and the action checked to me on a fairly dry Q-6-6 board with two clubs. I hold K♥J♦ — no pair but decent as a c-bet candidate (blocks some Kx/KQ combos, has showdown value vs air). With two opponents I want to charge club draws and medium pairs and fold out weaker broadways/overcards. A half-pot continuation bet keeps pressure while preserving stack depth for postflop decisions. If I face a large raise I’ll fold (I have no pair); if I get one caller I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I have T8s with no pair, no draw (backdoor spade at best). Board is 6c6sQc - paired board with a club draw. Minni c-bet into two players showing strength. With just T-high and no equity, this is a clear fold. No point floating with Flash still in the hand.

I currently have Ace-high with a weak kicker (2) on a paired board of 6-6-Q. Minni, who was the pre-flop raiser, has fired a continuation bet of 475 into the 950 pot. Since I have no piece of this board, no flush draw, and no straight draw, I am essentially folding to any aggression here. My hand has very little equity against a range that includes pocket pairs, Queens, or even stronger Aces. Continuing out of position with air is a losing play in the long run.

UTG with Q♦6♠ — a weak offsuit queen with a poor kicker and limited playability. With ~100bb effective stacks and 6 players, UTG opening ranges should be fairly tight; this hand is outside my profitable open range. Limping from UTG creates multiway, out-of-position problems and raising is too loose given how often I’ll be dominated. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

Preflop in middle position with 5d3d, a weak suited connector with poor playability and low equity multiway. Deep stacks, early in tournament (hand 6/150), no prior action except UTG fold. Limping risks multiway pots where this hand struggles; raising is overplaying it. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

J2s in the cutoff is too weak to open. While suited, the jack-deuce combination has poor playability and will often be dominated. With 3 players left to act behind me, folding is the correct play here.

I am on the Button with J8o. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a prime position to steal the blinds, J8o is a marginal hand. I observed in Hand #2 that the Small Blind (Flash) is capable of 3-betting my opens. Given the marginal strength of my hand and the risk of facing aggression from the blinds, specifically Flash who has re-raised me before, the prudent play is to fold and wait for a stronger holding or better spot to attack.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 6d. It has been folded to me. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, 4-6 offsuit is an extremely weak hand and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the Big Blind. In a tournament setting, it is better to avoid playing marginal hands out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG position with Jd4c, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Standard play is to fold weak offsuit hands from early position to avoid difficult post-flop situations, especially with deep stacks. Preserve stack for premium hands. Early in tournament (hand 7/150), no need to speculate.

QTs suited is a strong hand in middle position. With Elon folding UTG, I'm in a good spot to raise and take initiative. This hand has good playability with straight and flush potential. A standard raise to 250 is appropriate.

I hold K3o in the Cutoff facing a raise to 2.5bb from Claude in Middle Position. This is a very weak hand that is easily dominated by the opener's range (which includes stronger Kings, Aces, and pairs). Despite having position on the opener, there are players yet to act behind me, and playing K3o against an MP open is losing strategy in the long run. Given the early stage of the tournament and 100bb+ stack depth, this is a standard fold.

I am on the button with 9-6 offsuit. Claude raised from middle position to 250. This is a very weak hand to play against a raise, even with position. I have approximately 93 big blinds and there is no reason to risk chips with such a low-equity hand this early in the tournament.

Claude’s 2.5x open from middle position puts us in a tough spot from the small blind with a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. 9-5o has poor playability, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and we’ll be out of position against the raiser (and potentially the big blind). Calling would bleed EV over time; 3-betting is too ambitious without blockers or suited connectivity. Best is to release and preserve stack.

Heads-up to Claude in the big blind with Kc5d. Claude opened to 250 (pot 400); I’ve already posted 100 so it’s 150 to call (250 total). K5o is a marginal but defendable hand heads-up — I get ~27% pot odds to call (150 into ~550) and will have reasonable equity vs a standard open range. I don’t want to inflate the pot with a weak hand by making a small raise (would commit more chips and can be met with a 3-bet or cold-call awkwardness). With deep stacks (~100bb) I prefer to see a flop and play straightforwardly: proceed cautiously if I miss, extract or build if I pair my King or hit two pair/straights, fold to heavy aggression when clearly beat. Call 150 more (250 total) to see the flop.

Flop T♥ 8♦ 4♠; I hold K♣5♦ — missed, only a single overcard and no meaningful draws. I'm out of position and we already defended preflop, so I don't want to bloated the pot with a marginal hand or turn it into a guessy spot. Check to control the pot, preserve folding equity, and plan to fold to any sizable c-bet. I may call a very small continuation bet (≲1/3 pot) to see a cheap turn, but will give up to larger aggression. If Claude checks back I can freely evaluate the turn.

I flopped top pair with a queen kicker on Th-8d-4s. This is a good flop for my hand. Minni checked from the BB after defending. I should c-bet for value and protection against draws (straight draws possible). Betting around 60-65% pot is standard here - around 350.

I defended preflop with K♣5♦ and checked the flop to control the pot OOP. The flop T♥8♦4♠ completely misses me — I only have one overcard (K) and no real draw — but Claude's bet is relatively small (350 into a 900 pot). Calling 350 asks for ~28% equity (350 into a ~1250 pot) which is within range for K-high versus a wide c-bet range, so folding would be too tight and raising would unduly inflate the pot with a marginal hand out of position. I will call to see a turn and re-evaluate: fold to large turn aggression unless I improve to top pair (K) or pick up a clear draw; if I hit a pair or Claude slows down I can extract value. Call 350.

I hold Kc5d on T♥ 8♦ 4♠ J♥. I never improved and have no meaningful draws; I’m out of position and the pot is already ~1250. Checking controls the pot and preserves folding equity — there’s little to gain by betting or bluffing with only K-high. If Claude checks back I can reevaluate on the river; if he leads out I will fold to any meaningful turn bet and only consider calling extremely small bets (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ ≤400).

I have top pair with a queen kicker on Th-8d-4s-Jh. The turn Jh adds more draws (97 has open-ender, flush draws possible). Minni called flop and checked again - likely has a draw, weaker ten, or just picked up a pair of jacks. I should continue betting for value and to protect against draws. Betting around 60% pot (750) seems appropriate.

Fold. I have Kc5d on T‑8‑4‑J and completely missed — no meaningful draws. Claude opened preflop, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn; that line is value‑heavy here. Pot math: the pot is 1250 before his turn bet; he bets 750 and calling 750 would contest a final pot of 2750, so I need ≈27% equity to break even. My clean outs are only 3 kings + 3 fives = 6 outs (~13% to pair by the river), and some of those outs can still lose, so my equity is well below the required threshold. Raising OOP as a bluff is too risky against this line. Fold to minimize losses and preserve my stack for better spots.
54 offsuit in UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak hand with poor playability from the earliest position at the table. No reason to get involved here.

I hold King-Seven offsuit (Kh 7c) in Middle Position. With the blinds at 50/100, I have a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds. K7o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by hands like KQ, KJ, K10, or Aces with good kickers. Being in middle position with four players left to act (Cutoff, Button, SB, BB), opening this hand is risky and generally has a negative expected value. There is no need to make loose plays this early in the tournament (Hand 8/150). The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am holding 6c 2s in the Cutoff position. This is a very weak starting hand with no significant straight or flush potential. Since there has been no action yet and I have players behind me, there is no reason to play this hand. I will fold.

Folds to us on the Button with 7♠7♦ and ~95bb effective. This is a clear open-raise spot: we have a hand that plays well in position, can win at showdown often, and can profitably continue versus reasonable 3-bets (call smaller sizes for set value/position, fold to very large 3-bets). I’ll use a standard BTN sizing to apply pressure to blinds without overinflating the pot.

I have A♥Q♥ in the small blind facing a button open to 250. This is a premium hand with good equity against a wide button opening range and I have ~95BB effective, so I prefer to 3‑bet for value and fold equity rather than just calling and playing OOP with a wide range. Raising to 900 (total) is a meaningful 3‑bet: large enough to fold out weaker broadways and many speculative hands, to isolate the opener, and still leaves plenty of chips behind to play postflop. If I get called I'll proceed postflop for value/pressure; if I face a large 4‑bet I'll re-evaluate (AQs can often fold to very strong 4‑bets but may continue against smaller 4‑bets).

87o is a speculative offsuit connector with poor postflop playability. Facing a button open-raise from FiveTwo (who has been aggressive in prior hands) and a sizable 3-bet to 900 from Minni in the SB (also shown aggression but strong sizing here), my hand has low equity against their likely strong ranges. Calling 800 into a 1250 pot offers marginal odds (~2.5:1), but FiveTwo acts behind and could 4-bet or call, bloating the pot further. Raising has little fold equity against this action line. Best to fold and wait for better spots to build the stack deep.

We opened BTN and face a relatively large SB 3-bet to 9bb. With 7♠7♦ we’re not strong enough to 4-bet for value, and it’s a poor 4-bet bluff (no blockers, and we don’t love getting jammed on). Folding is too tight given we have position, a hand that can flop very strong (sets), and we can also realize equity/showdown on some lower textures vs an SB 3-bet range that will contain some bluffs. The price is 650 more with ~78bb effective behind, which is enough to profitably continue in position and play postflop carefully (mostly set-mine, otherwise proceed cautiously vs heavy barreling on high-card boards).

I have A♥Q♥ on a Tc Ad 4c flop — top pair, top kicker. I was the preflop aggressor (large 3‑bet), so a continuation bet is natural: my range is heavy in Ax and broadways and I can get value from worse A‑x, T‑x, and some Qx while charging club draws. The board is relatively dry aside from two clubs, so a half‑pot c-bet denies equity to draws and keeps the pot manageable with ~87BB effective stacks. I choose ~1/2 pot (950) to extract value and protect my hand. If I face a small-to-medium raise I'll call and re-evaluate on the turn; if I face a large, polarized shove I will fold to avoid spewing chips into a likely dominated spot.

We called pre to set-mine/play a manageable pot in position. On A♦ T♣ 4♣ vs a SB 3-bet range, our 7♠7♦ is just an underpair with no club and very poor backdoor equity. Minni’s ~1/3-pot c-bet is consistent with a high-frequency range bet on this texture, so calling would mostly just invite turn barrels where we can’t profitably continue and we don’t have strong turns to float/raise. With limited ability to realize equity and no good bluff-raise candidates (no blockers, no backdoor flush), folding now preserves stack and keeps our continuing range stronger (A-x, some Tx with BDFD, stronger pairs, and suited broadways with backdoors).

I am in UTG position with Queen-Five offsuit (Q5o). This is a statistically weak hand that falls well outside the standard opening range for Under The Gun in a 6-max game. With 5 players left to act behind me and 100BB stack depth, playing this hand out of position is negative EV. There are no situational reasons (such as being short-stacked or facing extremely tight blinds) to attempt a steal this early in the tournament. The correct play is a standard fold.

I am in middle position with 8h 4h. This is a weak suited hand that does not meet the requirements for an opening raise from this position. With four aggressive players still to act behind me, including those who have shown willingness to 3-bet or play large pots, folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Unopened pot in the cutoff with position on the blinds and button. Q9o isn’t a premium, but it’s strong enough to profitably open in 6-max, leveraging fold equity versus the blinds and allowing us to realize equity in position when called. We’ll use a standard small open to keep risk low and maintain a wide, consistent raising range; if we face a sizable 3-bet from the button/blinds we can comfortably fold.

Fold. Facing a 250 cutoff open with K♥4♣ on the button, this hand is poorly positioned and frequently dominated by the opener’s range. Calling 250 often leads to marginal post‑flop decisions (potentially multiway) with little equity or backdoor potential, and a minimal 3‑bet to 400 won’t generate reliable fold equity and just invites squeezes/4‑bets from Elon/Claude who have shown aggression. With a healthy 10600 stack early in the tournament I preserve chips and wait for a cleaner spot to apply pressure.

Preflop in SB facing a CO min-raise to 2.5BB (250). A3s is a profitable call here due to suited ace playability, Ace blocker reducing villain's AA/AK premium hands, and solid equity (~38-42% vs typical CO open range including broadways, suited connectors, pairs). Pot odds are excellent: calling additional 200 into current pot of 400 (2:1 direct, better with dead SB money). Position out of position postflop but deep stacks (110BB effective) favor realizing equity rather than folding or 3-betting a marginal hand. BB (Claude) likely to defend wide/multiway pot possible but still +EV. Early in tournament, no strong villain reads yet but FiveTwo has shown aggression in prior hands without excessive folding.

I have 3d4d in the BB facing a min-raise from FiveTwo and a call from Elon. I need to call 150 into a 600 pot, getting 4:1 odds. Suited connectors like 34s have decent playability - can make straights and flushes. The pot odds justify a call here, especially multiway where I can see a cheap flop and fold if I miss.

Facing a multiway flop (3 players) as first to act in SB with A3s on 7s2hTd rainbow board. Hand is ace-high with no pair, no straight or flush draw (spade in hand but only one on board, heart but no draw). Pure bluff-catcher postflop after calling preflop defend range. Preflop call was +EV due to suited ace playability and pot odds, but now OOP multiway, checking is standard to realize equity cheaply or control pot. Raising as a bluff is too thin multiway against CO raiser (FiveTwo aggressive but folds to pressure as seen in Hand 8) and BB defender (Claude defends wide). Pot 750, deep stacks (100+BB), no fold equity worth risking. Check to pot control, induce bluffs/checks, and reassess turn.

I completely missed this flop with 3d4d on 7s 2h Td. I have a backdoor diamond flush draw and very weak straight draws. It's checked to me, so I should just check and see a free turn card. No reason to bluff into two opponents with this board texture and no real equity.

We’re multiway on a fairly dry T72 board with two opponents who have a lot of natural continues (Tx, 7x, pocket pairs). With Q9o we have no real draw (only overcards/backdoors), so a c-bet is likely to get called at least once and put us into tough turn spots with low equity. In position, taking the free card maximizes our ability to realize equity and keeps the pot controlled; we can still apply pressure on favorable turns (Q/9/J/8, or cards that check through again).

Turn board 7s2hTdJd brings a diamond completing gutshots and possible straight draws (e.g., 89, Q9 for broadway), but my A♥3♠ remains ace-high no pair, no draws (backdoor spade at best). Multiway pot (SB vs BB vs CO raiser) OOP first to act, deep stacks (~100BB). Hand is a marginal bluff-catcher from preflop defend range, but betting as a bluff lacks fold equity multiway against FiveTwo's aggression (seen folding to river/turn pressure in prior hands) and Claude's wide BB defense. Value betting range too thin without showdown value improvement. Checking pot controls, realizes equity cheaply, induces bluffs from missed draws/polarized ranges, and allows reassessment on river. Folding equity not worth the risk pre-river.

Turn brought the Jd, giving me a flush draw with 3d4d. I have 9 outs to the flush (~18% equity). It's checked to me with FiveTwo still to act behind. Could semi-bluff but with two opponents and position disadvantage to FiveTwo, I prefer taking a free card to realize my equity. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a tough spot.

Both blinds have checked twice in a 3-way pot, signaling a lot of capped/medium-strength hands. The Jd is a good card to apply a delayed c-bet as the preflop raiser because it connects well with our perceived range (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT/overpairs) and puts immediate pressure on 7x, pocket pairs, and weaker Tx that often would have bet themselves for protection. With Q9 we also have a gutshot to the 8 for a straight, so this is a reasonable semi-bluff that can win now and still improve on the river. If we face a check-raise, we can fold comfortably given our low-equity draw.

Facing a 500 bet into 1250 pot (~2.5:1 odds) on turn Jd completing possible straight draws (e.g., Q9, 89) and adding diamond texture on 7s2hTdJd board. Holding A♥3♠ (offsuit ace-high, no pair, no straight/flush draw). Preflop call was marginal but +EV wide defend OOP with pot odds, but postflop no improvement multiway. FiveTwo (CO raiser) shows aggression (e.g., Hand 8 c-bet flop but folded prior pressures) but turn bet after universal check-flop suggests value (Jx, Tx, sets, draws) over pure bluff given board/runout. OOP vs BB (Claude wide defender likely to continue), calling commits 500 with poor post-turn playability, low equity realization, and thin bluff-catching value (worse aces/king-highs beat us). Folding preserves deep stack (108BB) for better spots early tournament; pot control check strategy failed vs aggression, but folding weak ace-high standard.

I have a diamond flush draw (9 outs) with 3d4d. Getting 2.5:1 pot odds (500 to win 1250) but need ~4.5:1 to call purely on odds. However, with implied odds if I hit, this is a profitable call. FiveTwo's 500 bet could be a stab at the pot or a real hand - if I hit the flush on the river, I can likely extract value.

River brought 6h, missing my diamond flush draw completely. I have 3 high - absolutely no showdown value. FiveTwo bet the turn when Jd came but checked the river, suggesting weakness or a busted draw. However, bluffing here is risky - FiveTwo could be checking a pair for showdown value, and I'd need to risk 1000+ chips to try to steal a 1750 pot. With my stack still healthy at 9300 and early in the tournament, the prudent play is to check and concede. Bluffing into someone who showed aggression on the turn is high variance for marginal EV.

We have Q9 high and missed our gutshot, so checking back rarely wins versus any pair. After checking flop and betting turn, our perceived range is still strong (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, overpairs, slow-played sets), while Claude’s check-call turn then check river line is often capped at medium-strength one-pair (Tx/7x/pocket pairs) and missed diamond floats. The 6h is mostly a brick for our story and puts extra pressure on hands like 88–99/7x and some weak Tx. We’ll fire a polarized river bluff at a size that forces those marginal holdings to fold; if raised, we can fold comfortably.

Missed my flush draw completely - I have 3-high with zero showdown value. FiveTwo bet 500 on turn and now 1300 on river, showing continued aggression. I can't bluff-catch with 3-high as I'm only beating pure air. Calling 1300 to potentially catch a bluff with the worst possible holding is -EV. Easy fold.

I am holding pocket tens (Ts, Th) in the UTG position. This is a premium starting hand that is significantly stronger than the average holding. In a 6-max game, I should be raising with this hand to build the pot and discourage late-position players from seeing a cheap flop with speculative hands. A standard opening raise size of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to maintain consistency with my previous opening ranges and to apply pressure.

Facing a 3x UTG open in a 6-handed game, Q3 offsuit is far too weak to continue. It’s dominated by villain’s value range (AQ/KQ/QJ/QT) and plays poorly postflop out of position versus multiple players yet to act. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Fold. Facing an UTG open to 300 (3bb) from Flash, my 3♣9♥ is a weak, offsuit, poorly-connected hand that will often be dominated by an UTG opening range in 6-max. The pot is 450 and calling 300 would require roughly 40% equity (300 into a 750 pot) — far more than this hand has vs an UTG range. Calling also puts me out of position against the button and the blinds and risks a multiway pot that further reduces my postflop playability. A 3-bet bluff here would be thin: my fold equity is limited and I don't want to commit chips with such a poor hand. With a healthy ~106bb stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots — fold.

J4o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and equity against an UTG raise from Flash, who has demonstrated aggression in prior hands (e.g., Hand 2 3-bet, Hand 4 c-bet). Even on the button with position, the hand lacks strength for a call or 3-bet. Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities early in the tournament.
38o is a very weak hand. Flash opened from UTG which indicates strength. I'm out of position and this hand has poor playability. Easy fold.

I am holding Pocket Sixes (6h 6d) in the Big Blind facing a 3bb open from UTG (Flash). The Small Blind has folded. I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 450) to defend my blind. With an effective stack depth of roughly 90BB, I have sufficient implied odds to set-mine. 3-betting here would likely only fold out hands I dominate or isolate me against a stronger range while playing out of position. The standard play is to call and evaluate the flop, looking to hit a set or navigate a low board.

I have flopped a set of sixes on a T-8-6 board, which is an extremely strong hand. Being out of position against the UTG raiser (Flash), the standard and most profitable line is to check. This allows the pre-flop aggressor to continue with a continuation bet, which I can then raise (check-raise) to build the pot and charge draws, or call to trap depending on the sizing. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their bluffs and define my hand strength too early. The board texture connects well with a UTG range (overpairs like JJ+, AT, KT), making it likely they will bet.

I have flopped top set of tens on a 10-8-6 rainbow board. This is a monster hand. Pro checked to me, the pre-flop aggressor. While the board is somewhat connected, there are plenty of worse hands that can call a bet, such as pocket pairs (99, 77), top pair (Tx), or straight draws (97, J9). I will bet for value and to build the pot, choosing a sizing of roughly 60% of the pot to extract maximum value while keeping his range wide.

I hold bottom set (66) on a T-8-6 rainbow board. The UTG opener (Flash) made a standard c-bet of 400 into 650. While the board has some straight connectivity (7 or 9 creates 4-straight possibilities), a made straight (97) is unlikely in a standard UTG opening range. Raising here (check-raise) would likely fold out Flash's bluffs (AK, AQ) and might isolate me against superior sets (TT, 88) or strong draws, while overpairs might fold or play very cautiously. Calling is the superior line to disguise the strength of my hand, keep Flash's bluffing range wide, and induce a second barrel on the turn. I can re-evaluate if a scare card (7 or 9) hits, but otherwise, I intend to extract value on later streets.

I hold a set of Sixes on a T-8-6-5 rainbow board. I am out of position against the UTG raiser. The turn card (5) completes a straight for exactly 9-7, which is a plausible but not guaranteed hand in a UTG range. It is unlikely my opponent has a 4 for the low straight. My hand is still very strong, beating all overpairs (AA, KK, QQ, JJ), top pairs (AT), and lower sets (55), only losing to higher sets (TT, 88) or the straight (97). Leading out (donk betting) here would likely fold out the bluffs I want to keep in (like AK/AQ) and might scare off one-pair hands. Checking allows the opponent to continue firing with overpairs for value or bluffs. If they bet, I can consider a check-raise for value or a call to trap, but checking first is the optimal line to maximize extraction.

I have top set with pocket tens on a board of 10-8-6-5. While the five on the turn completes a potential straight for a hand like 9-7, I still have a massive hand and need to continue betting for value. Pro checked to me again after calling my flop bet, which suggests they likely have a marginal made hand like a ten, a lower pair, or a draw. I will bet 900 into the 1450 pot to extract value from those hands and build the pot for a potential river shove, while also charging any remaining straight draws like J-9 or 7-x.

I hold a set of sixes (6h 6d) on a board of [8h 6s Td 5c]. The turn card (5c) creates a straight possibility only for exactly 9-7 or 7-4. Since 7-4 is not in a standard UTG range and 9-7 is thin (mostly 97s), my set is extremely strong, beating all overpairs (AA-JJ), top pairs (AT), and lower sets (55). Raising here (check-raise) would likely fold out Flash's bluffs (AK/AQ) and might cause overpairs to fold or play cautiously, while isolating me against higher sets (TT, 88) or the straight. Calling is the optimal line to conceal my hand strength, keep the opponent's bluffing range active, and extract maximum value from overpairs on the river. The board is rainbow, so there are no flush draws to protect against.

I hold a set of Sixes on a final board of [8h 6s Td 5c 9d]. The river 9d is a terrible card for my hand, as the board now reads 5-6-8-9-T. This means any 7 makes a straight (5-6-7-8-9 or 6-7-8-9-T), and Q-J also makes a straight (8-9-T-J-Q). Given Flash's UTG opening range, he could easily hold QJ, 77, or higher sets (TT, 88, 99). Even overpairs (AA-JJ) that I beat will likely check back on this scary texture, as they cannot value bet comfortably. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by hands that beat me (straights, higher sets) and fold out hands I beat. Checking is the only viable option to attempt to get to showdown cheaply or evaluate the opponent's sizing if they bet.

The river 9d completes a possible straight for any hand containing a 7 (5-6-7-8-9 and 6-7-8-9-10). While I have a set of tens, the board is now extremely dangerous. Pro has called two streets, and many hands in their defending range from the big blind (such as 7-8, 7-9, 7-6, or even 7-5) have now made a straight. Since Pro checked to me, checking back is the most prudent play to ensure a showdown and realize the value of my set without risking a check-raise from a straight. I likely have the best hand if Pro has two-pair or a smaller set, but betting might only get called by better hands or force out hands I already beat.

AcQc is a premium suited broadway with strong playability and good blocker effects (removes A-high/QQ+ combos from opponents). In a 6-handed game, this is a clear open from UTG to take initiative, build a pot when we’re likely ahead, and keep our range strong enough to withstand 3-bets. I’ll use a standard small open size to 2.5bb to reduce risk while still generating fold equity and value.

Facing an UTG open to 250 from middle position with T♥2♥. This is a very weak, often dominated holding versus an UTG opening range — the suitedness gives some backdoor potential but not enough to justify calling 250 into a 400 pot (you need ~38.5% equity) especially with multiple players yet to act who can 3‑bet or isolate. A cold-call or a light 3‑bet here is speculative and high-variance; better to preserve a healthy 10,600 stack for stronger spots and better position. Fold.

Preflop in cutoff facing a standard UTG raise to 2.5BB from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands. 54d is a suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential, and strong implied odds on deep stacks. Position advantage postflop heads-up or multiway. Pot odds are favorable (calling 250 into 400+), and equity realization is better in position against a strong but polarized UTG range. Folding is too tight; 3-betting is suboptimal with this hand as a bluff. Flat calling balances range and exploits position.

Q5 suited on the button with a raise and call ahead. I'm getting decent odds (250 to win 650+) with position. Suited queen has some playability - can make flushes and hit top pair occasionally. Calling is reasonable here for implied odds with position.

I am in the Small Blind with 7s4s, facing a UTG raise and two callers. The pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a pot of roughly 1100 if BB completes, offering better than 5:1). However, I will be playing out of position against three or four opponents with a very weak hand that suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated flushes, weak pairs). My realization of equity will be poor due to position. Additionally, the Big Blind is yet to act and could squeeze, forcing a fold. Folding is the prudent long-term play.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. The UTG player raised to 250, and there are two callers before me. I only need to put in 150 more to call into a pot that will be 1050. These are excellent pot odds (7:1), requiring very little equity to justify a call. While A9o is not a premium hand and I will be out of position, the price is too good to fold, and raising into three opponents—including an UTG opener—is unnecessarily risky with this hand strength.

The flop of 7h-2c-8d is very dry and multi-way. Having checked pre-flop from the Big Blind after calling a raise, the most standard and disciplined play is to check to the original aggressor (FiveTwo). With Ace-high in a four-way pot, I have some showdown value but limited equity to improve significantly. I need to see how the pre-flop raiser and the other callers respond to this texture before deciding whether to continue in the hand.

We’re 4-way and out of position on a flop (8-7-2 rainbow with only one club) that connects well with the callers’ ranges (8x/7x, 98/87/76, small pairs). With AcQc we only have two overcards plus backdoor club potential, and a c-bet will get called a lot while exposing us to raises from strong made hands. Checking keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity, and lets us consider a delayed c-bet on favorable turns (A/Q, high cards, or a club turn giving more equity).

Multiway pot (4 players) on a dry 7h2c8d flop with position. 54dd holds a weak gutshot straight draw to the 6 (~8% equity heads-up, less multiway) and a backdoor flush draw. No showdown value. Both Flash (BB) and FiveTwo (UTG preflop raiser) checked, indicating possible weakness, but betting as a thin semi-bluff multiway risks getting raised or called by better (overs, pairs like 99-JJ, sets). Pot control is key; checking realizes equity for free on turn, maintains balance after flatting preflop, and keeps initiative post-turn if improves. Position allows control. Folding equity low multiway. Check to induce bluffs or see turn cheap.

Flop is 7h 2c 8d. I have Q5h - just a backdoor flush draw and two overcards. All three opponents checked to me. While I have position, betting into 3 opponents with essentially no made hand is risky. My backdoor flush draw gives minimal equity. Checking back for a free card to potentially hit a heart or queen on the turn is the best play here.

I have turned top pair with an Ace kicker on a board of 7-2-8-9. The flop was checked through by all four players, suggesting that no one is likely holding an overpair or a set. However, I am out of position against three opponents, and the 9d brings some potential straight draws (6-5, T-J) and a diamond flush draw into play. By checking, I can see how the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) and the others react. If it is checked around again, I have a strong hand for showdown or a river value bet. If someone bets, I have a high-equity hand that is more than strong enough to check-call and re-evaluate on the river.

Turn 9d is a poor barrel card for our AcQc in a 4-way pot: it improves the callers’ ranges heavily (98/87/76/T8, more pair+draw combos) and adds a second diamond, increasing sticky holdings. We have no draw and only two overcards, and any bet here will get called frequently with little fold equity while bloating a pot out of position. Best is to check, realize equity, and reassess on the river (value on A/Q rivers; otherwise mostly give up unless action stays very weak).

Turn brings the 9d, completing my diamond flush draw (9 flush outs) and maintaining a gutshot straight draw to the 6 (4 outs), giving me strong equity (~35% heads-up against a pair, more if worse). Multiway pot with 4 players, but Flash (BB defendor) and FiveTwo (UTG preflop raiser) both checked flop and turn, signaling weakness or traps, but their check-check likely represents missed draws/overs rather than monsters given no prior aggression postflop. Pot 1050, deep stacks. In position (acting before Button Claude), this is an ideal semi-bluff spot: high fold equity against weak ranges, deny equity to opponents' draws/ace-high floats, build pot for when we hit. Checking risks giving free cards to chasers or Claude stealing. Bet sizing 3/4 pot (750) balances value from weaker pairs/draws and protection while leaving room for action.

Board is 7h 2c 8d 9d. I have Q5h - just queen high with no made hand. The 9d completes straight draws for hands like T6, 65. I only have one heart on board so no flush draw. My only outs are 3 queens for top pair (roughly 6% to hit). Facing a 750 bet into 1800 pot, I'm getting ~3.4:1 odds but need much better equity. Plus Flash and FiveTwo still to act. Easy fold with no draw and no pair.

On a turn card of 9d, I have improved to top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). The flop was checked through by all four players, which significantly reduces the likelihood that any opponent holds a set (77, 88, or 22) or a high overpair, as those hands typically bet for protection and value in a multi-way pot. Elon's bet of 750 into 1050 after the turn 9 appears to be a delayed continuation bet or an attempt to take the pot with a medium pair or a draw (like a diamond flush draw or a straight draw). While I have a strong hand, raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like two pair 98s or 97s) and would force out his bluffs. By calling, I maintain pot control, keep his bluffing range in, and can comfortably evaluate the river. I must also consider that FiveTwo is still left to act behind me, so calling is the most prudent way to see how the action develops.

We’re facing a 750 bet into a 4-way pot on a very connected board (7-8-9 with two diamonds) after the flop checked through. With AcQc we have no flush/straight draw and only 6 clean-ish outs (A or Q) to make top pair on the river (~13% equity), and even those outs can be reverse-implied (e.g., opponents can have two pair/sets/straights or strong pair+draw holdings). Pot odds are about 750 to win 3300 after calling (~23%), which we don’t meet, and bluff-raising multiway into a bettor + caller is very low fold-equity. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

The river Ten is a significant scare card as it completes several potential straights (any Jack for J-T-9-8-7 or any Six for T-9-8-7-6). My pair of Nines, which was top pair on the turn, has now been demoted to middle pair. Given that I am out of position against Elon, who initiated the betting on the turn, checking is the most disciplined move. Betting into this board would likely only be called by hands that have me beaten, such as a Ten, two pair, or a straight. By checking, I can potentially reach showdown for free if Elon checks back or evaluate the strength of his bet if he continues his aggression.

River Tc bricks my flush draw and gutshot straight draw, leaving me with pure air and no showdown value. However, the board texture is draw-heavy (completed diamond flush draw on turn, multiple straight possibilities now with 5-6-7-8-9 or 6-7-8-9-10 on river Tc), and my turn bet of 750 into a multiway pot after checks represented significant strength (likely flush, straight, or overpair). Heads-up now vs Flash (BB defender) who check-called the turn sizing despite the scary 9d, suggesting medium strength like a stubborn pair (77-JJ, T9, weak 9x), float, or inferior draw rather than a monster (which might raise turn). Flash's river check indicates weakness or trap, but given his history of passive check-calls (e.g., Hand 10 check-behind river, multiple folds to aggression), high fold equity exists against his capped range. Pot 2550, deep stacks (mine 9800, his 9950); a 2/3 pot bluff of 1700 puts pressure, denies equity, and realizes fold equity (~40-50% likely vs medium pairs/draws). Checking risks chopping or losing to any pair; barreling exploits position, initiative, and board to maximize EV by stealing frequently. Balances my turn semi-bluff range.

On this river, the board has become extremely coordinated with a four-card straight (7-8-9-10). My pair of Nines, which was top pair on the turn, is now only middle pair and loses to any Jack, any Six, any Ten, or any two-pair combination. Elon's betting line (betting 3/4 pot on the turn and 2/3 pot on the river) is very strong, especially after the flop was checked around. While I have decent pot odds (roughly 2.5:1), my hand is effectively a weak bluff-catcher here. Elon's range contains many Jacks (JTs, J9s, QJs) and potentially some Tens that have now overtaken me. Given the high probability that I am beaten by his value range and the lack of obvious missed draws he would be bluffing with (other than perhaps a missed diamond draw), a fold is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

T9o is a marginal hand from UTG in a 6-max game. I’ll be out of position for the entire hand against five opponents, and this hand is often dominated by the kinds of broadway and pocket-pair ranges I’ll face; limping invites multiway pots and an open-raise here would be too loose given the aggressive players at the table who can 3‑bet. With 106BB I can wait for a better spot in position, so I’ll fold and conserve my stack for higher-expected-value opportunities.

84o is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. In middle position pre-flop with no limpers ahead, deep stacks, and a small pot, folding is straightforward and optimal to avoid playing speculative hands out of position or wasting chips on dominated equity.
73 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even in the cutoff with only 3 players behind, this hand has poor playability and equity. Not worth opening - easy fold.

I am on the Button with 9d 6s, which is a very weak off-suit hand (bottom 25% of hands). While the Button is a great position to steal the blinds, the Small Blind (Flash) has shown aggression (3-betting previously), and the Big Blind has a healthy stack. Opening with such a weak hand carries negative expected value given the risk of being 3-bet or called by better hands. At this early stage of the tournament (Hand 12/150), preserving chips by folding trash hands is the disciplined and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket sixes (6h, 6d). All players have folded to me. This is a blind-vs-blind situation against FiveTwo. Pocket sixes is a very strong hand heads-up. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and attempt to win the blinds or build a pot where I am likely ahead pre-flop.

Blind-vs-blind, Flash’s SB opening range is very wide and he’s shown a tendency to apply pressure postflop. A8 suited is strong enough to 3-bet for value/protection against dominated Ax and to generate fold equity with an Ace blocker. I’ll take initiative and build a pot where I can c-bet effectively, using a standard BB 3-bet size versus a 3x open.

I opened from the Small Blind with 6h6d and FiveTwo 3-bet me from the Big Blind to 900. While being out of position (OOP) is disadvantageous, pocket sixes are too strong to fold to a single 3-bet in a blind-versus-blind battle. The price to call is 600 to win a total pot of 1800, giving me 2:1 odds (33.3% equity needed). I have enough depth to set-mine, and I might still be ahead of some of his 3-betting range which could include Broadway cards or smaller pairs. I will call and evaluate the flop.

On a flop of 9h 4c 2c, I am first to act as the Small Blind after calling a 3-bet pre-flop. My pocket sixes are an underpair to the nine but remain a relatively strong hand in a blind-versus-blind 3-bet pot. However, the standard play here is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donking) would likely only get called or raised by hands that beat me (like 99, TT-AA) and fold out hands I'm ahead of (like AK/AQ). By checking, I allow FiveTwo to continue with bluffs or check back with weaker hands. I will check and see how he proceeds.

Blind-vs-blind 3-bet pot and I have the nut flush draw (Ac8c) on 9h-4c-2c. Flash’s check caps him a bit and this board is good for a high-frequency small c-bet from the 3-bettor (I represent overpairs/strong Ax), while my hand has excellent equity when called. A small sizing pressures his broadway/air region, denies some equity, and sets up profitable barrels on many turns (any club, A, 8, and lots of high cards).

On a 9-high board, my pocket sixes are an underpair. However, FiveTwo's 3-bet range in a blind-vs-blind battle is wide, including many overcards like AK, AQ, and AJ. His 1/3 pot bet on the flop is a standard continuation bet. I have roughly 20% pot odds to call, and my hand likely has more than 20% equity against his range. I will call to see a turn and evaluate based on the next card and his subsequent action.

The Ace of Spades on the turn is a pivotal card that significantly favors the pre-flop 3-bettor's range. FiveTwo's range includes many strong Ace-X hands (AK, AQ, AJ, ATs) that have now improved to top pair. My pocket sixes are now essentially a weak bluff-catcher. I will check to the aggressor to see how he reacts to the Ace. If he bets, I will likely fold, as it is very difficult to continue given the board texture and his range advantage.

Turn A♠ gives me top pair plus the nut flush draw in a 3-bet pot. Flash’s check keeps his range wide (9x, small pairs, club draws, some Ax that called pre), and this card heavily favors my perceived range (strong Ax/overpairs). I want to keep initiative, get folds from his 9x/underpairs, and get value/protection versus draws while setting up a river jam on many runouts. A medium sizing accomplishes this without overcommitting vs a check-raise line.

The Ace of Spades on the turn is a disastrous card for my pocket sixes. As the pre-flop 3-bettor in a blind-vs-blind battle, FiveTwo's range is heavily concentrated with high cards, specifically Ace-King, Ace-Queen, and Ace-Jack, all of which have now paired. While I have 3:1 pot odds to call, my hand has very little equity against his value range and even some of his bluffs (like flush draws) have significant equity against me. I previously noted that I would likely fold if he bet into the Ace, and his half-pot sizing confirms he is representing a strong range. I will fold to preserve my stack.

UTG position in 6-handed NLHE with K4o, a weak offsuit hand that is dominated by any better king and has poor playability post-flop. Standard fold to maintain a tight opening range, especially with deep stacks and aggressive opponents observed in prior hands. No value in limping or raising.

76 suited in middle position with Elon already folded. This is a playable speculative hand that can make straights, flushes, and hidden two pairs. With 90BB effective, I have good implied odds. Opening to 2.5x is standard - if I get called, I'll have position on the blinds and can play postflop well with this connected suited hand.

I hold JTo in the Cutoff facing a 2.5x open raise from Claude in Middle Position. JTo is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard MP opening range (which includes AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, KT, KQ). Although I would have position on the raiser, calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the Button or Blinds and exposes me to reverse implied odds post-flop. Against a solid open, folding is the standard and most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button with 9s 4h, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has raised to 250 from middle position. Even with positional advantage, defending or 3-betting with such a poor offsuit hand is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude’s 2.5bb open from middle position puts me out of position in the small blind. Q2o is dominated by his opening range (all better Qx, most broadways, many suited hands) and has poor playability postflop, making a defend too -EV. It’s also not a good 3-bet bluff candidate given no suitedness/connectivity and limited blocker value. Preserve stack and move on.

I’m in the big blind with Q♠4♦ facing a MP open to 250. Pot is 400 and it costs 150 to call (150/(400+150) ≈ 27.3% pot odds). Q4o is frequently dominated by a standard MP opening range (AQ+, KQ, broadways, pairs, suited connectors) and plays poorly out of position — my postflop equity and playability are both weak. A minimum 3-bet is possible but not attractive here (Claude can call or 4-bet and I’d be OOP with a marginal hand). With 105 BBs I’d rather conserve chips and wait for a better spot, so I fold.
72 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. UTG position with 5 players behind me. Clear fold.

I hold a pair of fours in middle position (Hijack). In a standard 6-max game, this is often an open-raise, but it is at the very bottom of the opening range. I am facing aggressive opponents behind me; specifically, Flash in the Cutoff has previously 3-bet my opening raise from this same position (Hand #2), and FiveTwo on the Button has also shown aggression. Opening 44 here risks losing chips to a 3-bet (where I must fold) or playing a difficult pot out of position with a weak underpair. Given the table dynamics and 88BB stack depth, I prefer to avoid this marginal spot and preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Ten offsuit (Ac, Ts). This is a standard opening hand in this position after two players have folded. Opening with a raise allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a post-flop game in position. A 3x raise to 300 is consistent with standard play at this table.

Facing a standard CO open, 7♦4♣ is far below the playable threshold even on the Button: it’s dominated, realizes equity poorly, and creates tough postflop spots despite position. No need to take a marginal -EV defend or spew a light 3-bet this deep.

Facing a cutoff open to 300 from the small blind with J♣5♥: this is a weak, unsuited, and often dominated holding. I'm out of position to the raiser and will still have the big blind acting after me, making postflop life difficult. Calling 250 to play a marginal hand OOP is a poor use of chips, and 3-betting with J5o is too speculative and likely to be exploited. With a healthy stack and many hands left, I’ll conserve chips and fold.

KQo offsuit in BB facing a CO raise to 300 after folds behind. Pot 450, to call 200 (pot odds ~2.25:1). Heads-up if called as SB folded. KQo has strong equity vs CO opening range (dominates AJ, KJs, etc.), good playability postflop. Deep stacks, early in tournament. Calling preserves stack flexibility, sees flop OOP with positionally strong hand. 3-bet possible but prefer calling to pot control and realize equity.

Flop Js7c5c gives me the Kc flush draw (second-nut potential), overcards KQ (both likely live), and good blocker to top pairs. OOP heads-up vs Flash's CO raising range, which is wide and often misses this draw-heavy board (flush draw, OESD with 68, gutshots). Equity ~35-45% vs his range. First to act, checking is optimal: induces bluffs/bets from his weak Ax/high card/missed draws while controlling pot with non-nutted draw. Allows check-raise strong hands or check-call floats. Betting out OOP risks check-raise jams from better and folds out weaker; preserves stack in deep tournament (12050 effective). Will proceed based on his response.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board is Jack-high with two clubs. While I only have Ace-high, I maintain a range advantage over the Big Blind's defending range. Elon checked to me, which is expected. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (325) to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately. Having the Ace of Clubs also gives me a backdoor nut flush draw and blocks some of Elon's potential club draws.

Facing a half-pot c-bet (325 into 650) OOP on Js7c5c with KcQd: nut flush draw (Kc blocks AKc), live K/Q overcards (blocks top pair), ~38% equity vs Flash's CO c-bet range (polarized to Jx/sets/draws/A-high bluffs; board caps his range). His small sizing suggests weakness/polarization, high fold equity. Check-raise semi-bluff optimal: extracts value from worse (AJ/KJ floats, weaker draws), denies equity to Ax, gets dead money now (pot 975). 3x raise (975 total) standard, ~half pot raise sizing post his bet. Deep effective stacks (~7.8k), can fold to 3bet/jam but ahead if called. Calling passive (lets him float/improve cheaply); folding wrong (great pot odds/equity). Builds pot for when we hit; consistent with preflop call inducing action.

I am facing a check-raise from Elon on a Jack-high board with two clubs. While I have the Ace of Clubs (Ac) which provides a backdoor nut flush draw and acts as a blocker to his potential flush draws, Ace-Ten high is currently very weak against a range that includes Jacks, sets, or strong semi-bluffs. Although I am in position, the check-raise is a strong play on this board texture which connects well with a Big Blind defending range (77, 55, 75s, 86s). Calling here would likely force me into a difficult spot on the turn if I don't improve, and 3-betting the flop would be an overplay. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am holding Pocket Nines (9d 9c) in UTG position with a stack of 88 BB. In a 6-handed game, 99 is a premium enough pair to open for value and protection, despite the positional disadvantage. Folding is too tight, and limping caps my range and invites multi-way action which devalues the hand. The table standard for opening raises has been between 2.5x and 3x. I will raise to 250 (2.5 BB) to take the initiative. If 3-bet, I have deep enough stacks to call and set-mine or evaluate based on the opponent.

I am holding Jack-Five offsuit in the middle position. The UTG player (Pro) has opened with a raise to 250. J5o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against an early-position raising range. There is no reason to commit chips to this pot. I will fold.

Pro is opening from UTG, which should still be a relatively strong/tight range in 6-max. Q7o is dominated by a large portion of that range (Qx, broadways, better suited connectors), has poor playability, and tends to realize equity badly even in position due to reverse implied odds. A call is marginal at best and a bluff 3-bet with this hand is too loose versus UTG. Best stack-preserving decision is to fold.

I’m on the button with Td2d facing an UTG open to 250. Pro’s UTG open is likely a fairly strong range (pocket pairs, Axs, broadways), so my hand is often dominated — but it’s suited and has reasonable playability. The price to see a flop is small relative to my stack (250 into a 400 pot; calling requires ~38.5% equity to break even) and I gain the full positional advantage postflop. A 3‑bet here would be thin: I don’t have blockers to A/K or enough strength to isolate UTG profitably, so I prefer to keep the pot manageable and use position to play postflop. Plan: call the 250, fold to a sizable 3‑bet, and on the flop continue only with top pair, good draws (flush/straight) or favorable boards where I can float and leverage position. If I hit a ten or a strong draw I’ll be willing to apply pressure; with nothing I’ll concede to heavy aggression.

T6s is a speculative suited connector, but facing an UTG raise from Pro, who appears tight based on history (only one prior UTG raise that folded to 3bet, otherwise mostly folding or passive calling), and a button call from Minni. UTG range is strong (likely 10-15%: pairs, broadway, suited aces). Equity realization OOP in SB is poor multiway. Pot odds are about 3.25:1 before BB acts, but post-flop position is terrible, and speculative hand needs multiway pots with position, not vs strong range OOP. Deep stacks (130BB), early in tournament (hand 15/150), better to fold and wait for better spots. No raise or bluff value pre.

I have QQ in the BB - a premium hand. Pro opened from UTG (showing strength) and Minni called from Button. With pocket Queens, I should 3-bet for value to build the pot and potentially narrow the field. A raise to around 900-1000 is appropriate (roughly 3.5-4x the open with a caller). I'll make it 950.

I hold Pocket Nines (9d 9c) in UTG position. After opening to 250 and getting a caller on the Button (Minni), Claude in the Big Blind has squeezed to 950. This is a large 3-bet (almost 4x the open), which reduces my implied odds for set-mining (Claude has ~7250 behind, giving roughly 10:1 on the call). However, I will have position on Claude post-flop, which is a significant advantage. Minni's range is capped due to the flat call, and Claude's squeezing range may include bluffs or isolated broadways that I am ahead of. Folding 99 to a single 3-bet when in position is too tight and exploitable. 4-betting would likely only get action from better hands. Therefore, calling to navigate the post-flop in position is the best line.

I already planned to call the 250 open but fold to a sizable 3‑bet. Claude’s reraise to 950 (≈3.8x the open) and Pro’s call make this a multi‑way pot. The pot is 2,200 and I must call 700 more (to a total commitment of 950), which requires ~24.1% equity (700 / (2,200+700)). T♦2♦ is a weak, often‑dominated hand vs a 3‑bet/flatting combination (big pairs, broadways, strong Ax), with only backdoor diamond/wheel potential and much reduced implied odds multiway. A 4‑bet bluff lacks the necessary blockers and would be thin against two opponents. Calling here is a negative‑EV spot so I fold and preserve chips for a better, heads‑up or clearer spot.

I have QQ on a 5s Jh 5h board - an overpair on a paired, relatively dry board. Pro called my 3-bet from UTG, so their range includes hands like AK, AQ, JJ-TT, maybe 99. JJ is concerning but they might have 4-bet AA/KK preflop. My QQ is likely ahead most of the time. I should c-bet for value and protection against the heart draw. Betting around 50-60% pot (1100-1300) is standard here.

I hold Pocket Nines (9d 9c) on a board of 5s Jh 5h. I called a large 3-bet squeeze from Claude (BB) pre-flop. The flop texture connects well with a strong squeezing range, which typically includes hands like JJ, QQ, KK, AA, and AJ, all of which beat my 99. Claude has fired a continuation bet of 1100 (50% pot). While I am ahead of unpaired hands like AK or AQ, the opponent often checks those hands on this texture to realize equity. The bet signifies strength (likely an overpair to the 5s, a Jack, or a higher pocket pair like TT). Since I am drawing to only 2 outs against value hands and would face difficult decisions on later streets, preserving my stack of ~78 BB is the prudent play. I fold.

I am in the Under The Gun (UTG) position with Eight-Six of clubs. This is a speculative hand that is generally too weak to open from the earliest position in a 6-max game, especially with aggressive players like Elon and Claude still to act behind me. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

9♦4♦ is too weak to open from middle position with four players left to act who can 3-bet or take position, and limping just invites an isolation raise and puts us in tough spots with a dominated, low-equity hand. Preserve stack for higher EV opens.

Cutoff with A♣4♣ (ace-four suited) — late position, good stealing and postflop playability. There's been no raise yet (pot 150, to call 100), so I want to open and take advantage of position against the blinds and the button. I will raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) — a standard size that applies pressure, keeps the field manageable, and leaves plenty of stack to play postflop. If the button (Elon) 3-bets large I'll fold and preserve my stack; I will consider calling a small 3-bet to see a flop because the suited ace has good equity and implied odds. If called by SB/BB, plan to c-bet favorable flops and use position to control the pot on dry boards.

Preflop on the button with Kc7h offsuit facing a 3x raise from the cutoff (Minni, who has shown aggression in prior hands).
