Position is excellent, stacks are deep (~100BB), and K7o has decent playability with king high and straight potential. Direct calling exploits position for postflop play, getting good odds (300 to call into 450 pot), with implied odds against a wide range. Folding is too tight from BTN; 3-betting is unnecessary as it risks domination and commits more chips OOP. Will call and reassess postflop.

6h2d is a very weak hand with poor playability. Even with pot odds of about 3:1, this hand will rarely make strong hands and is easily dominated. Folding is the clear play here rather than investing more chips with a junk hand out of position against two opponents.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack suited (Ad Jd), facing a 3x open raise from the Cutoff (Minni) and a call from the Button (Elon). I have excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 750, approx 3.75:1) to close the pre-flop action. While a squeeze (3-bet) to ~1300 is a viable play to isolate opponents and capitalize on dead money, it bloats the pot while Out of Position (OOP) against two players with uncapped ranges. Given the deep stacks and my positional disadvantage, flat calling is the prudent, lower-variance play. It disguises my hand strength, keeps the opponents' ranges wide (including dominated hands), and allows me to realize equity with a hand that plays well post-flop.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) on a Jc-6s-2c board. Being out of position in a multi-way pot, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser (Minni). Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out opponent's bluffs and only get action from strong hands or draws. Checking allows the aggressor to make a continuation bet with a wider range, including bluffs and worse value hands (like KJ, QJ, or lower pairs), and gives me the option to check-call or check-raise depending on the action.

Flop: J♣ 6♠ 2♣ — I hold A♣4♣: the nut-flush draw plus ace-high. I was the preflop raiser; Pro (BB) checked to me and Elon (button) is still to act. This is a clear spot to c-bet: I have 9 outs to the nut flush and additional outs to pair the Ace, so I want to build the pot when I’m equity favorite to improve and use fold equity to take down the pot now. With two opponents I size down a bit but still bet for value/protection — roughly 1/2 pot is appropriate to charge draws and deny free cards while keeping weaker hands that pay me off. I’ll bet 475. If called I’ll reassess the turn: bet for value if I hit a club or pair the Ace, otherwise use pot control and evaluate opponent tendencies; if faced with a large raise I’ll be folding most of the time since I’m a draw and out of position.

Facing a c-bet of 475 into 1425 on Jc6s2c flop with Kc7h, giving me the nut flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity standalone) plus King overcard outs. Pot odds are excellent (475 to win 1425, requiring ~25% equity). Position on button allows cheap realization of equity and potential to take it away later if missed. Minni's cutoff raise and flop bet suggest a wide range including Ax, pairs, draws; Pro's BB check-call pre and check flop indicates weakness. Raising risks folding out better equity or getting jammed on by sets/straights, while calling keeps pot multiway controllable and exploits implied odds with deep stacks (~100BB). Folding too tight given draw strength and position.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-Jack on a J-6-2 board with a flush draw. Facing a half-pot continuation bet from the Cutoff (Minni) and a flat call from the Button (Elon), my hand is strong but I am out of position against two opponents. Raising (check-raising) here risks isolating me against sets, two-pair, or strong combo draws, while folding out the bluffs or weaker hands I currently beat. Calling allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity at a very favorable price (getting nearly 5:1 on a call), and keeps the opponents' ranges wide. It also disguises the strength of my hand, protecting my checking range. I will call and evaluate the turn.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (Ad Jd) on a wet Turn board of Jc-6s-2c-Ts. The Turn card connects with the board, bringing potential straight completions (KQ, 98) and a second flush draw (spades), as well as creating potential two-pair combos like JT. Being Out of Position (OOP) in a multi-way pot against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) and the Button (Elon), leading out would likely only get called by hands that beat me or strong draws, while folding out weaker hands or bluffs. The standard play is to check to the aggressor who c-bet the flop. This allows me to control the pot size, induce bluffs, or re-evaluate if facing significant aggression. My hand is strong enough to call a reasonable bet, but not strong enough to build a massive pot OOP on this texture.

I have A♣4♣ on J♣ 6♠ 2♣ T♠ — nut club draw plus ace-high. Pot is 2375 and it’s checked to me. I have ~12 clean outs (9 clubs + 3 non-club aces) — roughly a 25–27% chance to improve by the river — and I was the preflop/flop aggressor, so I have some fold equity. Betting here charges all non-club hands and weaker pairs that called the flop, builds the pot when I have strong equity, and can fold out pure air. With two opponents I don’t want to bloat the pot unnecessarily but still want meaningful pressure, so I’ll bet roughly half the pot to get called by worse (Jx, small pairs, draws) while keeping the price reasonable if called. If I get a big raise I’ll usually fold (I’m a draw out of position); if called I’ll value-bet when I hit or control pot size on a miss.

Facing a turn bet of 1200 into 3575 on Jc6s2cTs board with Kc7h (king-high, no pair, no made draw, only backdoor flush and gutshot potential with 7 but unlikely). Minni's CO raise pre, flop c-bet, and now turn bet indicate a strong range: likely top pair (J), sets, two-pair, or strong draws (spade flush draw possible with Ts). Pro's pre-call, flop call, and turn check suggest marginal strength or draw that missed. My equity with K-high is poor (~10-20% vs value range), pot odds require ~25% (1200/4775), but realized equity low post-turn multiway with aggressive opponent. Raising as bluff risky OOP for Minni, position helps but not enough; calling commits ~10% stack for minimal showdown value. Folding preserves deep stack (~122BB) for better spots in tournament.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (Ad Jd) on a Turn board of Jc-6s-2c-Ts. The opponent (Minni) is double-barreling for 1200 into a pot of ~2375 (approx. 50% pot). The Turn card brings additional draws (spade flush, straight draws) and potential two-pair combos (JT), but my hand remains very strong relative to the opponent's wider range, which includes semi-bluffs with flush draws and straight draws. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 1200 to win a total pot of 4775, requiring ~25% equity). Folding TPTK here would be too exploitable given the draw-heavy texture. Raising would isolate me against better hands (sets, two pair, overpairs) and fold out the bluffs I am currently beating. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to re-evaluate on the river.

The River card is the Nine of Clubs (9c), making the final board Jc-6s-2c-Ts-9c. This is a very dangerous card as it completes the club flush draw present since the flop, as well as several straight draws (KQ, Q8, 87). My hand, Top Pair Top Kicker (Ad Jd), has significantly decreased in relative strength and is now effectively a bluff-catcher. Leading out (donk betting) would be unwise as worse hands will fold and better hands (flushes, straights, sets, two pair) will call or raise. Checking is the correct play to allow the opponent (Minni) to check back with hands like Overpairs or weaker Top Pairs that are now scared of the board, or to potentially induce a bluff from missed draws (like spade flush draws). I intend to check and evaluate any bet based on sizing, but facing significant aggression on this texture would likely force a fold.

I hold A♣4♣ and the river (J♣ 6♠ 2♣ T♠ 9♣) gives me the nut flush (ace-high clubs). I’m heads-up with Pro who called my flop and turn bets — their calling line is consistent with Jx, a medium pair, some single-club hands or missed draws. Virtually nothing in their plausible range beats the nut flush (only a straight-flush would, which this runout does not allow), so this is a pure value-bet spot. I size to get called by one-pair hands, weaker clubs and missed draws without pricing them out: pot = 4,775, so I’ll bet ~50–55% pot. Action: bet 2,500 to extract value while keeping worse hands comfortable to call.

On the river, the 9c is a terrible card for my hand (Top Pair Top Kicker). It completes the club flush draw that has been present since the flop and completes major straight draws (KQ, 87). The opponent (Minni) has fired three barrels (Flop, Turn, River). Most of the logical semi-bluffs from the turn (clubs, straight draws) have now become value hands. The only draws that missed are spade flush draws. While Minni could be bluffing with missed spades, her range is heavily weighted towards value (flushes, straights, sets, two pair) given the runout. My hand beats nothing but pure air. Getting ~3:1 pot odds is not enough to justify calling against a range that has connected so well with the board. Preserving my stack of ~58BB is preferable to making a low-probability hero call.

6-handed UTG with AQs is a clear open for value and initiative. Limping invites multiway and lets blinds realize equity cheaply; raising pressures dominated Ax/Qx and keeps ranges defined. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x sizing to 250 to reduce callers while not overexposing myself to 3-bets.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 (2.5x BB) — an early-position raise that represents a fairly strong range. I hold Td7c: unsuited, disconnected, and frequently dominated by an UTG raiser. In middle position I will be out of position vs the raiser and still have CO, BTN, SB and BB to act, which increases the chance of a multiway pot or a 3-bet behind. The hand has poor postflop playability and little implied odds, so there's no reason to invest chips here with 130 BBs effective. Fold and wait for a better spot.

2h Ts is an extremely weak offsuit hand with minimal high card strength, poor connectivity, and low playability post-flop. Facing a standard UTG open-raise to 2.5BB from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands (frequent raises and pots won), there is no value in calling, squeezing, or defending. My deep stack (122BB) and cutoff position do not justify entering the pot with this trash hand, as it will be dominated by typical UTG ranges (strong broadway, pairs, suited connectors). Folding preserves chips for superior spots in this multi-player tournament.

KK on the button is a premium hand. FiveTwo opened to 250 and I should 3-bet for value. Standard 3-bet sizing from position is around 3x, so I'll make it 750. This builds the pot with a strong hand while I have position.

I am holding Jc 5h in the Small Blind, facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo and a 3-bet from Claude on the Button. This is a very weak hand (off-suit, unconnected, low playability) to be playing out of position against two players who have shown strength. The pot odds are poor, and the likelihood of being dominated is high. My stack is 58BBs, and preserving chips is far more important than getting involved in a bloated pot with trash cards. Folding is the only correct play.

I am in the big blind with 4c 8s, which is a very weak hand. UTG opened with a raise and the button responded with a 3-bet. Given the strength indicated by a 3-bet over an UTG open and the fact that my hand has almost no playability or equity in this spot, folding is the clear and only logical choice.

AQs is too strong to fold versus a BTN 3-bet, and calling keeps in Claude’s wider 3-bet range (KQ/KJ/QJ/AJ, suited connectors) that a 4-bet would often fold while mostly getting action from better hands. At ~100bb, a 4-bet also risks turning this into an awkward 4-bet/fold if Claude 5-bet jams a tight range (QQ+/AK). I’ll flat to keep the pot controlled OOP with a playable SPR, then proceed mostly check-to-aggressor on the flop and continue on A/Q-high boards and strong draws.

We’re OOP in a heads-up 3-bet pot versus BTN with AQs on K-5-3 (two clubs). This flop favors the 3-bettor’s range (AK/KQ/overpairs) and our hand has limited immediate equity (no club, mostly needing an Ace, plus some marginal Q-high showdown). Leading doesn’t accomplish much and risks getting raised or forcing us into awkward barrels. Best is to check our range, let Claude c-bet his advantage, and then decide based on sizing/frequency (continue vs small bets sometimes, fold more vs larger bets).

I flopped top set with KK on a 5c Kc 3s board - this is a monster. There's a club flush draw out there, so I need to bet for value and protection. FiveTwo called my 3-bet preflop and checked, so they could have hands like AK, QQ, JJ, or suited connectors with a draw. I'll bet around 60% pot to extract value and charge draws.

Claude’s ~60% pot c-bet in a heads-up 3-bet pot on Kc-5c-3s puts a lot of pressure on our range. With AdQs and no club, we have no flush draw, no real straight draw, and our “outs” aren’t clean: an Ace can still be dominated by AK/AA, and a Queen often just improves us to a second-best pair versus his Kx/overpairs. Given the sizing and range advantage for the 3-bettor on this texture, continuing would be a low-EV float that invites more barrels. Best is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

UTG with A♥4♠ — a marginal but playable suited ace. With 130bb effective and a relatively deep table, A4s has enough postflop playability (nut‑flush prospects and ace‑high protection) to warrant an open rather than a limp. UTG should remain reasonably tight, but suited aces are standard openers in 6‑max. I’ll open to the min raise (200) to take the initiative, keep the pot manageable and fold equity intact. Plan: c‑bet favorable textures, avoid bloating the pot multiway where my ace is easily dominated, and fold to large 3‑bets from tight/early opponents while calling small/isolating 3‑bets from the blinds to play postflop for position and equity.

J2o is a complete trash hand with no straight or flush potential, poor post-flop playability, and no domination potential against a typical UTG raising range from Minni. Folding pre-flop to an early position raise is standard to preserve stack for premium spots. Stack depth allows patience with 12200 chips early in tournament (hand 18/150).

65 offsuit in the cutoff facing a UTG raise is a clear fold. This hand has poor playability and equity against an UTG opening range, plus there are still 3 players behind me who could squeeze. Not worth the investment.

I hold 2s 9h on the Button, which is an extremely weak, unsuited hand. Minni opened with a min-raise from UTG. Despite the small raise size offering decent pot odds, 92o has terrible equity and playability against an UTG range. Furthermore, the blinds are still left to act behind me. There is no strategic justification for entering this pot. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s2c, which is one of the weakest hands in Hold'em. Minni opened with a raise from UTG. Given my poor hand strength and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against a player who showed strength by raising from early position, this is a very easy fold. I have a healthy stack and do not need to defend with garbage hands.

Minni’s 2x UTG open gives us an excellent price: we’re calling 100 more to win a pot that will be 450, needing only ~22% equity. 6c5h is low but has decent playability (straights/two-pair possibilities) and should clear that threshold versus a likely wider-than-tight 6-max UTG opening range, especially given Minni’s prior aggression. A 3-bet bluff is unnecessary here: we have no blockers, poor high-card coverage, and we’d inflate a pot OOP with a hand that realizes equity better via a call.

Heads-up BB vs UTG on K♠ T♦ 4♣, this texture heavily favors Minni’s preflop raising range (lots of Kx/broadways) while our 6♣5♥ has essentially no immediate equity—no pair, no flush draw, and only very thin runner-runner straight possibilities. A donk lead would be high-frequency punished and inflates a pot OOP with little ability to continue. Best is to keep our range protected by checking our entire range here and decide versus sizing (we can still defend vs very small c-bets sometimes, but default is check).

I opened UTG with A♥4♠ and got one caller (FiveTwo). Flop K♠ T♦ 4♣ gives me second pair (pair of 4s) with an Ace blocker to some Ax combos and I'm in position heads‑up — a good spot to try a small continuation bet. The board is relatively dry but contains a King and Ten which hit a fair share of the caller's range (Kx, Tx, broadways); however the caller also has many missed hands and draws that will fold to pressure. I want to extract value from worse pairs, pocket pairs below tens and draws, keep the pot manageable vs two‑pair/top‑pair, and retain fold equity. I'll make a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot = 150) so I get value and can fold cheaply to strong resistance: fold to a big check‑raise, call small raises and re‑evaluate on the turn, and barrel only when the turn helps or villain shows weakness.

Minni’s flop c-bet is small (150 into 600), but on K♠ T♦ 4♣ our 6♣5♥ has essentially no immediate equity: no pair, no gutshot/open-ender, no backdoor flush, and very weak backdoor straight potential. We’re being asked to realize ~20% equity (150 to win ~750), and we won’t get there against an UTG c-bet range on this board, especially OOP where our realized equity is worse. Raising is also a poor bluff here (no relevant blockers, and Minni continues with lots of Kx/Tx/pairs/broadways). Best play is to cut losses and fold.

32o is one of the worst possible starting hands in No-Limit Hold'em. As UTG in a 6-handed game, this is a clear fold to preserve the stack for much better opportunities. No equity, no playability, and acting first pre-flop. Opponents have shown aggression with stronger ranges in previous hands, so no reason to limp or raise with trash.

5s8h is a weak offsuit hand in middle position with 4 players still to act behind me. This hand has poor playability - not suited, gaps between cards, and I'll be out of position postflop. Standard fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Three offsuit (Td 3h). Action has folded to me. This hand is statistically very weak and falls well outside of a standard opening range, even for a late-position steal attempt. With active players still to act behind me (Flash on Button, and large stacks in the blinds), investing chips with this hand has a negative expected value. The correct play is to fold immediately.
Action has folded to me on the button. Ts 9d is a standard hand to open for a steal attempt against the blinds. I will raise to 300 to pick up the blinds or play the pot with position.

Button is opening a wide range and we’re in the SB with a hand that plays poorly as a flat (domination issues, low realization OOP, BB can come along). A♠8♣ has decent blocker value vs strong Ax and can win preflop often, while also playing acceptably in a 3-bet pot with initiative. I’ll 3-bet to a bigger OOP sizing to pressure the button and discourage the BB from overcalling; if Flash 4-bets, we can comfortably fold this offsuit A8.

Fold. Facing a small‑blind 3‑bet to 1,100 after a button open, 6♠7♣ (offsuit) is too weak to continue. This is likely to be a 3‑way pot where I will be out of position to both opponents; I have poor equity and significant reverse implied odds against their 3‑bet/value ranges (pairs, broadway Aces, strong suited hands). Calling 1,000 more (about 10 BB) to play multiway OOP is a losing long‑term play here, and a 4‑bet/shove with this holding is unreasonable. Preserve the 131 BB stack and wait for a better spot.

I opened from the button with T9o as a standard steal attempt. FiveTwo responded with a 3-bet to 1100 from the small blind. While I would have position post-flop, T9o is significantly behind a typical 3-betting range from the SB and does not realize equity well enough to justify a call of 800 more. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
72 offsuit in UTG - one of the worst hands in poker from the worst position. Easy fold.

I hold Kd 3s in Middle Position. This is a very weak hand (King-Three offsuit) that is well below the standard opening range for this position. With four players left to act behind me, including aggressive opponents in late position, opening this hand would be -EV. My stack of 58 BB is healthy, so there is no need to make loose desperate plays. The correct decision is to fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Seven suited. While this is a marginal opening hand in many situations, the table dynamics are currently unfavorable for a light open. FiveTwo (on the Button) has 3-bet my opening raises twice in the last few hands (Hand 19 and Hand 12), and Elon and Minni in the blinds have also shown significant aggression. Given that I have been forced to fold to 3-bets or post-flop aggression recently, I will tighten my opening range here and fold this speculative hand to avoid being exploited out of position by the aggressive players remaining in the hand.

Folded to us on the button, but Th5d is a very weak offsuit hand that performs poorly when called/3-bet, and both blinds (Minni/Elon) have shown willingness to apply pressure and defend/raise. A steal raise needs a high fold frequency to be immediately profitable, and a limp risks getting iso-raised and bleeding chips with a dominated holding. Best EV is to pass and wait for better button opens.

Everyone else folded, so this is heads-up vs the big blind (Elon). 4♣2♠ offsuit is a very weak, easily dominated hand with poor postflop playability out of position. The small cost to see a flop is only 50 chips, but calling or trying to steal by raising invites difficult postflop decisions or a 3-bet from an active opponent — negative EV spots I want to avoid early in the tournament with deep stacks. Preserve the stack for better spots and fold.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Five offsuit (Q5o), which is a statistically weak hand. With five opponents yet to act, the probability that someone holds a stronger hand is high. Additionally, playing out of position with a marginal hand is a losing strategy long-term. My stack of ~58 BB allows me to be patient and wait for better opportunities. Therefore, the correct action is to fold.
I am holding J2o in middle position. This is one of the weakest starting hands and does not warrant a play, especially from this position. Folding is the only viable strategic option.

Two folds in front, but 9♣4♠ offsuit is well below a profitable cutoff opening range. Limping is generally -EV (invites isolation and plays poorly multiway), and raising risks getting 3-bet or playing out of position vs the blinds with a dominated, low-equity hand. With three players still to act behind (BTN/SB/BB), folding preserves stack and avoids thin spots.

Button with K♥2♣ — marginal but playable because of position and fold equity. Pot is 150 and call is 100; I want to seize the initiative and steal the blinds, so I’ll open to a standard ~3x BB. Raising to 300 applies pressure to the SB/BB, keeps the pot manageable with a dominated hand, and allows me to use position postflop (c-bet favorable flops, pot-control on dangerous textures). If I face a sizable 3-bet from Elon or Claude I will fold; if I get one caller I’ll proceed aggressively on dry/K-high boards and be cautious on ace/connected boards.

AcTc is a strong suited ace from the SB facing a button open-raise to 3BB. Button ranges are wide, and this hand has excellent equity, playability, and domination potential against their Ax, broadways, and suited connectors. Position is poor OOP, so 3-betting is better than calling to build pot, deny equity, and take initiative. Standard 3-bet size around 3x their raise (900 total). BB may call or fold, but we have good post-flop prospects. Folding or flatting is too passive given hand strength and villain's aggressive history from late position.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon in the SB with ATo. Getting ~1.6:1 pot odds (800 to win 1300). ATo is a decent hand that plays reasonably well postflop. Not strong enough to 4-bet, but too strong to fold given pot odds. Calling to see a flop where I'll have position on Elon if Minni folds.

I opened to 300 with K♥2♣ to steal from the button, but Elon 3-bet to 900 and Claude called, creating a 3-bet multiway pot (pot ~2,100; I must call 600 more). K2o is poorly positioned here — heavily dominated by typical 3-bet ranges and has weak multiway postflop play and reverse implied odds. Calling for a good price isn’t worth the structural disadvantages, and 4-betting as a bluff is unprofitable with a caller already in. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Top pair top kicker (Tc pairs Ts, Ac kicker) on a coordinated board with spade flush draw and gutshot straight draws (e.g., 67, 96). Preflop 3-bet aggression gives us range advantage over Claude's BB calling range, which includes Ax suited, suited connectors, pocket pairs, and broadways but capped somewhat OOP. C-betting for thin value against worse pairs/draws, protection vs overcards or floats, and to deny equity to implied odds hands. OOP heads-up pot of 2100, standard 2/3 pot size (1400) balances value and fold equity without overbetting into potential monsters. Check-fold too passive, check-raise risks missing value.

Flopped top pair top kicker (TPTK) with ATo on Ts-8d-5s. Strong hand but the board has a spade flush draw. Elon 3-bet pre and is c-betting 2/3 pot. His range includes overpairs (QQ-AA where I'm crushed), AK/AQ (where I'm ahead), and possibly some suited connectors. TPTK is too strong to fold here - I'm beating his bluffs and missed AK/AQ. Raising commits me too heavily if he has an overpair. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me reevaluate on the turn with position.

TPTK on a very draw-heavy board (straight draws like 69, open-enders with 6x/9x, strong spade flush draws with three spades now). Preflop 3-bet range gives us significant range advantage over Claude's BB defending call (suited Ax, broadways, suited gappers/connectors, mid pairs). He flatted flop c-bet, which caps his range somewhat (less likely pure air, more pairs/draws/slowplays). Turn 7s is a scary card that hits many of his calling hands (e.g., 65s/97s/6s/9s improve to straights or stronger draws), but also bricks some overcards/floats. OOP, continuing aggression with a bet extracts value from worse pairs (e.g., 85/T5/T8/87), naked draws (e.g., KJss, 96s), and weaker Tpx (worse kickers), while protecting against overcards turning into pairs and denying equity to rivers. Pot control via check risks giving free cards to draws that beat us or check-behind value. ~2/3 pot bet (3200 into 4900) balances value, fold equity vs draws, and keeps stack committed reasonably (leaves ~6700 behind vs his 8950). Larger risks overplaying, smaller too thin/checkable.

I have TPTK plus the nut flush draw (As). Board is Ts-8d-5s-7s with 3 spades. The 7s turn adds a flush draw possibility for opponents and creates straight possibilities (9x makes a straight). My As gives me 9 outs to the nut flush (~18% on river). Combined with TPTK equity against some of his range, I'm around 30-35% equity. Pot odds are 3200/11300 ≈ 28%. With implied odds if I hit the nuts and the chance my top pair is good, this is a call. Can't fold the nut flush draw with top pair.

River 7c pairs the 7s, creating full houses with 77, trips with 7x, and improves some weaker pairs to two pair or trips (e.g., 75, 65 to boat possibilities or trips). However, it's a relatively neutral card for many turn calling ranges: doesn't complete the spade flush (still three spades on board), bricks some straight draws (e.g., 96, 69 partially), and doesn't heavily favor villain's range over mine. My AcTc remains TPTK, ahead of all weaker Tx (e.g., KTs, QTs, JTs, suited connectors like 98s/T9s that pair T or 8), naked draws that paired up poorly, and mid pairs that improved insignificantly. Preflop 3-bet + double-barrel aggression gives massive range advantage vs Claude's OOP BB calling range (defends ~20-30%: strong Ax suited/high broadway, pockets 66-JJ, suited connectors/gappers like 87s/65s). He flatted large OOP bets on draw-heavy board, capping somewhat at pairs/draws/slowplays but unlikely monsters given history of folding to aggression (e.g., Hands 3,5,11). Pot 11300 with short effective stacks (~5750 his side), first to act OOP: checking risks check-raise from nuts or free showdown for his marginal hands while missing value/check-behind; induces occasional bluffs but suboptimal vs calling station tendencies seen in priors (e.g., Hand 1 river call, Hand 16 multi-street). Value betting/shoving extracts from ~40-60% equity hands (worse Tpx, 8x pairs, 5x now two pair/trips?, missed/improved draws), fold equity vs air/semi-bluffs, and denies chop equity. 6750 shove (~60% pot) is pot-committing size, reps immense strength (sets, two pairs, straights, flushes in range), maximizes EV by polarizing while thin-valuing the middle of his range that hero-calls lighter OOP.

River paired 7s and I missed my flush draw. I have top pair top kicker but facing an all-in for my remaining 5750 into 18050. Pot odds need ~24% equity. Elon 3-bet pre, bet flop, bet turn, now shoved river - this is a very strong line. His value range includes: overpairs (JJ-AA), flushes (suited spades), full houses (77, 55, 88), and straights (96s). I'm only beating pure bluffs like AK/AQ with no spade. After 3 barrels, his bluff frequency is likely below 24%. My TPTK is essentially a bluff-catcher and the board runout favors his polarized range. Tough spot but folding is correct here.

I am in Under The Gun (UTG) position with Jack-Nine offsuit (J9o). In a 6-handed game, J9o is well below the standard opening range for this position. Opening here would likely put me out of position against several opponents with a weak hand that is easily dominated. Given the tournament context and my current stack, there is no strategic reason to play this hand from UTG.

We’re first in from middle position after UTG folds. K8 offsuit is too easily dominated (KQ/KJ/KT, A8, etc.) and plays poorly multiway and out of position versus the CO/BTN, especially with Elon/Minni capable of applying 3-bet pressure. With ~100bb effective, the small steal EV doesn’t outweigh the reverse-implied-odds and tough postflop spots. Best is to keep the range tighter here and pass.

Cutoff with Js4s after two folds is a good spot to seize the initiative. Js4s is marginal but suited and plays well post-flop in position against the button and the blinds, so I want to open for fold equity. I will open to 3x the big blind (to 300) — bigger than the min to discourage light calls from the blinds but not so large that I bloat the pot with a speculative hand. Plan: if Elon (button) or a blind 3-bets I will fold to significant aggression ( Js4s is easily dominated), but if I get one caller I will use position to c-bet on dry flops and control the pot on dangerous boards. This raise balances aggression and risk management to maximize EV over the long run.

Q3o is a trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, dominated by any queen or better. Facing a standard 3x raise from cutoff by aggressive Minni, even on button position doesn't justify calling or raising. Calling commits chips to a hand with little equity or playability postflop. 3-bet bluff range doesn't include this; better to fold and wait for better spots. Stack is deep at 180BB, no need to speculate.
J5o is a weak hand out of position against a cutoff raise. Minni has been active and aggressive. This hand doesn't play well postflop OOP - easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 5h. Minni raised to 300 (3bb) from the Cutoff. The action folded to me. The pot is 450, and I need to call 200, giving me pot odds of roughly 3.25:1 (requiring ~31% equity). K5s is a playable hand against a wide Cutoff opening range, often having around 35-40% equity. Although being out of position is a disadvantage, the suited nature of the hand and the good price justify a defense. Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary. I will call to see a flop.

I defended the Big Blind with Kh5h against a Cutoff raise. The flop comes 2c Qh 3d, which is a very dry board. I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw (one heart on board) and a gutshot wheel draw requiring an Ace and a Four, or just a Four to make an open-ended straight draw. I have very little immediate equity. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) on this texture is generally ill-advised as the board favors the raiser's range (sets, strong Queens). I will check and evaluate Minni's action.

I was the preflop aggressor (raised to 300) and got one caller from the big blind. The flop 2c Qh 3d is very dry and misses a large portion of the caller's range — a textbook spot to continuation-bet and try to take the pot down. I have no pair (Js4s) and only backdoor spade potential, so I want fold equity rather than building a big pot with a marginal hand. I'll c-bet about half the pot (300 into 650) to represent a Q or stronger, fold to a strong check-raise, and if called re-evaluate on the turn (continue only with improvement or clear equity/river-barrel opportunities). This sizing balances pressure and pot control against a generally passive caller.

On the flop of [2c Qh 3d], I have King-high with extremely weak backdoor potential (backdoor hearts and backdoor wheel). The board is dry and connects reasonably well with a Cutoff opening range that contains Queens and pairs. Facing a bet of 300 into a pot of 650 (approx 46% pot), I am getting roughly 4:1 odds, requiring ~24% equity to call. While I might be ahead of pure bluffs, I am drawing very thin against any value hand (pairs+). Playing out of position with just King-high and no immediate draws is unprofitable. The standard and disciplined play here is to release the hand.

6-handed from UTG, K8o is too weak and too easily dominated by the continuing ranges behind (especially with capable/aggressive stacks like Elon/Minni). Limping invites multiway play with reverse implied odds, and opening risks getting 3-bet and having to fold or play bloated pots out of position. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for stronger opens.

I’m first to act in middle with Q4o — a weak, offsuit queen with lots of players yet to act (Cutoff Elon, Button Claude, SB Pro, BB Flash). The pot is only 150 and calling 100 (or min-raising to 200) puts me into a multiway pot where I’ll be frequently dominated by better Qx and broadway hands. With deep stacks I should pick stronger, positionally advantaged spots; open-raising here is thin and calling risks tough postflop decisions and being 3-bet/raised by big stacks. Fold and wait for a better hand or position.

Preflop in cutoff with folds from UTG and MP, facing unraised blinds (pot 150, to call 100). T6s is a suited hand with straight and flush potential, playable in position. Late position after two folds is a good spot to open-raise for value/thin value or to steal blinds, as blinds may fold often. Opponents have shown aggression but also folds to raises. Standard open size around 2.5x BB (250). Raising builds pot in position and denies equity to worse hands.

87 suited on the button facing a min-raise from cutoff. This is a good spot to call with a suited connector - I have position and the hand plays well postflop. Elon has been active but calling in position lets me see a flop with a speculative hand that can make straights, flushes, and two pairs.

I am in the Small Blind holding 9s5d, which is a very weak unsuited hand. The action involves a raise from the Cutoff and a call from the Button. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.25:1 (calling 200 to win 650), playing a weak hand out of position against two active opponents is a losing strategy in the long run due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. Folding is the only standard decision here.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jd 6d. Elon (Cutoff) raised to 250, and Claude (Button) called. The pot currently stands at 650, and it costs me only 150 more to call. This gives me pot odds of approximately 5.33 to 1. While my hand is speculative and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the price is too good to pass up for a suited hand with some high card potential. I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a board of 6d-5c-3h. In a multi-way pot out of position against Elon (the original raiser) and Claude, checking is the standard play to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts to this board. This allows me to gauge the strength of their hands and potentially keep the pot small if I am outkicked or if someone is drawing to a straight/flush. I will check and see what develops.

Flop 5c 3h 6h gives me top pair (6s) with a Ten kicker in a multi-way pot (800). Board has two hearts, so strong flush draws possible, and straight draws like 2-4 or 4-7. I raised preflop from CO with T6s, which is in my range, and Flash (BB) checked first, suggesting weakness. Position on Claude (BTN). Betting for value against worse pairs/draws and protection against equity realization. Half-pot size (400) balances value/induction while keeping pot manageable deep-stacked. Checking risks giving free cards to draws.

Flopped an open-ended straight draw with 87cc on a 5-3-6 board. Any 4 or 9 gives me the nuts. I also have backdoor clubs. Getting 1600:400 = 4:1 pot odds, which is plenty with ~32% equity (8 outs twice). Calling is better than raising here - I have position, good implied odds, and can re-evaluate on the turn. Flash still to act behind me could squeeze if I raise.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a board of 6d-5c-3h. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has placed a half-pot bet of 400, and Claude has called. I am getting 5:1 pot odds (400 to win a total pot of 2000), which makes a call very profitable with top pair. While the board is wet with straight and flush draws, raising here might bloat the pot unnecessarily while I am out of position against two opponents. Calling allows me to see the turn, evaluate the board texture, and keep the pot manageable with a medium-strength hand.

The turn 5s pairs the board, giving me two pair (6s and 5s). I am out of position against Elon (the pre-flop aggressor) and Claude. Since the 5s is a card that could have easily improved someone to trips (especially since 5 is in many calling ranges) or is simply a neutral card, checking is the most appropriate action. It allows me to control the pot size and see if the others are willing to continue betting. I have a medium-strength hand that is worth seeing a river with, but not necessarily worth leading out into two opponents.

Turn pairs the board with 5s, improving my top pair sixes (6s pairs the 6h) with a strong Ten kicker to a strong hand in a multiway pot. Flash checks again, likely weak or trapping, but given history of calling stations and folds, unlikely premium. Board has heart flush draw still live and gutshots, but paired fives may have scared off some straight draws. My range is perceived strong as preflop raiser. Betting for thin value against Flash's possible weaker pairs, floats, or draws, and to charge Claude. Half-pot bet of 1000 builds pot without overbetting deep stacks, induces calls from worse, and protects against overcards or draw completion. Checking risks giving free cards to equity-heavy hands behind.

Turn paired the board with 5s. I still have my open-ended straight draw (4 or 9 completes). Pot is 3000, need to call 1000 - getting 3:1 pot odds. With 8 outs (~17% equity), direct odds aren't quite there, but I have position and implied odds if I hit. The paired board is concerning as trips/full house are possible, but my straight would still beat most hands. Flash still to act - if they raise I can fold. Calling to see the river with position.

I am currently closing the action on the turn. I hold Jd 6d on a board of 5c 3h 6h 5s, giving me two pair (6s and 5s) with a Jack kicker. Elon (the pre-flop aggressor) has bet 1000 into a 2000 pot, and Claude has called. The pot is now 4000, and it costs me 1000 to call, giving me pot odds of 4 to 1 (or 1000 to win a total pot of 5000). While the board pairing the 5 is a concern because it puts me behind any hand with a 5, my two pair is still relatively strong in a multi-way pot. Given the excellent price and the fact that I am closing the action, I have enough equity against their ranges (which include heart draws and smaller pairs) to justify a call and see the river.

I have two pair (6s and 5s) with a Jack kicker on a board of 5c-3h-6h-5s-Th. I am first to act on the river in a three-way pot. The Ten of Hearts is a dangerous card as it completes the heart flush draw and is an overcard to my pair of sixes. Given that Elon has shown aggression on every street and Claude has been calling along, there is a high probability that one of them has either a better two pair, a five, or has now completed a flush. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and see how the other two players respond to the river card before deciding whether to commit any more chips.

River Th pairs my Ten, giving me top two pair (Tens and Sixes with Td, 6s, board Th, 6h, 5c, 5s, 3h). This is a very strong hand on a paired board, beating any single pair, weaker two pairs, missed draws, and most bluffs. Heart flush is possible (three hearts now: 3h, 6h, Th), but Flash has checked four times (pre/call, flop check/call, turn check/call, river check), showing weakness or trapping, unlikely nuts given passive history. Claude called all streets behind, potentially on draws or medium strength. Pot 5000, value bet ~70% pot (3500) extracts max value from worse hands like pairs (fives, sixes, tens without pairing), gutshots, or missed flush/straight draws. Sizing pressures Claude's 4050 stack (all-in call) and Flash's 5725. Checking risks giving free showdown or check-raise, but betting builds on my aggressive image as preflop raiser. Deep stack (16400) allows this without commitment issues.

Missed my straight draw completely - I have 8-high with no showdown value. The river Ten of hearts also completed the heart flush draw. Elon has been betting every street and now fires 3500 into a 5000 pot. With a paired board, he could easily have trips, a full house, or a flush. I have zero equity here and cannot call 3500 (almost my entire stack) with 8-high. Clear fold.

Elon has triple-barreled into two opponents on a board that significantly improved throughout the hand. My hand, Jd 6d, provides me with two pair (6s and 5s), but the Ten of Hearts on the river is a very dangerous card. It completes the heart flush and provides a higher pair for any 10 in Elon's range. Given that Elon raised pre-flop and continued betting on every street, his range likely consists of overpairs (77-AA), trips (any 5), or a completed flush. I only beat a total bluff or a smaller pocket pair like 44 or 22, which is unlikely given the triple-barrel line into two players. Claude, who was calling along, has already folded, suggesting Elon's bet of 3500 into 5000 is extremely strong. I will fold and preserve my remaining 4725 chips.

UTG with J♥3♠ is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability and lots of dominated scenarios. In early position at a 6-handed table I need a tight opening range — limping or making a tiny 2x open here is easily exploited by the aggressive players behind and calling 100 to play multiway out of position is negative EV. Stacks are deep but that doesn’t change that J3o performs poorly postflop and offers little fold equity or implied odds. Preserve the stack, wait for a better spot.

7s4d is a trash hand: offsuit, low cards, no straight or flush potential. Preflop from middle position after UTG fold, with pot 150 and to call 100 (limp), folding is standard. No position advantage, deep stacks but no need to speculate with this. Opponents active but this hand unplayable. Preserve stack for premium spots.

In cutoff with 67o after two folds. This is a decent steal spot - connected cards with some playability. Stack is ~40BB which is comfortable for a standard open. I'll raise to 2.5x to put pressure on the remaining three players and try to pick up the blinds.

I am on the Button with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o). The Cutoff (Claude) has raised to 250. Q2o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity against a standard Cutoff opening range. Although I have position, the hand strength does not justify a call or a bluff 3-bet, especially against an active opponent who has shown willingness to play large pots. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind holding King-Jack offsuit. Claude (Cutoff) has opened for a standard raise of 2.5x (250). Claude is the short stack at the table with 38 BBs but has shown consistent aggression in previous hands. King-Jack is a strong enough hand to continue with against a late-position open, though playing out of position is a disadvantage. While 3-betting is an option to take control of the hand, Claude's shorter stack makes a 3-bet somewhat committal if he decides to shove. Calling 200 chips into a pot that already has 400 provides 2:1 odds, which is sufficient for a hand like KJo. I will call and evaluate the flop, keeping in mind that the Big Blind (FiveTwo) is still to act and could potentially squeeze.

We’re closing the action getting a decent price (150 to win ~600), but 34o is an extreme reverse-implied-odds hand: it makes weak pairs/straights that are often dominated in a raised, multiway pot, and we’ll be out of position to both the preflop raiser and the caller. Our equity realization will be poor and there’s no attractive squeeze option with this holding versus a short opener and a caller. Best decision is to give up the blind and wait for a better defend/squeeze candidate.

On this paired board of 9s-8s-9d, I am first to act after calling from the Small Blind. My hand (KJo) has missed the flop entirely. The board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range slightly, but it's also quite draw-heavy with the flush and straight possibilities. Since I am out of position against Claude, the standard play is to check to the raiser. I have very little incentive to bet here as I don't want to build a pot with only King-high, and checking allows me to see Claude's reaction. If he checks back, I might have the best hand or at least a free turn card. If he bets, I will evaluate based on his sizing, though KJo is a weak candidate for a check-call on this texture.

Flop is 9s8s9d - paired board with spade flush draw possible. I have 67o giving me an open-ended straight draw (any 5 or T makes a straight). Flash checked to me as the preflop aggressor. This is a good c-bet spot: I have position, the betting lead, and decent equity with my OESD. A small c-bet of around 1/3 pot should take this down often on this paired board that's hard to connect with.

On a flop of 9s-8s-9d, I have missed completely with King-Jack offsuit. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a total pot of 1000, or 4:1), my hand has very little equity against Claude's range. Claude, as the pre-flop aggressor, has all the overpairs (10s through Aces) and potentially a 9 in his range. Even if he is betting with air or a draw like a flush draw or straight draw (7-10, Q-J), I am out of position and will find it extremely difficult to realize my equity on future streets if I don't improve. King-high is rarely the best hand here when facing a continuation bet, even a small one. Given that I have the King of Spades, I block some flush draws, but this doesn't significantly improve my standing. I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than peeling a turn card out of position with a weak holding.

UTG with 2h7d, one of the worst possible starting hands in poker. No suitedness, connectors, or high card value. Early position means playing tight to avoid tough post-flop spots out of position. Pot is just blinds (150), to call 100 but folding is standard to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Long-term tournament play requires discipline.
45 offsuit is a weak hand in middle position with 4 players still to act behind me. Not worth opening or limping - should fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Four offsuit (4d Ts). This is a statistically very weak hand with no connectivity or suited potential. With three players yet to act behind me, including large stacks in the blinds who can easily defend, attempting to steal here is negative EV. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Action has folded to me on the button. While I have position, King-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to open with, especially since both the Small Blind (FiveTwo) and Big Blind (Minni) have shown significant aggression in previous hands and have larger stacks than me. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand to take advantage of my position.

Action folds to us in the SB with only the BB behind, so this is a prime steal spot. Even though 5-2 offsuit is very weak, the pot is already 150 and a small raise puts immediate pressure on BB’s widest/weakest range. Using a modest open size keeps risk contained and lets us fold comfortably to a 3-bet while still generating fold equity and initiative when called.

FiveTwo opened to 250 from the small blind. I’m in the big blind with T♣8♠ — a marginal but defendable hand against a likely wide SB opening range in 6-max. Calling 150 (250 total committed) gives good pot odds and keeps position postflop (button folded), allowing me to see a flop cheaply and play draws/top-pair hands for value. A 3-bet here would bloat the pot and risk committing chips with a marginal hand, so I’ll defend and evaluate on the flop.

We’re the preflop raiser SB vs BB on an A♣Q♣3♣ monotone flop. Our actual hand is very weak (no pair, no made flush), but we do have the 2♣ which blocks some of BB’s small flushes and makes this a good spot to leverage range advantage with a small, high-frequency c-bet. A small sizing pressures BB’s many non-club holdings (random broadways, weak pairs, offsuit trash) while keeping our risk contained when they continue with any club/strong Ax. If we get raised, we can comfortably fold; if called, we can reassess turns (notably a 4 gives us a wheel straight).

I defended preflop with T♣8♠ and the SB has c-bet the flop very small (175 into 675) on Qc–3c–Ac. I have no pair but I hold T♣, so I have a one-card-to-a-flush now (need one more club on turn/river). That gives me meaningful equity (~19% on the turn, ~35% by the river) plus a few pair outs. The price to see the next card is excellent — calling 175 is cheap and keeps weaker A/Q/high-card hands and bluffs in the pot. A raise is unnecessary and risky here: the board heavily favors the SB’s raising/calling range (many two-club combos already make a flush or have A/Q), so a raise would bloat the pot with only a draw and no pair. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to big turn pressure if I miss, but play aggressively (value/raise) if I pair my Ten or hit a club.

Turn brings the 4th club, giving us a flush but the absolute bottom one (A♣Q♣J♣3♣2♣). Betting for value is thin because most continues contain a club and virtually any club beats us; if we bet we mostly get called/raised by better flushes and fold out the no-club hands we actually want to keep in. Checking keeps the pot controlled with a vulnerable made hand and allows BB to bluff/semi-value bet worse (Ax/Qx without a club) at some frequency, letting us bluff-catch versus reasonable sizing.

I turned a made flush: T♣ with the board Q♣–3♣–A♣–J♣ gives me a very strong hand. With four clubs on board any club in FiveTwo’s hand makes a flush, and I beat all club-hands except K♣ (10♣ is in my hand so a royal is impossible). FiveTwo checked the turn, so this is a good spot to extract value and charge worse clubs, pairs, and bluffs. I’ll bet ~50% pot (425 into 850) — big enough to get called by worse flushes and pairs but not so large it commits me unnecessarily if I face a big check-raise (which would often represent the rare K♣). If FiveTwo raises big I’ll re-evaluate (they’re often representing K♣), but for now a half-pot value bet is the best line.

We’ve made a flush, but it’s the absolute bottom (2♣-high) on a 4-club board. Versus Minni’s line (call on monotone flop, then lead 1/2 pot when the 4th club hits), their betting range is overwhelmingly hands containing at least one club—which are all flushes that beat us. The portion of their range with *no* club (Ax/Qx/sets) is very unlikely to choose this sizing into a preflop raiser on a 4-flush texture, so there aren’t enough bluffs to justify continuing.

Even though we’re getting ~25% pot odds, calling is basically a bluff-catch only, and we also risk facing additional river pressure and having to fold anyway. Raising is worse since any continuing hand (a club) crushes us. Best play is to let this go and preserve stack.
3d 7s is a very weak hand - unsuited, disconnected, no high cards. From UTG position I need a strong range. This is an easy fold.

In a 6-handed game, holding Ace-Six suited (A6s) in Middle Position (MP) is generally a standard opening hand, as it falls within the Hijack opening range (usually A2s+ or A5s+ depending on tightness). With 55 big blinds, my stack is deep enough to play poker post-flop. Although the players in the blinds (Minni and Elon) have large stacks and could apply pressure, playing too passively by folding suited Aces will result in a dwindling stack over time. I will raise to 250 (2.5x BB), which has been a standard opening size at this table, to isolate or steal the blinds.

I am holding a small pocket pair (3s 3c) in the Cutoff. A middle position player (Pro) has raised to 250. The stack-to-call ratio is approximately 21:1, which makes set-mining mathematically viable. While there are active players behind me (FiveTwo, Minni, and Elon), calling here is a standard play to try and hit a set on the flop. If I miss the set and face significant aggression, I can easily fold later.

On the button with Q8hh versus a MP open (250) and a CO flat, this is a good squeeze candidate: we have position, suited playability, and there’s meaningful dead money (650) to win. A 3-bet also discourages the blinds from coming along/squeezing and puts pressure on Pro/Flash ranges that are often capped/over-fold to aggression in these spots. If we get 4-bet (especially from the 52bb stacks), we can comfortably fold; if called, we’ll have initiative and position to c-bet many flops and realize our equity when we pick up draws.

Facing a button 3-bet to 950 after Pro opened to 250, the pot is 1600 and it costs me 900 more to call ( ~36% pot odds ). I'm in the small blind with 8♠9♣ offsuit — a marginal, poorly-playing hand out of position that performs badly multiway. The button's 3-bet range is likely strong or at least polarized and Pro could still be involved; 4-betting as a bluff is thin here given effective stacks and the likelihood of callers or a 5-bet. I should preserve my stack and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

Jd6h is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Preflop action shows Pro's open-raise from middle position, Flash's call from cutoff, and FiveTwo's 3-bet from button to 950 (9.5BB). Facing a raise and 3-bet multiway, this hand has no equity realization potential against likely strong ranges. Pot odds (850 to call into 1600) are insufficient for speculative hands like this. Standard fold from BB to preserve stack for better spots.

I opened A6s from MP and was called by the Cutoff, then 3-bet (squeezed) by the Button (FiveTwo) to 950. While I have a healthy stack of ~50 BB, calling a squeeze 3-bet out of position with A6s is a losing play long-term. My hand is often dominated by the 3-bettor's value range (AK, AQ, AJ) and plays poorly post-flop in this spot due to reverse implied odds. FiveTwo has shown aggression previously (Hands #12, #19), but defending here is too loose. I need to preserve my chips for better spots.

I am holding 3s 3c in the Cutoff. After my initial call of Pro's raise, FiveTwo on the Button has 3-bet to 950. To continue, I must call 700 more. To profitably 'set mine' with a small pair, I generally look for implied odds of at least 15:1 or 20:1. In this scenario, my remaining stack is 4275 and the call is 700, giving me implied odds of roughly 6:1. This is insufficient to justify a call, especially as I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against an aggressive opponent. Folding is the mathematically sound decision here.

I am in UTG position with King-Six offsuit (Ks6h). This is a statistically weak hand, especially to play from early position where I have five opponents left to act. The risk of being dominated by better Kings or Ace-X hands is high, and the hand plays poorly post-flop due to lack of connectivity and suitedness. With a healthy stack of over 50 big blinds, there is no pressure to make loose moves. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better spots.

Holding Queen of Clubs and Three of Diamonds in middle position is a very weak hand. It lacks connectivity and has poor post-flop potential. With several players still to act, there is a high likelihood of being outplayed or facing a raise. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket 7s is a clear open from the cutoff 6-handed: it plays well in position, can win preflop often, and has strong postflop value (overpairs to many boards + set potential). With no raise in front, I want to apply pressure to the blinds and deny them a cheap flop. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x sizing that keeps the pot manageable versus deeper stacks while still generating folds and building value when called.

Button with A5s vs a standard cutoff open to 250 — this is a clear keep-in-position hand. Calling 250 is the best line: A5s has good playability and blockers, and I can use position to outplay opponents postflop. Pot is 400, so calling 250 gives ~2.6:1 immediate odds to see a flop; I avoid bloating the pot or getting 4-bet by Elon with a small 3-bet. A min 3-bet to 400 is too small to accomplish much and a larger 3-bet commits too many chips out of position to potential aggression from the blinds. If Claude (short stack) jams I can reassess, but for now preserve stack and play postflop in position. Calling keeps options open to c-bet for value/bluff or fold to heavy resistance.

AKo is a premium hand, top of my range from SB facing a CO raise and button call. FiveTwo's CO opening range is wide, including many Ax, broadway, suited connectors, pairs. Minni's button call is likely wide and loose based on history. I should 3-bet for value and isolation, as AKo performs well heads-up and can fold out worse broadway hands. OOP multiway calling is bad. 3-bet sizing to ~3.6x the raise (900 total) accounts for the call behind, building a pot while denying equity to weaker hands. BB (short stack) may call or squeeze, but we have fold equity and post-flop advantage. Deep effective stacks support this.

Facing a 3-bet from Elon (who has been active) with A8o in the BB. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against a 3-bet range. With 4300 stack, calling 800 commits nearly 19% of my stack preflop with a hand that will often be dominated or have difficult postflop decisions. FiveTwo and Minni could still continue. Standard fold here.

We’re facing a small blind 3-bet after I opened and the button cold-called. Elon has shown a willingness to apply pressure and take aggressive lines, so his 3-bet range here should be fairly wide (not just premiums), especially given the dead money. With 7♠7♣ I’m not thrilled to 4-bet (we’d often just get shoved on or called by better and play a bloated pot), but folding is too tight given the price and implied odds.

Calling keeps in dominated overcards/bluffs, lets me realize strong set value with ~10k effective behind, and I’ll have position on Elon postflop (though potentially sandwiched if Minni comes along). Plan postflop: continue on low/neutral boards, be cautious on high, coordinated textures when facing heavy pressure, and happily stack off when we flop a set versus this range density.

So I’ll flat the 3-bet and proceed.

A5s on the button facing Elon’s SB 3-bet to 900 after I called the cutoff’s 250 open. Call to 900 (650 more) is the best line: I keep position vs both opponents, preserve a large portion of my stack, and A5s has excellent postflop playability (nut‑ace blocker + flush potential). The immediate pot odds (~650 into a 2150 pot) are attractive and I can realize equity and apply positional pressure on many flops. A small 4‑bet here is unlikely to fold out enough and a larger 4‑bet or shove is too committal against an aggressive SB and a caller. I’ll call, proceed cautiously on a hostile 4‑bet, and play the flop exploiting position (c‑bet when favorable, check/control or fold to heavy resistance).

Flop Qs8hTc is a mixed board: queen-high with some straight draw potential (J9 gutshot, possible KJ for overcards/broadway potential), rainbow but spade flush draw possible. My AKo completely misses: ace-high with two overcards (6 clean outs to pair A/K, but vulnerable to any pair/draw). Multiway pot OOP (SB) after both villains called my preflop 3-bet sizing; their ranges are capped somewhat but include pairs (88-QQ, maybe TT), two-pair combos, suited broadway, connectors/draws that hit like Qx, Tx, 98s, J9s, KQs. FiveTwo (CO original raiser) likely strongest range, Minni looser button call. Betting OOP risks check-raise from made hands or floats, poor SPR postflop (~12:1 effective to pot, but deeper). Checking realizes equity for free, induces bluffs/bets from weaker shows, allows pot control. If bet into me, can call once with backdoor potential/overcards vs floats. Folding to bet ok if large. Maximizes EV long-term by avoiding OOP spew.

We’re in a 3-bet pot, multiway, on a very dynamic/high-card flop (Q♠ T♣ 8♥) where our 7♦7♣ is a clear underpair with essentially no meaningful draws—mostly just set-mining equity. Elon’s check doesn’t automatically mean weakness; in this texture he can easily be checking some strong hands for pot control/induce (AQ/KQ/QQ/TT/88) or giving up with AKo-type hands. Betting here into two players will get called frequently by any Qx/Tx, 99-JJ, and combo draws, and we’re in a tough spot if the button continues or raises. The best way to realize our equity and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand is to take the free card and reassess turn action.

I’ll check back.

Pot is 2,800 and it’s checked to me on Q♠ 8♥ 10♣ with A♠5♠ on the button. I currently have the nut Ace as showdown value and a backdoor spade possibility; both opponents checking suggests a lot of their ranges missed. This is a good c‑bet spot to extract folds from Kx, Jx, underpairs and other unpaired/barely-connected hands and to take the initiative in position. I’ll make a medium-sized bet (~40% pot = 1,200) to preserve fold equity without bloating a multiway pot. Plan: if I take it down I win the pot; if I get one caller I’ll use position to control the turn and only continue aggressively with improvement (paired Ace, a spade, or clear reads). If I face a sizable raise I will respect that and likely fold this unimproved Ace‑high since it has limited value vs heavy action.

Facing Minni's 1200 bet (30% pot) into a checked flop multiway OOP with AKo (ace-high, two overcards, 6 clean outs to pair but vulnerable). Board Qs8hTc offers straight draws (J9 gutshot), flush draw (spades), pairs Q/T/8 possible. My preflop 3-bet range (QQ+, AK/AQ, bluffs) misses entirely. Minni (loose button caller of 3-bet) bets after two checks: range polarized to value (Qx like AQ/KQs/QTs, TT+/sets/two-pair combos like Q8/T8 rare, QQ crushes) or semi-bluffs/draws (J9s, 97s, spade draws, floats). FiveTwo (CO raiser, checked) caps somewhat but still to act. Calling OOP builds poor SPR (~2:1 immediate, deeper later), invites check-raise or tough river spots on drawy board. Equity ~25-35% vs her range (sims approximate), but realizes poorly OOP multiway with backdoor equity only. Pot odds ~30% insufficient after rake/tourney dynamics. Raising bluff blocked by AK (weakens nut blockers), poor vs calling station Minni history (aggressive but calls down like hand 16), fold equity low OOP. History shows Minni bets wide (e.g., hand 25), but folding premium air here preserves stack for better spots (hand 27/150, deep 204bb). Check-fold exploits position and range advantage long-term.

We’re in a 3-bet pot and checked a very connected Q♠ T♣ 8♥ flop. Minni cold-called pre and now bets after two checks; once Elon folds, her range is still heavily weighted to made hands and strong draws here (Qx/Tx, 99-JJ, KJ/J9, sets), with fewer pure bluffs than in a single-raised pot. With 7♦7♣ we’re mostly hoping to bink a set (very low frequency) and we’re out of position, so our ability to realize equity and/or profitably bluff later is poor. Getting ~23% pot odds isn’t enough given our likely equity and the reverse-implied-odds when she improves. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

King-Four suited is too weak to open from Under the Gun (UTG) in a 6-max game. Standard opening ranges in this position are significantly tighter, typically requiring at least KTs or KJs. Given my stack of approximately 52 big blinds, there is no reason to play a marginal hand out of position. I will fold.

Unopened pot with only blinds in. 9c5c is a weak suited gapper for middle position in a 6-handed game, and we still have two aggressive stacks (Minni/Elon) plus blinds who can 3-bet or defend, putting us in tough postflop spots with dominated equity. Limping is low-EV, and opening here risks getting pressured off our hand too often. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Cutoff with KcQc — a strong opening hand with good playability and backdoor club equity. Pot is 150, to call 100, min-raise to 200. I want to take the initiative, build a pot heads-up and apply fold equity against the blinds and the button. Standard sizing of ~3x the BB balances value and protection, so I’ll raise to 300. If everyone folds I collect the blinds; if called, I’ll use position vs the button/BB and c-bet favorable flops. If Elon (button) 3-bets small (~800–1,200) I’ll generally defend (call) and play postflop; if he 3-bets big (>1,500–2,000) I’ll tighten up and likely fold to avoid marginal, bloated spots. Against multiway calls I’ll play more cautiously and avoid large turn commitments without improvement.

5h9d is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability. Minni's raise from cutoff suggests a strong range. On the button, I could defend wider due to position, but this hand is too weak to call or raise profitably against an aggressive opponent like Minni, who has shown frequent raising tendencies. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this deep-stacked tournament.

Q2o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a cutoff raise. Out of position with a poor kicker, this hand doesn't play well postflop. Minni has been active and aggressive. Standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind facing a standard raise to 300 from Minni in the Cutoff. The pot is currently 450, and I need to call 200, giving me pot odds of roughly 30%. Ten-Seven suited is a reasonable defending hand against a late-position opener due to its ability to make straights and flushes. Minni has been active and raising frequently, suggesting a wide range, which improves my hand's relative equity. Although I will be out of position post-flop, the price and hand playability justify a call to see the flop.

I have flopped Top Pair (Tens) with a 7 kicker on a [4h 5s Ts] board. While top pair is strong, my kicker is mediocre, and I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni). Donk betting here often folds out worse hands that I want to keep in the pot (bluffs) or isolates me against better hands (AT, KT, Overpairs). Checking is the standard line to allow the aggressor to c-bet. If Minni bets, I intend to call given the strength of top pair relative to a cutoff opening range. If Minni checks back, I can re-evaluate on the turn.

I opened to 300 from the cutoff and got one caller (BB). Flop 4h-5s-T is a relatively dry, heads-up board where I have two overcards (KQ) and a backdoor club draw but no pair. As the preflop aggressor I want to keep initiative and apply pressure — a half-pot continuation bet will fold out many weak holdings and underpairs while extracting value from worse broadways/Ax hands that will call. I size to ~325 (≈1/2 pot). If I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if I face a sizable check-raise I’ll strongly lean to folding since I have only overcards/backdoors and am behind to any ten/pair combinations.

I have flopped Top Pair (Tens) on a [4h 5s Ts] board facing a standard half-pot continuation bet (325 into 650) from the Cutoff. The pot odds are favorable (requiring ~25% equity), and my hand is well ahead of the opponent's bluffing range and flip-flopping against draws, though behind better kickers (AT, KT) and overpairs. Raising would likely fold out worse hands and isolate me against strength. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, keeps the pot manageable while out of position, and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn.

The Turn card is the Ace of Spades, making the board [4h 5s Ts As]. This is a very bad card for my hand (Pair of Tens). The Ace is an overcard that connects heavily with a Cutoff opening range (Ax), and it completes the potential flush draw present on the flop (now 3 spades). Being out of position with second pair and no flush draw, I cannot lead out. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply. I must check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate their action. If they bet, it will be hard to continue, but checking is the only correct first step.

Check. I have K♣Q♣ — two overcards and only a backdoor club. After my flop c-bet (325) he called, and the A♠ on the turn is a very bad card for my holding: it adds a lot of Ax combos and completes spade flushes on a 4♥–5♠–T♠–A♠ board. Betting here has poor risk/reward — many hands that called the flop (T‑x, pocket pairs, spade draws, some A‑x) now beat me or will shove/call, and a turn bet will often get called by better or fold only the weakest hands. With his relatively short stack (4,600) a turn bet invites a shove and forces a difficult fold/call decision with a marginal hand. I prefer pot control and to realize equity — check and re-evaluate on the river. If he bets the turn I’ll fold to a sizable bet; if checked through I’ll consider a small river action on safe rivers or value/bet if I pair up.

The river card is the King of Spades, making the board [4h 5s Ts As Ks]. This puts four spades on the board and three broadway cards. My hand, a pair of Tens, has been counterfeited significantly and is now very weak; I lose to any spade (flush), any Ace, any King, or QJ (straight). Although the opponent checked back the turn, their range still contains many random spades, Aces, or Kings. Bluffing into this board is ill-advised as the opponent likely connected with something or has a spade in their wide Cutoff range. My only path to winning is if the opponent checks back a weaker hand (like pocket 9s or pure air). I must check and plan to fold to any bet.

I check to control the pot and go to showdown. With K♣Q♣ I did pair the King on the river, but the Ks completing the board is very dangerous: there are four spades on board now, so any opponent with a single spade has a flush, and A‑x or T‑x also beat me. The Big Blind called preflop and my flop c‑bet, then checked the turn — his range likely contains tens, pocket pairs, A‑x and spade combos. Betting here has poor risk/reward: I’ll get called/raised mainly by hands that already beat me (A, any spade, T) and won’t fold enough of those hands by firing. With no bet to call, I’ll take the showdown and avoid bloating a pot where I’m often behind; if I face a sizable river bet I’ll fold or proceed very cautiously.

UTG in a 6-max game, 72o is the weakest starting hand and performs terribly versus any reasonable calling/3-betting ranges. Opening it burns EV and risks getting squeezed or forced to continue out of position in a bloated pot. With deep enough stacks and plenty of hands left, best strategy is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I have T6o in middle position after UTG (FiveTwo) folded. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to play; min-raise would be 200. This is a marginal, poorly-playing hand out of position against players left to act (notably Elon in the cutoff who has shown aggressive opening/3-bet tendencies, plus the button and two blinds who defend). Limping is passive and invites multiway action; a small open will frequently be 3‑bet or called and put me in tough post‑flop spots. With a deep 161 BB stack I can wait for a better, more playable spot. Fold preflop.

Unopened pot after UTG and MP folds, in cutoff position with QTs offsuit (Qh Ts). QTo is a strong raising hand from late position due to playability, position advantage, and fold equity against blinds. Standard raise size around 2.5-3x BB (250-300) to steal the 150 pot or play postflop favorably. My deep stack (204 BB) allows aggression while maintaining image as an aggressive player from history. Blinds have moderate stacks and may fold weak hands.

I have T8o on the button facing a 3x open from Elon in the cutoff. This is a marginal hand but I have position. T8o has some playability and can flop straights/draws. Elon has been active. Calling 300 is only 7% of my stack, and I'll have position throughout the hand. Not strong enough to 3-bet, but worth a call to see a flop in position.

I hold 6d 3h in the Small Blind, facing a raise from the Cutoff (Elon) and a call from the Button (Claude). My hand is extremely weak (unconnected, offsuit, low cards) and I would be playing out of position against two players if I called. The pot odds of 25% are not sufficient to justify playing this hand, especially given the poor playability and positional disadvantage. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here to preserve my 45bb stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc4h. Elon (Cutoff) raised to 300 and Claude (Button) called. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 750. While the pot odds are roughly 3.75-to-1, J4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against two opponents. The likelihood of being dominated if I hit a Jack is high, and the hand has almost no straight or flush potential. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Preflop raiser out of position heads-up against Claude's button calling range, which is wide including suited connectors, Ax, broadways. Flop Td Jh 6c is rainbow and somewhat coordinated (JT9, J65 straight draws possible), but hits my CO raising range reasonably with overpairs QQ+, AKQ, AJs+, KJs, some Tx like AT, JT suited. My QhTs completely misses (queen-high no pair/draw), but as aggressor I should c-bet at high frequency for balance and fold equity. Claude has shown tendency to fold to postflop aggression in prior hands (e.g., Hand 3,9,21). Pot 750, bet ~60% pot (450) to apply pressure without overcommitting deep stacks. This maintains aggressive image while extracting value thin or bluffing effectively early in tournament.

I flopped top pair with T8 on Td Jh 6c. Elon c-bet 450 into 750 (60% pot). Top pair is a solid hand here but with a weak kicker. Jx beats me, and there are straight draws possible. Calling is the right play - I have showdown value and position, but my hand isn't strong enough to raise for value. I'll call and evaluate the turn.

Turn board brings the Ad, a strong card that hits Claude's button calling range hard (Ax, Ad specifically for nut flush draw). My QhTs is pure bluff catcher now: queen-high with no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), backdoor spade at best. Preflop and flop c-bet established aggression, but OOP on a scary turn, checking is standard to pot control and realize equity or induce bluffs/checks from weaker hands. Betting as a bluff risks getting called by improved hands (top pair, two pair, sets, combos) while deep-stacked; Claude has shown willingness to call aggression but folds sometimes (e.g., prior hands). Pot 1650 is decent to check down or face a bet I can fold profitably. Maintains balanced range by checking weak hands OOP post-cbet.

Elon checked after the Ace hit on the turn. I have top pair (tens) with a weak kicker. The Ace is a scare card - any Ax hand now beats me, and Elon could have AK, AQ, or suited aces in his opening range. His check could be a trap with a stronger hand, or giving up with air/weaker holdings. With my medium-strength hand, checking back is optimal - I control the pot size, get to see the river for free, and can make a decision based on river action. Betting here risks getting check-raised off my equity.

River 7c is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete many draws (club flush draw missed unless backdoor, no obvious straight completes beyond existing ones like 89 or KT broadway). My Qh Ts remains queen-high with no improvement, a marginal bluff-catcher at best against Claude's button calling range, which includes Ax (now top pair or better), broadway combos (KQ, AK for broadway straight), sets, two pairs from flops like JTs/J6/T6 suited. Preflop and flop aggression represented strong hands, but checking turn signaled caution post-Ace, balancing range with medium strength like overpairs JJ-QQ or missed draws. Claude's turn check-behind suggests weakness: unlikely nuts (would bet for value), more likely medium pairs, missed draws, or floats planning to bluff but deterred by board. OOP, checking realizes equity (queen-high may win vs pure air or worse high cards) without risking stack deep-stacked; value betting thin unlikely as few worse hands call (AJ-QJ might, but risky), and bluffing commits to pot OOP vs button who can call light or raise. Pot 1650 is small relative to 20k stack; check preserves stack for better spots in long tournament, consistent with balanced play shown in history.

River is 7c completing the board Td Jh 6c Ad 7c. I have top pair tens with 8 kicker. Elon checked turn and river after c-betting flop. The Ace on turn is a major scare card. His double-check could be: 1) giving up with air/missed draws, 2) trapping with Ax or Jx. My top pair weak kicker has showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet - I'd mostly get called by better hands (Ax, Jx, two pair) and fold out worse. Checking back to see showdown is the optimal play here.

UTG with J♥9♦ is a marginal offsuit hand and I have five players left to act behind me. In 6-max early position I should be tight — this hand is frequently dominated and has poor postflop playability out of position. Raising invites 3-bets from big stacks (Elon/FiveTwo) and possible shoves from shorter stacks (Claude/Pro), while calling would leave me OOP in multiway pots with low equity. Preserve the 161BB stack for clearer spots — fold and wait for a better position/hand.
