KQo offsuit is a strong hand in middle position after UTG fold. No limpers, so standard to raise for value and to take initiative. Raising to 3x the big blind (300) is a good size given deep stacks and builds the pot without overcommitting. Observes opponents' tendencies from history: aggressive play pays off here as seen in previous hands where raises preflop often succeed.

K2o is a weak hand, especially facing a raise from middle position. Elon has been active and has a big stack. With only 35BB, I should preserve chips and wait for better spots rather than calling with a dominated hand.

I hold Q4 suited on the Button facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from Elon in Middle Position. Elon is the chip leader and has been active, but Q4s is a weak hand that plays poorly against an opening range, even in position. The hand suffers from domination issues (lower kicker than most Queens) and does not have enough equity to justify a call given the pot odds. 3-betting is too loose here. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my 45bb stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 5s, which is a very weak starting hand. Elon has raised from Middle Position to 300. Calling here would put me out of position for the remainder of the hand against a player with a significant chip lead. Given the hand's low equity and the disadvantageous position, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

We’re getting a good price closing the action: calling 200 to win a pot of 450 (~2.25:1), needing ~31% equity. 7♦5♦ has sufficient raw equity versus a middle-position open and plays reasonably with suitedness and straight/flush potential. A 3-bet bluff here is lower EV because we’ll be out of position in a bigger pot and Elon appears capable of continuing/4-betting with a solid range; calling keeps his range wide and preserves implied odds when we hit strong draws.

This flop (A♣ 9♣ 2♦) heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (lots of Ax, strong broadways), while 7♦5♦ has essentially no immediate equity (no pair, no gutshot, no flush draw—only weak backdoors). Donk-leading would be high risk with poor blockers and we don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with a hand that can’t continue well. Best is to check, keep the pot manageable, and respond to Elon’s sizing/range on the turn/river if we pick up equity.

Preflop raise with KQo from middle position was standard for initiative and value. Flop Ac9c2d completely misses my hand; I have king-high with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw. Board is ace-high with a strong club flush draw, hitting many calling ranges from the big blind (Ax, suited connectors, pocket pairs). I'm out of position against FiveTwo, who checked (possibly weak or trapping). Continuation betting here as a bluff risks getting called or raised by better hands, and I have poor showdown value. Checking preserves my stack, controls the pot, and allows me to realize any fold equity if opponent bets turn or to fold cheaply if needed. Given deep stacks and tournament stage, avoiding difficult spots OOP with air is optimal long-term EV play. History shows aggression works when I have equity, but not here.

Flop checked through, which caps Elon’s range somewhat (lots of unpaired broadways and medium-strength Ax that didn’t c-bet). The 8h is a relatively better card for my BB defending range (98/87/86/85/22/88 show up more for me than for MP), and while 7d5d has very little showdown value, I did pick up a small gutshot to the 6. Best way to realize EV is to probe as a bluff for a small-to-medium size, targeting folds from his air and some marginal holdings while keeping risk controlled; if raised, I can comfortably fold.

Holding KQo on Ac9c2d8h board, king-high with no pair or draw after preflop raise. Flop checked through, opponent's turn bet of 300 into 650 (now pot 950) likely represents value: top pair Ax, two-pair like A9/A8/98, or draws that improved. Bet size is ~half-pot, polarizing range to strong hands or bluffs, but check-fold flop then bet often strong. Calling gives poor implied odds OOP with no equity (24% needed vs 3:1), facing tough river decisions. Raising as bluff risks call/raise from better and commits more chips OOP. Deep stacks (21k), but folding small bet preserves stack for premium spots. Opponent history shows aggression, but no reason to hero here with zero equity.

UTG in a 6-handed game with J8o offsuit is a clear fold. This hand has poor playability, dominated potential against calling ranges, and low equity multiway. No fold equity worth risking from early position. Deep stacks don't change this; preserve stack for better spots.
J4 suited is a weak hand in middle position. With 35bb stack, this isn't strong enough to open - need to be more selective. Folding is the standard play here.

I hold 4c 5d (unsuited connectors) in the Cutoff. The action has folded to me. While unsuited connectors have some playability, 54o is too weak to profitably open-raise, especially with active players behind me in the Button and Blinds (Flash and Minni have shown aggression or have deep stacks). The Expected Value (EV) of opening this hand is negative. Therefore, the disciplined and standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with an Ace. Everyone has folded to me. This is a prime opportunity to raise and try to steal the blinds or take control of the pot. Ace-Three offsuit is well within the standard range for a button open in a 6-max game. I will raise to 3 big blinds (300) to apply pressure on the blinds.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb Button open with Q4o. This hand is dominated too often when we continue, plays poorly out of position, and defending by calling invites the big blind to come along or squeeze, creating a tough multiway spot. A light 3-bet bluff is possible in theory, but Q4o is a low-quality candidate (weak blocker value, terrible playability when called) and we don’t need to take a marginal, high-variance line here. Best EV is to cut losses and wait for a better defend/3-bet hand.

I’m in the big blind with Qc2c (Q2 suited) facing a 300 button open. Pot is 450 and I need 200 more to call — with deep stacks (I = 160BB) Q2s has playable postflop equity and the pot odds are attractive (risk 200 to win ~650). I don’t want to 3-bet a marginal hand out of position into a wide opener (min 3-bet to 500 is too small to fold many hands and just builds an OOP pot). Plan: call to see a flop, continue on favorable textures (Q, clubs, 2, or strong draws) and fold to heavy, non-giving action on dry boards.

Check. I have no pair with Qc2c but a strong club draw (Qc and 2c plus 9c and 3c on board = 9 clubs left → ~34% to hit a flush by river) and a small chance to improve by pairing. I’m OOP to the preflop raiser (Flash) so I don’t want to donk-build a pot with just a draw. Checking realizes equity, lets him barrel bluffs or small c-bets that I can call for good pot odds, and preserves the option to see a free turn if he checks. Plan: check-call a standard/small–medium c-bet (my equity plus pot odds justify it), but fold to very large overbets or raises that commit too much without improvement.

I have two pair (Nines and Treys) with an Ace kicker on a paired board. While a nine is in Minni's calling range from the big blind, it is statistically unlikely. I have a strong hand that should be played for value and protection against overcards or club draws. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot.

Call 350. I have Qc2c on 9c 9s 3c — a strong four‑to‑a‑flush (9 outs) plus a few extra pairing outs (3 queens, 3 twos). Calling 350 into the current pot gives me break‑even equity of about 25.9% (350/(1000+350)). My chance to make a flush by the river is ≈35% (and pairing adds marginal extra equity), so calling is +EV versus a wide button c‑bet range. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to build the pot with a marginal semi‑bluff; a call realizes equity and keeps options open. Plan: see the turn — if I hit a club/Q/2 I can proceed aggressively; if I miss and face large turn aggression I will fold unless the price or read justifies continuing.

Check. I’m OOP with Qc2c on 9c 9s 3c J♠ — I have a four‑to‑a‑flush (9 clean club outs, ~19.6% to hit on the river) plus a few pairing outs (queens and deuces) that lift my combined chance to improve to the river to roughly the low‑30% range. Pot is 1,350 and Flash is relatively short (≈4,425). I don’t want to bloat an OOP pot as a semi‑bluff when my fold equity is low and the villain showed strength with a large flop c‑bet; checking realizes my equity and keeps options open. Plan: check and see his action — call small/medium turn bets that give correct pot odds (bets up to roughly the mid‑hundreds are marginally +EV depending on how many “dirty” outs I credit), fold to very large overbets or shoves without improvement, and if he checks back take the free river and extract value or bluff appropriately when I hit or read weakness.

I have two pair (Nines and Treys) with an Ace kicker. The Jack of Spades on the turn is an overcard that could have improved Minni's range if she was calling with Jx or if she has a Jack-high flush draw. While I have showdown value, betting here doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I want to avoid a check-raise from a Nine or a Jack. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown on the river.

Check. I missed the club flush with Qc2c on 9c 9s 3c Js Ts and I have no pair or meaningful showdown value — just Q‑high. Flash’s line (button open, a big flop c‑bet/raise, then check turn) is consistent with either a very strong made hand (9x, Jx, TT, or similar) or a missed club draw/semi‑bluff. The river Ten helps a lot of hands in his value range (JT, Tx, still‑good 9x) and gives him reason to call a thin bluff. I have very little fold equity and little to gain from firing — betting would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to fold enough better hands and would get called by many worse/paired hands. I’ll check and see if he blinks; plan is to fold to any medium/large river bet and only consider calling a very small bet (cheap enough that bluff frequency + pot odds justify a call).

The board runout of Jack and Ten has counterfeited my pair of Treys, meaning my best five-card hand is now the pair of Nines on the board with an Ace kicker (9-9-J-T-A). Minni has checked the river after the turn went check-check. While my Ace kicker gives me some showdown value against missed club draws or lower pocket pairs (which I now out-kick), I lose to any hand containing a Jack or a Ten, both of which are likely to be in the Big Blind's range. Betting offers no value as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I am unlikely to fold out a better hand like a weak Jack or Ten. I will check and take the free showdown.

A4o from UTG is too weak to open. It's a marginal hand that plays poorly multiway and from early position. With 35 BBs and 5 players behind me including some aggressive opponents, folding is the disciplined play.

I am in Middle Position (effectively Hijack in 6-handed play) with King-Five suited (K5s). The UTG player has folded. While suited Kings have potential, K5s is generally below the standard opening threshold for this position, which typically includes K9s+ or sometimes K8s+. Opening here risks domination by better Kings or facing 3-bets from the deep-stacked players behind me (Button and Blinds). With a stack of ~45 BB, playing a marginal hand out of position against potentially aggressive deep stacks is not optimal. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten of Hearts and Eight of Clubs (T8o). With a stack of 5775 chips (approx. 58 BB), T8o is generally too weak to open-raise from this position, especially with aggressive players like FiveTwo and Elon still to act behind me. Opening here would likely lead to a difficult post-flop situation or a loss to a 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

On the button with 66 after it folds to us, we should be opening for value and initiative. Pocket sixes play well in position, can win pre-flop often, and have strong post-flop potential (set mining) when called. With deep stacks in the blinds, raising also lets us apply pressure and avoid giving a cheap flop. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x open size to keep my range consistent and deny equity while still allowing folds.

I have A♠T♥ (Ace-ten, offsuit) in the small blind facing a standard button open to 2.5bb (250). This is a good but non-premium hand and I’ll be out of position postflop against the opener. With deep stacks (I have ~154bb) I don’t want to inflate the pot unnecessarily or face a larger 3-bet from the big blind. A min 3-bet here (400) or a larger shove isn’t necessary — calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves implied odds, and lets me realize equity and play postflop without bloating the pot OOP. I will call the 200 more chips (making my total committed 250) and see the flop; I’ll proceed based on flop texture (look for top pair, strong draws, or pot-control with marginal holdings).

AcJc is a premium suited broadway hand, strong enough to 3-bet for value against a wide button opening range from FiveTwo and a likely weak SB calling range from Minni. Great implied odds deep-stacked, position on SB postflop, and initiative. Folding or flat-calling is too passive; 3-betting thins the field and denies equity. Sizing to 900 (roughly 3.5x the raise) is standard, builds pot with strong hand, and has fold equity vs button steal attempt.

Elon’s 3-bet to 900 over my 250 open and Minni’s cold call is a fairly standard sizing, and we’re deep enough that pocket sixes perform best as a call-in-position rather than a 4-bet. Folding is too tight getting a good price, and 4-betting turns a hand with strong implied-odds value into a bluff that can’t profitably continue versus a shove. Calling keeps dominated hands and wider ranges in, preserves stack depth, and lets us realize our equity by set-mining (and occasionally navigating favorable low boards). Postflop plan: continue strongly when we flop a set; otherwise play cautiously versus big c-bets on high-card boards and look for cheaper turns/river lines when the texture favors our range.

My original plan was to call the button’s 2.5bb open and see a flop, but Elon (BB) just 3-bet to 900 and the button cold-called — this is now a three-way pot with me out of position. Calling 650 more would require ~24% equity to break even (650/(2050+650) ≈ 24.1%). ATo out of the small blind will often be dominated vs a polarized 3‑bet range from an aggressive, deep-stacked player and a caller on the button, and playing a bloated multiway pot OOP is a low-ROI spot. 4-betting isn’t attractive either — ATo lacks value to 4-bet for value and a light 4-bet/shove would commit too much out of position and be easy to exploit. Preserve stack, avoid a marginal, high-variance spot — fold.

Preflop 3-bet with AcJc as a premium suited broadway hand thins the field effectively, and FiveTwo's button call suggests a decent but capped range (Ax, broadway, mid-pairs, suited connectors). Flop 7c9s5d is a dry, low, uncoordinated board that misses much of villain's wide calling range while giving me the nut advantage with Ace-high plus backdoor flush (clubs) and straight draws. As OOP in a 3-bet pot with initiative, a small c-bet continues aggression, builds pot for when we hit, denies equity to villain's floats/dominating Ax/draws, and has fold equity against bluffs/marginal hands. 1/2 pot sizing (1000) balances value from worse Ax/pairs and protection while keeping pot manageable OOP. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws or induces unnecessary bluffs we beat.

Elon’s ~1/2 pot c-bet in a 3-bet pot is standard and his range is heavy on overpairs/AK/AQ. With 66 on 9-7-5 rainbow we’re behind his made hands, but we do have a gutshot to the 8 plus two set outs (6 total outs), and we’re in position with some chance he slows down on certain turns. The price is good (1000 to win 4050), and when we improve (especially to a set) we can often win a large pot at this low SPR. Raising would mostly get action from overpairs and turn our hand into a poor bluff, so the best line is to peel in position and continue only on favorable turns.

Turn 9c pairs the 9 (board now 7c9s5d9c) and completes the club flush draw, giving me the nut flush draw with AcJc (Ac is nut blocker) plus Ace-high equity. This improves me dramatically from flop Ace-high + backdoors to a monster with 15 flush outs + A overcards (~50%+ equity vs villain's range). Villain's button 3bet-call + flop call range: strong Ax (A9s+, AKo), mid pairs (88-TT/JJ), broadway, suited connectors/gappers like 86s,98s,76s,T8s that connect (97s,95s etc.). Paired board helps some (99/97s/9x) but villain unlikely to have many flush draws (only 7c on flop). My 3bet/cbet range is strong (sets, two-pair, overpairs, combo draws), so continue aggression OOP for thin value vs worse Ax/pairs/draws, protection vs his equity (str8 draws like 68/T6), and fold equity vs marginal floaters. Pot 4050 OOP vs 7825 stack, bet ~3/4 pot (3000) sizes well: builds pot, commits villain if he has it, allows fold to raise if villain bombs (unlikely given history), but extracts from 8x/6x/Tx clubs/Ax clubs/worse. Checking gives free card to overcards/str8 draws or induces bluffs we crush, suboptimal OOP. Tournament deepstack, maximize EV by getting value now.

Turn pairs the 9 and Elon fires 3000 into 4050 (~74% pot) in a 3-bet pot, which heavily weights him toward overpairs (TT+), strong 9x, and some occasional boats—much less toward pure AK/AQ air. With 66 we’re now basically drawing: 4 outs to an 8 for a straight plus 2 outs to a 6 for a full house = ~6/44 ≈ 13.6% equity. We need ~30% to call (3000 to win 10050), and implied odds are poor because we’d have only 4825 behind and an 8 is a very “scary” river that may not get paid. Raising is effectively committing our stack as a bluff and will run into jams from his value range. Best play is to preserve chips and fold.

I am in UTG position with King-Four offsuit (Kc 4s). This is a very weak hand to play from early position, as there are 5 players yet to act who could easily have stronger holdings. With a stack of 45.5 big blinds, there is no pressure to make loose moves. Opening here would be -EV. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I have Queen-Jack offsuit in the Hijack position (Middle) in a 6-max game. Pro has folded, leaving the pot unopened. QJo is a standard opening hand from this position. While not a premium hand, it's strong enough to raise for value and fold equity, especially given the stack sizes behind me. I'll raise to 3x the big blind to 300 to take control of the hand.

Flash’s 3bb open from middle position is likely a reasonably strong but not super-tight range. Tc8c isn’t strong enough to 3-bet for value and makes a somewhat marginal bluff 3-bet versus an MP opener, but it plays well in position with suited equity and good playability (pairs + draws). With ~78bb effective vs Flash (~55bb), calling keeps the pot manageable and lets us realize equity; if we face a squeeze from the button/blinds we can reassess and comfortably fold.

Hold 2c6h — a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Flash opened to 300 and FiveTwo called, so I’m facing at least one caller and likely a multiway pot or a strong open-range. Calling 300 into a 750 pot with virtually no postflop playability is negative EV; a small raise to 500 is a thin attempt to steal that invites squeezes (Claude’s short stack could shove, Flash may 3-bet) and is unnecessary with a healthy 15,200 stack. Preserve chips and wait for a real spot. Fold.

72h is one of the weakest hands in poker, even suited. Facing a middle position raise from Flash and a cutoff call from FiveTwo, the pot is multiway with strong ranges. I'm OOP in SB, which makes postflop play very difficult. Pot odds are about 3:1 (250 to call into 750), but my equity is poor against villain ranges, and implied odds are limited by shorter stacks behind. Clear fold to preserve deep stack for premium spots in this long tournament.

I have A9o in the BB facing a 300 raise from Flash and a call from FiveTwo. Pot is 750 and I need 200 to call, giving me 3.75:1 odds. A9o is playable here - too weak to squeeze/3-bet but too strong to fold with these pot odds. I'll defend my BB with a call.

Flopped nothing on Qd Ks 3c with A9o. No pair, no draw. This board favors the preflop raiser (Flash) and caller (FiveTwo) who likely have broadway-heavy ranges. Checking is standard here - no value in betting and bluffing into two players on this texture is unprofitable.

I have flopped middle pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of Kd-Qd-3c. Claude checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. While I have a pair, the King is an overcard, and there are two other players in the pot (FiveTwo and Claude). Betting here serves to protect my hand against random draws and to get value from weaker Queens or straight draws like J-T or T-9. However, checking is also a viable option to keep the pot small and avoid being check-raised by a King. Given the board texture and my position, a small 'continuation' bet of about half the pot is effective to define my hand and extract value from drawing hands. I will bet 475 into the 950 pot.

We’re facing a 1/3-pot c-bet in a 3-way pot on KQ3r-ish (only one club). With Tc8c we have no pair, no real gutshot (only very thin runner-runner straight possibilities), and just a backdoor club draw. Multiway makes float/bluff-raise much less profitable because ranges are stronger and our fold equity is lower, and we also risk the BB (short stack) springing a check-raise/jam that forces us off our equity. This is a clean spot to conserve chips and fold.

I have A9o on Qd Ks 3c - complete air with just ace high. Flash c-bet 475 after FiveTwo folded. This board heavily favors Flash's preflop raising range (KQ, KJ, AK, AQ, QJ, etc.). I have no pair, no draw, and no realistic way to win this pot. Calling would be burning chips - clear fold.

Qd 4d is a very weak hand to play from Under the Gun (UTG) in a 6-max game. Standard opening ranges from early position are much tighter, typically including strong suited connectors, pocket pairs, and high cards. Q4s does not provide enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a limp from this position, especially with several aggressive players behind me. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here.

Unopened pot with only blinds in and we’re in middle position holding 7♠3♥ offsuit—one of the weakest hands with poor playability, dominated when we make a pair, and no strong semi-bluff potential. With several players left to act (including strong-position stacks on the button/cutoff), opening or limping here burns EV. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Cutoff with T8o (8c Td) is a marginal but openable hand — I want to avoid limping and take the initiative. UTG and Middle folded, only Button and the blinds to act; raising here both applies pressure and narrows the field. Table tendencies show standard opens around 2.5–3x, and given my deep stack (152bb) I’ll use a standard 3x sizing to 300 to get fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet from Elon (button) since this hand is often dominated; versus a single blind caller I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play position against the blinds; multiway I’ll proceed cautiously and often check to showdown. Raising is higher EV than limping or folding here, so raise to 300.

Qc8d is a marginal offsuit hand with a queen high card but poor post-flop playability, dominated by many hands in Minni's cutoff raising range, which is wide but strong enough on average. On the button, position is advantageous, but Q8o typically folds to a 3x raise in deepstack play per standard charts (call Q9o+, 3-bet QJo+). Minni has been aggressive in history (frequent raises from late position), but no need to get involved with a speculative hand preflop when better defending spots exist. SB and BB stacks are playable but not squeezing here. Folding preserves deep stack for premium opportunities to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

I have Ah6h in the SB facing a cutoff raise to 300. Suited ace is playable here - I have good implied odds if I hit a flush draw or top pair. With 31.5 BB effective, calling to see a flop makes sense. 3-betting would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand against Minni who has been active.

I am in the Big Blind facing a Cutoff raise and a Small Blind call. The pot is offering me 4.5:1 odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 900), which requires approximately 22% equity. My hand, 3d 5d, is a suited one-gapper that plays well enough in multi-way pots due to its straight and flush potential. Although I am out of position relative to the aggressor, closing the action guarantees I see all five community cards if I choose to on later streets or see the flop cheaply now. Given the deep stacks (44 BB) and the excellent price, calling is a positive expected value play. I will call to see a flop.

Excellent flop for my hand - I have the nut flush draw with Ah6h on Kh Jh 8s. Nine outs to the nuts. I'm out of position against the preflop raiser (Minni). Standard play is to check here and let the aggressor continue. If Minni bets, I can call with my strong draw. If it checks through, I get a free card to hit my flush.

I hold 3d 5d on a board of 8s Jh Kh. I have missed the flop completely, holding only 5-high with no straight or flush draws. The board texture is heavy with broadway cards, which connects very well with the Cutoff's raising range and the Small Blind's calling range. As the second player to act in a 3-way pot with the pre-flop raiser yet to act behind me, betting would be a fruitless bluff. My only option is to check and fold to any bet.

I opened preflop and now have T8o on a K♥ J♥ 8♠ flop — that’s only a single pair of eights with a mediocre Ten kicker. Both the small blind (Claude, very short stack ~2.9k) and the big blind (Pro, ~4.25k) checked to me, so I’m out against multiple opponents with two overcards and likely ranges that include many Kx/Jx and strong broadways. In a multiway pot with limited fold equity and vulnerable showdown value, I should prioritize pot control rather than barreling and inflating the pot where I can get raised or committed. I’ll check back, keep the pot manageable (pot = 900), and plan to call small-sized bets to realize equity but fold to large aggression. If checked through, I’ll reassess on the turn and look to get value on safe cards or take a controlled stab only when it’s cheap to do so.

Still have the nut flush draw (Ah6h) with 9 outs. The flop checked through, indicating weakness from both opponents. The turn 5c is a blank. This is a good spot to semi-bluff - I can take down the pot now or still have strong equity if called. Betting around 50% pot to apply pressure while keeping my investment reasonable with a draw.

I hold bottom pair (5s) with a very weak kicker on a board of [8s Jh Kh 5c]. The Small Blind, who checked the flop, has led out for half-pot on the turn. This line typically indicates a hand that has improved (like two pair, trips, or a better 5), a delayed c-bet with a King, or a semi-bluff with a draw. Given that the Cutoff (pre-flop raiser) is still to act behind me and my hand is extremely vulnerable and likely dominated (even by better 5s like A5 or 65), I cannot profitably continue. The pot odds of 4:1 are decent, but my equity is likely very low and reverse implied odds are high.

I opened preflop with T8o and checked the flop to keep the pot controlled — on the turn I have middle pair (8s) with a mediocre Ten kicker on K♥ J♥ 8♠ 5♣. Claude (very short stack ~2.45k) checks the flop and now bets a small 450 into the pot; that sizing looks consistent with a wide probing/steal attempt or a small-value Kx/Jx rather than an all-in commitment. Calling 450 is cheap (pot odds ~20–25%) to realize equity against a wide range and keeps us in position to fold to large aggression on the river — raising here would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Plan: call the 450, reevaluate on the river (call small river bets for value vs bluffs, fold to large raises/shoves where we are likely behind).

My flush draw bricked on the river. I have ace high on a Ks Jh 8s 5c Js board with paired jacks. Minni called my turn semi-bluff, suggesting they have some showdown value - likely a king, a jack (now trips), or perhaps a busted draw. Bluffing here is risky: if they have a jack they're never folding trips, and a king might call given pot odds. With my stack at 2450 and pot at 1800, a meaningful bluff commits too much of my remaining stack. Better to check and potentially show down ace high - I might even beat another busted flush draw.

Heads‑up vs Claude (very short ~2.45k). I hold T8o — one pair (eights) with a weak Ten kicker on K J J 8 5. Claude called preflop and made a small turn raise, then checked the river: his range contains lots of Kx/Jx that beat me, but also many missed broadways and small pocket pairs that I beat. With the pot checked to me I want a small value bet to deny a free showdown and extract from worse pairs/missed hands while keeping risk limited. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (600 chips). If he responds with large aggression/all‑in I will fold — too many hands in his turn‑raise range beat my pair.

My flush draw bricked. I have only ace high on a paired board (Ks Jh 8s 5c Js). Minni called my turn semi-bluff and is now betting 600 into 2400 (25% pot). This small sizing could be thin value with a king or even trips trying to extract value. With just ace high, I don't beat any value hands - even K2 beats me. Calling 600 and showing down ace high is burning chips. Fold and preserve my 2450 stack for better spots.

UTG 6-handed we should open a relatively tight range. 4♠3♦ is offsuit, low, and poorly playable multiway, with reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs. With several aggressive opponents behind and no incentive to defend for just 100 more, folding preserves stack and avoids negative-EV spots.

3♣8♠ unsuited is a very weak hand with poor playability from middle. There are several players to act behind (Cutoff Elon with a very large stack, Button Claude, SB Pro, BB Flash) and history shows Elon and others are willing to raise and apply pressure — this hand plays poorly against 3-bets and postflop. Calling 100 (or making a small open) to see a flop is not +EV given the likely action and weak equity; better to preserve my deep stack for a better spot. Standard fold preflop.

Preflop, unopened pot after UTG and MP folds. Holding 5c6d, an offsuit connector with good playability postflop, in cutoff position with deep effective stacks. Position allows stealing the blinds (150 dead money) and realizing equity if called. Standard CO opening range includes low offsuit connectors like 56o ~25-35% of hands. Opponents: short button (Claude 24BB) may 3bet shove wide but fold equity still positive; blinds defend but often fold weak hands. Raising small maintains fold equity while keeping pot manageable. Size to 2.25x BB (225) balances value/protection and induces light calls we dominate.

75s on the button facing a raise from cutoff. With only ~24.5BB effective stack, this is too weak to call - 75s is a speculative hand that needs deeper stacks to realize equity. Folding is correct here.

I am in the Small Blind with J9o facing a standard open raise from the Cutoff (Elon). J9o is a weak off-suit hand that performs poorly out of position. Although the raise size is small (2.25BB), calling leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind and forces me to play the rest of the hand out of position with a marginal hand that is easily dominated. Defending ranges from the SB typically include suited broadways, pairs, and stronger off-suit broadways. J9o falls below the threshold for a profitable call or a strategic 3-bet bluff. Conserving the stack of 42BB is prioritised.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7h 4h. Elon has raised to 225 from the Cutoff, a 2.25x open. I need to call 125 more to see the flop with a total pot of 500, giving me 3:1 pot odds (requiring 25% equity). While 7-4 suited is a weak hand, it has decent playability and is generally a mathematical call in this spot against a standard late-position opening range. I will be out of position against Elon, who has a massive stack and has shown significant aggression in previous hands, so I must be prepared to fold if the flop does not improve my hand or provide a strong draw.

I have a heart flush draw (9 outs) on a flop that likely hits Elon's opening range from the Cutoff quite well (containing many Kx and Qx hands). Being out of position, the most standard and flexible play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how Elon reacts to this coordinated board. If he bets a standard size, I have sufficient equity with my flush draw to call and try to realize my hand on the turn. Leading out here would likely get called or raised by his stronger range, making it difficult to play the rest of the hand.

Flop board Kh-8d-Qh is draw-heavy with broadway cards and a heart flush draw, hitting BB calling range (KQ, KJ, QJ, Kx, suited broadways, flush draws). Flash checks, indicating possible weakness or medium strength without top pair/top kicker. As preflop raiser in position with deep stacks (his 62BB, mine deeper), standard to c-bet for value/thin value range balance and fold equity vs weak checks. 56o is pure bluff but good postflop playability if floats/call, blocks few strong hands but range-appropriate semi-bluff. Bet 60% pot (300) pressures floats/draws while keeping SPR manageable (~15:1 post-bet if called). Checking back risks giving free cards to draws/improvers and forfeits initiative.

I have a flush draw on a board that significantly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Elon in the Cutoff). Elon's continuation bet is 300 into 500. I need to call 300 to win 800. My direct pot odds are approximately 27%, while my equity to hit the flush on the turn is about 19% (and about 35% by the river). Given the implied odds—Elon is an aggressive player with a large stack who is likely to continue betting on later streets—calling is the most appropriate play to realize my equity. Check-raising here is risky against a range containing many Kx and Qx hands that are unlikely to fold. I will call and evaluate the turn.

The turn card is a second Queen, which pairs the board (Kh 8d Qh Qd). This card is very good for Elon's range as the pre-flop aggressor from the Cutoff, potentially giving him trips or a full house. Since I am out of position and only holding a flush draw (7h 4h), I will check to the aggressor. My goal is to see a free river card or evaluate a bet from Elon. Leading here would be ill-advised against his range.

Turn pairs the Qd, bringing possible trips but also completing straight draws like JT and flush draws if holding hearts. Flash checks again after calling flop c-bet, which could represent weak Qx, missed draws, or traps, but his range likely includes Ax, Kx, Qx suited, draws that didn't hit perfectly. As preflop raiser with position and initiative, continuing with a balanced bet exploits his checking range for fold equity while denying equity to any remaining draws (e.g., heart flush draw still live). 56o is a prime bluff: low equity but dynamic, no showdown value, and we've repped strength. Pot 1100, bet 65% pot (700) maintains SPR ~10:1 if called, pressuring medium strength without overbetting. Checking risks giving free cards to draws/improvers or allowing bluffs on river; betting maximizes fold equity and pot control in deep-stack tournament play. Opponent history shows Flash calls light but folds to aggression (e.g., Hand #10 river check, Hand #12 fold to turn bet).

On the turn, the board has paired with a second Queen (Kh-8d-Qh-Qd). I am holding a 7-high flush draw. Elon, an aggressive big stack, has bet 700 into a pot of 1100. My pot odds are approximately 28% (700/2500), but my equity to hit the flush on the river is only about 19.5%. Furthermore, the paired board introduces significant reverse implied odds; even if I hit my flush, I could already be losing to a full house (if Elon has a Queen or pocket 8s) or potentially a higher flush. Given that I am out of position and the price is not right to continue chasing a low flush on a dangerous board, the most disciplined play is to fold.

Open-raise to 300 (3x) with A♥9♣ from UTG in a 6-max spot. A9o is borderline but perfectly acceptable to open here — I want to take the initiative, collect blinds, and avoid multiway pots where the hand loses equity. Table history shows many standard 250–300 opens, so 300 is a natural size that reduces light 3-bets compared with the min-raise of 200. Stacks are deep (I have ~162bb) so I can play postflop if called, but I will not bloat the pot unnecessarily preflop. Plan: fold to very large 3-bets (preserving stack), consider calling small-to-medium 3-bets to play postflop, c-bet single callers on favorable Ace-high/clean boards, and proceed cautiously multiway or on very wet boards.

K7s is a speculative suited hand with some playability due to suited broadway potential and flush/draw possibilities, but facing an UTG raise from Minni, who has shown aggression but in early position likely has a strong range including premium pairs, big aces, and broadway hands. My position is middle, stacks are deep but pot odds to call 300 into 450 offer about 2.5:1, which isn't great for multiway pots likely with blinds and later players to act. Equity against UTG range is around 35-40%, not enough to call profitably OOP. Raising is off-table as it's not strong enough to isolate or apply pressure effectively. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament phase.

J6o is a weak hand, especially facing a UTG raise from Minni. With my short stack of 2450, I shouldn't be calling 300 with such a marginal hand that will be difficult to play postflop. UTG raises typically indicate strength. Easy fold.

I hold 5s3c on the button, which is a very weak unsuited hand (trash). Minni, the chip leader, has opened with a raise to 300 from UTG, representing a strong range. Despite having position, my hand has very poor equity against an UTG opening range and poor playability post-flop. Calling or raising would be throwing chips away. The correct and standard play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Small Blind holding 8-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni opened with a 3x raise from Under The Gun (UTG). Even though I only need to call 250 more to see a flop, 8-3o has very poor playability and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against an opponent who likely has a strong range given their table position. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

Pocket 2s in the big blind versus a single UTG 3bb open with deep-ish stacks (effective ~74bb) plays best as a call. We’re getting a good price (200 to win 450) and can profitably set-mine, while a 3-bet bluff is unattractive OOP against a strong UTG range and risks getting 4-bet or called and forced to navigate postflop with a very weak hand. Calling keeps ranges wide and realizes our main equity when we flop a set.

We missed our set and have a pure underpair on a coordinated board (9s-8h-4s) versus a strong UTG range. Leading doesn’t represent much and just bloats the pot OOP with minimal equity (mainly two outs to a set plus a weak backdoor spade). Best is to check and respond to Minni’s sizing (mostly folding to any reasonable bet).

I opened UTG and got one caller (the BB). On 4s 9s 8h I hold Ah9c — top pair with the best kicker. This is a clear spot to c-bet for value and protection: the board has two spades so there are meaningful flush draws to charge, and a ~2/3-pot bet will extract value from worse 9x, medium pairs and charge spade draws while folding out unpaired floats. If I face a very large check-raise I’ll strongly consider folding (range is usually very strong); I’ll call or continue versus small-to-medium resistance and re-evaluate on the turn. Bet sizing: 425 (~65% pot) to balance protection and value.

We’re facing a small-ish c-bet (425 into 1075) but with 22 on 9s-8h-4s we have essentially two outs to improve (set) plus a very thin runner-runner spade possibility. Against an UTG c-betting range (overpairs, strong 9x/8x, big spade/Broadway draws) our equity is far below the ~28% needed to call, and the implied odds to set-mine on the turn don’t justify investing 425 when we’ll often have to fold turn and won’t reliably stack UTG even when a 2 comes. Best is to exit and preserve stack.

UTG position with A5o, a weak offsuit ace with poor playability and dominated by better aces. Standard play is to fold early position to avoid difficult post-flop situations. My deep stack doesn't necessitate speculative limping or raising. Prioritizing position and hand strength to maximize long-term stack growth.
7d4d is a weak suited connector. From middle position with 4 players yet to act, this is a clear fold. Not worth investing chips with this marginal hand when I'm already short-stacked at 2450.

I am in the Cutoff position with Qc9c, a respectable suited gapper that plays well post-flop and functions well as a steal candidate. With 42BB, I have a healthy stack to open-raise. The action has folded to me. A raise here puts pressure on the Button and Blinds. My standard opening size in previous hands has been 2.5x (250), so I will maintain consistency to avoid sizing tells. If 3-bet, I can easily fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. The Cutoff (Pro) has raised to 250. Jack-Deuce is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, and even with the advantage of position, it does not warrant a call or a 3-bet against an open. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re in the small blind facing a cutoff open. T6o is dominated by Pro’s value-heavy continues (Tx, broadways, pairs) and plays very poorly out of position in a raised pot. Even with decent pot odds, realizing equity OOP vs the opener (and with the big blind still to act) is tough, and 3-bet bluffing is unattractive given Pro’s short stack and higher likelihood to shove/call. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

Pro opened to 250 from the cutoff. I’m in the big blind with T♣9♦. This is a very defendable hand against a single open — good connectivity and reasonable equity against a typical cutoff raising range. Calling 150 more (250 total) gives me about 27% break-even equity (150 into a 400 pot becomes a 550 pot after my call), so the pot odds justify a defend. I don’t want to 3-bet light out of position with a marginal hand; a min-raise to 400 will often get called and leave me OOP to play postflop. Plan: call, see the flop, continue with top pairs/strong draws or good turn action, and fold to heavy aggression on unfavorable textures.

I called preflop with T♣9♦ and the flop came 8♣ 9♥ Q♦. I have second pair (9s) with a decent ten-kicker and a J-gutshot — no realistic flush (only one club on board and one in hand, so can’t make a five-card club flush). Pro opened from the cutoff so their continuation range includes many Qx and broadways that will continue; my hand is good vs bluffs and worse overcards but behind most Qx/overpairs. I’m out of position and prefer pot control with this marginal made hand, so I check to see how Pro reacts. Plan: call small c-bets for pot odds (keep weaker hands in), fold or proceed cautiously to heavy aggression, and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have flopped top two pair (Queens and Nines) on a rainbow board (Qd 9h 8c). This is a very strong hand, though vulnerable to the completed straight (JT) and sets (88). Given the connected nature of the board, I need to bet to extract value from worse made hands (KQ, QJ, QT, A9, T9) and to charge potential straight draws (T7, J8). Checking back gives a free card on a texture where many turn cards (T, J, 7, K, A) could be scary or kill my action. I will bet approximately 55% of the pot to build the pot and deny equity to draws.

Villain (cutoff) c-bet 300 into a 850 pot heads-up. I hold T♣9♦ on 8♣ 9♥ Q♦ — second pair (9s) with a decent ten-kicker and a J-gutshot; no realistic flush draw. Calling 300 gives ~26% break-even equity and vs a typical cutoff c-bet range I beat many overcards and bluffs, so I have enough equity to defend. I’m OOP, so raising with a marginal made hand is high-variance (will often bloat the pot or run into stronger hands / shoves given Villain’s ~3.6k stack). Plan: call to keep the pot controllable and reevaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, and extract value or apply pressure if I pair the T/make two pair/straight or get a favorable turn runout.

I improved to two pair (Tens and Nines) on the turn (board 8♣ 9♥ Q♦ T♦). Cutoff (Pro) opened preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his range contains many Qx, some overcards/bluffs and a few made hands/sets. My two pair is ahead of most of that continuation range but vulnerable to sets, Qx+T (two pair Q/T) and the small number of straights/trips — so I want value and protection without folding out worse hands. A half‑pot sized bet (~600 into 1150) charges Qx and single pair hands and forces draws to pay to continue, while not overcommitting if I run into a set/full house. If he raises big or jams I’ll re‑evaluate (those raises usually represent the small subset of hands that currently beat me). Betting 600 maximizes value vs the hands I dominate and keeps the pot controllable.

I hold top two pair (Queens and Nines) on a board of [8c 9h Qd Td]. The Turn Ten is a very dangerous card as it puts four cards to a straight on the board (8-9-T-Q). Specifically, any Jack now makes a straight. My opponent (Minni) called my pre-flop raise and my flop c-bet, a line consistent with holding a draw like Jx (open-ended on the flop) or a pair plus straight draw. Minni's lead (donk bet) of 600 into 1100 on the Turn immediately upon the straight completing signifies immense strength, likely preventing me from checking back. Although I have a strong two pair, I am dead to a straight, and drawing very thin (4 outs) to a full house. The pot odds do not justify chasing a full house, and my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher against a range heavily weighted towards the straight. Preserving my stack of ~36BB is preferable to bleeding chips in a spot where my opponent's range crushes the board texture.

K7o from UTG is too weak to open. This hand is easily dominated by better kings and plays poorly out of position. With only 24.5 BBs, I need to be selective about my opens, especially from early position. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold [9c 5h] in middle position, which is a very weak unsuited hand with poor playability. With four players left to act behind me and a stack of roughly 36 BB, there is no strategic justification to enter the pot. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Cutoff position with KhTs. This is a standard opening hand in a 6-handed game. Since it has folded to me, I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially pick up the blinds or play a pot in position.

Button vs Cutoff open, A3o is not strong enough to flat purely for value, but it has an ace blocker and plays well as a light 3-bet in position. Flash has shown a tendency to open and then fold to larger pressure (e.g., folding to 3-bets in prior hands), and his stack is only ~55bb, making him more likely to give up marginal opens rather than defend wide. A 3-bet also denies the deep blinds an easy squeeze/overcall spot. I’ll use a standard in-position size that applies pressure without bloating too much if called.

Facing a button 3-bet (FiveTwo made it 900 over Flash's 300). I'm in the small blind out of position with 8♣4♠ — a very weak, easily dominated hand with very poor equity versus a 3-bet range. Calling 850 into a 1,350 pot gives only ~1.6:1 pot odds (needs ~38% equity), which 4-8 offsuit can't provide. A light 4-bet is possible but unnecessary and risky here (Elon in the big blind can still act and the stacks are deep enough that I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with junk). Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot, so fold.

Q3o is a trash hand with very poor equity and playability multiway or heads-up against a cutoff raise and button 3-bet. Facing significant action preflop with 800 to call into a 1350 pot, but my hand is easily dominated and doesn't warrant defense from the BB. Deep stacks but no need to speculate with this holding early in the tournament (hand 38/150). Standard fold to preserve stack.

I opened with KhTs from the Cutoff, and FiveTwo 3-bet me from the Button. While KhTs is a strong opening hand, it performs poorly against a 3-bet range, especially when I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. FiveTwo has shown aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #12). Calling 600 more requires me to play a bloated pot out of position with a dominated or marginal hand. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo). In a 6-handed game, this hand is generally too weak to open profitably from early position. It is frequently dominated by hands that opponents will call or raise with (such as AQ, KQ, AJ, AT, and pocket pairs), and playing it out of position post-flop is difficult. With a stack of roughly 36.5 BBs, I am not in a desperate push/fold mode and should preserve my chips for stronger starting hands or better positional spots. Therefore, the standard and disciplined play here is to fold.

I am in middle position with Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o). In a 6-max game, A8o is generally too weak to open-raise from this position, especially with aggressive players like FiveTwo and Minni still to act behind me. Additionally, Claude is sitting on a short stack in the Big Blind, increasing the likelihood of a shove that I wouldn't want to call with this hand. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

7♠3♣ offsuit is too weak to profitably enter, even in the cutoff. Limping creates dominated, low-realization spots, and opening this bottom-tier hand is unnecessary given the big blind is short and likely to defend/jam more, plus we can find better steal candidates in later hands. Preserve stack and avoid negative-EV marginal steals here.

Button with Qc6d — a marginal hand, but position and a Queen blocker make it reasonable to attempt a steal. Pot is 150, to call 100; I’ll open to a standard 3x (300) to apply pressure to the short-stacked BB (Claude) and deny cheap odds to Elon in the small blind. I’m committing a very small fraction of my 17k stack to try to pick up the blinds; if I face a meaningful 3-bet or a shove from a player representing a strong range I’ll fold Q6o (no calling large 3-bets with this hand). If called heads-up or by one blind, I’ll use position to play postflop and c-bet or check based on the flop texture.

Preflop in SB with Jc5s offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, no high card strength, and dominated in most scenarios. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has a wide range but still stronger on average. BB yet to act. Calling OOP would lead to difficult postflop spots with a speculative hand that rarely makes strong hands. Raising is unprofitable without equity. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Facing a button raise to 300, I have 67o in the BB needing to call 200 into a 450 pot (2.25:1). While 67o is marginal, it has straight potential and the price is reasonable. I'm out of position but can play fit-or-fold postflop. With my shorter stack, I don't want to be too exploitable by folding too much in the BB.

Flop completely missed me - 67 offsuit on 3h Qh 9s gives me no pair, no flush draw (no hearts), and no straight draw. I should check and fold to any bet from Minni who likely has better hands or will c-bet her button range.

I raised preflop from the button with Qc6d and flopped top pair on Qh‑3h‑9s. Claude (BB) checked; his calling range is fairly wide and he’s relatively short (≈21 BB). A continuation bet here achieves value and protection — it gets called by worse queens, pair+draw hands and 9x/3x, and charges heart/straight draws. I’ll size about half‑pot to extract and deny equity while keeping the pot controllable. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (barrel safe cards, slow down on A/K/9/heart completes). If he check‑raises all‑in I’ll strongly consider calling because his stack is short and my top pair is often ahead of his continuing range.
Complete miss on this flop with 67o. No pair, no flush draw (only one heart), and no meaningful straight draw. Facing 350 into a 1000 pot with just backdoor possibilities isn't worth it. Easy fold.

I am in the UTG position with Ten of Spades and Eight of Hearts (T8o). This is a weak starting hand to play from the earliest position at a 6-max table. Standard strategy dictates folding such hands to avoid playing a weak range out of position against potentially stronger hands from the remaining five players. Given my stack size of 5550 chips, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings.

We’re facing only the big blind (to call 100) with several players still to act behind who can isolate/3-bet (Minni/Elon deep). 74o has poor playability and reverse implied odds from middle position; limping is a leak and opening it is too thin in this lineup. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Qc9h is a marginal but playable hand from the cutoff with no prior raise and very deep stacks. I want to take the initiative, deny the blinds free equity, and isolate, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp. A standard 3x-ish size (raise to 300) gives fold equity while keeping the pot manageable vs the big stacks (Elon on the button) and still doesn’t overcommit vs short-stack shoves. Plan: c-bet favorable dry/Q-or-9 flops and pot-control or fold on connected/flushy textures; fold to a large 3-bet (well above ~800–900) but consider calling a short-stack SB shove given the pot odds and his wide shove range.

Preflop on the button with 72o, one of the worst possible hands with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Facing a raise to 300 from the cutoff (Minni), who has been aggressive in prior hands. Pot is 450, but equity is abysmal against any reasonable range; folding preserves my deep 235x BB stack for better spots in a long tournament with 110 hands left. No steal value here as it's a standard fold from button vs cutoff open.

Q8o in the small blind facing a cutoff raise. With only 21BB stack and being out of position, Q8o is too weak to defend here. Minni has been active with raises. Folding to preserve chips for better spots.

The opponent Minni raised to 300 (3BB) from the Cutoff. I am in the Big Blind with J8o. The pot odds are roughly 30.7% (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). Although J8o has some equity against a wide Cutoff range, it suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds, especially out of position. The 3x raise size makes the price significantly worse than a min-raise, pushing this hand from a marginal defend to a fold. Preserving my 35.5BB stack for better opportunities is the standard play here.

UTG in a 6-max lineup, Q6s is too weak to open from first position: it’s frequently dominated by better Qx, plays poorly out of position multiway, and doesn’t realize equity well when facing 3-bets (especially with deeper stacks behind in CO/HJ). Limping invites isolation and puts us in tough spots with a marginal suited hand. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

Hand: T♥5♦ in middle position with pot 150 and 100 to call. There are four players yet to act behind me (Elon CO with a big stack, Claude BTN short stack, Pro SB, Flash BB), so raising here would likely face a call/3-bet or a shove from the short stack and create a tough multiway spot out of position. T5o is a weak, unsuited hand that is frequently dominated and has poor multiway equity; calling 100 for a pot of 150 (2.5:1) requires ~28.6% equity — unrealistic against several live ranges. I’ll conserve chips and fold preflop, waiting for a better spot or position to apply pressure.

Unopened pot after UTG and MP folds. In cutoff position with deep stack (235 BBs), J6o is a playable stealing hand with good position and fold equity against blinds. Button is short (21 BBs) but likely to fold weak hands or shove only premium. Raising standard 3x to 300 builds pot or takes it down preflop. Folding too tight early in tournament with position.
Q7o is a weak hand facing a raise. With 21 BBs, I should be selective and wait for better spots. This hand doesn't play well postflop even on the button.

I am in the Small Blind holding Q7o facing a 3x raise from the Cutoff (Elon). Q7o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Against a standard Cutoff opening range, I am likely dominated and lack the necessary equity (need ~36% immediate pot odds, but have closer to 32% raw equity with poor realization). With a 35 BB stack, I should preserve chips for better opportunities. The Big Blind is also yet to act. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I have Ace-Queen of spades, a premium hand in 6-max poker. Elon raised from the cutoff to 300. Given the strength of my hand and the need to protect my position and build a pot, a 3-bet is the standard play here. I will raise to 1000 to put pressure on his opening range and take control of the hand before the flop.

Facing a 3-bet from BB after my CO open-raise with J6o. This hand has poor post-flop playability OOP, mediocre high card strength, and is easily dominated by BB's 3-bet range (which includes pairs, suited connectors, Ax, KQ+, etc.). Pot odds to call 700 into 1350 (~2:1) aren't great without implied odds for a weak hand like this. 4-betting risks a shove from BB's 45BB stack, turning my stack into a coinflip unnecessarily early in the tournament. Folding preserves my deep 232BB stack for better spots. BB's history shows aggression but folding here is standard GTO.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 4♠2♠ — one of the weakest playable hands. Out of position against five opponents (including aggressive players behind like Elon and Flash), limp-calling or raising invites multiway trouble and expensive postflop decisions. The cost to see the flop (100) isn’t justified by the hand’s playability or equity, so preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

Qh3h is a weak suited hand with poor playability and low equity multiway. In middle position after UTG fold, with pot 150 and BB 100 to call (limp), limping invites multiway pots where this hand performs poorly. Raising is too loose given the hand strength and positions behind (including short-stacked Claude who may shove). Folding preserves our healthy 232BB stack for premium spots in this deep tournament.

Q5o is a weak hand. With only 21 BBs, I should be selective with my opens. Q5o is below a standard cutoff opening range, especially with a short stack where I can't afford to bleed chips with marginal hands. Better to wait for a stronger spot.

I am on the Button with 6d 8s, a very weak off-suit hand (86o). The action has folded to me. While the Button allows for a wide opening range to steal the blinds, 86o is generally too weak to include, usually falling outside the top 40-50% of hands. Furthermore, the Small Blind (Flash) has shown aggression recently (3-betting in Hand #2, raising Hand #41), and both blinds cover my stack. Opening here risks losing chips to a 3-bet or being called by better hands out of position. Folding is the prudent play.

It is folded to me in the Small Blind. I am holding Queen-Six offsuit (Q6o), which is a marginal hand. The player in the Big Blind, FiveTwo, has shown a tendency for aggressive play and frequent 3-betting in previous hands. Raising with Q6o here is risky because it likely won't stand up to a 3-bet, and limping out of position is generally a weak play against an aggressive opponent. To preserve my stack for better spots in this 150-hand tournament, I will make the disciplined fold.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 3s8s, a weak suited connector/gapper with low cards and poor post-flop playability. Standard tight range from early position requires folding trash like this to avoid difficult multiway pots and preserve deep stack for premium hands. No reads justify a loose open here.

Q5s in middle position with 21BB stack. This is a marginal hand - suited with a queen but weak kicker. With 4 players still to act behind me including aggressive players, and my shorter stack, I should be selective. However, Q5 suited has some playability with flush potential. I'll fold here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with A6 suited, which is a standard opening hand in 6-max No-Limit Hold'em, as it has good playability and blocker value (blocking AA/AK/AQ). My stack is 35 BB, allowing me to open-raise comfortably. Although the players behind me (Flash on Button and FiveTwo in SB) have shown aggression with 3-bets in previous hands, folding A6s here would be too tight. I will open to 250 (2.5x BB), planning to fold to a 3-bet from the aggressive stacks behind me, but hoping to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds.

I am holding 8h 4c on the button. This is an extremely weak starting hand with no connectivity or high-card strength. A raise has come from Pro in the cutoff. Even with position, there is no strategic reason to play this hand. Folding is the standard professional play here to preserve my stack.

CO opens 2.5bb and we’re in the SB with J2o, a heavily dominated offsuit hand that plays very poorly out of position and multiway (BB still to act). Even getting ~33% immediate pot odds (200 to win ~600 if we call), our realized equity will be significantly worse due to reverse implied odds and difficulty continuing vs c-bets. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive because Pro is short (can jam over) and BB has a big stack behind. Best is to surrender the SB and wait for a better spot.

Pro opened to 250 from the cutoff. I’m in the BB with Q♠2♥ — a marginal, easily dominated hand. Calling 150 into a 400 pot requires ~27% equity, so defending is reasonable given the cheap price and Pro’s frequent 250 opens. 3-betting with Q2o is poor — OOP, weak kicker, and I don’t want to build a bloated pot with little equity. Plan: call to see a flop (total commitment 250), fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, and only continue aggressively if I hit a real piece or have clear backdoor equity.

I have Q♠2♥ on K♦ T♣ A♦ — I completely missed. There are no meaningful backdoor draws (two diamonds on board, I hold none) and my queen kicker is irrelevant vs a c-betting raiser’s range (Axs, Kx, Tx, etc.). Pro is a frequent 250 opener and will c-bet this texture often, but bluffing OOP with such a weak hand and no blockers is a losing line. Pot control is correct: I check to keep the pot small and avoid putting more chips in with virtually no equity. If Pro leads into me I’ll fold to any sizable bet or shove (I won’t call large aggression), and only continue if he checks back or gives me an unusually cheap probe bet.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (Ah6h) on a very coordinated board of [Kd Tc Ad]. While I have a strong hand, the board texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many two-pair combinations (KT, AT, etc.) and the nut straight (QJ). Betting here opens me up to a check-raise which would put me in a difficult spot, forcing me to fold significant equity or commit to a bloated pot with a marginal holding. By checking back, I control the pot size, induce bluffs on later streets, and can comfortably call a turn bet or value bet the turn if checked to again. This is a classic pot control situation.

I will check. Q♠2♥ on K♦ T♣ A♦ A♣ is a complete miss — no pair, no real draw, and the turn pairs the ace which heavily favors Pro’s preflop opening range (many A‑x, Kx, Tx, or pocket pairs). Betting as a bluff here has very little fold equity (I don’t block Aces and Pro checked the flop, which can indicate either a slowplay or a marginal hand happy to see a cheap turn), so risking chips in a small pot is a losing line. Optimal line is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Pro checks back I’ll see the river for free, but I’ll fold to any turn bet or significant river action unless the board or my hand suddenly improves in a way that gives real showdown value.

On the flop, I checked back top pair with a weak kicker for pot control, as the board heavily favors the Big Blind's range. The turn [Ac] improves my hand to Trip Aces. While the straight (QJ) and full houses are possible, checking twice on this texture would be too passive given the presence of two flush draws (diamonds and clubs) and available value from King-x hands. I need to protect my equity against draws and extract value from worse made hands. A bet of approximately half-pot strikes a balance between getting value and avoiding over-commitment against the top of Minni's range.

I hold Q♠2♥ on K♦ T♣ A♦ A♣. Pro (cutoff) opened preflop, checked the flop and now leads the turn for 275 into a 825 pot. Calling 275 to win 1100 gives ~25% pot‑odds, but my realistic equity to improve (pair Q or pair 2) is only ~10–13% and many of those outs still lose to Pro’s range (A‑x, Kx, Tx, or pocket pairs). I have no meaningful blockers or backdoor draws and OOP bluff/raise has very little fold equity here. This is a clear fold — follow the preplanned pot‑control line and conserve chips rather than calling a negative‑EV turn bet.
Pocket tens in UTG is a premium hand. With only 21BB stack, I should raise for value. Standard 2.5x open to 250. This hand plays well and I want to build the pot while maintaining fold equity.

I hold K6 offsuit in middle position facing a 2.5x raise from UTG (Claude), who is currently the short stack with about 18.5 big blinds. K6o is a very weak hand that is dominated by most of an UTG opening range (e.g., KQ, KJ, KT, AK). Calling would be losing chips in the long run, and 3-betting is ill-advised against a short stack who is likely to shove if he has a hand worth opening. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Eight suited. Claude, who is sitting on a very short stack of 18.5 BBs, has raised from UTG. While T8s is a decent speculative hand, calling a UTG raise from a shallow stack is risky as his range is likely polarized towards strong hands or pairs that dominate me. Additionally, there are three players behind me who could easily 3-bet, forcing me to fold and lose my investment. Given the stack dynamics and my position, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Claude is opening from UTG with only ~18.5bb behind, which usually implies a relatively strong/linear range and increases the chance he 4-bet jams if we 3-bet. Q9o performs poorly against a short-stack jam range, so 3-betting would either force us into a marginal call or burn chips by 3-bet/folding. Flat-calling also isn’t attractive: we invite squeezes from two deep blinds (Minni/Elon) and Q9o is dominated often when we do see a flop. With plenty of stack depth (76bb), the best EV and lowest-variance choice is to let this go and preserve better stealing/3-betting spots versus wider ranges.

Claude opened UTG to 250 (2.5bb). I’m on the small blind with J♦4♥ — a weak, easily-dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. Calling 200 to see a flop is a marginal defense that will force tough decisions against an UTG opener (and a big blind yet to act). 3‑betting is also undesirable — this hand has poor equity and few blockers. Preserve chips and fold to avoid playing a dominated hand OOP.

Kh7o is a weak king-high hand with a poor kicker, offsuit and unconnected. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has a strong range, and I'm OOP in the BB. Effective stack is Claude's 1850 (~18BB), making postflop play difficult. Pot odds are about 2.7:1 but equity and playability are insufficient. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in a long tournament.

I am holding 6h 4d in the UTG position. This is an extremely weak hand (unsuited low connector with a gap) and I am in the earliest position at the table, which is the worst position to play from. With 38 BBs, I need to preserve my stack for better spots. There is no strategic merit in limping or raising here. The standard and optimal play is to fold immediately.

I am in Middle Position with Ac 8c, which is a strong starting hand in a 6-max tournament. Since the action has folded to me, I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300. This allows me to take control of the pot and potentially pick up the blinds. I am mindful of Claude's short stack in the Big Blind (21.5 BBs), but opening here is standard for a professional player.

Flash is opening from middle position to 3bb with a stack that suggests he can open fairly wide and has shown willingness to fold to 3-bets. With JTo in the cutoff, we have reasonable playability and strong positional advantage, but it’s a bit thin to 3-bet (offsuit, can be dominated when called) especially with the Button and blinds behind who can squeeze, and a short-stacked BB who could jam over a 3-bet creating an awkward spot. Flat-calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity in position, and lets us outplay postflop versus a likely wide range.

I’m on the button with 4h7c facing a standard MP raise to 300 and one caller (pot 750, to call 300). 4-7 offsuit is a very weak, easily-dominated hand with poor playability in a multiway pot — I’d need ~29% equity just to break even on a call, which I’m unlikely to have against a raiser+caller. 3-betting as a bluff to the min (500) is thin here: Flash (raiser) and FiveTwo (caller) are both deep enough to call or 4-bet, and Claude in the BB is short enough to jam and force tough decisions. I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot rather than spew with a junk hand.

52o is one of the worst hands in poker with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Facing a raise from Flash (aggressive player from history) and a call from FiveTwo in cutoff, from small blind with no position post-flop. Pot odds to call 250 into 750 (~3:1) not justified by the <30% equity needed against typical raising/calling ranges. Deep stack allows waiting for better spots in tournament with 105 hands left. Fold preserves chips.

I have ATo in the BB facing a raise to 300 and a call. Pot is 750 and I need 200 to call - getting 3.75:1 odds. ATo is a decent hand, and with these pot odds it's an easy call. My stack is short at ~21.5 BB so I don't want to bloat the pot with a 3-bet OOP against two opponents. Calling to see a flop is the best play.

Missed the flop completely with Ad Th on 3c 7d Qs board. I have just ace high with no draws - no flush draw, no straight draw, and no pair. Standard check here. If opponents bet, I'll likely fold since ace high has minimal showdown value against a preflop raiser and caller on this board.

On this dry, queen-high board (Qs 7d 3c), I hold Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage here, as my range includes all the strong queens (AQ, KQ) and overpairs that my opponents likely would have three-bet pre-flop. Claude's check is expected. I will fire a continuation bet of 425, which is approximately 45% of the pot. This sizing is effective on dry boards to fold out weaker holdings and undercards while maintaining a healthy stack if I am raised or called.

Flop is Q-7-3 rainbow and we have JTo with no pair, no real straight draw, and no backdoor flush possibilities (offsuit on a rainbow board). In a 3-way pot, Flash’s small c-bet range can still contain a lot of Qx and overpairs that won’t fold to pressure, and Claude can continue with any Q/7/PP as well. Without equity or good turns to barrel, floating or bluff-raising is unnecessarily spewy; preserve stack for better spots with real draws or stronger range advantage boards.

I have Ad Th on 3c 7d Qs - complete air with just ace high. No pair, no draws. Flash c-bet 425 into ~950. With only 1950 in my stack, calling 425 (22% of my stack) with no equity is terrible. Easy fold.

In a 6-max game, Ten-Eight suited is a marginal hand from Under The Gun. While it has some speculative value, opening it from first position with several aggressive players and two relatively short stacks (Claude and Pro) behind me is risky. If I raise and one of the short stacks shoves, I would be forced to fold, losing 3 big blinds. Given my current stack of 65 big blinds, I prefer to wait for stronger hands to open from early position.

We’re first-in from middle position after UTG folds. K6o is too weak to open profitably here: it’s heavily dominated by the calling/3-betting ranges of CO/BTN and plays poorly postflop, and limping just invites multiway trouble and reverse implied odds. With decent stack depth, better to preserve chips for stronger opens/spots.

Cutoff open with 7♣6♥ (connected, offsuit) — marginal but playable in position. There have been two folds, blinds are posted (pot 150) and no raise yet, so I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds and play postflop in position. I’ll open to 300 (3×BB) — a standard sizing that applies pressure, discourages limp-calling, and keeps my options postflop. If I face a large 3‑bet or Claude shoves his short stack (~1900) I’ll fold to heavy action; I’ll proceed postflop if called and use positional advantage to realize equity.

Ad9c on the button facing a standard 3x open from the cutoff (Minni). A9o is a strong hand in position with good playability, ace blocker, and straight potential. Facing a single raiser with deep stacks, calling realizes equity well postflop while maintaining position. Folding is too tight for button range; 3-betting is possible but flatting keeps the pot smaller and disguises strength against an aggressive opponent like Minni, who has shown frequent raises and c-bets in history. Pot odds are favorable (300 to call into 450, plus implied odds). Will induce action from blinds if they defend.

I have 3h7h in SB with 1900 chips (19 BBs). Minni raised, Elon called, and I need 250 to call into a 750 pot (~3:1 odds). While suited, 37s is a weak holding that plays poorly out of position against two opponents. With my short stack, I should preserve chips for better spots rather than chasing marginal draws OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 3d. Minni (CO) raised to 300 (3BB) and Elon (BTN) called. While the direct pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win 750, requiring ~21% equity), my hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. The larger 3x open reduces the attractiveness of defending speculative hands compared to a min-raise. Equity realization with 63s OOP against an aggressive opener and a deep-stacked caller will be low, likely turning a marginal theoretical call into a negative EV play in practice due to difficulty navigating post-flop. Preserving my 37BB stack for better spots is the disciplined choice.

I opened cutoff to 300 and Elon (button) called; flop is 5♠-5♦-3♣ and the pot is 750. My 7♣6♥ has no pair and only a backdoor wheel (4) — basically air — but this is a very dry, low paired board that heavily favors my preflop raising range. A small continuation bet leverages my range advantage and has good fold equity against Elon’s likely calling range (A/K/Qx, single overcards, some small pairs). I want to keep the pot manageable versus stronger holdings (33/55/pocket pairs) while folding out weaker hands, so I’ll make a small c-bet ~1/3 pot. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I check the turn and re-evaluate (look to fold to significant aggression unless a 4 hits or I pick up equity); if he check-raises or jams I’ll fold to avoid playing big pots with essentially no showdown value.

Preflop call with Ad9c on the button was standard for position and playability against Minni's cutoff open. Flop of 5s5d3c is a paired low board, giving me ace-high with no pair, no straight draw (9 too high for gutshot), and only backdoor flush potential with the Ac blocker. Minni's 250 bet into 1000 (1/4 pot) is a small c-bet consistent with her aggressive history of frequent raises and continuation bets. Her range includes many pairs (55,53,35, any pocket pairs), sets (55 specifically blocked somewhat but still), better aces, and bluffs, but my equity is mediocre (~30-40% vs typical cbet range) and poor on future dangerous streets (runner-runner draws or turn pairing the 3). Calling commits poorly without position advantage outweighing the dead money risk; folding preserves deep stack (22k) for superior spots in this multiway tournament phase. No need to raise as bluff blocker thin here.

We’re UTG 6-handed with 9♣7♥ offsuit. This hand is too weak to open from the earliest position: it’s dominated by opponents’ calling/3-bet ranges, plays poorly out of position, and doesn’t realize equity well without suitedness. With plenty of playable stack depth (73BB), we don’t need to take a thin, negative-EV spot. Best is to preserve chips and wait for stronger opens/steal spots from later positions.

Holding J♠4♥ in middle position with no raise yet — this is a weak, easily dominated hand and not worth an MP open-raise (I don’t want to get 3‑bet/isolate with such a rag). The cost to see a flop is small (100 into a 150 pot) so I’ll take a cheap flop and preserve stack depth for better spots. There are aggressive players behind (Elon with a big stack, Claude short on the button who could shove), so I will fold to meaningful preflop aggression or any large raise; if checked to me on the flop I’ll play cautiously, only continuing with a jack or clear improvement and otherwise check/fold to pressure.

Preflop in cutoff with 6c2h, one of the worst possible hands with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. A single limper (Minni) from middle position doesn't justify entering the pot; this hand has zero equity realization potential and will be dominated or outdrawn postflop. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in a tournament setting aiming to maximize over 150 hands.

T3o is a weak hand. Even on the button with position, this isn't worth playing with only 19 BBs. Folding is the correct play here - no need to waste chips on marginal spots when I'm already short-stacked.

I am in the Small Blind with Ah 3s. There is one limper (Minni) and the Big Blind left to act. The pot is 250 and it costs me only 50 chips to complete the bet to 100. This offers excellent pot odds (5:1) for a hand that contains an Ace, which often has reasonable equity against a limping range. However, A3o plays poorly post-flop out of position, making a raise (isolation) too risky against a deep-stacked opponent. Folding is too tight given the price. The best line is to complete (call) and proceed cautiously post-flop, likely check-folding if I don't improve significantly.

I have 4c5c in the Big Blind. The action has been limped around to me by Minni and completed by Pro in the Small Blind. Since I am already in the pot and have a speculative hand that plays well in multi-way pots but is not strong enough to value raise against a deep-stacked limper, I will check to see a free flop and attempt to realize my equity.
