I have flopped Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker, but significantly, I also have the Nut Flush Draw (holding Ah on a Qh 5h board). This is a very strong hand in a limped pot. Being out of position in a 3-way pot, checking is the standard play. It allows me to keep the pot controlled, induce bluffs from opponents who might stab at the pot, and leaves open the option to check-call or check-raise depending on the action. Leading out might fold out worse hands or isolate me against two-pair hands, though I have great equity against almost anything. I will check to see how the action develops.

I have bottom pair (fives) on a board with an Ace and a Queen. In a three-way limped pot, this is a marginal hand. Checking is the best play to see the turn cheaply and gauge the strength of my opponents' hands. If someone bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is standard.

I have J♠4♥ on Qh Ac 5h — no pair and only a backdoor heart draw (needs both turn+river hearts). It’s been checked to me multiway and the pot is small (300) relative to stacks. A flop bet here is a thin bluff that will usually get called or raised by Ax/Qx or decent draws, so I prefer pot control and to realize equity cheaply. I’ll check and only continue if I pair or pick up a meaningful draw; otherwise fold to significant aggression.

I have improved to trips (Three of a Kind, Aces) on the turn with the Ad, and I also hold the Nut Flush Draw (Ah). The board is now Qh Ac 5h Ad. Given that the flop checked through, it is unlikely my opponents have a strong Ace, and there is only one Ace left in the deck. My kicker (3) is weak, so I am technically vulnerable to a better Ace (A6+), but I am ahead of Queens, Fives, and flush draws. Since my opponents (Minni specifically) have shown passive tendencies (limp-calling), they are unlikely to bet a Queen or a draw if I check. Therefore, I should bet to extract value from worse hands like Qx or heart draws. A bet of roughly half the pot (150 into 300) is appropriate to get calls from these marginal hands while keeping the pot manageable if I am outkicked.

On a board of Qh-Ac-5h-Ad, my hand (a pair of fives) is extremely weak. While I have bottom pair, the board has two Aces and a Queen, meaning any opponent with an Ace or a Queen has me significantly outkicked or has a better two-pair/trips. Pro has led out for 150 into a 300 pot on the turn after a checked flop. This bet could represent an Ace, a Queen, or a heart draw. Furthermore, Minni is still left to act behind me. Given the multi-way nature of the pot and the fact that my hand is likely behind a wide range of hands that my opponents would limp and then bet/call with, it is best to fold here rather than chase a marginal hand.

Fold. On Qh Ac 5h Ad with J♠4♥ I have no pair and no meaningful draw (the runner-runner heart possibility is dead on the turn). Pro (SB) leads out 150 into a 450 pot; calling 150 would require ~25% equity (150/(450+150)), but I only have 6 outs (3 jacks + 3 fours) → 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river, so calling is -EV. Even if I pair the river I’m frequently behind to Ax, trips, or a made full house; raising as a bluff is too risky against a player showing aggression here and would bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win. This matches my pre-stated plan to check and fold to significant turn aggression — I fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

A6o from UTG in a 6-max game is marginal but it's an Ace and worth opening — I prefer to take the initiative instead of limping into a multiway pot where my weak kicker loses a lot of value. Table open-sizing has been around 3x the big blind, so I'll make a standard 3BB open to 300 to get reasonable fold equity and discourage easy flats/squeezes. Plan: if I'm single‑raised big by an obvious 3-bettor (e.g. Elon) I'll fold given my weak kicker and OOP. If I'm called, I'll continuation-bet selective dry flops and play cautiously; if a short stack (Claude ~19BB) shoves over my raise I'll call because of his wide shove range and favorable pot odds. Overall raising here maximizes fold equity and keeps control of the hand rather than letting the pot go multiway for free.

4d5d are suited connectors with good playability and implied odds given deep effective stacks (mine 22k+, Minni 17k). Game history shows loose action with frequent calls and check-calls, increasing multiway pot potential where this hand thrives. UTG raise from Minni is standard, but calling from middle position offers position on blinds and good pot odds (300 into 450+). Folding is too tight; 3-betting not ideal OOP vs UTG range. Will call and play postflop flexibly.
73 offsuit is a trash hand. With only 19 BBs and facing a raise with a caller, I have no reason to get involved with such weak holdings. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with 8d 6d, a suited one-gapper. The player UTG (Minni) raised to 300 (3BB) and the player in Middle Position (Elon) called. My stack is 3900 (39BB). While 86s plays well in position and multi-way, calling requires committing nearly 8% of my stack pre-flop against an UTG opening range, which is typically strong, even from an active player like Minni. Additionally, with a stack of roughly 40BB, implied odds for speculative hands are reduced compared to deep-stacked play. The risk of being squeezed by the blinds or failing to realize equity post-flop against larger stacks makes folding the most prudent long-term decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Minni (UTG) raised to 300 and Elon (Middle) called. Q3o is a very weak hand, and calling here would put me out of position against two players with significant stacks. Even though I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, the post-flop playability of this hand is extremely poor, and I am likely dominated by Minni's opening range. It is best to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Pocket TT is well ahead of Minni’s UTG opening range and Elon’s flatting range, but it plays awkwardly multiway and out of position if we just call. A squeeze 3-bet both builds a value pot vs worse pairs/broadways and denies equity to overcards, while also often isolating to 1 opponent. I’ll use a larger OOP sizing versus open + caller to discourage a cheap multiway flop and define ranges; if faced with a big 4-bet, we can re-evaluate rather than auto-stacking off 70+ BB deep.

I opened UTG with A♦6♥ to 300, but facing a 3‑bet to 1,500 from the big blind (FiveTwo) I need to call another 1,200 into a 2,150 pot — about 36% equity required to break even. A6o is frequently dominated by a typical BB 3‑bet range and plays poorly out of position, especially if Elon (who already called my open) continues. 4‑bet bluffing here would be thin (I’m not comfortable committing more with a weak kicker) and calling commits chips into a spot where I’ll be behind or multiway and have poor post‑flop prospects. Preserve the stack and wait for a cleaner spot — fold.

Facing a BB squeeze 3-bet to 1500 after UTG open and my MP call with 4d5d suited connectors. Pot odds are favorable: 1200 to call into 2150 pot (~36% equity needed post-call, and 45dd has strong playability vs a wide squeeze range including Ax suited, broadway, connectors, bluffs). My stack is deep at 22k, opponent's 5.8k allows implied odds if we hit. Postflop OOP but hand realizes equity well multiway or heads-up. History shows FiveTwo aggressive with frequent raises and isolation attempts, suggesting polarized range we block draws and pairs. Folding is too tight given prior call and dynamics; 4-betting speculative hand risks domination and low fold equity. Calling keeps pot multi-street potential and flexibility.

We’re heads-up after squeezing preflop, and this A-high board is generally better for the 3-bettor’s range (we have lots of Ax/overpairs in range even if we personally don’t). With TT we’re an underpair that benefits from folding out Elon’s whiffed broadways and some medium pairs, while keeping the pot controlled versus his continuing Ax. I’ll use a small c-bet to apply pressure and deny equity; if raised, we can comfortably fold, and if called we’ll often shut down/check turns unless we improve or get a great barreling card.

Heads-up on Ac8h4c flop with 4d5d, flopping bottom pair plus diamond flush draw (9 outs to flush, plus 3 outs to trips/set 4s, total ~30% equity vs typical squeeze value range like top pair, two pair, sets; even more vs polarized including bluffs). FiveTwo's 900 bet into 4250 pot (21% size) is small and could be thin value, protection vs club draws, or probe. Pre-flop squeeze range is wide-polarized, we block some Ax/4x combos and have good playability. Pot odds excellent (900 into 4250 = 17.5% equity needed, we have far more). Deep effective stack (his ~5k remaining) offers massive implied odds if we improve; SPR post-call ~1.3:1 allows controlled aggression later if we hit. Folding squanders pre-call investment and equity realization; raising OOP risks bloating pot with marginal hand vulnerable to better Ax/sets and low fold equity vs aggressive FiveTwo (history of frequent raises/isolations). Calling keeps pot manageable, realizes equity multi-street, disguises strength, and sets up turns where flush/straight draws complete or pair improves.

Turn SPR is ~1 (pot 5150, we have 4925), so any bet we make basically commits us. After Elon calls our flop c-bet on A84, his range is heavily weighted toward Ax/8x/sets and some floats; the Jh is a particularly bad barreling card because it improves a chunk of his floats into Jx (QJ/KJ/JT) while we still have a marginal underpair with little equity vs his value range. Jamming as a bluff risks running into Ax too often, and betting small doesn’t really accomplish anything given stack sizes.

Best is to check and realize whatever showdown value we have, while being ready to fold to significant aggression (and occasionally get a free river if he checks back with missed broadways).

Heads-up on turn Ac8h4cJh with 4d5d for bottom pair + nut diamond flush draw (~9 flush outs + pair for showdown = high equity ~35-45% vs polarized range). FiveTwo's flop c-bet (small sizing into large pot) after preflop squeeze, then turn check OOP, suggests possible medium strength (Ax no heart/club, missed draw, or thin value/protection), weakness (bluff catcher or air), less likely monster given check-back frequency in history. Board drawy (clubs, hearts now with J), J brings T9 straight poss. but blocks few strong hands. OOP but checked to me: betting extracts value from worse pairs/draws/floats, denies equity to his draws (clubs, hearts), builds pot for implied odds on diamond river (his 4.9k stack vulnerable to shove). Check risks giving free card to overcards/draws or check-raise trap, but pot control also valid; however, hand strong enough to bet thin value given dynamics, deep stack allows flexibility, low SPR (~1:1 post bet) maximizes pressure. Size ~60% pot (3000) balances value/protection, induces calls from Ax/87/76 etc. while folding out pure air. Consistent with prior postflop calls realizing equity aggressively when checked to.

Elon’s line is very value-dense: cold-call pre vs UTG then call a squeeze, call flop on A84, and now fire 58% pot on the J turn with an SPR that effectively forces us toward stacks. That sequence heavily weights him to Ax (including AJ improving), two-pair/sets (A8s/88/44), and some Jx that now beats TT. Our equity is poor (we basically need him to be bluffing a lot, and our only meaningful “improvement” is the 2 outs to a ten for a set). Calling is also awkward because we’ll be left with ~1.9k and face a near-automatic river jam, so we won’t realize enough bluff-catching value to justify the turn call. Shoving doesn’t generate fold equity because he’s getting a great price to call the extra ~1.9k with any Ax/Jx/value. Preserve the remaining stack and fold.

Qs7s is a weak suited hand from UTG in a 6-handed game. Standard range to open UTG is tighter: pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways, and select suited connectors like 87s+. Q7s has too much gap and dominated potential OOP. With shorter stacks behind (Claude 19BB, Pro 39BB), risk of tough spots postflop. Deep stack but no need to speculate early position. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

A2o in middle position with 19BB stack. Marginal hand but I have an ace and need to start building chips. With my short stack, I should either open-raise or fold. A standard 2.5x open to 250 puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to take down dead money. Folding every marginal ace will let blinds eat my stack. Opening here.

I am in the Cutoff with 8h Td, facing a raise to 250 from Claude in Middle Position. Claude has a short stack of 1650 (16.5 BB). T8o is a weak hand that is dominated by many hands in an MP opening range. I am not closing the action and calling would leave me vulnerable to squeezes from the Button or Blinds. The pot odds do not justify calling with this hand given the reverse implied odds and lack of playability. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am holding Ten-Four offsuit on the button. Claude, who is extremely short-stacked with only about 16 big blinds, has opened for 2.5x. My hand is well below the threshold for calling a raise or 3-betting, especially against a player whose stack size suggests they are likely looking to commit their chips soon. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude is opening to 2.5x with a short stack (16.5bb), which reduces the EV of light 3-betting since he can jam and force us into a marginal call. K5s is a reasonable SB defend vs this sizing (good playability/flush potential), and calling keeps the pot smaller while letting us realize equity. We’ll proceed cautiously out of position and can still fold if the BB squeezes big.

Facing a MP open to 250 with the SB calling makes this a 3‑way pot. I hold J♠7♥ — a marginal, easily dominated hand. 3‑betting/raising to isolate would bloat the pot and likely induce a shove from Claude (short ~16.5bb) or a call from the SB; I don’t want to build a big pot with this hand. Calling is cheap: I need to put in 150 more (250 total) to contest the 600 pot, which only requires ~20% equity. With my deep stack I can see a flop, look for a J or decent draw, and fold to heavy aggression if I miss. Plan: call now, play the flop cautiously and avoid committing without a clear improvement.

This flop (9-8-7 with a diamond draw) is extremely connected and hits both the preflop raiser’s broadway/medium-pair range and especially the BB’s defending range. With K5cc we have no pair, no straight draw, and only very thin backdoor club potential, so betting into two players has low fold equity and risks getting check-raised or floated with better equity. Best is to check and realize whatever equity we have while keeping the pot controlled OOP.

I flatted preflop to see a flop cheaply and I’ve hit a marginal made hand — middle pair (7s) with a Jack kicker on 8♠‑9♦‑7♦. This board is highly connected: it favors the MP raiser’s range (9x, 8x, 10x, pocket pairs, connectors) and there are two diamonds on board, so opponents can have strong diamond draws. Claude is short (≈16.5bb) and capable of shoving as a pressure move if I bet; FiveTwo (SB) called preflop and can have lots of draws/overcards. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a vulnerable one‑pair hand. Pot control is the priority: check and let the preflop raiser c‑bet into me so I can call small bets and fold to large aggression, or realize equity for free if checked through. If checked to the turn I can reassess (barrel only with improvement or clear fold equity).

Flop is 8s-9d-7d - extremely coordinated and wet board. I have Ac2d - complete air with no pair, no draw (not even backdoor diamonds since I only have 2d). This board heavily favors calling ranges with all the straight possibilities and diamond draws. With two opponents checking to me, a c-bet would be pure bluff into a board that connects well with their ranges. With only 1650 chips (16.5BB), I can't afford to bluff here. Check and give up unless I hit an ace.

Turn is still very dynamic and multiway on 9-8-7-4. With K5cc we have no made hand and only a thin gutshot to a 6 (and occasional K-high showdown / K river pair), while this board heavily connects with both BB’s defending range and Claude’s continuing range. Leading as a bluff has poor fold equity (tons of pairs + draws) and risks getting jammed on by Claude’s short stack, forcing us to abandon equity. Best is to check, keep the pot small OOP, and decide versus any bet based on sizing/opponents.

I have J♠7♥ on 8♠-9♦-7♦-4♠ — middle pair with a decent kicker but very vulnerable. This started as an MP open and became a multiway pot; both opponents showed passivity by checking the flop and SB checked the turn. Claude is short (~1,650) and is capable of shoving if I bet, which would force a difficult fold/call decision with only a single pair. There are many hands in the opener’s range that currently beat me (9x, 8x, T/x, pocket pairs and diamond combos), and I have very little protection (no real flush or straight outs). Priority is pot control and preserving stack equity: check the turn, see the river for free, call small river bets with showdown value, and fold to large aggression (or only continue if I improve).

Turn brought 4s - a complete brick. Both opponents checked to me again. I have Ac2d - pure air with no pair and no meaningful draws. While their double-checks show weakness, betting here with no equity is risky with only 1650 chips (16.5BB). If I bet and face resistance, I'm torching my stack. Check back, take the free card, and hope to hit an ace on the river.

We arrive at the river with pure K-high on a very connected 9-8-7 texture that will contain a lot of one-pair hands (9x/8x/7x) and some slowplays from both the BB defend range and Claude’s short-stack open range. Even though flop/turn checked through, multiway it’s still hard to make *both* opponents fold a pair, and if I bet I also reopen the door for Claude to jam and force me to burn chips with a mandatory fold. Taking the free showdown is best: we occasionally win versus double-missed draws/overcards, and we avoid lighting money on fire with a low fold-equity bluff.

Check. I have J♠7♥ on 8♠–9♦–7♦–4♠–4♣: middle pair with a decent kicker but very vulnerable (many hands in the raiser’s range beat me — 9x/8x/pocket pairs/trips). This was a multiway pot and both opponents showed passivity by checking flop and turn, so my bluff equity is low and a bet would mainly fold air or get called by hands that beat me. Claude is short (~1,650) and capable of shoving as a pressure move — betting risks inflating the pot and facing an all‑in I don’t want to call with a single pair. Priority is pot control and realizing showdown value. Plan: check to showdown; if an opponent barrels a small bet (~≤1/3 pot ≈ ≤250) I’ll call for pot odds and because we beat many missed hands, but I’ll fold to large raises/shoves since those ranges are usually ahead.

River paired the 4s. Both opponents checked to me again on a 8-9-7-4-4 board. I have Ac2d - just ace high. Their triple-check line suggests weakness, likely missed draws or weak showdown hands. With ace-high, I actually have some showdown value - I could beat missed diamond draws, K-high, Q-high. Betting would only fold out hands I beat and get called by hands that beat me. With my short 16.5BB stack, preserving chips is important. Check back and see if ace-high is good.

J5 suited from UTG with only 16.5 BB is a clear fold. This hand is too weak to open from early position, especially with a short stack where I need to be selective. J5s lacks the high card strength and connectivity needed to profitably open from UTG.

I hold Queen-Ten suited (Qh Th) in Middle Position. This is a strong starting hand that plays well post-flop and warrants an opening raise, especially when folded to in a 6-handed game. With a stack of 39 BBs, I want to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds or play in position against the blinds. A standard open raise of 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate here to balance aggression and risk.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Eight offsuit (Ts 8c). A player in middle position (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. Ten-Eight offsuit is a weak hand that does not play well against a middle-position opening range. Calling would likely lead to being out of position or dominated post-flop, and there are aggressive players behind me (FiveTwo, Minni, Elon) who could squeeze. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Pro is opening from middle position to 2.5bb with ~36bb behind. On the button we have position, but 8♠4♠ is too low in equity and playability versus an MP range, and it performs poorly multiway if the big stacks in the blinds come along. It also doesn’t make a good 3-bet bluff here because Pro’s stack depth makes jam/continue ranges relatively tight but punishing (we’d be forced to fold to a shove and burn chips). Best chip-EV is to preserve stack and wait for stronger/button stealing spots.

I’m in the small blind with J♥7♠ facing a standard 2.5bb open to 250 from Pro. J7 offsuit is a marginal, easily‑dominated hand vs an opener — and I’ll be out of position against the raiser and the big blind. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives only ~3:1 (need ≈25% equity) but multiway pots or a BB squeeze (Elon has a big, aggressive stack) will crush my post‑flop prospects. A 3‑bet bluff is unattractive here against two players with deep/medium stacks. Preserve chips and fold to pick a better spot later.

AcTc is an excellent hand from the big blind facing an isolated middle position raise to 250. It has excellent equity against Pro's likely raising range (dominating weaker aces, suited connectors, broadways), strong suited playability with flush and straight potential, and positionally we're OOP but can take the initiative preflop. Pro has shown willingness to call down light in spots but also folds to aggression (e.g., Hand 21). With deep stacks (Pro 3650, me 25k+), a 3-bet to 900 (3.6x the raise) builds the pot when ahead, isolates, and applies pressure. Calling is fine but gives up initiative and plays OOP multiway potentially; folding is never correct. Raising maximizes value and fold equity.

I opened to 250 with QhTh and faced a large 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind (Elon). While QTs is a decent hand, the 3-bet size is large (3.6x), offering poor immediate odds. My stack is relatively short (starting 36.5 BB), and calling would commit nearly 20% of my stack pre-flop, leaving me with an awkward Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of roughly 1.5 against the chip leader. QTs is easily dominated by hands like AQ, KQ, and AT in a 3-bet range. Given my position as the second-shortest stack, preserving chips to look for spots where I can apply pressure (rather than calling it off) is the better strategy. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Three offsuit (Q3o), which is a very weak hand. Given the poor position and lack of playability or strength, this is a standard fold. I have 36.5 BBs and should preserve my stack for better opportunities.

T5o is a very weak hand and does not meet the requirements for an opening range from middle position. With several players left to act behind me, including the short-stacked Claude and the massive chip leader Elon, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Cutoff is an opening spot, but 8♥3♥ is too low to profitably open in a 6-max high-roller dynamic, especially with two big stacks behind (Button and SB) who can 3-bet/pressure and a short BB who can jam. With poor high-card strength and dominated flush potential, this is a clean fold rather than burning chips trying to steal 1.5bb.

I'm on the button with A♠ J♠ — a strong, positionally powerful hand. There were folds to me and the pot is 150 with 100 to call; the minimum raise is 200. I want to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and build a pot I can play in position, while keeping the sizing manageable against Elon's large stack and Claude's short stack (1550) who may shove. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is the right balance: it pressures the blinds, isolates or folds weaker hands, and still allows me to comfortably call a Claude shove or adjust if Elon 3-bets. Raise to 300.

Pocket Queens is a premium hand, top of our range. Action folded to Minni on the button, who raises frequently with a wide range based on history (often steals or marginal hands). We should 3-bet for value, fold equity, and to isolate/deny equity to BB short stack. Raising to 1000 (about 3.3x the open) is a standard size: builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against worse hands like AJ+, KQ, suited broadways, pairs below QQ. Calling risks multiway pot with BB; folding is too weak. If shoved on, QQ is crushingly ahead.

K7o facing a 3-bet with only 15.5BB stack. Calling 900 would commit 58% of my stack with a marginal hand. K7o plays poorly against a 3-bet range, and if I call I'm essentially pot-committed with only 650 behind. This is a clear fold - not worth risking most of my short stack with such a weak holding.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♠J♠ and Elon (SB) 3‑bet to 1000. Claude (BB) folded so it’s a single 3‑bettor out of position. With AJs in position against an aggressive, often wide 3‑betting opponent and deep effective stacks (my 17,550 vs his 24,825), the highest EV line is to call the 700 more (total 1000). Calling preserves my positional advantage, realizes equity (pot ~1400; calling 700 gives ~3:1 odds), and keeps post‑flop options open — I can c‑bet strong flops, pot‑control or fold to heavy resistance on bad textures, and exploit any postflop aggression. Folding is too weak with this hand; a small 4‑bet would bloat the pot and risk a big 5‑bet shove. Call 700 more (1000 total) and evaluate the flop.

Pocket QQ remains strong post-flop on this K-8-4 rainbow board, which is relatively dry with minimal draws (guts shots or backdoor flushes at best). As the pre-flop 3-bettor from SB, my range is perceived as strong (AK, KK, AA, sets), and QQ blocks some of villain's value. Minni's button calling range includes Kx (KQ, KJ, ATs+), weaker pairs (JJ-TT), and some bluffs/floaters. C-betting continues aggression, denies equity to overcards/draws, extracts value from Ax/Qx/Tx worse, and applies fold equity against floats. Checking risks giving free cards or facing a bet where I have to fold too often. Size to ~2/3 pot (1400) balances value/protection/fold equity without overbetting into a calling station. Deeper stacks allow post-flop flexibility if called.

Elon 3‑bet me preflop and I called to 1,000 with A♠J♠. On 8♦ K♠ 4♣ he makes a small c‑bet of 1,400 into a 3,500 pot — a sizing consistent with a wide continuation range from an aggressive 3‑bettor. I have two overcards, a decent kicker and backdoor spade equity, plus positional advantage. Calling (1,400 more, 2,400 total committed) gets ~3.5:1 pot odds, keeps his bluffs and weaker holdings in the pot, and preserves options to float and apply pressure on later streets or extract value if I pair up. A raise would bloat the pot and risk getting shoved or priced out with only Ace‑high; folding is too passive versus a small probe. Plan: call now and reevaluate on the turn — continue with aggression if I improve or can credibly take the pot, otherwise control the pot size or fold to heavy aggression.

Turn card pairs the King (Kd), making the board 8d Ks 4c Kd - very dry, no flush draws complete (only two diamonds), straight draws minimal (gutshots like 5x). Preflop 3-bet and flop continuation bet represent AK, KK, sets strongly; villain's button calling range vs 3-bet (KQ+, AJs+, TT+) narrows post-flop to primarily Kx (AK, KQ, KJ), sets (88/44 unlikely), or QQ/JJ (blocked somewhat). Most of villain's value range now has trips (Kxx), beating QQ, but they improve unimproved hands and hit villain's floats/draws minimally. QQ beats all bluffs, weaker floats (AJ/QJ post-flop calls), and any trapped pairs below. Aggression continues profitably: value from worse (unlikely but AJ-type overpairs/floats), fold equity vs marginal trips (K9o/KTo don't love facing pot bet into paired board), protection vs river draws (backdoor flush/straight). Check risks free card to villain's draws or check-raise jam with Kx. Pot-sized bet (4900) ~1x pot maximizes pressure, extracts thin value/call-downs, denies equity; villain can't call profitably without top of range (AK/KK), given SPR low (~3:1 effective after). Deep stacks but heads-up vs aggressive Minni (history shows folds to pressure like hand 16/34); consistent with image as preflop aggressor.

Elon 3‑bet pre, I called; he c‑bet the K♠8♦4♣ flop and I called. The turn pairs the board with K♦ and Elon now puts in a large 4,900 into a 9,800 pot. Calling 4,900 to win 14,700 requires ~33% equity. I have A♠J♠ — no pair, only single‑card outs (A/J) that often don’t beat a King in his hand. Elon's line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop c‑bet, big turn bet on a paired King) is heavily weighted toward Kx/trips, sets or other strong value hands; while bluffs are possible, their frequency is low here. Raising would commit a large portion of my stack with very poor equity and little fold equity. Fold and preserve chips for a better, more favorable spot in position.

I am in the UTG position with 9h 6s, which is a very weak hand to play from early position. With the table dynamics including some short-stacked players and several aggressive opponents, there is no strategic value in entering the pot with this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

No one has entered the pot yet and we’re ~47bb deep. A4s is a standard open from middle in 6-max: it has decent playability (nut-flush potential, wheel/straight potential), blocks strong Ax continues, and benefits from picking up blinds/antes-equivalent dead money. I’ll use a small raise to keep risk down versus the CO/BTN (especially Elon) who can 3-bet aggressively; plan is to continue selectively (mostly fold) versus larger 3-bets, and play postflop with position on the blinds when called.

Facing a 2.5bb open from MP with J♠8♥ in the cutoff. J8o is a marginal hand — frequently dominated by the opener’s MP range and it has poor playability out of position. There are dangerous players behind: Elon (button) is very aggressive and likely to 3-bet, Claude (SB) has a short stack that could shove, and Pro (BB) will defend. Calling invites multiway action and ugly postflop decisions; a tiny raise to 400 is too small to be a proper isolation or a credible 3-bet. With a healthy 154BB I don’t need to force marginal spots — I’ll fold and wait for a stronger opportunity.

Q2 offsuit is a complete trash hand with no straight or flush potential and dominated by any queen or better. Facing a preflop raise from FiveTwo in middle position into a 400 pot, calling 250 OOP would be a loose play committing chips to a hand with poor postflop playability and equity. My deep effective stack (28k) and button position are irrelevant for this speculative holding. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in this multiway tournament dynamic, as evidenced by FiveTwo's occasional aggression in prior hands but no reason to speculate here.

Kc6c in the SB facing a min-raise from FiveTwo. With a suited king, I have decent playability - potential flush draws and can hit top pair. Getting 2:1 pot odds (call 200 to win 400). My stack is short at 1500 (15 BBs), but calling here is reasonable given the odds and suited nature of my hand. 3-betting would commit too much of my stack with a marginal hand. Will call and play postflop carefully.

I am in the Big Blind with [2d 8s], facing a raise from FiveTwo (MP) to 2.5BB and a call from Claude (SB). The pot is 600 and I need to call 150. This gives me pot odds of 5:1 (16.6%), but 82o is one of the worst hands in poker. It has very poor playability and equity, especially out of position in a multi-way pot. Despite the good price, defending here is negative EV. I will fold.

Flop is 4d8d7s - completely missed my Kc6c. I have king high only, no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), and no straight draw. This board likely connects better with FiveTwo's raising range. With only 1300 chips left, I should check and re-evaluate based on their action. No reason to bluff into a board that hits many hands in their range.

Heads-up in position with a short-stacked SB (effective ~1050 into 600, SPR ~1.75). This flop (4-8-7) connects fairly well with SB’s defend range, but they also have plenty of whiffs/overcards that will just have to fold versus a c-bet, and my A4 has enough showdown value that I don’t want to inflate the pot with a big sizing. A small c-bet denies equity from two overcards, can take it down immediately, and keeps me uncommitted so I can comfortably bet/fold versus a check-raise jam (which will be heavily weighted to 8x/7x/sets/strong draws).

I have Kc6c on 4d8d7s - complete air with king high. No pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw. FiveTwo's 1/4 pot bet is small but I have zero equity to continue. Calling 200 with no showdown value and no draws is just bleeding chips. With 1300 left (13 BBs), preserving stack is important. Easy fold.

6-handed UTG we should still play a relatively tight range. 6♥2♠ is dominated, plays poorly postflop (no high-card value, weak straight/flush potential), and we’re out of position against stacks that cover us. Limping invites squeezes and multiway pots; opening would be burning chips with little fold equity versus capable opponents. Preserve the 50BB stack for better spots.

Holding Ac8d in middle position — a playable Ace with a medium kicker. I want to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and isolate the short-stacked button while not overcommitting against the big stacks behind. A standard 3×BB open to 300 is the right sizing here: it’s large enough to discourage limp-calls and give me postflop leverage, but small relative to my 15k stack. Plan: call a short-stack shove, but re-evaluate (and often fold) to a large 3-bet from Elon/Flash unless the price and reads justify continuing.

K4o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit king with a weak 4 kicker. Facing a standard raise from Minni in middle position, this is a clear fold from cutoff despite position. The hand has poor playability postflop, is easily dominated by better kings (KQ, KJ, etc.), and doesn't block strong hands well. Pot odds are not compelling (calling 300 into 450), and with deep stacks and opponents behind (including short-stacked button), better to wait for stronger holdings to realize equity. Minni has shown aggression in past hands but no need to speculate here. Folding preserves our large stack (283 BBs) for premium spots to maximize long-term EV.

4d5h is a weak hand, and with only 1300 chips (13 BBs), I can't afford to play speculative hands. Calling 300 is 23% of my stack with a marginal holding. Even with button position, 54o doesn't play well against a raise, and my short stack limits implied odds. Better to wait for a stronger spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 5s, a very weak offsuit hand. Minni has raised to 300 from Middle Position. I am facing a 3bb raise and would be out of position for the rest of the hand against a player with a massive chip stack. My hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify calling 250 chips into a pot of 450. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 9d and need to call 200 more to see a flop against a 3x raise from Minni in Middle position. Minni has been very active and currently holds a large stack, suggesting a wide opening range. With pot odds of approximately 2.25 to 1, Q9o is a standard hand to defend the big blind. I will call and attempt to realize my equity post-flop, though I must be cautious playing out of position against an aggressive chip leader.

I am out of position on a board that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Tc-Td-As). My Q9o has very little equity and no real potential for improvement. I will check to see how the aggressor proceeds; if they bet, I will almost certainly fold.

I opened preflop and got one caller (Flash). Flop Tc Td As: I have A♣8♦ — top pair but a weak kicker and there are combos that beat me (TT trips, AT full house) or dominate me (AK/AQ). Villain checked to me and I have position, so I should take the initiative to extract value from worse Aces and weaker holdings (A2–A9, Kx, some floats) and deny free cards. A bet of ~60% pot (~400 into 650) puts pressure on his wide calling range, narrows his continuing combos, and gives me fold equity; if he check‑raises large I will give credit to trips/AT and fold, while I’ll call smaller raises and reevaluate on the turn.

I am out of position on an A-T-T flop which is very favorable for the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have Q-high with no draw and zero showdown value. Minni's 400 bet into a 650 pot represents a strong range or at least a standard continuation bet that I cannot profitably continue against with my current holding. Following my previous logic, I will fold and conserve my stack.

I'm UTG in a 6-handed 50/100 game with A♥7♦. In 6-max A7o is a borderline but playable opener — it has a strong blocker (Ace) and decent postflop equity, so I want to take the initiative rather than limp. I will open to 3x the BB (300) — larger than the min-raise to generate fold equity and narrow the field against the SB (6000) and BB (4925). With a deep stack (15800) I can apply pressure, but I will be cautious postflop out of position: c-bet selectively and control the pot. If I face a large 3-bet from a big stack I'll fold; versus small 3-bets or single calls I'll proceed to play for value/top-pair or use blockers to bluff on later streets.

Ts4h is a complete trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, low cards with poor playability. Facing a UTG raise from Minni (who has been aggressive but UTG suggests a stronger range like pairs, broadway, suited connectors), calling or raising would be a massive mistake out of position with many players left to act. My stack is deep at 28k, pot is small, better to fold and wait for premium hands to maximize EV over the tournament.

I have QQ with only 1300 chips (13 BB). This is a premium hand and I'm short-stacked. Against Minni's UTG raise, QQ is easily strong enough to shove. With my stack size, I can't afford to flat and play post-flop - I need to maximize value with this premium holding. All-in is the clear play here.

I hold 3s 4c on the Button, which is a very weak hand (7-high unsuited connector). The action in front of me is heavy: Minni opened to 300 from UTG, and Claude 3-bet to 1300 from the Cutoff. Facing a raise and a large re-raise, I have absolutely no equity to justify calling, especially since calling 1300 represents over 35% of my remaining 3500 chip stack. This is a trivial fold to preserve chips.

I am holding K2o in the Small Blind. There has been an UTG open from Minni and a substantial 3-bet from Claude. K2o is a very weak hand in this context, lacks post-flop playability, and is heavily dominated by the ranges of an UTG opener and a 3-better. Folding is the only viable strategic choice to preserve my 6,000 chip stack for better opportunities.

We’re in the BB with Q4o facing an UTG open and then a 3-bet to 1300 that appears to be Claude’s all-in (stack shown as 0). Calling 1200 to win ~2950 gives about 41% required equity, but Q4o is dominated and performs very poorly multiway versus an UTG range plus a short-stack 3-bet/jam range. There’s no profitable squeeze either—if we raise, we’re mostly just lighting chips on fire against stronger continues. With a 49bb-ish stack for this level (4925 at 50/100), preserving chips and waiting for better spots is best.

I opened UTG to 300 with A♥7♦ and Claude shoved/3-bet to 1300 (he's shown as all-in). The pot is 1750 and it costs me 1000 more to call (total commitment 1300), so I need ~36% equity to break even (1000/(1750+1000) ≈ 36.4%). A7o has an Ace blocker and reasonable equity versus a short-stack/cutoff shove that is likely to include a wide mix of pairs and Ax hands. The extra 1000 is a small fraction of my 15,500 stack (~6.5%), and given the pot odds and blocker value this is a +EV call versus a reasonably wide or mixed shove. I can't reraise (he's all-in), and folding would surrender too much equity. I will call the 1300 to see a showdown.

UTG position in a 6-handed game with 8c2s offsuit, one of the worst possible starting hands. No high card value, no suitedness, poor connectivity. Folding is standard to avoid playing out of position post-flop with such a weak range. Stack is deep at 28k, blinds low; better to wait for premium hands to maximize edge over 150 hands.
73s is a very weak hand, even suited. From middle position with 27.5 BB, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such poor holdings when there are still 4 players to act behind me.

I am in the Cutoff with Pocket Nines (9c 9s), which is a very strong hand in a 6-handed game. With a stack of 35 BB, I am in a comfortable zone to open-raise for value and to deny equity to random hands in the blinds. My standard opening size has been 2.5x (250), which balances pot control with fold equity. Limping is too passive and folding is out of the question. I will raise to 250 to isolate the button or blinds.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. The cutoff (Pro) has opened for a standard raise to 250. Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand and does not justify a call or a 3-bet, even with the advantage of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We’re in the SB facing a CO open with 6♦2♦. Although the immediate price (200 more) looks tempting, we’ll be out of position for the entire hand versus the raiser, and the BB is deep and can come along or squeeze, making realization of equity very poor. This hand makes too many dominated weak pairs and low flushes/straights that are hard to profitably navigate OOP, especially versus a shorter-stacked opener who can apply shove pressure. Best EV is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

Pro opened to 250 from the cutoff. I’m in the big blind with Q♦7♦ — marginal but suited and with decent post‑flop playability. Pot is 400 and it costs me 150 to call (~27% pot odds), so defending here is +EV versus a CO opening range. I don’t want to 3‑bet OOP with a speculative hand (min‑raise to 400 has little fold equity), and folding would be too tight given the pot odds and hand potential. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position — continue with top pair/strong draws, otherwise control the pot or fold to heavy aggression.

Flop: Qc 6h 7c — I have Q♦7♦: two pair (Queens & Sevens). Against the CO opener this is a very strong holding on this texture: it beats most of his continuing range other than sets or the occasional slow-played two pair. There are two clubs on board, so I need to charge club draws (A♣x, K♣x, T♣x) and deny free cards. Checking just gives him a free turn to improve or realize equity; betting extracts value from worse Qx / 7x / mid pairs and protects against draws. I’ll size to around half-to-two-thirds pot to get called by worse but still charge draws — bet 300 (~55% pot). If he raises I’ll re-evaluate (sets are the main worry); a small raise I’m prepared to continue/call, a large shove from his short stack is likely polarized and will be handled based on sizing and reads.

I am facing a donk bet of 300 into a pot of 550 on a Qc 6h 7c board. My opponent, Minni, has a large stack and a history of leading out on flops (Hands 22, 28, 39). Her range likely includes Qx, flush draws, straight draws, and pairs like 7x or 6x. With Pocket Nines, I have an overpair to the lower board cards and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). I am ahead of her draws and lower pairs. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (Qx) or strong draws, while folding is too weak given her aggressive tendencies. Calling allows me to use my position to evaluate the turn, keeping her range wide. I call to see the next card.

I have Q♦7♦ for two pair (Queens & Sevens) on Qc–6h–7c–5d. Heads‑up vs the CO’s opening/call range, very few hands beat me — mainly sets (66, 77) or a slow‑played QQ — while most Qx, 7x and one‑pair hands are behind. Pro checked the turn, showing either weakness or a trap; I want to charge club/straight draws and extract value from worse pairs without giving a free river. Betting around half‑to‑two‑thirds pot accomplishes that. I’ll make a value/protection bet of 650 into the 1,150 pot. If he check‑raises large it’s likely a set and I’ll re‑evaluate (fold to clear polarization/all‑in); a small raise or call I’ll continue to play and re‑assess river.

I hold a pair of Nines on a board of Qc 6h 7c 5d. The turn card is dangerous, completing straights for 89 and 34, and connecting with many two-pair combinations in Minni's Big Blind defense range (56, 75, etc.). Minni has taken an aggressive line by donk-betting the flop and barreling the turn for 650 into a pot of 1150. While I have picked up an open-ended straight draw (an 8 makes a 5-to-9 straight), my direct pot odds (approx. 26.5%) are worse than my probability of hitting the straight on the river (approx. 17%). Additionally, I am currently behind almost all of her value range (Qx, two pair, sets, straights) and only ahead of pure bluffs. The situation offers poor reverse implied odds and risks a significant portion of my remaining stack in a marginal spot against the chip leader. Preserving my 24 BB stack is the better strategic play.
JJ in UTG is a premium hand. With 27.5 BB stack, I should raise to build value and narrow the field. Standard 2.5x raise to 250 is appropriate.

I hold 9s 2h, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em (9-2 offsuit). Facing a raise from the Under-the-Gun player (Claude) while sitting in Middle Position, I have no strategic incentive to enter the pot. My equity against an UTG opening range is extremely low, and my stack of roughly 30 big blinds is valuable enough to preserve for better opportunities. Folding is the only correct play.

T8o is a weak hand to play against an under-the-gun (UTG) open raise. Claude's opening range from early position should be stronger than T8o, and I have three players acting after me, including the chip leaders. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude is opening UTG quite frequently and is only ~25bb deep (2500). On the button, AdTh plays well in position and benefits from isolating the shorter stack while also discouraging squeezes from the aggressive blinds. A 3-bet leverages our ace blocker, can win the pot preflop often, and sets up a manageable SPR if called. Versus a shove from Claude, the pot odds will generally justify a call given his likely reshove range at this stack depth.

I’m in the small blind with 4♦J♥ — a weak offsuit jack. Claude opened UTG to 250 (a relatively strong range) and the button (FiveTwo) 3‑bet to 750. I’ll be out of position postflop versus the button (and likely the BB), and J4o is heavily dominated by a typical 3‑bet range. Pot is 1,150 and calling 700 gives only ~38% equity required, with poor playability and little implied odds. A 4‑bet/iso would have to represent a very strong hand or be a substantial bluff — neither is attractive with this holding and no reads. Preserve stack for better spots and fold.

Hand 3d4c is a complete trash hand with very low playability, no high card value, disconnected, offsuit. Facing UTG raise from Claude (tight/aggressive based on history) and button 3bet from FiveTwo (loose/aggressive raiser). Pot odds are ~1.8:1 but multiway potential low with SB fold, yet postflop position terrible from BB, no fold equity if I shove given my deep stack vs their shallower ones (Claude 2500 total, FiveTwo 4125). Equity crushed vs their ranges. Standard fold to avoid spew.

JJ facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo. With only 2500 chips (25BB), calling leaves me in an awkward spot post-flop. At this stack depth, 4-bet shoving is standard with JJ - I'm likely ahead of their 3-betting range and don't want to play a guessing game post-flop with a committed stack. Shoving for value.

Claude’s 4-bet to 2500 effectively commits him (only 250 behind), creating a big pot where folding now would burn our 750 3-bet and likely overfold versus a short-stack who’s been opening frequently. With AdTh we have an ace blocker, position, and enough equity vs a plausible reshove/4-bet range that can include some worse Ax and occasional bluffs—not just premiums. Calling keeps our cost lower than jamming and also preserves the option to realize equity (e.g., check back some flops) while being trivially priced in if he puts the last 250 in postflop. Losing doesn’t bust us and we still retain a playable stack, so taking this spot is reasonable chip-EV wise.
Flopped a monster - full house JJJ66. I have the nuts here (only J6 beats me which is impossible given preflop action). With only 250 chips left and a 5150 pot, I'm shoving for value.

Claude’s flop bet is effectively his last chips after committing preflop. We’re getting an extremely favorable price (250 to win a 5400 pot), so folding would be a huge EV mistake. Even with just ace-high we have plenty of equity versus his range and can still improve on turn/river. Easy call.

I hold 3c 7h in the UTG position. This is an extremely weak hand (7-3 offsuit) with no playability or connectivity. Given my position (acting first) and the poor quality of the hand, the only standard and profitable play is to fold immediately to preserve my stack of roughly 30 big blinds.

I am holding 9c 5s in middle position. This is a very weak hand with no high-card value, no connectivity, and no flush potential. In a 6-max tournament setting, opening or calling with such junk from this position is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Pocket 5s is a clear open from the cutoff: we can win the blinds often, and when called we have good implied odds to flop a set in position. With ~21bb, limping is too passive and gives up fold equity; shoving is unnecessary and gets called by better too often. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open to apply pressure without committing my stack and keep my range credible versus the button and blinds.

Button with Kc3d — a marginal, easily dominated hand. FiveTwo opened to 250 from the cutoff; I have 150BB so the 250 call is cheap (~2.5 BB) and I get position. 3-betting K3o is suboptimal: not a good value hand and a weak bluff that will run into resistance or multiway action. I’ll call to see a flop and use position to either take the pot down or fold to strong aggression. If the SB/BB 3-bet large I’ll usually fold; postflop I’ll only continue with real equity (top pair, strong draw) or a good board to steal.

Preflop in the small blind with J6o, facing a cutoff raise to 2.5BB from FiveTwo (short stack ~19BB) and a button call from Minni. J6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated equity against typical raising/call ranges, and I'm out of position postflop. Pot odds to call 200 into 650 (~3:1) are insufficient given the multiway pot potential and reverse implied odds. Folding preserves my deep stack (282BB) for better spots; no need to speculate OOP with trash.

I have pocket 7s in the BB. FiveTwo raised to 250 and Minni called. Getting 650:150 pot odds (4.3:1) which is excellent for set-mining. 77 is a solid hand multiway. Just calling to see a flop and hopefully hit a set is the best play here - 3-betting would bloat the pot out of position with a hand that doesn't play great postflop unless I improve.

Missed my set with 77 on Ad Qs 6d. I have an underpair with two overcards on board and a diamond flush draw present. Out of position in a 3-way pot, checking is standard - no value in betting and I can't profitably call large bets with this holding.

Multiway flop (BB checked, BTN still to act behind me) on A♢ Q♠ 6♢ is very favorable for the callers’ ranges: plenty of Ax, Qx, and diamond floats. With 5♥5♣ I’m just an underpair with no relevant backdoors, and at ~18bb with pot 800 (SPR ~2.3) a c-bet risks committing chips in a spot where I’ll get called too often and then be forced to give up. Checking keeps the pot small, realizes my thin showdown value, and lets me fold comfortably if BTN/BB apply pressure.

Check. On A♦ Q♠ 6♦ with K♣3♦ I have no pair and only a very remote runner-runner diamond possibility. FiveTwo opened from the cutoff and Claude—a defending BB—checked, so both can easily hold A/Q, broadways, or middle pairs that dominate or have decent showdown equity. With position and a dry, Ace-high board, I don’t want to invest chips with air; a c‑bet here will often be called by better hands and face resistance from the short-stacked opener. I’ll keep the pot under control, see the turn, and only continue with real improvement (pair, two pair, strong draw) or a favorable card to credibly apply pressure. If either villain leads out for a meaningful size I’ll fold; I may call a very small probe to see one more card depending on sizing.

Turn brought Th, adding another overcard to my 77. Now facing Ad Qs 6d Th board with three overcards. It's checked to me and I can see a free river. My underpair has minimal showdown value - betting accomplishes nothing positive. Check and hope to get to showdown cheaply or hit an unlikely set on the river.

Both opponents checked the flop and the BB has now checked the turn on A♦ Q♠ 6♦ T♥. That double-check line caps them a bit and contains a lot of underpairs, floats, and missed broadways/diamond hands that we can fold out. With 18–19bb, winning this 800 chip pot uncontested is a big boost, but we also can’t afford to bloat the pot and get forced into stack-off spots with 55. A small stab applies pressure and denies equity while keeping my risk low; if I face a check-raise, I’m done.

I have K♣3♦ on A♦ Q♠ 6♦ T♥ — no pair and no live draw (flush is impossible with one card to come). FiveTwo (short stack, cutoff opener) leads small: 300 into a 1,100 pot. Purely by raw showdown equity I only have ~12–13% to pair on the river while the call needs ~21% to break even, so this is a slightly negative equity call alone. However the bet is small relative to my 14,800 stack and I have position — calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity on the river, and lets me see the river cheaply to potentially fold to real strength or take the pot away if he checks/weakens. Raising is a pure bluff here and risks being isolated or shoved by a short stack; folding surrenders position and potential to win a marginal pot. Plan: call the 300, evaluate the river — fold to significant aggression if I miss, or proceed if I pair up or opponent gives a sniff.

Facing a 300 bet from FiveTwo and call from Minni with 77 on Ad Qs 6d Th board. I have an underpair with three overcards on board. My equity is minimal - I'm essentially drawing to 2 outs for a set (roughly 4%). Getting 1400:300 (~4.7:1) but need way better odds to chase a set. Both opponents have shown interest in this pot, likely holding at least one pair or a draw. Folding is correct - continuing with an underpair OOP against two players is burning chips.

River K♥ is a very bad card for our 55 after BTN called the turn stab. The board is now A♦ Q♠ 6♦ T♥ K♥, which means any hand containing a J makes broadway, and Kx also improves to at least a pair—both are very plausible in BTN’s turn-calling range (AJ/QJ/JT/J9, KJ, etc.). Our hand has essentially no value when facing a bet, and a river shove would need an unrealistically high fold frequency given how much of their range improved. Best is to check, realize the small chance they checked turn with a busted diamond draw / air and now check back, and otherwise check-fold to any bet.

I called the turn with K♣3♦ to keep the pot manageable and use position. The river K♥ pairs my king and gives me second pair, but the board has an Ace and Queen — many of FiveTwo’s preflop opening hands (A‑x, A‑Q, sets/two‑pair) still beat me. FiveTwo led the turn with a small 300 into the ~1,100 pot (then checked the river), which reads as pot‑control or a thin value/probe from a short stack (1,575). Betting here would be thin value at best and can get called by better hands (A‑x, two‑pair, sets) while folding out only the weakest holdings; raising would be a pure bluff with poor blockers. With position and a showdown‑worthy but vulnerable hand, the +EV play is to check and see the showdown rather than bloat the pot or bluff.

AJo is a strong starting hand in a 6-max game, and opening from UTG is standard to build the pot and exert pressure. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind). This size is enough to thin the field while remaining committed to playing against the short stacks if they choose to shove.

We’re ~15.8bb effective with T8s facing a UTG 3x open. Calling is awkward: we’d be putting in ~20% of our stack with a hand that’s often dominated, and we invite squeezes from CO/BTN and the blinds with very little room to maneuver postflop (low SPR). Shoving doesn’t look great either versus an UTG range that should call fairly tight; when called we’re in bad shape and we likely don’t generate enough fold equity. Best stack-preserving play is to pass and look for a better reshove/raise spot.

6♦2♥ is a junk hand with almost no showdown or play equity. UTG opened to 300 and there are still the Button (big stack) and both blinds to act — high chance of 3-bets or a multiway pot where my hand is crushed. Calling 300 into a 450 pot gives ~40% pot odds but my actual equity vs an UTG opening range is far lower. 3-betting would be reckless and out of position. Preserve my 15,900 stack and wait for a much better spot — fold.

5h9c is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raise from Flash, who has shown aggression but an early position open indicates strength (pairs, broadway, suited connectors/AKs). Position on button is nice, but calling 300 into a 450 pot with trash is -EV long-term in a tournament setting where we want to maximize stack over 150 hands by avoiding marginal spots. SB and BB still to act but unlikely to squeeze wide. Folding preserves our deep stack for better opportunities.

K4o in the SB facing UTG raise. This is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. K4o is easily dominated by hands in Flash's UTG range (AK, KQ, KJ, KT). Not worth calling 250 to play OOP with this marginal holding. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c 2c facing a raise to 300 from the UTG player (Flash). The pot is 450 and I need to call 200, giving me pot odds of approximately 30.7%. However, 32s is one of the weakest hands in poker, prone to domination (higher flushes) and difficult to play out of position against a strong UTG range. Given my stack size of 28.5 BB and the positional disadvantage, the potential reverse implied odds and low equity realization make this a clear fold.

Td2o is far too weak to enter the pot UTG 6-handed, especially with ~16bb where every 100-chip mistake matters. Limping invites isolation and puts us in tough postflop spots with dominated equity; raising would be spew and risks getting jammed on by better hands. Preserve stack and wait for better opening/shove opportunities.

In middle position with A♣7♦ — a clear open in 6-max. UTG has folded and I have position on the BTN and blinds if they call; A7o is worth opening to seize the initiative. There are aggressive players behind (Elon on the cutoff with a very large stack, SB/BB capable of calls/3-bets), so I want a size that reduces multiway pots but is standard: a ~3x open. I'll raise to 300 to take the lead. If I face a large 3-bet from Elon or heavy pressure post‑flop I will fold A7o; versus a small isolation or a shove from the short SB I'll re-evaluate/call depending on price and pot odds.

97o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a middle position raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in prior hands. In cutoff position, even with good post-flop position, calling or raising risks too much for this speculative hand with multiple players behind, including the button and blinds. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a tournament setting where survival and stack accumulation over 150 hands matter more than marginal plays.

J6o is a weak offsuit hand facing a middle position raise. While I have button position, this hand lacks the playability and equity to profitably call or 3-bet here. Minni has been active but J6o doesn't play well post-flop even with position. Standard fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs5s facing a 3x raise (300) from Minni in Middle Position. My stack is 28 BBs. While Q5s has some playability due to being suited, it is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive chip leader. The pot odds are around 35%, which is close to my raw equity, but my equity realization will be poor playing OOP. Calling also risks a squeeze from the Big Blind. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni raised to 3x the big blind from middle position. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), this hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. In a high roller tournament context, defending with such a wide and weak range against a standard raise is not profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Fold. 9♣6♦ from UTG in a 6‑hand game is a very weak, easily‑dominated hand with poor postflop playability. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives ~40% pot odds but this hand has far less equity vs typical opening ranges; raising would be out of position and unnecessary risk. Several players at the table are capable of large bets/3‑bets (and one short stack could shove), so preserving a deep 160+ BB stack and waiting for a stronger, positionally appropriate spot is the correct long‑term play.

Preflop in middle position with T7o, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity. Standard play is to fold to avoid entering the pot with marginal holdings, especially with multiple players behind including short stacks who might shove. Pot odds to limp are poor given position and hand strength. Preserve stack for better spots.

Pocket tens in the cutoff with two folds already. This is a premium hand that I should raise for value. Standard open to 2.5x BB (250) to build the pot while not committing too much with a medium pair.

I am holding Jd 4s on the Button, facing a raise to 250 from Claude in the Cutoff. J4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity. With a stack of 28 BB, I cannot profitably call or raise with this holding against an open raise. The standard and correct play is to fold.

In the Small Blind with K-Qo, I am facing a standard 2.5x open from Claude in the Cutoff. K-Qo is well ahead of a typical Cutoff opening range. While calling is an option, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and discourages the short-stacked Big Blind (FiveTwo) from squeezing or realizing their equity. A raise to 850 (roughly 3.4x the open) is a standard size to put pressure on the opener and define the hand. If the Big Blind shoves for their 1475 chips, I will have a profitable call given the pot odds.

We’re very short (14.75 BB) and facing an open + 3-bet with 98o out of position. Calling 750 would leave ~725 behind, creating an extremely low SPR where we’ll be effectively committed with a hand that has poor equity and is often dominated versus Flash’s 3-bet range and Claude’s continuing range. A shove has limited fold equity and is likely called by better hands. Best play is to preserve stack for a cleaner spot.

Facing a 3-bet with pocket tens from the SB. TT is too strong to fold here but 4-betting risks getting called only by better hands (QQ+, AK) while folding out hands I'm crushing. Effective stacks are around 50BB so calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me play postflop with position. I can evaluate on the flop - if it comes low, I'm ahead of much of Flash's 3-bet range.

On this very dry, low-connected board (5-8-3 rainbow), my 3-bet range from the small blind retains a significant range advantage over Claude's calling range. Most of his range consists of medium pocket pairs (66-TT) or big suited cards that missed this flop. By continuation betting approximately 1/3 of the pot, I can put pressure on his non-pair hands and sets up a barrel on any overcard turn (Jack, Queen, King, or Ace) which would be excellent for my actual holding. Given the stack depths, a bet of 600 allows me to define the hand without committing too much of my stack if he shows strength.

Excellent flop for my pocket tens - 5-8-3 rainbow is very dry and my overpair is ahead of most of Flash's 3-bet range (AK, AQ, KQ all missed, and I beat JJ-99). Flash's small 1/3 pot c-bet (600 into 1800) looks like either a probe with overcards or trying to extract thin value. I should raise for value here - calling lets AK/AQ get free cards, and I want to build the pot with a strong hand. Raising to ~1500 keeps pressure on and sets up for potential all-in on later streets.

Claude has raised my continuation bet on a very dry board (8-5-3 rainbow) after calling a 3-bet pre-flop. My King-Queen high has no direct connection to this board, and although the pot odds to call the raise are somewhat enticing (900 to win 3900), the strategic situation is unfavorable. Claude's range for raising here is heavily weighted toward sets (88, 55, 33) or medium overpairs (99, TT, JJ) that he chose to play cautiously pre-flop. Given that he is effectively committing himself to the pot with this raise, a bluff is less likely, and even if he is bluffing with a hand like 6-7s, I am only marginally ahead and will be out of position on the turn facing a likely shove. Preserving my remaining 4,000 chips is more important than chasing a 6-outer with King-high in a bloated pot.

UTG with 5d7h, an offsuit low connector/gapper with poor playability and low equity. Standard fold from early position to avoid tough postflop spots with a weak range. Preserve deep stack (281BB effective) for better spots in tournament. No stealing incentive preflop UTG against 6 players.
9s5h is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity. In middle position with multiple players behind, this is a clear fold. No need to invest chips with such a marginal holding.

I am in the Cutoff with K7o and 28 big blinds. Action has folded to me. While the Cutoff is a standard steal position, K7o is at the very bottom of the opening range. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has a reshove stack size (14bb) and the Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader with nearly 160bb, making them likely to defend or apply pressure. Opening here risks bleeding chips or facing a shove I cannot call. It is better to preserve my stack for a stronger hand or better position.

I am on the button with KTo. Everyone has folded to me. KTo is a strong opening hand in this position. The Small Blind, FiveTwo, is quite short-stacked (about 14 BBs), so I must be prepared for a potential shove. However, opening for 3x (300 chips) is a standard play to steal the blinds or play in position against Minni, the big stack. If FiveTwo shoves, I will evaluate based on pot odds, but I am generally looking to play this hand aggressively from the button.

We’re ~14BB in the small blind facing a button open. K4o plays very poorly out of position, and flat-calling invites the big blind to come along (or squeeze) while leaving us in low-SPR, awkward postflop spots. A shove needs an unrealistically high fold rate given the price Flash is getting and typical button opening/calling dynamics, and a small 3-bet commits too much while still being easy to jam over. Best EV and tournament-preserving line is to fold.

Facing a 300 button open with 5♣7♦ in the big blind (pot 450, to call 200). 5♣7♦ is marginal but has enough connectivity to realize decent equity versus a wide BTN open, and the pot odds (~31%) justify a defend. I don't want to 3‑bet this hand: I'm out of position, the holding lacks useful blockers and would be a poor OOP bluff. Folding surrenders too much equity for a small loss (100 already posted). I will call to keep the pot manageable and see a flop. Postflop plan: play straightforward — continue with pairs/strong draws, fold to big sustained aggression on dry boards, and control the pot size unless I hit a favorable flop or can exploit clear aggression.

I defended preflop with 5♣7♦ but missed the flop — I have no pair and only a two-card backdoor club flush (needs both turn and river), so my equity is very low. The board (8♣ T♣ 2♣) heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range and I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bluff-c-bet into a wide calling range or inflate the pot with a weak hand. I’ll check to control the pot, see a free turn, and only continue if I pair or pick up a meaningful draw; otherwise fold to significant aggression.

On a monotone flop of 8c-Tc-2c, I have top pair with a King kicker. While I do not hold a club, my hand is currently strong but very vulnerable. Minni has checked to me. I need to bet for value against middle pairs, lower tens, or single-club draws, and to deny equity to random cards that could improve on the turn. A standard continuation bet is appropriate here, though I must be cautious if I face significant resistance or if a fourth club appears. I will bet approximately half the pot.

Flash c‑bets small (325 into a 975 pot). I hold 5♣7♦ on 8♣ T♣ 2♣ — this is a four‑to‑the‑club flush (9 club outs, ~35% to make the flush by the river). Adding pair outs (remaining 5s/7s, with 7♣ overlapping) gives ~14 unique outs (~50% to hit at least a pair/flush by river), though some pair hits can still lose. I only need ~25% equity to call (325 into ~1300 effective pot), so the draw justifies seeing a turn. I’m out of position and lack useful blockers to the BTN’s club combos, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a semi‑bluff raise that’s often dominated. Plan: call to realize equity, then re‑evaluate on the turn — proceed aggressively if I make the flush or a strong pair; fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss.

I check. I’m out of position with 5♣7♦ on 8♣ T♣ 2♣ 3♦ — I still have a four‑to‑the‑club draw (9 club outs) and a few pair outs (the three remaining fives + two non‑club sevens), for 14 unique river outs total. Pure flush equity to the river is 9/46 ≈ 19.6%; flush-or-pair unique outs are 14/46 ≈ 30.4% (many pair hits can still be second‑best). Flash’s line (BTN open, small flop c‑bet) is consistent with a wide range that includes clubs and top pairs, so leading out OOP with just a draw would be thin and would often put me in a tough spot. With no bet to face on the turn I’ll control the pot and realize equity — check and see a free river. If a club comes I’ll look to extract value; if I miss and Flash checks I’ll show down or do a small bluff selectively; if he barrels the river I’ll call reasonably sized bets that price my ~30% improvement chance (roughly up to ~1k) but fold to large overbets/ships that represent a made hand.

On a monotone board of 8c-Tc-2c, I have top pair with a King kicker. The turn 3d is a total blank. While my hand is currently strong, the presence of three clubs makes it very vulnerable. Minni has checked to me for a second time. If I bet here, I am primarily betting for protection against a single club or value against a weaker Ten or an Eight. However, if Minni has already flopped a flush or has a high club draw (like the Ac), betting might put me in a difficult position if she raises. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, ensure I see a river card, and can potentially induce a bluff on the river or reach a cheaper showdown. Given the stack sizes and the texture of the board, checking is a prudent 'pot control' play with a marginal made hand.

I have 5♣7♦ on 8♣ T♣ 2♣ 3♦ 2♥ — I missed the flush and have no pair, so I have essentially zero showdown value. Flash’s line (BTN open, small flop c‑bet 325, then check‑check) is consistent with medium pairs (T‑x, 8‑x), some clubs, and a lot of marginal hands that will call a reasonable river bluff. The pot is 1300; to make a river bluff profitable I’d need a substantial fold frequency (roughly a third or more for a half‑pot/size bet), and I don’t expect Flash to fold that often here given his line and stack. With no blockers to deuces and no showdown equity, the least‑risk, most +EV choice is to check and show down rather than invest chips into a thin bluff. If Flash bets into me after I check I’ll fold to meaningful sizing (and only consider calling very small bets that give correct pot odds).

I have top pair with a King kicker on a monotone board. After checking the turn for pot control, the river pairs the board with a deuce. Minni has checked for a third time, indicating weakness or a trap. Since my hand has significant showdown value but might not get called by many worse hands if I bet, checking back is the safest play to realize my equity and win the pot against missed draws or weaker pairs.
K2o is a very weak hand to open from UTG. Easy fold.

I am in Middle Position with Pocket Eights (8s 8c) and a stack of 28 BB (2800). The UTG player folded. This is a strong starting hand that merits an opening raise to thin the field and build a pot. Limping invites overcards and difficult post-flop spots. A standard open raise to 2.5x (250 chips) balances value and protection while leaving room to maneuver if faced with aggression from the larger stacks in the blinds or a shove from the short stack on the button.

I am in the Cutoff with 2c 3c, a very weak suited connector. Pro opened for 2.5x from Middle Position. With a short stack like FiveTwo (14 BB) behind me on the button who might shove over a call, and the overall weakness of my holding against Pro's opening range, this is a clear fold. I want to preserve my stack for better spots.

We’re 14bb effective and facing a 2.5bb open. 76o is too weak to defend here: calling puts ~18% of our stack in and creates awkward SPR postflop, plus we still have two big stacks in the blinds who can squeeze and force us to fold. A shove doesn’t perform well either—Pro’s calling range (pairs, broadways, suited aces) has us in rough shape and we don’t realize enough fold equity with this offsuit connector. Preserve the stack for better jam/call spots.

Holding 7♥4♥ in the small blind facing a MP open to 250 is a weak spot. The hand has poor playability out of position against the raiser and the big blind (Elon) who still acts and is very deep/aggressive. Calling 200 to see a flop will often put me in a multiway, out-of-position pot with marginal equity and bad reverse implied odds; 3-betting is also unjustified since this hand blocks nothing and has little showdown value. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

9c2h is an extremely weak hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor connectivity. Facing an early/mid position raise from Pro after folds, this is a standard fold from the big blind. Defending this wide OOP would be -EV, especially with deep stacks. Preserve chips for premium hands.

I am in UTG position with K6o (King of Hearts, Six of Spades). My stack is 29.5 BBs. In a 6-handed game, K6o is significantly below the opening threshold for UTG. The hand is easily dominated, plays poorly post-flop due to being offsuit and disconnected, and I have 5 players acting behind me, including a short stack in the CO who might shove and deep stacks in the blinds/BTN who can apply pressure. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.

I have pocket sevens in middle position, which is a standard opening hand in a 6-max game. I will raise to 300 (3x BB) to establish initiative. I am aware of FiveTwo's short stack behind me, but 77 is generally a strong enough hand to continue with if they decide to shove their remaining 14 BBs.

We’re only ~14 BB deep (1425) facing a 3 BB open from Flash in middle position with two big stacks still to act behind (BTN/SB) plus a playable BB stack. 9c7c is fine as a call when deeper, but at this stack depth calling 300 creates an awkward low SPR spot where we’ll often have to commit on marginal pairs/draws, and it also invites a squeeze that we can’t comfortably continue against. A 3-bet jam is also too loose here given the opener’s likely MP range and the increased chance of getting called by players behind who cover us. Best stack-preserving/EV line is to fold and wait for a better shove/3-bet spot.

7♠3♣ is one of the worst hands — no real playability or equity. Flash opened to 300 (MP) and both blinds (Elon SB and Claude BB) still have to act; calling often makes this multiway where 7-3 collapses, and a light 3‑bet/bluff to 500 is too thin against the stacks/players behind who can defend or 3‑bet. With a healthy stack and plenty of hands left, preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

AJ diamond suited is a premium hand from the small blind facing a middle position open raise to 3BB. My stack is massively deep at 28k compared to Flash's 5k and others shorter, giving me huge leverage. AJs has excellent playability postflop, strong equity vs calling range, and fold equity on Flash who may fold weaker hands. 3-betting isolates, builds the pot OOP while denying equity to BB, and applies pressure given stack disparities. Sizing to 900 (3x the open) is standard and balanced. Folding or calling is too passive with this strength.

AQo is a strong hand facing Elon's 3-bet to 900. Elon has been very aggressive throughout the tournament with a large stack (27k). Folding AQo here is too weak. Calling leaves me OOP postflop. A 4-bet to ~2400 puts pressure on Elon and represents strength. With 6800 stack, this is a reasonable spot to 4-bet for value/protection against his wide 3-betting range.

I opened to 300 with 77, but the action behind me has become extremely heavy. Elon 3-bet to 900 from the small blind, and Claude 4-bet to 2400 from the big blind. Claude is committing more than half of their stack, signaling extreme strength (likely JJ+, AK). My pocket sevens are likely crushed by at least one of their ranges, and I don't have the implied odds to set-mine given the high price to call (2100 more). Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my remaining 46.75 BBs.

AJ♦ J♦ is a premium suited connector with excellent playability and blockers to key hands like AA, AK♦, AJ. Facing Claude's 4-bet squeeze to 2400 (24BB) from BB after Flash's open and my 3-bet, his range is polarized: value like QQ+, AK, and bluffs like AQs+, KQs, suited broadways, lower pairs. My equity is strong ~37% vs tight value calling range (QQ+/AK), crushes bluffs (~70% equity), and has fold equity vs marginal hands he can't call a 5-bet jam off with his remaining ~2100 (21BB effective). Shoving all-in applies max pressure, leverages my 270BB depth (risking <10% of stack), builds huge pot when called and ahead, and isolates heads-up. Calling 1500 OOP into 5100 pot is too passive with flush/straight potential, vulnerable to his aggression postflop. Folding is -EV for top-5% hand. Tournament stage mid (hand 63/150), 6 players, stacks shallow for others but mine deep—optimal jam per GTO.
