With KTo we have no pair, no flush draw, and only very thin runner-runner straight/heart possibilities, so our equity is marginal and—more importantly—realizes poorly out of position with a ~3.5 SPR (we’ll face lots of turn pressure and can’t profitably continue). Even though the pot odds are ~29%, this is a clear -EV float versus this sizing and texture. Best play is to conserve the 32bb stack and fold.

Qd5s is a very weak offsuit hand from UTG in a 6-handed game. It has poor playability post-flop, low equity against calling ranges, and is easily dominated by better queens. Standard tight strategy dictates folding to avoid difficult spots and preserve our deep stack (18725) for better opportunities in later position or with premium hands. No need to limp or raise as it doesn't play well multiway.
J2o is a weak hand in middle position with 4 players behind. Not worth opening - easy fold.

Current stack is 21 BBs (2100 chips), which is the shortest at the table. Hand T9o in the Cutoff is a marginal holding. While opening from late position is standard with a wider range, T9o is at the very bottom of that range and often a fold, especially with 20-25BBs where implied odds are lower and reverse implied odds exist. There is a risk of being 3-bet by the button (Flash with 24BB) or the blinds, forcing a fold and wasting chips. Preserving the stack for a better spot (high cards, pairs, suited connectors) to shove or raise-fold is the better strategy here.

I am on the button with 83o, which is one of the weakest hands in the game. While everyone has folded to me and this is a standard spot to attempt a steal, 83o is far too weak to justify a raise, especially against a Big Blind (Minni) who has shown significant aggression and has a massive stack that can easily absorb a 3-bet or a defend. With 24 Big Blinds, I should look for better spots to commit chips rather than wasting them on a low-equity bluff.

Folds around to the blinds and we’re heads-up versus a big stack in the BB. With 7♢4♠ we’re too weak to build a pot out of position, but getting a great price to complete lets us realize some equity (straight/2-pair potential) and keep SPR high. If BB attacks with a bigger iso-raise we can comfortably fold and preserve our 31.5bb stack.

I’m in the big blind with K5o facing a single limp from the small blind. K5 offsuit is too marginal to open-raise for thin value (a 200 total raise only buys a tiny amount of fold equity into a caller). With deep stacks I prefer seeing a flop heads-up and playing postflop where I can fold to heavy action or extract value if I hit. Plan: check, evaluate the flop — call small c-bets with decent equity or top pair, fold to large aggression on dry boards, look to pot-control and avoid bloating the pot with a weak kicker.

Limped SB vs BB pot means ranges are extremely wide. On Q-5-4 rainbow we’ve flopped bottom pair, which is too weak to check-call multiple streets comfortably, but strong enough to deny equity versus random overcards (A-high/K-high) and to pick up the pot immediately a good chunk of the time.

Leading small here also prevents BB from realizing equity for free and keeps the pot manageable—if we get raised, we can just fold without regret. I’ll use a simple, low-commitment sizing.

I have K5o and the flop 4c‑5d‑Qh gives me middle pair (5s) with a strong king kicker. Villain (FiveTwo) was a single limp then made a small flop bet (100 into ~300) — a very wide c‑bet range including many bluffs, small pairs, and some Qx. Calling 100 is the best play: it’s cheap (good pot odds ~4:1), keeps the pot controlled, and allows me to extract value from worse hands or fold to real aggression on the turn. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand that can be dominated by Qx/overpairs, and folding is too tight given our showdown value. Call 100 and reevaluate on the turn.

Turn brings the 6c giving us a significantly better holding: bottom pair + an open-ended straight draw (3 or 8), and we’re still in a blind-vs-blind spot where ranges are extremely wide. I prefer continuing with a small-to-medium barrel to (1) keep initiative, (2) deny equity to random overcards/A-high that peeled flop, and (3) fold out some weak 4x/5x/air that would otherwise take a free card. Sizing stays controlled versus the big stack to avoid inflating the pot; if we get raised, we can comfortably bet/fold and preserve our stack since raises here skew toward strong Qx/two-pair/straights.

Call 200. I have K♥5♣ — a middle pair (pair of 5s) with a strong king kicker. Villain limp-called preflop and has taken a small-aggression line (100 on the flop, 200 on the turn into a ~600 pot), which smells like a wide range (weak Qx, small pairs, floats and bluffs). With one card to come I have virtually no meaningful draw (with one club in my hand and two on board I cannot make a flush by the river; likewise a 5/4/6 run can’t become a five-card straight with a single card), so this is primarily showdown value. Calling 200 is cheap (you only need roughly ~20% equity to break even) and keeps the pot controlled; folding would be too nitty and raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand that’s often dominated by Qx/6x. Plan: call, see the river, then fold to clear strength (a large shove or a value-heavy river line) or extract value if villain barrels with worse.

River improves us to two pair (7s and 4s), which is a clear value hand in a limp/blind-vs-blind spot where BB can arrive here with plenty of Qx and one-pair hands (7x/6x/5x/4x) after calling two small barrels. The main concern is that any 3x or 8x has now made a straight, so I don’t want to size big and get put in tough spots; I’d rather bet small to get crying calls from one-pair hands and comfortably bet/fold versus a raise (which will be heavily weighted to straights/two-pair+).

I call 300. I have K5o — one pair (5s) with a very strong king kicker — reasonable showdown value. Villain limped and has used very small, consistent sizing (≈100 flop, 200 turn, 300 river), which is indicative of a wide, thin range (weak Qx, small pairs, floats and some bluffs) rather than a polarized monster. The river board (4‑5‑Q‑6‑7) is somewhat connected so straights (3x or 8x) and sets are possible, and those hands beat me, but the bet is small. Calling 300 into a 1100 pot requires only ~21% equity to break even, and my hand will beat a large portion of a limp‑caller’s range while keeping my deep stack intact. Raising would bloat the pot and get value from many hands that currently dominate me; folding would be too nitpicky given the pot odds and line. Call to see showdown.

8s2s is a very weak hand from UTG position. Even being suited, this hand has poor connectivity and terrible equity against any calling/raising range. From early position, I need much stronger hands to open. Easy fold.

I am in Middle Position with a stack of 21 BBs (2,100 chips). The hand J5s (Jack of Hearts, Five of Hearts) is significantly below the opening range for this position, especially with active players behind and big stacks in the blinds. My stack is getting shallow, so I need to preserve chips for higher equity spots or shove opportunities. Opening here is loose and risky. Therefore, folding is the only standard and profitable play.

I am in the Cutoff position with Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o). This is a standard opening hand in a 6-max game, especially with two players already folded. My stack is relatively short at 2400 chips (24 big blinds), so I want to play aggressively to either take down the blinds and antes or build a pot where I have positional advantage. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard open here.

44 plays poorly as a flat here: effective stack vs Flash is only ~21bb so set-mining isn’t priced in (calling 300 to win at most ~2100), and the SB/BB can squeeze with deep stacks. Flash has shown willingness to open/fold to 3-bets, so the best EV comes from using position and fold equity with a 3-bet. Plan: 3-bet/fold versus a shove or any significant 4-bet action.

6♣4♠ is a very weak, unsuited hand — easily dominated and with poor playability. I’m out of position to both the Button and Big Blind post‑flop, facing a 3‑bet to 900 with the pot 1,350. Calling 850 requires ~38.6% equity (850 / (1350+850)), which 6‑4o does not have vs a 3‑betting range. A raise/bluff here is thin against a short but capable 3‑bettor and risks getting isolated or facing a shove; there’s no constructive implied odds. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better position and stronger holdings.

A♦T♠ is a strong ace offsuit hand from the BB facing a button 3-bet after a cutoff open-raise. Pot odds are excellent: calling an additional 800 into a 1350 pot (~37% of the final pot post-call). FiveTwo's button 3-bet range over a CO raise is wide (pairs, broadway, weaker aces, bluffs), against which ATo has good equity (~40%+ heads-up) and blockers to AA/AK. Playability postflop is solid with ace-high and straight potential. Flash (short stack, 21BB) may fold or shove over, but if folding, we get a good price heads-up OOP vs button; if multiway, dead money helps. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with a non-nutted ace; folding wastes equity and dead money. Calling maximizes EV long-term in tournament by realizing equity cheaply.

I opened for 300 with A9o from the Cutoff, but faced a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo on the Button and a cold-call from Elon in the Big Blind. A9o is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by the ranges of a 3-bettor and a cold-caller. My stack is relatively short (24 BB total), and calling another 600 chips represents nearly 30% of my remaining stack. Given that I would be out of position to the original 3-bettor post-flop and the high probability that I am behind, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my remaining 2100 chips for a better spot.

Flop is Ac9cKc, a highly coordinated monotone board favoring villain's button 3-bet range (KcAx, QJc flush/straight draws, sets, etc.). AdTs is ace-high with no pair, no flush draw, and only a weak gutshot (8 for broadway), making it a marginal bluff-catcher OOP. Villain likely c-bets frequently for value/protection; calling risks stacking off to better aces/Kc while folding realizes fold equity preservation in a tournament spot with 178bb effective depth. Checking back would invite free cards to villain's equity but check-fold maximizes EV by avoiding difficult rivers OOP vs polarized range.

Elon has checked to us in a 3-bet pot with a very low SPR (~1.4), on a monotone A♣ K♣ 9♣ board. While this board is generally good for the 3-bettor’s perceived range, our specific hand (4♥4♣) has no club and very poor ability to continue versus a check-raise or even a single call (Elon continues with any A/K, many 9x, and any hand containing clubs). Betting here risks effectively committing our stack with near-zero equity when called and allows Elon to leverage the low SPR with a raise.

Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity (we can spike a set, or sometimes win at showdown versus hands like QQ-JJ/TT with no club that chose to check), and preserves the option to apply pressure on many turn cards if Elon checks again (especially non-club, non-A/K turns).

Turn board pairs the 9h, creating Ac9cKc9h (two 9s, club flush draw active, K-high straights possible like QJ, runner-runner hearts). AdTs is still ace-high with Ad blocker to top pair Ac/Kc combos and AA/AK, but no pair, no strong draw (T is gutshot blocker at best). OOP vs Button 3-bettor who checked flop on scary monotone board (missed value/draws? slowplay monster like KK/sets?). Their check-behind flop often reps weaker 3-bet range (Ax non-premium, broadway missed, bluffs) but polarized post-turn. Pot 2150 (~21bb), villain eff ~30bb (3000). Betting risks raising by nuts (KcAc, 99, KK full potential) or calling by better Ax/draws, low fold equity vs 3-bet range. Checking controls pot, induces bluffs from missed draws (QJc spades, flush draws whiffed), free river showdown with ace-high blocker (plays well vs floats), denies equity. Check forces villain to bet thin/protect or check back weak. EV-max OOP spot post-check-flop.

Turn is a much better bluffing node than the monotone flop: the 9 pairing reduces the relative strength of one-club/flush holdings (full houses now exist), and our flop check-back keeps our range strong (we can plausibly have AA/KK/AK/A9/K9 that checked to control). Elon’s check again suggests a lot of capped hands (pocket pairs without a club, broadways without a club) that can fold to pressure.

With our low SPR, I don’t want to polarize to a shove (needs too much fold equity). Instead I’ll take a one-and-done stab that risks a manageable amount to win a big pot immediately. If Elon check-raises, we simply fold; if he calls, we’ll proceed cautiously and mostly give up unless we improve (river 4) or get a very favorable runout/check.

Board is Ac9cKc9h, a dry paired turn after scary flop check-check. Villain's button 3-bet pre, check flop (often weak/missed/monster), now donk bets 800 into 2950 pot (~27% pot) with 2200 stack (~22BB eff pre-bet, 1400 behind). Polarized range: value (AK/AQ clubs, K9/A9/99 trips/full potential, KK/sets slowplayed) or bluffs (missed QJc spade draw, weak Ax, floats). AdTs ace-high blocks nuts (AA/AK/Ad flush draws), but OOP with gutshot at best, equity <25% vs value-heavy range (dominated by better Ax/Kx), ~50%+ vs bluffs. Pot odds 3.7:1 require ~21% equity; achievable overall but river OOP facing ~pot shove commits us awkwardly to showdown value thin while denying equity realization. Raising all-in overbet crushingly OOP vs short stack low fold equity (value calls, bluffs snap). Calling invites disaster rivers (river club/K/Q pairs board worse). Folding maximizes EV preserving deep stack for superior spots, check-fold consistent post-flop plan vs aggro button 3-bettor. Long-term tournament play: avoid marginal bluff-catchers OOP multi-street.

I am in UTG position with a very weak hand (Ten-Deuce offsuit). My stack is short (21 BB), and there are five players yet to act. This hand has negligible playability and negative expected value if played from early position. The only correct strategic move is to fold immediately to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in middle position with 2s 4d, which is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. Given my relatively short stack of 2100 chips (21 big blinds) and the poor quality of my hand, there is no reason to enter the pot. Folding is the only viable strategic move here to conserve chips.

We’re first-in from the cutoff with J6 offsuit. This hand is dominated by the calling/defending ranges we’ll face from the button and especially the blinds, and it performs poorly postflop (hard to realize equity, reverse implied odds when we make a jack). With ~51bb effective for us, there’s no need to open marginal offsuit junk just to steal 150; we can pick better CO opens and avoid tough spots versus competent/aggressive blinds.

Button with Q♥9♥ — a playable, suited queen-high that wants to be raised in position. There are only the blinds left to act; both SB (Elon) and BB (Claude) defend reasonably often, but I still have strong positional fold equity and post‑flop playability. Standard exploitative open on the button: raise to 3x the big blind (300 total). Size is large enough to pick up the blinds a good portion of the time while not overcommitting vs a 3‑bet. Plan: if I face a substantial 3‑bet (big, polar size — e.g. >~700–800) I will fold Q9s; I will consider calling smaller squeezes to play postflop in position. If called heads‑up I’ll c‑bet on favorable textures (hearts, connected/low boards) and check/control on dry A/K high boards; if multi‑way I slow down and look to realise equity. Goal: steal the blinds and use position to play small pots with implied odds.

5d8c is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position against a button raise. Minni's button open is likely wide given history of aggression, but 58o has low equity and little potential postflop. SB defense shouldn't include such trash. Deep stack allows waiting for better spots without risking chips unnecessarily. Standard fold to preserve stack.

Holding ATs against a button min-raise. This is a solid hand to 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot. Minni has been aggressive with opens. ATs plays well as a 3-bet from BB against button steals - we have blockers to strong hands and good playability. 3-betting to around 950.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♥9♥ but Claude 3-bet to 950 — a sizing well above the ~700–800 threshold I set for folding this holding. With that large, polarized 3‑bet and Claude’s aggressive/value tendencies, Q9s has marginal equity and will face difficult post‑flop decisions even in position. Calling 650 for ~33% pot odds isn’t enough vs his likely range, and 4‑betting as a bluff is too risky here. Preserve the 16k stack for better spots and fold.

I am in the UTG position with Jack-Four offsuit. This is an extremely weak hand to open with, especially from the earliest position at a 6-max table. My stack is currently 2,100 chips (21 BB), which gives me enough room to wait for a stronger starting hand rather than risking chips on a low-equity play. Folding is the standard and most profitable move here.

K8o is too weak to open from middle position in 6-max, and limping invites isolation from the aggressive stacks behind (Minni/Elon/Claude) while we’re often dominated when called. With ~51bb, we don’t need to take thin, reverse-implied-odds spots—better to fold and preserve stack for stronger opens/steals.

Cutoff with 9h6h — a suited, speculative hand that plays well multiway but isn’t strong enough to isolate facing three players yet to act (Button, SB, BB) who are capable of sizable 3-bets. The cost to see a flop is only 100 into a 150 pot, so calling preserves stack, keeps pot manageable, and allows us to realise equity (flush/straight/backdoor opportunities). Plan: call and evaluate the flop — continue aggressively if we pick up a strong draw or pair; fold (or proceed very cautiously) to significant preflop 3-bets or heavy postflop pressure.

Preflop on the button with 5h9c offsuit, a weak gapped hand with poor playability postflop. Minni limped from cutoff, which suggests a wide range, but calling would lead to a multiway pot where this hand performs poorly. Raising risks isolation with a marginal hand against potential defends from blinds, including short-stacked Pro who defends wide. Folding conserves chips for better spots in position; 59o is below standard limper-raising range on button in a tournament setting with active opponents.

I have 8d5d in the SB. Minni limped from cutoff, and I only need 50 more to see a flop in a pot of 250. Getting 5:1 odds with a suited hand that has straight and flush potential makes completing an easy decision. Not strong enough to raise for value or as a bluff here.

I hold a premium pair (QQ) in the Big Blind. Facing a limp from the Cutoff and a completion from the Small Blind, my goal is to isolate the limper, build the pot for value, and deny equity to random holdings. With a stack of 2000 (20 BB), I have a decision between a non-all-in raise and a shove. Shoving is a viable option to maximize fold equity and realize raw equity, but raising to a standard isolation size allows me to extract value from hands that might fold to a shove (like weaker pairs or broadway cards) while denying profitable set-mining odds (since 4.5 BB is a large portion of my stack). I will raise to 450, creating a low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (~1.2) post-flop, which allows me to comfortably commit my stack on most boards.

I limped the cutoff with 9♥6♥ intending to see a cheap flop, but the BB (Pro) just raised to 450 — a sizeable preflop aggression. This is a speculative, out-of-position hand; calling another 350 into a 650 pot requires ~35% equity and often leaves me dominated or playing a bloated multiway pot with the SB still involved. My earlier plan was to fold to significant preflop 3-bets, and with a deep stack I can wait for a much better spot. Folding preserves my stack and avoids getting committed with a marginal hand OOP against aggressive ranges.

8d5d is a weak suited hand. Pro raised to 450 from BB after a limper - he only has 1650 total so this is a significant commitment (27% of his stack). I need 350 to call into 650 pot (1.85:1 odds). With Pro being short-stacked, implied odds are poor. 85s doesn't play well OOP against a raise, especially vs a short stack who could easily shove on any flop. Easy fold.

6-handed but still UTG (first to act) with Q4 offsuit: dominated by any Qx and many broadways, poor playability, and too weak to open or limp profitably given opponents can isolate and we’d be out of position multiway. With ~52bb we don’t need to take marginal spots—preserve stack for better opens.

I have J♥9♦ in middle position (6-handed). This is a marginal but openable hand — I want to take the initiative and avoid a multiway limp where J9o loses equity. The pot is 150 and to call is 100; a standard 3×BB open (300 total) is the right sizing here: it gives fold equity, isolates the blinds, and keeps the field smaller. If I face a large 3‑bet from Elon or Claude I’ll fold (this hand struggles vs 3‑bet ranges); I’ll continue post‑flop with top pair or strong draws and play cautiously when I miss. Raise to 300 to apply pressure and play the hand heads‑up or against a single caller.

Held 3d2h, absolute trash: low offsuit non-connected cards, zero showdown value, terrible playability postflop. Facing a standard open-raise to 300 (3BB) from Minni in middle position after UTG fold. Minni has been active/aggressive historically, but her range is still strong (top ~15-20%: pairs, suited connectors/broadways). In cutoff with deep stacks (~178BB effective), but no fold equity worth 3-betting as blockers are awful and playability postflop is nightmare (often dominated, floats rarely work). Calling gets horrible odds (pot 450, call 300 for ~3.3:1 implied but realization low with this hand). Standard fold to preserve stack for superior spots; 60+ hands left, ICM/stack preservation priority in tournament.

7d8c on the button facing a min-raise from Minni. This is a playable suited connector type hand (though offsuit) with good position. I have direct position on the raiser and can play postflop well. The 300 call is small relative to my stack of 16700. Folding is fine too but I'll take a flop in position with this connected hand that can make straights.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o, a very weak hand. Facing a raise from Minni and a call from Claude, I am getting pot odds of roughly 3:1, but my equity is poor and playability out of position multi-way is terrible. My stack of 22.5 BBs is too short to splash around with trash hands but large enough to wait for a better spot. Folding is the only reasonable option here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o) and a relatively short stack of 2000 chips (20 BB). Minni opened from middle position and Claude called on the button. While I am getting 3.75-to-1 pot odds (200 to call into 750), K2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. Given my stack size, I want to preserve my chips for a more profitable spots where I can commit my stack with better equity or fold equity. Defending with a hand that is easily dominated and hard to play post-flop would likely lead to further chip loss.

Heads‑up vs Claude (button caller of my 3x open). Flop Kc Jc 8s with Jh9d gives me top pair with a medium kicker — a hand worth extracting value from but vulnerable to Kx and club‑containing hands. Claude’s calling range includes many worse Jx, mid pairs and straight/club draws (T9, Q10, two clubs, pocket pairs) so I want to charge those hands and deny free cards. A half‑pot bet both gets value from worse and protects against draws while keeping the pot manageable if I face resistance. Plan: bet for value now (~50% pot). If Claude check‑raises large I’ll fold most of the time (he’s usually strong), if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (continue on blank turns, slow down vs clubs or a king).

I flopped bottom pair (8s) with a weak 7 kicker on a Jc Kc 8s board. This is a very unfavorable texture for my hand - high cards that hit Minni's preflop raising range well (KX, JX, broadway), plus a flush draw present. My pair is vulnerable and I have no club for backdoor equity. Minni c-betting 50% pot is standard and they likely have plenty of hands that beat me. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I have 8♣2♠ (8-2 offsuit) UTG in a 6-handed game — one of the worst starting hands. Out of position with multiple players to act (including several large stacks and aggressive raisers), this hand has very poor showdown equity and is frequently dominated. Limping or calling 100 invites multiway pots where 8-2o performs terribly; raising would be a clear negative EV play from UTG. I’ll preserve my healthy stack and fold, waiting for a much stronger spot.

J4o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability from middle position facing a preflop raise (to 200). Pot odds are 1.5:1, but equity against a typical raising range is insufficient, and postflop playability is poor without position or suited/connected value. Stacks are deep enough to wait for better spots in a tournament with 60 hands left. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities.

K9o in cutoff with two folds ahead. This is a standard open-raise spot with a playable hand in late position. I have a healthy stack and can apply pressure on the remaining players. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I am on the Button with QdJs and a stack of 2250 chips (22.5 BB). The action folded to Claude in the Cutoff who raised to 250 (2.5 BB). QJo is a marginal hand facing a Cutoff open; it is often dominated by the opener's calling range (AQ, KQ, AJ) if I were to 3-bet shove, and plays poorly as a flat call due to reverse implied odds and the risk of being squeezed by the blinds. With a stack of over 20 BB, I still have room to wait for better spots to enter the pot or re-steal. Preserving my stack is preferable to risking my tournament life with an easily dominated hand against a large stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 4s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Claude has raised to 2.5x the big blind from the Cutoff. My stack is currently around 19.5 big blinds, and calling here would leave me out of position with a marginal hand against an active opponent. Given the hand strength and the risk of being squeezed by the Big Blind (FiveTwo), the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

We’re getting a very good price (150 to win 400, needing ~27% equity). Even though T5o plays poorly postflop and we’ll be out of position, big blind defense versus a cutoff 2.5x open is still wide enough to include hands like this at some frequency. A 3-bet bluff is unattractive here (bad blocker value, dominated when called, and Claude is deep enough to put us in tough spots), so the best EV line is to realize equity via a straightforward defend and play fit-or-fold postflop on unfavorable textures.

We flop top pair (5s) with a weak kicker plus a small gutshot to the 3. This low, dynamic board hits BB’s range well, but our specific hand isn’t strong enough to start inflating the pot out of position via a donk or a check-raise that could get us played back at by overpairs/strong draws. Best is to check and let the preflop raiser c-bet his overcards/heart draws; we can comfortably check-call versus a normal sizing and re-evaluate on turns (bet ourselves if he checks back).

I have K9o on a 5d 4h 2h board - complete miss with just two overcards. FiveTwo checked from the BB. This low, connected board with a flush draw isn't great for my range, but I have the initiative. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot can take this down often since the board likely missed BB's calling range too. If raised, I'll fold.

Claude’s small c-bet gives us an excellent price (175 to win 900 total after we call, needing ~19–20% equity). On 5d-4h-2h we have top pair with a weak kicker, plus a gutshot to the 3 and some backdoor improvement potential. Raising would bloat the pot OOP and risks getting 3-bet/played hard by overpairs, better 5x, and strong draws, while folding is far too tight given the price and equity realization. Best line is to check-call and re-evaluate turn (continue on many bricks/small barrels; be cautious versus big sizing on overcards/hearts/board-pairing).

Turn pairs the 2 (5d-4h-2h-2c), which is a range-shift card that reduces the urgency to “protect” versus two overcards and often slows down the preflop raiser’s barreling frequency. With 5hTs we have a marginal made hand (top pair, weak kicker) that doesn’t want to build a big OOP pot versus overpairs/A5/strong heart draws, and we gain EV by letting Claude continue with bluffs and thin value rather than leading into his entire range. Checking also keeps our range protected (we can have 2x, slowplayed sets, and some strong draws), while preserving the option to check-call reasonable bets and value-bet rivers if he checks back.

Turn brought a deuce, pairing the board. I have complete air with K9o - no pair, no draw. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet and checked again. While the paired board slightly favors my range, firing another barrel with nothing is risky since villain showed interest on the flop. Checking behind gives me a free river card and pot control. I can potentially bluff a scare card river or just give up.

River improves us to two pair (5s and 2s) with a decent kicker, and Claude’s turn check caps a lot of his range to overpairs (66–AA), one-pair 5x/4x, and missed overcards/draws that wanted a free card. The 3h is a scary card (flushes and A-high straights get there), so I don’t want to polarize or build a big OOP pot, but I also don’t want to check and let those overpairs/weak pairs check back too often. A small value/block bet targets exactly those bluff-catch hands, sets a price versus a potential stab, and lets me comfortably fold if he comes back with a raise that heavily represents flush/straight/quads.

River completed both a straight (A-5) and a heart flush. I have K-high with no pair, no draw hit. FiveTwo's small 350 bet into 900 is likely thin value from a 5, small pair, or better. I beat absolutely nothing except pure bluffs. With only 22% pot odds needed, this might look tempting, but K-high has zero showdown value here - any pair, straight, or flush beats me. Easy fold.

UTG position with 7h-2c offsuit, one of the worst possible starting hands in poker. No suitedness, connectivity, or high card strength. Deep stacked tournament (17k effective), but early position demands a tight range to avoid difficult postflop situations out of position. Pot is small (150 from blinds), to call 100 is not worth it with zero equity realization preflop. Folding preserves stack for better spots, aligning with maximizing EV over 150 hands by avoiding marginal plays.

Ad7d is a playable suited ace from middle position. With Elon folding UTG, I should open raise here. Standard 2.5x open to 250. Suited aces have good playability postflop with flush potential and top pair possibilities.

I hold 8h7c in the Cutoff facing a raise to 250 from Claude in Middle Position. My stack is 22.5 BBs. 87o is a weak offsuit connector that performs poorly against an MP opening range. Calling would cost over 10% of my stack with a hand that rarely flops well and is easily dominated. With players behind left to act and a relatively short stack, preserving chips for a better spot is the clear choice. I will fold.

I am on the button with 8d 6c, which is a very weak hand. Claude raised to 250 from middle position. Given my relatively short stack of 1,950 (19.5 BB), I should look for better spots to commit chips. 86 offsuit is well outside the range of hands I should call or 3-bet with against an open raise, even with the positional advantage. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Claude’s MP open range is strong enough that K7o is frequently dominated (Kx, broadways), and calling from the small blind is the worst-position line with poor realization and reverse implied odds. This hand isn’t a good 3-bet bluff candidate either (blocks too little, plays poorly when called), so best EV is to conserve chips and fold.

I’m in the big blind with A♠J♣ facing a standard MP open to 250 (to call 150). AJo is a strong hand here — ahead of much of Claude’s likely opening range and with good playability postflop. I want to take the initiative, isolate Claude and get fold equity rather than letting him see a cheap flop with dominated Ax and broadway hands. With deep stacks a modest 3-bet is low risk but high reward: I’ll raise to 1,000 total (≈10bb) — big enough to apply pressure and charge calls, but small relative to my 16k stack. If Claude 4-bets large I can fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll continue aggressively with position/distribution considerations on the flop.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni in the BB with Ad7d. A7 suited has decent playability with flush potential and backdoor draws. I have position postflop, and the 750 call into a 1300 pot gives reasonable odds. Not strong enough to 4-bet, but too good to fold given the suited diamonds. Calling to see a flop in position.

I 3-bet preflop to isolate Claude and he called, so his continuing range contains many Kx, medium pairs and some pockets that connect with this board. The flop Kc 4d 4c is relatively bad for my A♠J♣ (I have only Ace-high and a single club), but because I was the preflop aggressor I should continue with a controlled c-bet to deny equity to weak hands (Qx, missed broadways, some Axs) and to keep the pot manageable against the parts of his range that hit (Ks, 44, sets). I’ll make a small, value- and fold-equity-oriented bet ≈1/3 pot (700 chips) — big enough to get folds and represent strength, small enough that I can fold comfortably to a sizable raise and preserve my deep stack. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with improvement (pairing A/J or a favorable runout); if he check-raises large I’ll fold to avoid playing big pots when likely behind.

I have Ad7d on K44 with two clubs. I completely missed this flop - no king, no four, just ace-high with a backdoor diamond flush draw. Minni 3-bet preflop and is continuing with a 1/3 pot bet. Against a 3-bet range, I'm behind hands like KK (full house), KQ/KJ/AK (top pair), QQ/JJ/TT (overpairs). My backdoor flush draw needs two perfect cards. While pot odds are decent (700 into 2750), I have poor equity and will face difficult decisions on future streets with just ace-high. Disciplined fold here.
9s8c offsuit from UTG is too weak to open. This hand lacks the connectivity of a suited connector and plays poorly out of position against 5 opponents. Clear fold.

I am in Middle Position with a stack of 22.5 BBs holding 74o (Four of Diamonds, Seven of Hearts). This is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability and equity. With action folding to me, there is no strategic merit in entering the pot. I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Seven offsuit and a stack of 19.5 big blinds. While opening wide to steal blinds is a viable strategy in some tournament contexts, T7o is too weak to merit an open from this position, especially with active players like Minni and Elon behind who have large stacks and can put pressure on me. Limping is not an option, and raising with this hand is too risky given my stack size. I will wait for a better spot.

We’re on the Button with J2o, which is a very low-playability, heavily dominated hand. While stealing the blinds is attractive, both blinds are deep and have shown willingness to fight back (defend/3-bet), which makes opening this particular offsuit trash hand likely negative EV—too many bad flops and dominated top-pair situations. With a healthy ~56bb stack, I’d rather preserve edge and open a stronger/wider-but-playable range instead of forcing a marginal steal.

Folded to me in the small blind with As6s. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to complete, but As6s is a playable suited ace and I want to seize the initiative and try to steal the big blind. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x BB) — large enough to apply pressure and price out a lot of weak hands, but not so large as to overcommit versus a 3-bet. If Elon folds I pick up the 150. If he calls I have good postflop playability with a suited ace and can continue selectively (c-bet favorable flops, check/realize equity on dry or Ace-high boards). If he 3-bets big I’ll usually fold — A6s is too often dominated versus a strong 3-bet range — and only entertain calls versus small, non-committal 3-bets depending on his tendencies. Raising keeps the initiative and maximizes EV in this spot, so action = raise to 300 total.

Preflop in BB facing a standard 3x raise from SB after all others folded. 5h6h suited connectors have excellent playability postflop, strong implied odds with deep effective stacks (mine 176+, hers 171+), and pot odds are favorable (200 into 400 for 3:1, requiring only ~20% equity which we exceed against SB's wide iso range). Calling extracts value and sets up multi-street play.

Flop is Ts Kh Td, pot 600 and I’m first to act (I opened to 300 preflop). I have As6s — only Ace-high with a very weak backdoor spade possibility (needs both turn+river). Elon's flat-call preflop means his calling range is weighted toward Kx, Tx and medium pairs — many hands that already beat or heavily dominate my Ace-high. Because this board heavily connects with his range, a continuation bet from me will have poor fold equity and will be called by better hands, so betting OOP just bloats a pot I’m unlikely to win. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to realise equity rather than build a big pot with a marginal hand. Plan: check and evaluate Elon’s reaction — call small probes to see a turn, fold to strong aggression, and only press when I improve (Ace/pair or clear turn/spade help) or if he shows clear weakness.

Preflop defense with suited connectors was correct for playability and implied odds in position. Flop Ts Kh Td is dry paired board that misses my hand completely - no pair, no strong draw, only backdoor heart and straight possibilities. Minni's SB iso-raise range includes many Tx, Kx, broadways that connect well here, and her check could be weakness or trapping slowplay. With no equity to realize by betting and deep stacks, checking back controls the pot, avoids difficult spots, and allows turn improvement or cheap fold if she bets. Bluff betting risks getting raised off equity-free hand against her likely calling range.

Check. I opened the hand preflop with A6s and exercised pot control by checking the paired-ten, King-high flop (Ts Kh Td). Turn 3h is a complete blank — I didn’t improve and the backdoor spade route is dead (I needed both turn+river spades). Elon’s flop check can be either weakness or a slow-play of T/K/paired hands; either way his continuing range is heavy with hands that dominate my lone Ace (Kx, Tx, sets, etc.). Betting OOP here would bloat a pot I’m likely behind in and will mostly get called by better hands, so I’ll keep the pot small and realize equity. I’ll check to the river, fold to significant aggression, and only press if I pair an Ace or see clear weakness on a favorable river.

Turn board 3h is a blank for my range and adds minimal new draws (gutshots or runner-runner straights/flushes). I have complete air - no pair, no draw beyond negligible backdoors. Minni's check after checking flop suggests either a weak hand (missed flop, giving up) or a trap with a strong hand like trips/two pair/set. Betting pure air OOP risks folding out her weaker hands while getting called/raised by value; SPR remains high (~29:1), but with no equity, realizing bluff equity on river is better after check-check. Checking keeps pot small (600), controls damage, and preserves fold equity for river bluff if she checks weak or induces her bluff. Consistent with preflop plan of playability without overcommitting to air.

I check. Hand: As6s on Ts Kh Td / 3h / 5d, pot 600, heads-up and I’m out of position. I never improved — only Ace-high and the backdoor spade route is dead — and Elon’s line (call preflop, check-call flop and turn) is weighted toward Kx, Tx, sets or medium pairs that beat me at showdown. Betting here has very poor fold equity: most of the hands that would fold to a river bluff are also hands the villain likely didn’t slow-play, while Kx/Tx/sets will simply call or raise. With deep stacks and a small pot, the +EV play is pot control and to realize equity rather than bluff into a range that dominates me. I’ll check to see a showdown and conserve chips; if Elon suddenly leads for a meaningful size on the river I’ll fold (only consider calling a tiny probe if it looks like a pure bluff).

River 5d pairs my 5h exactly, giving me a pair of 5s with 6 kicker on a board of Ts Kh Td 3h 5d. This is a surprisingly decent hand given the action: Minni's SB isolation raise preflop followed by checks on flop (paired T/K), turn (blank 3h), and river (5d blank for most draws). Her triple check strongly caps her range to missed hands like unpaired Ax, QJ, low suited connectors without improvement, or weak broadways—few monsters slowplay three streets OOP deepstacked. Strong Tx trips or Kx two-pair likely bet at least one street for value/protection. My 5s blocks some of her possible 5x value bets, and beats her entire bluffing/missed range while being a decent bluff-catcher vs. semi-bluffs. Checking wins at showdown against worse but forgoes value extraction from her frequent weak calls. Pot 600, SPR ~29:1, small bet ~60% pot gets called by worse often enough for +EV thin value, with fold equity vs. better. Optimal sizing 350 to maximize stack growth late tournament.

Elon check‑called preflop and then checked the flop and turn — a line weighted toward Kx, Tx (including trips/sets), and medium pairs that largely beat my Ace‑high. Pot is 950 and he bets 350; calling 350 to win 1,300 requires ~27% equity (350 / (950+350) ≈ 26.9%). With A♠6♠ on T♠ K♥ T♦ / 3♥ / 5♦ I have no pair and essentially no backdoor outs, so my showdown equity versus his likely range is well below the required ~27%. A raise as a river bluff is unlikely to fold enough of his value range and would just bloat the pot when I’m usually behind. I’ll conserve chips and fold to this small river probe rather than calling or bluff‑raising in a spot where I rarely have the best hand.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), which is widely considered one of the worst starting hands in poker. With a stack of 22.5 BB, I cannot afford to bleed chips playing trash hands from early position against five opponents left to act. This is a trivial fold.

I am holding Ace-Trey offsuit in Middle Position with approximately 19.5 big blinds. While an Ace is often a candidate for an opening raise in 6-max, A3o is a very weak holding that is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. Given the aggressive 3-betting tendencies observed from players like Minni, Elon, and Claude in previous hands, opening for a standard raise here risks losing a significant portion of my stack if I'm forced to fold to a re-raise. I will wait for a stronger hand or a better late-position opportunity to commit my chips.

Folded to us in the cutoff with Q3o. While we have position, this hand is below a profitable CO opening range in 6-max: it has poor playability, is dominated by most continuing queens, and performs badly versus 3-bets. With three deep stacks behind (BTN/SB/BB) who have shown willingness to fight for pots, our raise won’t get enough folds to compensate for how often we’ll be put in tough, dominated postflop spots. Preserve stack and wait for a better steal candidate.

I have A♣ K♥ on the button — a top-tier opening hand. With only the blinds to act and the pot at 150, I want to take initiative, build value, and isolate. I’ll raise to 300 (3×BB): a standard button open that gives good fold equity vs the blinds, keeps SPR reasonable for postflop play, and leaves plenty of stack to maneuver. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet appropriate flops; if I face a 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate (call standard-sized 3‑bets to play postflop, 4‑bet shove or fold depending on sizing and opponent tendencies).

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call after posting SB). My hand 6d9s is offsuit, poorly connected, and weak with no high card strength or suited potential for speculative value. Button raiser (Minni) has a wide opening range, but defending OOP with trash like this leads to difficult postflop spots where I'm dominated often and out of position. Pot odds are ~2:1 effective (250 into 700), insufficient for the poor equity and playability. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no value in calling or 3-betting this hand. Consistent with GTO ranges for SB vs BTN open.

JTs in the BB facing a button open of 300. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). JTs is a strong defending hand - good connectivity and playability postflop. Standard call here to close the action.

I flopped the nut flush draw with Jh on 8h2h6h. With no action to me (0 to call), I should check here. I can either check-raise as a semi-bluff if Minni c-bets, or take a free card if they check behind. Leading into the preflop aggressor with just a draw isn't optimal. Checking keeps my options open with strong equity (~35% to hit flush by river).

I opened preflop with Ac Kh and the flop 8h 2h 6h gives me two overcards plus a 4‑card heart draw. I have lots of equity (9 heart outs + 3 kings + 2 non‑heart aces = 14 unique outs — ~30% to improve on the turn, ~51% by the river), so this is a textbook semi‑bluff c‑bet in position. Claude defended from the big blind and will fold many small unpaired hands and some medium pairs; a ~60% pot bet both builds the pot when I hit and applies pressure to deny correct odds to his draws. I’ll bet 400 to take the initiative; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (continue when I improve, pot‑control or fold to heavy aggression on bricks).

I have the nut flush draw with Jh on 8h2h6h. Minni bet 400 into 650, pot is now 1050. I need to call 400 to win 1050 (~2.6:1 or 28% pot odds). With 9 outs to the nut flush, I have ~35% equity to hit by river. Calling is straightforward - good pot odds plus implied odds when I hit. Check-raising is possible as a semi-bluff but calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize my equity profitably.

The 7h completed the four-flush board. I now have a Jack-high flush with Jh. This is a strong made hand - only Ah, Kh, or Qh beats me. With 0 to call, I should bet for value. Minni could have a lower flush (9h, Th, etc.), a single heart, or be willing to call with trips/two pair. Betting around 60% pot for value - if raised significantly I can reassess, but most of Minni's range that called flop won't have a higher heart.

Turn gives me K♥ with A♣ — a king‑high flush (only beaten by A♥). Claude called preflop and my flop c‑bet, then raised when the fourth heart hit. That line is strongly weighted toward hearts but includes many non‑Ace hearts and some bluffs. Pot is 2300; calling 850 requires ~27% equity, which my K‑high flush has vs Claude’s likely raising range (I crush Q♥/J♥/T♥ and most bluffs, only lose to A♥). Raising is thin — it won’t fold out the nut (A♥) and needlessly bloats the pot and my risk. I’ll call 850, keep worse hearts/bluffs in, control pot size, and re‑evaluate on the river.

River 7s paired the board. I have Jack-high flush with Jh - only Ah, Kh, Qh beats me. Minni called my turn bet, likely holding a flush or single heart. Board pairing creates some full house potential but unlikely given preflop action. Should value bet targeting lower flushes (9h, Th). Sizing around 50% pot to get called by worse flushes while keeping pot manageable if raised.

I hold K♥ A♣ — after the turn I have a king‑high flush (second‑nut; only A♥ or a full house beat me). Claude’s line (BB call preflop, check‑call my 400 flop c‑bet on the three‑heart flop, then raise when the fourth heart landed) is heavily weighted toward hearts — most likely a lower heart flush or a bluff; full houses (pocket 8s/6s/2s) are possible but less likely given the passive flop action. Pot = 4,700; call = 1,550 → break‑even equity ≈ 1,550/(4,700+1,550) ≈ 24.8%. My K‑high flush has far more equity vs Claude’s turning/rivering raising range than that, so a fold would be overly tight. Raising is thin — it won’t fold the one hand that beats me (A♥) and only bloats the pot against the worst hands. I’ll call 1,550 to realize equity and keep worse hearts/bluffs in the pot.

I am UTG with 19.5 BBs and a marginal hand (9h 6h). In a 6-max tournament setting, opening 96s from the earliest position is too loose, especially with several aggressive, deep-stacked players behind me (Minni, Elon). Preserving my stack for a better spot is the optimal play.

Unopened pot after UTG folds and we’re effectively first-in from middle position. QTo is a reasonable 6-max open: it has broadway connectivity, can make strong top pairs/straights, and benefits from taking initiative versus the blinds and two late-position stacks. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x sizing to apply pressure and reduce multiway limped pots, while keeping the risk controlled; if a tight/large 3-bet comes behind from the big stacks, this hand can comfortably fold.

I’m in the cutoff with Jh3h facing a 250 MP open. J3s is a speculative, in-position hand — it plays well postflop (backdoor hearts/straight/2-pair possibilities) but is too weak to thin 3-bet with. A min 3-bet to 400 would be tiny and ineffective (won’t fold enough hands) and a larger 3-bet risks getting 4-bet by Elon or Claude; folding is too tight given the small price to see a flop from late position. I’ll call 250 to keep the pot small, realize equity in position, and use position to barrel or fold based on the flop texture; if a big raise or shove arrives I’ll re-evaluate (fold to large non-forced pressure, call/shove only with good equity).

Preflop on the button with Ts5d, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low playability. Facing a raise from FiveTwo (middle position, stack 5325) and a flat call from Minni (cutoff, deep stack 20075), the pot is 650 and to call is 250. While button position offers postflop flexibility, T5o is easily dominated by typical raising/calling ranges (e.g., any pair, suited connectors, broadway). Calling risks multiway pots where equity suffers against stronger holdings. Raising as a bluff is unnecessary with two players already invested and villain ranges strong enough to continue. Folding preserves my deep stack (180 BB effective) for superior spots in a tournament with 56 hands remaining and active table. Standard fold to avoid spew.

J2s is a weak hand, even suited. I'm in the small blind facing a raise and a call, with Pro still to act behind me who could squeeze. Out of position multiway with this holding is not profitable. The pot odds of 3.25:1 aren't good enough to justify playing J2s out of position. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 7h, facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo and a call from Minni. I have 2150 chips remaining (approx 21.5 BBs). The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a pot of 650, roughly 18.75% equity required), but 67 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. Given my relatively short stack, I prioritize preserving chips for better opportunities to shove or re-steal rather than bleeding chips defending weak offsuit connectors that will have difficulty realizing equity. My history shows a tendency to fold similar weak offsuit hands (e.g., Q6o, K2o, J7o) in the blinds.

Monotone flop (Jc Tc 8c) heavily favors the caller’s range and creates lots of raise pressure versus a c-bet. With QhTd we have a marginal made hand (second pair) with poor playability on many turns (any club/overcard) and no club blocker to continue comfortably versus a raise. Checking keeps the pot controlled, protects my checking range on this texture, and lets me respond based on Minni’s sizing (often calling smaller bets, folding to big pressure).

I have top pair (Jh) with a very weak 3‑kicker on a three‑club flop (Jc Tc 8c). The MP preflop raiser (FiveTwo) checked the flop — his range is wide (broadways, medium pairs, suited connectors and club draws). In position I want to get value from worse Jx/T/8x and charge club/straight draws while denying free cards. Slowplaying is too risky with a weak kicker on a monotone board. I’ll make a constructive c‑bet about half the pot (325) to accomplish protection and extract value. If he check‑raises big I’ll strongly favor folding (he likely has a flush or better), and if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn: continue on blank turns or proceed cautiously if a club or scary card appears.

Minni’s 1/3-pot stab on a monotone board is likely very wide (made flushes, single-club pressure hands, some Jx/Tx, and occasional air), and folding all non-club pairs here would overfold versus this sizing. With QhTd we have a marginal showdown hand (pair of tens) plus a gutshot to the 9 and some improvement outs to Q/board-pairs, so we can profitably defend and keep the pot controlled. Raising without a club is high-variance and risks getting blown off our equity versus Minni’s stronger club-heavy continues, so I’ll just call and re-evaluate turn runouts/sizings (continuing more on Q/9/paired boards, tightening up versus big barrels on club/overcard turns).

Turn brings the 4th club (Kc) which massively increases Minni’s value density (any single-club hand, made flushes, and strong Kx/Jx with a club) and makes our pair of tens much less valuable. We also have only thin/dirty improving equity (A or 9 for a straight that can still lose to a flush), and we hold no club blocker to credibly lead or to defend aggressively versus a raise. Leading just bloats a pot where we’re often behind and invites pressure. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and then continue only versus reasonable sizing while comfortably folding to big barrels.

Turn Kc completes a very dangerous texture: Jc Tc 8c Kc — four clubs on board. I hold Jh3h: top pair but a very weak kicker and no club. FiveTwo was the preflop raiser and check‑called my flop c‑bet, a line consistent with club combos, Kx, medium pairs and some disguised strong hands. The turn now gives a made flush to anyone with a single club and improves Kx hands, so a turn bet would be thin value and very vulnerable to a check‑raise or a river shove. I have poor fold equity and few worse hands that will reliably call a turn bet, so I choose pot control: check behind, keep the pot manageable, and re‑evaluate on the river. Plan: if he checks I can bet small for value on safe rivers; if he barrels I will give up to sizable pressure (only calling small, non‑committal bets as a bluff‑catch).

River is a brick (6s) after Minni checked back the 4th club turn. With QhTd we only have a marginal showdown hand (pair of tens) on a board where Minni can easily have any single-club hand/flushes, and we hold no club to block those. Betting doesn’t get called by many worse hands and risks getting raised or value-owned, while bluffing without a club blocker is poor. Best is to check and let Minni either check back worse showdown hands or bet (at which point we can consider calling only versus small sizing and fold to bigger pressure).

Board: Jc Tc 8c Kc 6s. I hold Jh3h — top pair but a very weak kicker and I have no club. FiveTwo was the MP opener who called my 325 flop c‑bet and then checked the turn (the turn was the 4th club) and checked the river. The turn completed many flushes: any single club in his hand now beats me, and Kx hands also beat my J. There are relatively few worse hands that will reliably call a river bet (maybe some non‑club Jx or Tx), so I have very thin value and almost no fold equity. Betting risks getting check‑raised or called by many better hands; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me go to showdown with a marginal hand without creating unnecessary risk. I’ll check and take my showdown, folding only to a river raise.

We’re UTG 6-handed with 6♦4♥ offsuit and ~50bb. This hand performs poorly multiway and even worse versus the aggressive iso/3-bet tendencies shown by several opponents (Claude/Elon/Minni). Limping invites isolation and puts us in tough postflop spots out of position; opening is too loose UTG. Preserve stack and wait for better +EV spots.

Q♦2♣ is a very weak hand in middle position. UTG already folded and there are four players left to act (cutoff, button, SB, BB) — many of them have big stacks and are capable of 3-betting or isolating. Limping/calling 100 surrenders position and will frequently leave me dominated when a Q hits; open‑raising to 200 is asking for trouble against players who 3‑bet a lot (Elon/Claude) or for short stacks (Pro/Flash) to shove. Pot is 150; calling 100 gives poor pot odds vs the hand’s true equity and creates uncomfortable postflop decisions out of position. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot. Action: fold.

Folded to me in the cutoff with J7o, a speculative hand with decent playability and high card value. Position is excellent with only button, SB, and BB behind. Blinds are relatively short-stacked (Pro 21BB, Flash 18.5BB), making a steal attractive as they may be forced to defend wide or fold. No action before me indicates weakness from UTG and MP. Standard open-raise size of 3x BB to build pot and take initiative. Deep stacks allow for postflop play if called. Raising maximizes fold equity and pot control.

Q9o on the button facing a cutoff open from Elon. This is a marginal but playable hand in position. I have good stack depth (~118BB) and position advantage. Calling is standard here - folding would be too tight and 3-betting Q9o isn't ideal. Will play postflop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 9c, holding a stack of 2100 (21 BB). The Cutoff raised to 300 and the Button called. My hand is a weak off-suit one-gapper that plays very poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. Calling 250 represents over 10% of my stack, and I would be proceeding with a hand that rarely flops well and is difficult to realize equity with. A squeeze shove is too risky given my hand has very low equity if called, and it is likely one of the two opponents will call. The correct play is to fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 4c and have a stack of 1850 chips (18.5 BB). Elon (CO) raised to 300 and Claude (BTN) called. The pot is currently 750 and it costs me 200 to call. While the pot odds are attractive (3.75 to 1), my hand is quite weak and I will be playing out of position against two much deeper stacks. With my current stack size, I am in a range where I need to be cautious about bleeding chips in multi-way pots where I'm unlikely to realize my equity. Folding allows me to preserve my stack for a better shove or defending opportunity with a stronger hand.

Preflop raise with J7o from cutoff was a standard steal attempt, called by Claude on the button who has position postflop. Flop of 3c-4s-Th completely misses my hand; jack-high with no pair, no draws (no gutshot, no flush draw relevant). Board is fairly dry: low cards with a ten overcard, rainbow-ish, few strong draws (possible 5-2 but narrow). My continuation range appears strong (overpairs, Ax, Tx, sets), while Claude's button calling range is wide (suited Ax, connectors, pocket pairs). Out of position, but c-betting maintains initiative, generates fold equity against his marginal hands, and pot control if checked. Small-medium sizing ~50% pot pressures without overcommitting. If called or raised, easy fold turn. Deep stacks (177BB vs 115BB) allow bluffing profitably. Checking risks giving free card to draws or check-raise, better to bet now.

Flop is 3c 4s Th - completely missed my Q9o. I have two overcards but no pair, no draw (just backdoor straight). Elon's c-bet of 400 into 750 is standard on this dry board. While I have position, floating with Q-high against a c-bet with minimal equity is -EV. If I call, I need to either hit my 6 outs (~12%) or successfully bluff later streets. Given the dry texture, Elon likely continues with made hands. Disciplined fold here.

UTG in a 6-max game with T♠9♣ is marginal but playable — I want the initiative and to deny correct odds to the blinds. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300) rather than limp: that sizing gives fold equity, helps isolate the short stacks (Pro on the button) and discourages cheap multiway calls. Plan: proceed cautiously if I face a large 3-bet from a big stack (fold to heavy, priced 3-bets), but consider calling or isolating a shove from a very short opponent depending on sizing. Postflop I’ll c-bet favorable textures and control pot size when I miss, using position and stack depth to extract value or fold to strong pressure.

2c4h is one of the worst possible starting hands, with very low equity against an UTG opening range from Minni, who has a large stack and raised to 3x from early position, indicating strength. No straight potential, offsuit, and I'm out of position post-flop. With deep stacks (185BB) and many hands left (54/150), folding preserves my stack for premium spots where I have an edge. No bluff-raising value here.
T5o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. Minni has been active and aggressive throughout, but T5o has poor playability and is dominated by most opening ranges. Easy fold from the cutoff.

I hold Ad 3c (A3 offsuit) on the Button with a stack of 2100 (21 BB). The UTG player (Minni), who is the massive chip leader, has opened for 300 (3 BB). A3o is a weak hand to play against a UTG opening range, as I am frequently dominated by better Aces (AK-A5) and pairs. Calling 3 BB represents a significant portion of my stack with a hand that has poor post-flop playability. A 3-bet shove for 21 BB is too risky against a UTG range, and there is no reason to get involved lightly. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Six offsuit (K6o). Minni, the table chip leader, has raised to 300 from UTG. My stack is quite short at 1800 chips (18 BB). K6o is a very weak hand to defend out of position against an UTG raise, especially given that Minni has been playing many hands aggressively. Calling would leave me with 1500 chips in a bloated pot, and shoving for 18 BB is too loose for this hand strength. The best strategy is to fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my remaining chips.

Facing an UTG open (6-handed but still strongest position) from the table chip leader, Q5s is too dominated and has poor realization out of position. The direct pot odds (200 to win 450) look tempting, but the reverse implied odds vs a strong opening range (KQ/QJ/AQ and better queens, plus bigger suited hands) make defending unprofitable, and a light 3-bet risks getting 4-bet or called by a player who’s shown willingness to apply pressure. Preserve stack and wait for better BB defend candidates.

Preflop UTG in a 6-handed game with 63o, an extremely weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity, and dominated by any calling range. Deep stack (185BB) allows waiting for premium hands rather than spewing chips in early position. Opponents' history shows aggression, but this hand folds to any raise or call anyway. Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities in tournament with 150 hands total.

93 suited is a weak hand in middle position. With several players still to act behind me, including aggressive opponents, this hand doesn't have enough equity or playability to justify entering the pot. Easy fold.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Ten suited (Ad Td), a strong starting hand. With 21 big blinds (2100 chips), I am in a 'push or fold' or 'standard open' territory. Given the position and hand strength, a standard open raise is preferable to an open shove to induce action from worse hands while maintaining flexibility, although I am pot-committed against short stack shoves. Folding is not an option. A raise to 2.5x (250) is standard for this table dynamics.

I am on the button with a weak unsuited hand (Q4o). Pro has raised to 2.5bb from the cutoff. With a stack of 18bb, I am looking for spots to double up or pick up blinds, but Q4o is too weak to flat-call or 3-bet as a bluff, especially against an opponent with a similar stack size who is likely to be committed. Folding is the correct play to preserve my tournament life for a better starting hand.

Pro is opening from the cutoff with a short ~18.5bb stack, so a 3-bet from the small blind tends to get jammed on frequently. Q9s doesn’t perform well enough versus a typical short-stack 4-bet jam range to 3-bet/call, and 3-bet/folding would burn too much equity when they don’t fold often. With 3:1 immediate odds (200 to win 600 after we call) and a hand that flops playable equity (top pairs, strong draws), the best play is to defend by calling and keep the pot manageable out of position. If the big blind squeezes large, we can comfortably release.

I’m in the big blind with Jh7d facing a cutoff open to 250 and the small blind calling. The pot is 600 and it costs me 150 more to call (i.e. call to 250 total), giving roughly 150/750 ≈ 20% break-even equity — so I can defend here without risking much. J7o is marginal but acceptable to defend from the big blind against a cutoff open, especially with the SB calling (I don’t want to face a multiway pot after folding). I won’t 3-bet with this hand out of position — a raise would bloat the pot and is a poor bluff given the live callers and the short-stacked raiser. Plan: call to see a flop; continue only with a pair, a strong draw, or a favorable board texture; fold to heavy aggression on dry, unfavorable flops to avoid playing big pots out of position with a weak hand.

Multiway and out of position on A♣-8h-7s, our Q♣9♣ has very little immediate showdown value (no pair vs the ace-heavy range) but does retain some backdoor equity (clubs, T/J turns for straight pressure, overcards to 8/7). Leading risks getting raised/jammed by the short-stacked preflop raiser and bloats the pot against the big blind’s stronger continuing range. Best is to check, let Pro decide whether to c-bet in a 3-way pot, and continue only versus small sizing / on favorable turns.

I have J♣7♦ (second pair) on 8♥ A♣ 7♠ — a hand worth continuing but vulnerable to A‑x and overpairs. The cutoff (Pro) opened and is now fairly short (≈1850), and the SB has checked; I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position or induce a short‑stack shove with a marginal hand. I’ll control the pot and see Pro’s action: check here. Plan after the check — call a small/standard c‑bet (rough guideline ≤ ~½ pot ≈ 350–400) and fold to large raises or shoves, since I’m often dominated by Ace‑high hands.

I have Top Pair with a good kicker (Ace-Ten) on an A-8-7 rainbow board. While the board is somewhat connected (straight draws possible with 56, 69, 9T), I am likely ahead of my opponents' ranges (worse Aces, middle pairs, straight draws). Checking back is an option for pot control given my 18BB stack, but betting extracts value from draws and worse Aces immediately and denies free equity realization. Given the stack-to-pot ratio of ~2.5, I want to build the pot slightly while maintaining maneuverability. A continuation bet of roughly 40% of the pot (300 chips) is standard to get value and protection without over-committing against two-pair/set hands.

Pro’s 300 c-bet into 1050 is small, but in this 3-way, OOP spot our Qc9c has essentially no immediate showdown value versus a CO opening range that’s very ace-heavy on A♣-8h-7s. We only have thin backdoors (runner-runner clubs, very weak straight backdoors) and our overcards often won’t be clean, especially with the BB still to act behind us (risk of getting squeezed or going multiway to the turn with a dominated/low-equity hand). Turning this into a raise is also poor: Pro can jam his short stack and the BB’s continuing range on this texture is strong. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

I have J♥7♦ — second pair (sevens) on 8♥ A♣ 7♠. Pot is 1,050 and Pro bets 300; calling costs 300 for ~22% breakeven equity. Cutoff opened preflop and SB called, so Pro’s flop c-bet is small and can include a wide range (A‑x, Kx, pairs, and bluffs). Out of position with a vulnerable one‑pair hand, I want pot control and to realize equity rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I will call the small c‑bet. If Pro shows heavy aggression (large raise or shove) on this street or the turn and I don’t improve, I will fold — raise would be overplaying second pair OOP.

Check. I have J♥7♦ — second pair (sevens) with a weak kicker on 8♥ A♣ 7♠ K♥. The cutoff opened and has shown aggression earlier, so his range is heavy in A‑x/K‑x and medium pairs; my hand is vulnerable and I’m out of position. There’s no bet to call on the turn, so I’ll pot‑control and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Checking preserves fold equity on the river (lets him bluff), lets me realize equity if a harmless card comes, and keeps my options simple: call a small/medium river bet but fold to large raises/shoves unless I improve.

I hold Ace-Ten (Top Pair) on an A-8-7-K board. The pot is 1350 and I have 1550 chips remaining (SPR ~1.15). While I have a strong hand, the King on the turn connects with some of the Big Blind's defending range (two pairs, or improving a float). Betting now commits my stack, and I likely only get called by better hands or strong draws, while folding out weaker hands I beat. By checking back, I exercise pot control, avoid being check-raised, and induce bluffs from missed draws on the river. I can comfortably call a river bet or value bet the river if checked to again, keeping the opponent's range wide.

Board: 8♥ A♣ 7♠ K♥ K♦ — I hold J♥7♦: second pair (sevens) with a weak kicker, out of position. I’ve been heads‑up with Pro since the flop (SB folded). Pro’s line — cutoff open, small flop c‑bet, check on turn — is wide and somewhat cautious (can include A‑x, K‑x, medium pairs and bluffs). The river pairing the board with a second King leaves my hand vulnerable: any King, most A‑x, any 8, and many pocket pairs beat me. I have very little fold equity and little value to extract by betting. Best play is pot control and take this to showdown: check and see a showdown rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand. If Pro fires a small river bet I’d consider calling depending on size; I’ll fold to large bets/shoves unless he shows an unusually weak line.

I hold AdTd (Top Pair) on an A-8-7-K-K board. I raised pre-flop and bet the flop, then checked back the turn for pot control. The river pairs the King. Minni checks to me again. The pot is 1350 and I have 1550 behind. Betting here is thin; worse hands (missed draws, lower pairs) likely fold, and better hands (Trips with a King, Full Houses, or better kickers) will call or raise. While a weak Ace might call a small bet, the risk of running into a trap or being check-raised off my equity is too high given my stack size (15BB). Checking back guarantees I realize my showdown value against missed draws or lower pairs while avoiding losing my stack to a monster.
T6o is a weak hand from UTG position. With 5 players behind me, I need a much stronger range to open. This hand has poor playability and will often be dominated when facing resistance. Easy fold.

I am in Middle Position with T8o (Ten of Hearts, Eight of Diamonds) and 29 BBs. This is a weak off-suit hand that performs poorly post-flop and is easily dominated. With action folded to me, there is no reason to enter the pot. I will fold to conserve my stack.

I am in the Cutoff with Jack-Trey offsuit (J3o). This is a very weak hand with poor playability. My current stack is 1,800 chips, which is 18 big blinds. While I am the shortest stack at the table, J3o is not a hand worth opening or defending from this position. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Folds to us on the button with a suited hand. Even though 8♥3♥ is weak, it’s playable in position and benefits from fold equity; both blinds are deep and can defend wide, so I prefer a small, efficient open that risks little and keeps my stealing range wide. If I face a 3-bet, I’ll fold and preserve my 46.5bb stack.

I’m in the small blind with 7♥4♠ — a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with very little playability or blockers. Button opened to 200 (a standard 2bb open). Calling 150 would put me out of position postflop against the opener (and possibly the big blind), with poor equity and high chance of being dominated. A 3-bet bluff here is thin: multiple players behind could still act and the hand is a poor blocker. With a healthy stack (20,600) I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

64o is a trash hand: offsuit, low, disconnected, poor postflop playability. Button raise after multiple folds likely wide but still includes many better hands that dominate mine. Pot odds 3.5:1 require ~22% equity, but 64o has low equity (~20-30% vs typical button range) and bad implied odds due to reverse implied odds if I hit. Deep stack but no need to defend this; fold to preserve chips for premium spots in late tournament.

I am in UTG with A8o and a stack of 29 BBs. In a 6-handed game, A8 offsuit is a marginal hand to open from early position. It is easily dominated by better Aces (AT+, AJ+) that opponents will call or 3-bet with, and it plays poorly out of position post-flop. Given the stack depth and the risk of bleeding chips with a dominated hand, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot or position.

I am in the middle position with Jd 9s. This is a weak offsuit hand. While it's 100 to call 100 into a 150 pot, the risk of a raise from the Cutoff, Button, or the blinds is high. My stack is relatively short (1800 chips, 18 big blinds). J9o is not a hand I want to play for a significant portion of my stack out of position, and open-calling usually invites more aggression or puts me in a tough spot post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

7♣3♦ offsuit is a bottom-tier holding that plays poorly postflop (dominated, no connectivity, weak high-card value) and creates reverse implied odds when we make marginal pairs. While we could attempt a cutoff steal, the Button still has position on us and both blinds (especially Claude/Elon from prior hands) have shown willingness to fight back, making this an unnecessarily high-variance spot with a 48bb stack. Best decision is to preserve chips and wait for a better opening candidate.

Button with 6♣5♣ vs only the blinds — this is a prime spot to open. Suited connectors play very well deep and I want the initiative and fold equity rather than limp. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3×BB) — big enough to put pressure on the blinds but small enough to keep post‑flop play manageable with our deep 206bb stack. Plan: c‑bet favorable dry flops, play cautiously vs large resistance or 3‑bets (fold to large 3‑bets, call small ones and realize equity in position).

Facing a button raise to 300 (3bb) from Minni in the small blind with Kc4d, an offsuit king-high hand with a weak kicker. This hand plays poorly out of position post-flop, with limited equity against a wide button range. Pot odds are about 2:1 (calling 250 to win 450, effective 1.8:1 after), but implied odds are poor OOP with this speculative hand. Minni has been aggressive from late position in prior hands, often stealing or value betting. Standard play is to fold weak hands like this from blinds to preserve stack for better opportunities in a deep-stack tournament phase (hand 99/150). No fold equity justifies a 3-bet bluff here as K4o lacks strong blockers or post-flop playability.

Holding 56o in the BB facing a button raise to 300. This is a weak offsuit connector - not suited which limits playability. I'm getting 2.25:1 odds to call 200, but being out of position against Minni who has me covered significantly makes this a marginal spot. 56o doesn't flop strong hands often enough and plays poorly OOP. Fold is the disciplined play.

I am in UTG position with King of Diamonds and Trey of Spades (K3o). This is a very weak hand to open from early position, especially with a stack of 1800 (18 big blinds). In this tournament situation, I need to be more selective with the hands I play to preserve my remaining chips. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

We’re effectively facing an unopened pot where calling 100 is just limping. 9♣5♠ is too weak and too dominated to open from middle position in a 6-max lineup, especially with strong/aggressive stacks behind (CO/BTN) who can isolate and put us in tough spots. Limping creates reverse implied odds and invites multiway play where we rarely realize equity well. With ~48bb, there’s no urgency to take a marginal spot—best is to preserve stack and wait for better steal/3-bet candidates.

Folded to me in the cutoff with Kc7h. Pot is 150 and calling is 100 (limp); I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the button and blinds. K7o is marginal but has a valuable King-blocker and plays well enough postflop from position — I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the BB to 300 to maximize fold equity without committing too much. This size isolates the short-ish BB (Pro ~28bb) and puts the button to a decision; postflop I’ll c-bet favorable textures and fold to significant aggression when I miss.

On the button with QJo offsuit facing a standard 3x open from Minni in the cutoff. Position is excellent, and QJo plays well postflop with good equity against her likely wide opening range (worse broadways, suited connectors, Ax). History shows Minni is aggressive but folds to resistance frequently (e.g., Hand 51, 82, 87). SB (Claude) and BB (Pro, short stack 2800) may fold or shove, but fold equity is high. 3-betting for value/thin value and to isolate/steal the pot now (pot 450, to-call 300). Sizing to 900 (~3x her raise) balances fold equity and pot control. Calling keeps pot small but allows multiway; folding too tight late tournament with deep stack and 50 hands left. Prioritizing stack growth via aggression.

J4o is a very weak hand to call a 3-bet with, especially out of position. Elon has 3-bet to 900 after Minni's open, showing strength. I'd be playing a marginal hand OOP against an aggressive player with a deep stack. Clear fold.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Button after a Cutoff open. I hold Q7o in the Big Blind with a stack of 28 BB. This hand is significantly weaker than the ranges of both the opener and the 3-bettor. Calling would require committing nearly 30% of my remaining stack with a dominated hand out of position. The pot odds do not justify a call with this equity. My best course of action is to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I opened to 300 from the cutoff with K♣7♥ to take the initiative. Elon 3-bet to 900, making the pot 1,350 and putting 600 into my decision. Calling 600 into a 1,950 pot requires ~30.8% equity. K7o offsuit is frequently dominated by a typical button 3-bet range (AK, AQ, KQ, broadways and pairs) and has poor post‑flop playability out of position.
