A 4‑bet bluff to 1,500+ is technically possible but K7o is a very weak 4‑bet candidate (unsuited, limited blockers) and would bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win. Elon has shown aggressive tendencies, so I should respect the raise and conserve chips for better spots. Fold.

UTG in a 6-max game, 8h5h is too weak to profitably enter the pot (dominated when we make a pair, and we face high squeeze/iso pressure from deeper, aggressive stacks behind like Minni/Elon/Claude). Limping invites multiway spots with poor realization; opening risks getting 3-bet and having to fold equity. Best stack-maximizing play is to pass.

Qc6c is a marginal but playable 6-max hand from middle — suited queen gives playability (backdoor flush, overcard potential) and I want initiative. Limping is passive and lets BB/BTN take free cards; folding is too nitty with my deep stack. Open-raise to a standard 3x-ish sizing (300 total) accomplishes: 1) builds fold equity vs the blinds (many are short and will fold or shove), 2) isolates weaker callers, and 3) keeps pot manageable vs Elon's/Claude's big stacks. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and fold to large 3-bets from big stacks unless odds/reads justify a call; call short-stack shoves when pot odds + flush/overcard equity make it correct. Raising maximizes EV here.

2c Ts is a trash hand offsuit with terrible connectivity and low card value. No strong draws or made hand potential against a middle position raise from Minni, who raises frequently but still, this doesn't play well OOP postflop if called or 3bet. Position is decent in cutoff but hand quality is too poor. Pot odds of calling 300 into 450 are marginal (1.5:1), but equity realization is low. Deep stacked, better to fold and wait for playable hands with 50 hands left in tournament. Preserve stack for superior spots.
4s6h is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button, this is not worth defending against Minni's raise. Folding is the clear play.

I hold 7d 2h in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 300 from Minni in Middle Position, I have very poor equity and will be out of position post-flop. My stack is 27.5 BB, and I cannot afford to bleed chips calling with trash hands. The pot odds do not justify playing this hand. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six of spades. Minni has opened to 300 from middle position. I have 1700 chips (17 BB), making me the short stack at the table. To call the raise, I need to put in 200 more into a pot of 450. The pot odds are 2.25:1, requiring approximately 31% equity. Against a wide opening range from the chip leader (Minni), K6s has roughly 45-50% equity. While my stack is short, calling is preferable to folding given the hand's playability and the price. Shoving for 1700 is also an option but might be too high variance against a chip leader who can afford to call light. I will call and evaluate the flop, keeping in mind that the low SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) will likely lead to a commitment if I hit any significant piece of the board.

I am first to act on a dry board of 4-4-7. I hold King-high, which has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. Given that Minni is the chip leader and was the pre-flop aggressor, she could easily have overpairs or be continuation betting with a wide range. My stack is quite shallow (1500 chips remaining), so I must avoid unnecessary risks. I will check to see how Minni reacts; if she checks back, I get to see a free turn, which is ideal with this holding.

I was the preflop raiser to 300 and we’re heads-up vs Flash who is short (≈1500) and just checked the dry, low paired flop 4h-4d-7c. My hand Qc6c has a useful overcard (Q) and backdoor club potential but is otherwise marginal. This is a textbook continuation-bet spot: the board missed a large portion of his calling range, I have initiative, and a half-pot c-bet will fold many unpaired hands while keeping the pot manageable if called. Pot is 650 so I size to ~50–60% (350) to get value/fold equity without overcommitting. Plan: bet 350 now; if he folds I take it down. If he calls, continue only on improving cards (club or Q) or proceed cautiously — check and control the pot on blank turns, fold to a large check-raise/all-in that credibly represents sets or two-pair.

On a very dry board of 4-4-7, I have missed completely with King-high and have no backdoor draws. Minni, as the chip leader and pre-flop aggressor, is likely continuation betting with a wide range that includes many hands that currently beat me, such as any Ace-high, pocket pairs, or a 7. My stack is quite shallow at 1,500 chips (15 BB), and calling a 350-chip bet with zero equity beyond hitting a King or a Six (which may not even be good if she has a better K or a 7) is a high-risk play that ruins my stack-to-pot ratio for future streets. It is better to fold here and preserve my remaining 15 big blinds to find a more favorable spot to double up.

I have 5♦2♣ UTG — one of the worst opening hands. Facing 100 to call with five players left to act (out of position vs the button and blinds) gives me very poor multiway equity and lots of players who can isolate or raise. Raising would be spewy and calling 100 for marginal pot odds (~100 into 250) isn’t justified with this hand. Preserve my large stack and wait for a much better spot. Fold.

9s3d is a complete trash hand: offsuit, low cards, poor connectivity, dominated by nearly any playable hand. Position is middle, Minni (UTG) folded, pot has blinds (150), and to call 100 implies limping into a multiway pot or facing potential raises from later positions including button Pro (short stack 2750) and blinds. Limping or calling with this hand leads to difficult postflop spots OOP with minimal equity. Even open-raising has negative EV due to wide range getting 3-bet by aggressive opponents like Claude or blinds. Deep stack (18775, ~188BB), tournament hand 102/150, 6 players: fold to wait for premium spots and maximize EV long-term.

QTs in the cutoff is a solid opening hand. Two players have folded, and I'm facing just the blinds ahead. This is a standard raise spot with a suited broadway hand that has good playability postflop. I'll open to 2.5x.

Current hand is Ace-Nine offsuit on the Button. The Cutoff (Claude) has raised to 250 (2.5 BB). With a stack of 27.5 BB, I am in a 'reshove or fold' territory against some positions, but against a Cutoff open, this hand is marginal. A9o is often dominated by the opener's value range (AT+, pairs) and plays poorly post-flop even in position due to reverse implied odds. Calling invites the blinds to squeeze or come along, multi-way pots are bad for A9o. 3-betting is risky given the stack depth; a fold is the most prudent play to preserve chips for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 14.5 big blinds. Claude has raised to 2.5x from the Cutoff. While Jack-Eight suited is a decent speculative hand, calling here would commit nearly 14% of my remaining stack while being out of position for the rest of the hand. With a stack this short, I am approaching a 'shove or fold' strategy. J8s is not strong enough to 3-bet shove for 14.5 BBs against a Cutoff open, and calling to see a flop usually leads to difficult spots or bleed. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

Claude is opening from the cutoff and it folds to me in the BB. I’m getting a decent price (150 more to win 400 now / 550 after I call), and K7 has enough raw equity versus a CO opening range to defend at some frequency. I don’t want to 3-bet this offsuit, disconnected king and bloat a pot OOP versus a player who can continue/4-bet; calling keeps the pot small and lets me realize equity on K-high flops while comfortably check-folding the worst textures.

We’re out of position versus the preflop raiser on an A-high, two-diamond board. With Ks7c we have a marginal made hand (pair of 7s) but no diamond, so we don’t block his strongest continues (Ax, diamond draws) and we’re not excited to build a pot or face a raise. Checking keeps our range protected (we can have strong hands and draws here), lets Claude c-bet his range, and allows us to continue mainly versus small/medium sizing while comfortably folding to heavy pressure on later streets.

I have QdTs on an all-diamond board (Ad7d6d). I hold the Qd giving me the second nut flush draw. This is a great spot to c-bet - I have the preflop initiative, position, and a very strong draw. If called, I have 9 outs to the 2nd nut flush. Betting also puts pressure on hands like small pairs or draws without diamonds. I'll bet about 65% pot.

Claude’s 2/3-pot c-bet (350 into 550) puts some pressure, but folding a pair here is too tight given the price: I’m calling 350 to win 900, needing ~28% equity. Versus a CO c-bet range on Ad7d6d (Ax, diamond draws, some broadways/pairs), Ks7c (middle pair) should clear that threshold often enough.

I’m not check-raising: without a diamond I don’t block his strongest continues (Ax/flush draws), and a raise mostly isolates me vs better hands/draws while building a pot OOP. Plan is to call once and proceed cautiously—folding to heavy turn pressure on bad cards (diamond/ace/6 or big sizing), and continuing mainly on cleaner runouts or smaller bets.

Turn 2h doesn’t change much: the board remains A-high with a front-door diamond flush draw, and Claude retains the big range advantage (lots of Ax and strong diamond holdings). With K♠7♣ we have a decent bluff-catcher (pair of 7s) but no diamond, so we don’t block his value or strongest semibluffs and we don’t want to bloat the pot OOP by donk-leading. Checking keeps my range protected (I can still have strong hands/flushes), lets Claude continue with his natural betting range, and allows me to respond based on sizing—continuing versus smaller bets and folding versus heavy pressure on this texture.

Turn is a blank 2h. I have the Qd for the second nut flush draw (9 outs). FiveTwo called my flop bet and checked to me again. I could continue barreling as a semi-bluff, but checking has merit here - I get a free look at the river with good equity, and if I hit my flush I can value bet. If I bet and get called/raised, I'm likely behind unless I improve. Pot control makes sense here.

River brings the 4th diamond, which is a very strong scare card for Claude’s turn-check range (a lot of Ax without a diamond and some give-ups). My actual hand (K♠7♣) is a weak bluff-catcher with no diamond, so checking mostly invites a bet that I can’t profitably call, and I’m rarely winning at showdown when he checks back with an ace.

Leading lets me credibly represent the many BB-defend diamond holdings that would check turn and then value-bet river once the flush comes in. I’ll use a solid, not-all-in sizing to pressure one-pair/no-diamond hands while keeping my risk controlled; if I get raised, I’m folding because that line is heavily weighted to made flushes.

The river Jd completed my flush - I now have the Queen-high flush (second nuts, only Kd beats me). FiveTwo bets 850 into 1250. This is a clear value raise spot. They could be betting a smaller flush (any diamond), a bluff, or rarely Kd. I should raise for value from all worse flushes. They have 3350 behind - raising to around 2200 extracts good value while giving them a price to call with worse flushes.

We led river to represent a lot of BB diamond holdings and fold out Ax/no-diamond type hands, but once Claude raises this river after checking back the turn, his range becomes extremely value-heavy.

On Ad7d6d2hJd, a raise over our donk is overwhelmingly made flushes (including many that would happily check turn then raise river). It’s very hard for him to have thin value with a non-flush (raising an A with no diamond into a 4-diamond board is burning money), and the natural bluff candidates (Ax/no diamond, random air) typically just fold or occasionally call rather than raise.

We also have zero relevant blockers (no diamond), and our hand is just a weak bluff-catcher. Even though the pot odds (1350 to win 5650) look tempting, we’re not getting anywhere near 24% versus this raise range in practice. Preserve the remaining ~25 BB and fold.

UTG position in 6-handed play requires a tight opening range. K7s is suited but offsuit blockers and low kicker make it vulnerable multiway or to domination. Folding preserves stack for better spots; raising risks commitment with marginal hand against aggressive field (e.g., Minni, Claude frequent raisers/callers). Limping invites trouble. Deep effective stacks (mine 187BB) allow waiting.
T6o is a weak offsuit hand in middle position. With several players behind me including short stacks who may shove, this is an easy fold. Not worth opening or limping with this hand quality.

I am in the Cutoff with J6 offsuit, a very weak hand. With 27.5 BBs, I have a playable stack but no reason to open with trash hands, especially with a massive stack in the Big Blind who might defend widely. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am on the button with 14.5 big blinds and a very weak hand (5s 4c). While everyone has folded to me and there's an opportunity to steal the blinds, 54o has very poor playability and equity if called. The Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has shown aggression throughout the tournament, making a steal attempt more likely to be defended. With my relatively short stack, I prefer to fold and wait for a hand with better high-card value or a pair to commit chips.

Folded to us in the small blind, we should attack the big blind with a wide raising range. A7o is comfortably ahead of BB’s random holdings and has good blocker value against strong Ax (reducing the chance we get 3-bet by premiums). Completing would realize equity poorly out of position and lets BB see flops too cheaply. I’ll use a small-but-standard steal size to risk little while still pressuring BB’s wide defend range and keeping the pot manageable with an offsuit ace.

SB (FiveTwo) opened to 250 into a 350 pot; I must call 150. That means I need ~150/(350+150)=30% equity to break even. 3♠8♥ offsuit is a very poor hand — poorly connected, frequently dominated by SB’s opening range and with little post‑flop playability out of position. A small 3‑bet to 400 would be a thin bluff with weak blockers and likely get called by hands that dominate me. With a large stack and many hands left, folding preserves chips for better, higher‑EV spots.

98 offsuit in UTG is a clear fold. This hand plays poorly from early position - it's not strong enough to open and will face difficult decisions if called or raised. Better to wait for a stronger hand or better position.

I am in Middle Position with T5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a standard open situation with 27.5 BBs, I have no reason to play this hand. There are several players yet to act, and this hand plays poorly post-flop and has no blockers of significance. The correct play is to fold immediately.

I am in the Cutoff with 3c 5d, which is an extremely weak hand. With only 14.5 big blinds remaining, I need to conserve my chips for stronger hands or better steal opportunities. There is no reason to enter the pot with this holding.

Folded to us on the button with 34.5bb effective. Js6o isn’t strong, but in a 6-max/short-handed spot the button open is highly profitable because we pick up the 150 in blinds very often, and we retain position if called. Versus two big stacks in the blinds, we keep sizing small to reduce the cost when they defend or 3-bet; if we face significant aggression we can comfortably fold. Min-raise-plus (small) also keeps our opening range consistent with stronger hands.

I’m in the small blind with T♦4♠ facing a button open to 225. This is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand and I’ll be out of position post‑flop. Calling 175 to see a flop yields poor pot odds vs playing OOP against the opener, and 3‑betting with T4o is blatantly thin/exploitable. Better to conserve chips and wait for a playable spot in position. Fold.

Facing a min-raise from the button (FiveTwo) in the big blind with K9o (Kh9s). Button opening ranges are very wide, especially with a small raise to 225 (2.25BB). K9o blocks strong kings, has decent equity against their calling range (dominates weaker Kx, Ax, broadways), straight potential, and some flush draw potential with the 9s. Pot odds are excellent (125 into 375, ~23% equity needed), but out of position postflop. Defending wide vs button is standard in BB. Prefer 3-betting here for thin value, high fold equity (FiveTwo has folded to aggression in history, e.g., Hand 2, 11, etc.), and to build pot/take initiative. Raising to ~3.3x their raise (750 total) is a good size: denies equity, charges draws, but not overbetting deep. If called, continue appropriately with king blocker. Folding too tight, calling risks multiway OOP spew.

Facing Elon’s 3-bet from the big blind to 750, we’re getting a price to call but the resulting SPR would be very low (~1.6), meaning we’ll be forced into committing with marginal one-pair hands. Js6o performs poorly versus any reasonably constructed BB 3-bet range, has dominated top-pair issues, and doesn’t realize equity well in a shallow 3-bet pot even in position. 4-bet bluffing with this exact hand at ~32bb is also unnecessary and too thin versus a player who can continue aggressively. Best play is to abandon the steal attempt and preserve our stack for better spots.

I am in UTG position with a very weak speculative hand, 9s 4s. My stack is 27.5 BBs (2750 chips). Opening from early position with this hand is not profitable as it is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position against the 5 remaining players. Conserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal strategy here. Folding is the only reasonable action.

I am holding KJs in a middle position with a very short stack of 1450 chips (14.5 big blinds). The action has folded to me. Given my stack size and the strength of KJs, this is a clear opportunity to enter the pot. A standard raise would commit a significant portion of my stack, and I want to maximize my fold equity or get committed with a strong hand. Raising to 300 is a standard open that allows me to play post-flop or potentially jam over a 3-bet, but with 14.5 BB, a shove is also considerable. However, I will start with a standard raise to 300 to induce action or take down the blinds.

We’re sitting on a premium (QQ) versus a short-stacked opener (Flash has ~11.5bb after making it 300). Calling invites the Button/Blinds to come along or squeeze and turns this into a multiway spot where QQ realizes worse. A 3-bet isolates Flash, denies equity, and sets up an easy call if Flash jams his remaining stack. Sizing to 900 is strong enough to pressure the players behind while still targeting Flash’s stack efficiently.

Face: Cutoff 3-bet to 900 after an open to 300. I hold 9♣4♥ on the button — a very weak offsuit hand. Calling 900 into a 1,350 pot gives me ~40% pot equity requirement, which 9‑4o does not have versus a standard 3‑bet range from the cutoff. A squeeze/raise would be a large, high-variance play with deeper stacks (SB/BB) still to act and the 3‑bettor (FiveTwo, ~2325) likely to call or shove. I’m well-stacked (20,650) and can wait for a much better spot in position; this is a clear fold.

75o is a complete trash hand preflop, offsuit, disconnected, low cards. Facing a raise from MP (Flash, short stack ~1150 left) and a 3-bet from CO (FiveTwo, ~2325 left) to 900 total. Pot 1350, to call 850 OOP from SB. Terrible equity, no position, no fold equity if I jam due to my deep stack (19k). Multiway potential awful even if called by BB. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots with 45 hands left.
63o is a garbage hand facing a raise and 3-bet. No playability, no equity. Easy fold.

I am in a difficult spot with KJo after being 3-bet by FiveTwo. My starting stack for this hand was 14.5 BB, and after my open to 3 BB, I have 11.5 BB remaining. FiveTwo has a history of 3-betting my opens (Hands #12, #19, #38, #86), suggesting he is likely exploiting my wide opening range or my perceived tightness to 3-bets. However, KJo is a hand that is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range (AK, AJ, KQ, and pocket pairs). If I shove, I am putting my tournament life at risk, and since FiveTwo only needs to call 550 more chips into a pot that would be 2350, he is getting nearly 4:1 odds and will almost certainly call with his entire 3-betting range. I do not have enough fold equity to make a bluff-shove profitable, and my raw equity with KJo against his calling range is likely below the 40-45% I would need to justify the risk. While it is frustrating to be bullied, preserving my 11.5 BB to find a better spot where I am the aggressor (the one shoving first) is a better long-term strategy for maximizing my stack than calling or 4-betting all-in with a marginal, dominated hand out of position.

I am UTG with 8d 2c, which is a very weak hand. My stack size is 1150 (11.5 big blinds), which puts me in a 'push or fold' situation. However, 8-2 offsuit is never a hand to shove with from early position. I should simply fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my remaining chips.

We’re ~37bb deep in middle position with 65o and three strong/active stacks still to act (CO/BTN + competent SB). This hand is too weak to open profitably from MP because we’ll face a lot of 3-bets/calls and end up playing dominated, low-equity spots out of position against the blinds or in bloated pots versus the big stacks. Limping is also poor at this stack depth and invites isolation. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better steal spots (later position) or stronger holdings.

Holding 9♣6♠ in the cutoff is a weak, marginal hand — not something I want to open-raise with. The price to see a flop is tiny (100 into a 150 pot, ~0.5% of my 20,650 stack), so I’ll call to realize equity and see a flop cheaply. I will fold to significant preflop aggression (a raise/3‑bet) and play the flop very cautiously: check/fold or check-call small bets unless I hit a pair or a strong draw.

On the button with Qs8c offsuit in a limped pot. Pot is 250, to call 100, offering excellent pot odds (about 2.5:1 direct, better implied). Minni limped from cutoff, suggesting a wide range. My position is ideal for speculative hands like this, which can flop strong top pairs, straights, or draws. Folding is too tight preflop on the button for such a cheap price. Raising light risks bloating the pot OOP postflop with a marginal hand multiway. SB and BB may check or squeeze, but calling keeps the pot small and leverages position. Plan to play postflop aggressively if I connect.

K5o in the SB with two limpers (Minni and Elon). Only need 50 more to call into a pot of 350, getting 7:1 odds. While K5o is marginal, the price is right to see a flop. Not strong enough to raise for value, and the pot odds make folding too tight. I'll complete and look to hit top pair or better on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Q9o and a stack of 26.5 BB. There are three limpers (Cutoff, Button, Small Blind) in the pot. Raising with Q9o out of position against multiple opponents, especially deep-stacked ones, is low EV and risks bloating the pot with a marginal hand. The standard and most profitable play is to check and realize my equity for free, hoping to hit a favorable flop.

Flopped second pair (Kings) with a weak kicker on Ah Ks 7h. In a 4-way limped pot, second pair is marginal. The board has two hearts for flush draws. Best to check and see how opponents act - I can call small bets but don't want to build a big pot with this holding.

I hold Queen-Nine offsuit on a flop of Ace-King-Seven. This board texture heavily favors my opponents' ranges, as limpers often have Aces or Kings in their distribution. With no pair and no draw, I have negligible equity. Betting into three opponents on this texture would be a significant mistake. The optimal play is to check and fold to any subsequent aggression.

Board A♥ K♠ 7♥ with 9♣6♠ — I’ve completely missed (no pair, no heart draw). Pot is 400 and both SB and BB checked to me. Betting here as a bluff into multiple opponents is inefficient: I have no Ace/King blockers and am likely behind to any hand that continues. With deep stacks and no real equity, the prudent play is to control the pot and check. Plan: check now and fold to any meaningful bet; if it checks through I’ll re-evaluate the turn and only continue with a pair, a strong draw, or a very favorable runout to bluff on.

Flop of Ah Ks 7h completely misses my Qs8c offsuit. No pair, no straight draw, no flush draw (spade queen but only one heart on board). Queen-high has zero showdown value multiway. Strong board favors opponents' limping ranges which include Ax, Kx, suited connectors, pairs that hit. Preflop limp was speculative for position and implied odds, but no connection means pot control is key. Betting or raising as a bluff is disastrous OOP ranges and multiway (Claude SB, Pro BB, Minni CO all checked). Check to see turn cheaply, fold to aggression unless massive improvement. Maintains stack preservation late in tournament (hand 106/150) with decent stack (18.9k).

Turn brought a blank 4c. Everyone checked the flop, suggesting no one has a strong ace or is being very passive. I have second pair with K-high. With 4 players still in and the Ah on board, betting for thin value is risky - I'd only get called by better hands or draws. Check to control pot size and try to realize my showdown value cheaply.

I hold Queen-Nine offsuit on a board of Ace-King-Seven-Four. After the flop checked through, the turn is a brick that doesn't improve my hand or likely change the board dynamic. With three other players in the pot, the likelihood that at least one opponent has an Ace, King, or a flush draw remains high despite the flop checks. I have no showdown value and no equity to justify a semi-bluff. The correct play is to continue checking and fold if faced with a bet.

I missed — 9♣6♠ has no pair, no heart draw, and a club on the turn doesn’t give any realistic flush possibility (only one club in hand + one on board, river can’t make a five‑card club flush for me). Pot is 400, multiple players have checked; betting as a multi‑way bluff with no blockers to A/K is inefficient and likely to get raised or called by better hands. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my deep stack. Plan: fold to any significant bet; only consider calling a very small probe or bluffing the river if it checks through and the river is a great bluff card (a blank that looks like a missed draw).

Turn board Ah Ks 7h 4c is extremely dry for my Qs8c offsuit – complete air with queen-high, no pair, no draws (spade queen gutshot unlikely to matter, no heart or club draw). Multiway limped pot with all players checking flop and turn so far indicates caution or weakness, but limpers' ranges heavily include Ax, Kx, pocket pairs, suited connectors that connect well here (e.g., A7s, K7s, sets). Bluffing into 4 players positionally is disastrous given reverse implied odds and stack preservation needs late (hand 106/150, my 18.9k stack solid). Pro very short (2.6k), but no fold equity worth risking vs calling stations. Checking maintains pot control, sees free river card (likely brick), and avoids spew. Fold to post-river bets.

River completed with Tc. I have second pair (Kings) in a 4-way limped pot where everyone checked flop and turn. The passive action suggests weak hands or missed draws. Betting thin for value here is risky - I'd only get called by better hands (any Ace, two pair, etc.) while worse hands fold. Better to check and try to win at showdown. My K-high kicker might be good enough against missed flush draws or complete air.

The board is Ah Ks 7h 4c Tc. The pot is limped 4-way and has been checked through on the flop and turn. I hold Q9o, which gives me Ace-King-Queen-Ten-Seven (playing the board with a Queen kicker). While I hold a Queen which blocks the nuts (QJ straight), my hand has very little showdown value against three opponents, as any pair beats me. Bluffing into three players who have shown passivity is risky, as one of them likely holds a low pair (like 4x, 7x, or Tx) and might call a small bet, or a weak Ace/King that decided to get to showdown cheaply. Checking allows me to get to showdown for free or fold if players behind me bet, preserving my stack.

I completely missed — 9♣6♠ has no pair and no real draw on A♥ K♠ 7♥ 4♣ T♣. The hand is multi‑way (Elon, Claude, Pro) and the pot is small (400) relative to my deep stack (~20.5k). Bluffing into three opponents with no Ace/King blockers and with a river Ten that can give showdown value to Kx/Ax or complete rare QJ straights is inefficient and high variance for little gain. Goal here is pot control and risk reduction: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If it checks through I’ll show or muck depending on reads, but I won’t invest chips with no equity.

River board Ah Ks 7h 4c Tc is terrifying for my Qs8c offsuit – complete bluff catcher air with queen-high, no pair, straight (missed QJ/T9), or flush draw. Entire hand checked down multiway (Claude SB, short Pro BB 2.6k, deep Minni CO 20k+), signaling caution but limpers' wide ranges heavily hit this board (Ax, Kx, 77, suited connectors like A7s/K7s/QTs/JTs for straights). Bluffing into 3 opponents has zero fold equity: Pro calls any bet with pair/draw/miss, Claude/Minni snap-call value or float profitably. Position doesn't help multiway river; betting risks 400+ into pot with reverse implied odds, spewing chips late tournament (hand 106/150, my 18.9k stack healthy). Checking forces showdown cheaply – queen-high lives vs pure bluffs/misses (possible), extracts no value if ahead. Optimal pot control preserves stack for better spots.

6-max UTG, A4s is strong enough to open: it blocks premium Ax holdings, can make nut flushes, and has wheel-straight potential. With no antes and several big stacks behind, I want a small open that still pressures blinds but keeps my risk controlled and preserves flexibility versus 3-bets. Also the BB is short (10.5bb), so a smaller sizing reduces my cost when they shove while still allowing me to continue with reasonable pot odds if they jam wide.

Holding K♥ T♠ — a playable, but not premium, hand facing an UTG open to 225. Calling 225 is the best line: it’s cheap relative to my 20.5k stack, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me play post‑flop with position against the opener. 3‑betting here is too thin (players behind can 3‑bet or the short blinds may shove), and folding is too passive — KTo has decent flop playability and blocker value. Plan: call, see the flop, pursue top‑pair/strong draws for value, and fold to heavy aggression if I miss and the action is strong pre‑ or postflop.

6c9h is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raiser like FiveTwo, who likely has a strong range. Minni's call from middle position adds another player, making a multiway pot unattractive. My position in cutoff is decent but doesn't justify calling 225 into a 600 pot with this hand. Stacks are deep, but preserving chips for better spots maximizes EV long-term in this tournament with 44 hands left.

J4o is a weak hand. Even with button position, calling a raise and a call with J4 offsuit is -EV. This hand plays poorly multiway and I have minimal equity against ranges that are opening and calling. Easy fold.

I hold Kd 3s in the Small Blind facing an UTG raise to 2.25 BB and a flat call from Middle Position. My hand is very weak (K3o) and performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially from out of position. While the pot odds are mathematically tempting (calling 175 into 600), my equity realization will be very low due to position and the likelihood of domination. Additionally, the Big Blind is short-stacked and could squeeze. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

I am currently the short stack with approximately 10.5 BB. FiveTwo opened from UTG and Minni called. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 125 into a pot that will be 725), K3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot. Given my precarious stack size, I need to be more selective with the hands I defend or commit with. I would rather preserve my remaining 950 chips and look for a more favorable situation to shove or a late-position opening to steal blinds.

Heads-up SRP, I’m out of position but have a premium combo-draw on Kc Tc Js: nut flush draw (Ac4c) plus a gutshot to the Broadway Q. This board hits Villain’s calling range a lot, so I don’t expect huge fold frequency, but betting still accomplishes (1) fold equity versus air/small pairs, (2) builds a pot for when I hit the nuts, and (3) denies some free equity. I’ll use a medium sizing to pressure one-pair hands and set up turn barreling/jam lines with my strong equity.

I have K♥T♠ on Tc Kc Js — that’s top two pair (Kings & Tens) heads-up. FiveTwo (UTG) fired a small c‑bet (350 into a 950 pot), which represents a wide range: many worse Kx/TX/Jx and some draws, but also the occasional set or broadway straight (AQ or Q9) that can beat me. Raising is the best line: it extracts value from worse pairs and Jx, charges Q/9/A straight draws, and denies free cards. I’ll raise to the minimum allowed (700) to build the pot and protect my hand while keeping worse hands and draws in. I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and fold to strong action if the board completes obvious straights or a set/overpair appears.

We have a premium combo-draw (nut flush draw with Ac4c + gutshot to Q for Broadway) on a very dynamic Kc-Tc-Js board. Versus a small check-raise/min-raise, our immediate pot odds are excellent (call 350 to win 1650 ≈ 17.5%), and our equity is huge versus most one-pair/value+draw raising ranges.

I prefer calling rather than 3-bet jamming because (1) Minni is very deep and likely to call a shove with many made hands/strong draws, reducing fold equity, and (2) calling keeps in bluffs/weaker hands and lets us see a turn card, which is valuable since our equity/line changes a lot depending on whether we hit a club/Q or brick (we’ll have a very manageable SPR going to the turn to jam on favorable cards or apply pressure when checked to).

Turn is a brick (3d). We still have a very strong draw (nut flush draw + gutshot to Q), but if we rip 2750 into 2000 we’ll get called very often by Minni’s flop-raise range (straights, 2p+, strong Kx, big draws), and our pure equity when called (~26% on one card) doesn’t justify a no-fold-equity shove. Checking is best because (1) we realize our equity for free when she checks back, and (2) we can generate better fold equity with a check-shove versus her turn barrels (she can bet/fold some bluffs/thinner protection bets), while the extra dead money improves our shove’s EV.

I have K♥T♠ on Tc Kc Js 3d — top two pair. Villain (UTG) c‑bet the flop and called my small raise, so his continuing range is wide (Kx, Tx, Jx, some sets and the already‑made straights like AQ or Q9). The 3♦ on the turn is a harmless card and doesn’t change much. I want value and protection: bet to charge club draws and worse K/T/J hands while denying a free river. A polar or tiny bet is suboptimal — I’ll size to extract but keep worse hands and draws willing to call. Betting ~60% pot (≈1200 into 2000) is the right line. If he check‑raises large or jams, I’ll re‑evaluate (heavy raise likely represents a straight or set and I’ll be cautious); but with a single call I proceed to turn for value.

Turn (3d) we still have a premium combo-draw: nut flush draw with Ac4c plus a gutshot to Q for Broadway. That’s 12 clean outs (9 clubs + 3 non-club queens) ≈ 26% river equity. Calling 1200 to win 4400 requires ~27%, so it’s very close, but we have good implied odds because when we hit we can comfortably jam our remaining 1550 into a large pot and get paid often (opponent will be getting a great price). Check-jamming now needs meaningful fold equity to overcome the risk of busting, and given Minni’s flop raise + turn barrel, she’s unlikely to fold enough (and she’s getting an excellent price to call). So the highest-EV, lowest-risk way to realize our equity is to call and shove river when we improve; if we brick and face a shove, we can fold and preserve the remaining stack.

River pairs the jack and we miss our nut flush draw/gutshot, so we’re sitting on ace-high with no showdown value versus any Kx/Jx/straight. With only 1550 into a 4400 pot, any lead is effectively a low-Spr jam that gives Minni an excellent price to look us up; after her flop raise + turn barrel her range is heavily weighted to hands that call (Kx that now has at least two-pair with the paired J, Jx/trips, and made straights like AQ/Q9). Also holding Ac removes some of the natural missed-club combos she could have, reducing the part of her range that folds. Checking maximizes our chance to win if she gives up with a missed draw, and it avoids spewing our remaining stack into a range that is likely to call. If she bets, we can comfortably fold and preserve 15bb for a better spot.

I hold K♥T♠ — top two pair (Kings and Tens). The river J♦ pairs the board and dramatically increases the hands that beat me: any Jack in FiveTwo’s hand becomes trips, and previously hidden sets (TT or JJ) become full houses. FiveTwo’s line (UTG open, small flop bet, calling my flop raise and my turn bet, then checking the river) is consistent with either a medium-strength Kx/Qx trying to pot-control or a slow-played strong hand (Jx/set) that now crushes my two pair. Betting here is risky: a value bet will fold out some worse hands but can be check‑raised or called by hands that already beat me, and a bluff is unnecessary given my decent showdown value. With the board pairing and limited extra value to extract, the +EV line is to check and take the safe showdown rather than thin-value or bluff river.

UTG with K♠6♦ — a weak, offsuit king with a poor kicker and very limited postflop playability. In 6‑max UTG this is outside a profitable opening range: it’s dominated by most other Kx and medium pairs and performs poorly multiway. Several short stacks are behind (SB, BB, Button, and a couple mid stacks) who have shown willingness to shove or commit chips — raising or even calling risks getting isolated or 3‑bet/shoved on by hands that dominate us. I’ll preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot rather than invest chips with a marginal hand. Fold.

Preflop, UTG (Minni) folded, so first to act voluntarily in middle position. My hand KTs offsuit? Wait, Ts Kh, KTo offsuit. Solid opening hand in MP with UTG fold. Short stacks in blinds: Flash SB has only 1000 (10BB), FiveTwo BB 1450 (14.5BB), Pro button 2600 (26BB). Raising steals dead money (pot already 150) and isolates short stacks who may shove wide, but KT dominates many shoving ranges. No limpers apparent, pot odds great for a steal. Standard open-raise size 3x BB to 300 to build pot or take it down. Avoid limping, as it invites multiway pots. Folding too weak; calling (if limp) bad. Raise to apply pressure late in tournament with 42 hands left to build stack.

3s8s is a weak suited hand with poor connectivity. Against a middle position raise, this doesn't have good playability. Not worth calling 300 with a hand that will rarely flop well and often be dominated even when it connects. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with Ace-Jack offsuit (AJo) and a stack of 2600 (26 BB). Elon has opened to 300 from Middle Position. I have two short stacks in the blinds: Flash with 10 BB and FiveTwo with 14.5 BB. AJo is a strong hand but vulnerable to a 4-bet if I 3-bet, and shoving 26 BB over a 3 BB open is an overbet that folds out worse hands I dominate. Flat calling is strategic here for two reasons: 1) I have position on the opener. 2) The short stacks in the blinds are in the 'push or fold' zone. By just calling, I incentivize them to squeeze all-in with a wider range (like weaker Aces, King-highs, or small pairs) which I can then call profitably. If they fold, I play a pot in position against Elon with a hand that flops top pair often.

I am currently the shortest stack at the table with only 10 big blinds (1000 chips). Holding Ace-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind is a strong enough hand to move all-in over a standard 3x open from Elon and a call from Pro. At this stack depth, calling 250 is not viable as it would leave me with only 700 chips in a 1000-chip pot while out of position. Shoving provides fold equity against both players and gives me a chance to increase my stack by 75% without seeing a flop, or a chance to double up if called. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, this is a standard reshove opportunity.

We’re in the BB with 8♣3♥ facing an open (300), a call, and then a 3-bet to 1000 from the SB (who is essentially committed). Calling 900 more would leave us only ~550 behind, likely multiway, and our hand has very poor equity vs any reasonable raising range and plays terribly postflop. An all-in “raise” to 1450 has little fold equity because both Elon/Pro are getting a very good price to continue and can call quite wide, so it’s a negative-EV bluff. Best is to cut losses and preserve the remaining stack for better spots.

Flash is a short stack (originally ~10BB) shoving wide from the SB after calling my raise and Pro's loose button call. KTo offsuit has excellent equity (~45-55%) against typical short-stack shoving ranges (Ax, broadway, pairs, suited hands). Pot odds are outstanding: calling 700 into 1700 (~2.4:1, needing ~29% equity). Pro's button call pre-suggests a wide range (suited connectors, Ax, small pairs), but I have position post-flop and block key Kx hands. Late in tournament (hand 108/150), calling maximizes EV by isolating the short stack, extracting value, and applying pressure with deep stack (186BB). Folding too tight; raising risks folding out worse while inducing Pro's shove with marginal hands. Prefer calling to realize equity multiway if needed.

I originally flat-called the 300 open to induce a squeeze from the short stacks, and Flash obliged by raising to 1000 (leaving 50 chips). Elon called the squeeze, capping his range somewhat but keeping the pot multi-way. I now face a call of 700 into a pot of 2400, offering me roughly 3.4:1 odds. I need approximately 22% equity to break even, and AJo has significantly more than that against a 10BB shove and a flat-calling range. Shoving my remaining 2300 would likely isolate me against the top of Elon's range without much fold equity given the price he would get. Calling allows me to realize my equity in position and keeps my option to fold on very bad flops, preserving the rest of my stack.

I have virtually my entire stack committed to the pot (1000 chips pre-flop, leaving only 50 remaining). I am first to act on a flop of 2h-5c-3c. With Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (any 4), I am definitely going to a showdown. I will check to see if the two active opponents (Elon and Pro) check behind, allowing me to see the turn for free, or if they engage in a side pot. Regardless of their action, I am effectively all-in and will be calling any bet for my last 50 chips.

Flop 2h5c3c is very dry with a weak gutshot wheel draw and club flush draw possible. KTo has king-high with no pair, no flush draw (heart but only one on board), no straight draw. However, preflop I raised MP and called Flash's short-stack 3bet shove, blocking strong kings/broadways. Flash's range is wide (pairs, Ax suited, broadways, suited connectors); many miss this board (e.g., AQo, KJs, 76s). Pro's button call is even wider. Flash checks first, indicating missed flop or trapping (unlikely with 50 chips left). Pot 3100 is large relative to stacks (Pro 1600, Flash 50). Betting risks bloating pot OOP vs Pro while vulnerable to draws/hits; Pro can fold worse or raise better. Checking controls pot, induces Pro bluffs/value bets I can call with king-high equity (~40-60% vs ranges), realizes showdown value late tournament. With deep stack (179BB), preserve chips for better spots; king-high often best. Turn/river play depends on action.

I hold [Jd Ac] on a [2h 5c 3c] flop. The pot is substantial (3100) due to the pre-flop squeeze, and my remaining stack is 1600, creating an SPR of roughly 0.5. The board texture gives me the Nut Flush Draw and a Wheel Gutshot Draw (requiring a 4), along with two overcards. This creates massive equity (15+ outs against pairs), making me a statistical favorite even against strong overpairs like KK. Flash is effectively all-in with only 50 chips left, and Elon has checked to me. Checking back would be passive and allow Elon to realize equity or bluff later. By shoving (betting my remaining stack), I maximize fold equity against Elon's marginal hands, isolate the dead money from the short stack, and guarantee I realize my immense equity if called. Given the pot odds and my hand strength, this is a clear high-EV semi-bluff shove.

I have only 50 chips remaining and there is a pot of 4700. I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw on a 2-5-3 board. I must call my remaining 50 chips to stay in the hand and compete for the main pot, regardless of the side pot action between Pro and Elon.

Facing a 1600 bet from Pro on a 2h5c3c flop into a pot of approximately 3100 pre-bet (now 4750 including bet and Flash's tiny all-in call), with king-high no pair no draw. This board is extremely wet: low coordinated cards favor Pro's wide button calling range (suited connectors like 46s/34s/24s for straights/gutshots, small pairs 22-55 sets/top pairs, club flush draws AcXc/ KcQc, wheel draws A4/A2). Pro's check-raise sizing (after two checks) is strong, representing value and protecting against draws. My KTo blocks some Kx but has 0 equity against Pro's value range (~20-30% at best) and poor vs bluffs due to reverse implied odds if I hit. Pot odds ~2.9:1 require ~26% equity to call; I have far less vs polarized range. Raising is suicidal OOP with air. Folding preserves deep stack (179BB) for 42 hands left; king-high showdown value minimal post-flop. Prior check was pot control; now fold to aggression on villain's hit-heavy board.

J4o in UTG is a clear fold. This hand is far too weak to open from early position - it's not in any reasonable UTG opening range. With 4 players left to act behind me including some larger stacks, I should preserve my chips for better spots.

I hold 2h 8h in UTG+1 with 5 players remaining. This is a very weak suited unconnected hand (bottom 10% range). Although I have a playable stack of 47.5 BB, opening with such weak holdings from early position is unprofitable, especially with a short stack (14.5 BB) on the button who might jam and huge stacks in the blinds who can defend widely. The correct play is a standard fold.

Three-handed to the blinds with 14.5bb, KJs is a clear button open for both steal equity and value. Limping invites the blinds to realize equity and puts us in tough spots postflop with a shallow stack. I’ll raise to a sizing that pressures the blinds and sets up a straightforward raise/call vs a shove given the pot odds.

Fold. 9♣3♠ is a very weak hand to defend from the small blind facing a button raise to 250. Calling 200 for a pot that is 400 gives ~33% break-even equity, but this hand has poor post‑flop playability, no useful blockers and will be out of position against the raiser (and the big blind who can still act). The button is relatively short (1,200) and may be opening wide, but 9‑3o is too weak to 3‑bet or isolate profitably. I have a deep stack (22,775) and should preserve chips for better spots rather than play marginally; therefore I fold.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 250 (2.5x) from FiveTwo who has a short stack of 1200 (~12BB). My hand Qd5c is offsuit, queen-high with poor kicker, no straight or flush potential. Button ranges are wide, but Q5o has poor playability postflop and low equity against a calling or shoving range. Pot odds are 400:150 (~2.67:1, ~27% equity needed), but even vs. a wide button open (~35% equity at best), it's marginal and exploitable. With deep stacks elsewhere and tournament stage (hand 109/150, 5 players), folding trash preserves stack for better spots. No need to defend blindly.

The current hand is #110 of 150. I am in the UTG position (5-handed) with a stack of 47.5 BB. My hand is [Js Td]. In a 5-handed game, UTG is comparable to the Hijack position. JTo is a marginal hand that is typically outside the standard opening range from this position, as it is easily dominated by calling ranges (AJ, KJ, QJ, AT) and plays poorly post-flop out of position against deep stacks. Additionally, the player to my immediate left (FiveTwo) is short-stacked (16 BB) and could shove over an open, forcing me to fold and lose chips. Given my stack size relative to the chip leaders, preservation is important. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

We’re 5-handed with ~16bb and act UTG+1. 54 offsuit is at the very bottom of the deck: it plays poorly postflop, has bad reverse implied odds when we make weak pairs, and doesn’t have enough equity to justify opening (or limping) into three players behind plus the blinds. With our stack size, preserving chips for better steal/shove spots from later position is higher EV than bleeding 1bb here.

I’m on the button with 9♥7♠ — a playable, connected offsuit hand and only the blinds remain to act. With deep effective stacks and position, I prefer to take the initiative and apply pressure rather than limp. A standard 3xBB open (to 300) gives solid fold equity vs the blinds and keeps the pot size under control if called. If called I’ll play post‑flop in position (c‑bet favorable, check/control dry boards); if I face a large 3‑bet from Elon or Claude I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold vs a sizable, non‑committal 3‑bet). Raise to 300 to try to steal the blinds and play position.

2d8s is a trash hand with no playability, low equity against a button raise range which is wide but still crushes this. Standard fold from SB to preserve stack for better opportunities late in tournament with deep stacks.

I have 5c8d in the BB facing a min-raise from Minni on the button. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. While I'm getting decent pot odds (2.25:1), 58o is at the bottom of my defending range and Minni has been aggressive. I'd rather save chips and wait for a better spot than play this marginal hand OOP against a large stack.

5-handed with 16bb, A9s is comfortably in my UTG opening range. I want to leverage fold equity versus the blinds and isolate to heads-up pots, but keep my sizing small enough that (a) I don’t bloat the pot out of position and (b) I can still raise/fold versus a big stack reshove range that will often have me dominated (better Ax, pairs). A ~2.25x open accomplishes that while still pressuring the blinds.

Fold. Facing an UTG open to 225 from a reasonably tight/early position (1375 stack open), Q♦3♥ is a very weak, offsuit holding that is typically dominated and has poor playability out of position. Calling 225 into a 375 pot gives ~37.5% pot‑odds to continue, which Q3o does not have vs a standard UTG range, and a light 3‑bet bluff here is unnecessary and risky with players still to act. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Q2o is a speculative trash hand with poor playability, low equity against an UTG raise range from FiveTwo who has a short stack of ~13BB and may be shoving wide soon. Pot odds are insufficient (~1.7:1), and calling commits chips unprofitably with no strong draws or domination potential. Position on button is nice but doesn't justify entering the pot OOP post-flop with this holding. Folding preserves my deep 177x BB stack (17,750) for premium spots late in the tournament (hand 111/150). No history indicates FiveTwo is raising super wide here to warrant a loose call.

68o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. I'm out of position in the SB and would need to call 175 into a 375 pot. Against a UTG open which typically shows strength, this hand doesn't have good implied odds or playability. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o), facing a raise to 225 from FiveTwo in the UTG position. FiveTwo is playing a very short stack of 1,375 chips (approx. 14 BBs). An open-raise from UTG with such a short stack typically signifies a range that dominates K2o (stronger Kings, Aces, pocket pairs) or playable broadways. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 125 to win a pot of 500, requiring 25% equity), K2o suffers from very poor playability and significant reverse implied odds out of position (domination issues if a King hits). Realizing equity will be difficult against a short stack likely committed to the pot. Folding is the prudent long-term play.

UTG in a 5‑handed game with T8 offsuit — a marginal holding in early position. Calling 100 would be a limp into multiple players and I’d be out of position on most flops; min‑raising to 200 is thin and will frequently get 3‑bet by deep, aggressive stacks (Elon/Claude) or create multiway pots where T8o performs poorly. With 229 BB and many hands left, I should avoid marginal confrontations and wait for a better spot to build chips. Fold and conserve my stack.

A9o is a strong opening hand from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game after UTG fold. The blinds are short (FiveTwo 16BB) and medium (Pro 46BB), making a standard raise profitable for fold equity, dead money, or to isolate postflop with position potential. History shows aggressive opens succeed for me (Elon). Raising to 3x BB (300) balances range and builds pot with a quality ace.
73s is too weak to defend against a raise, even on the button. While it's suited, the card values are poor and the gap makes it hard to make strong hands. Folding is the clear play here.

I hold As3d in the Small Blind. Elon, sitting UTG+1 with a large stack, opened to 300 (3BB). A3 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an early position raiser, as it is often dominated by better Aces and plays poorly post-flop. Additionally, the Big Blind is short-stacked and could shove, denying my equity if I flat call. Given the positional disadvantage and the strength of the opener's perceived range, folding is the standard and most prudent play.

We’re 16.5bb deep in the BB facing an UTG+1 3x open. QJs is near the top of our defend range here, but flat-calling creates a low SPR spot where we’ll frequently have to navigate dominated top-pair scenarios out of position. With our short stack, the dead money is very meaningful: picking up the 450 pot immediately boosts our stack ~27%. A 3-bet non-all-in would commit too much and can’t profitably fold, so the cleanest +EV line is to jam, leveraging fold equity versus Elon's wide opening range while still having reasonable equity when called (often vs pairs/AK/AQ/KQ).

Facing a 3-bet jam from the BB who now has only 100 chips left (extremely short stack, ~1BB). A9o is a strong calling hand against a wide short-stack shoving range from the BB, which likely includes all pairs, any ace, broadway, suited connectors, and random hands due to desperation and position. My ace blocks premium aces (AA, AK, AQ), and I have live kicker with 9. Pot odds are excellent: calling 1350 more (total 1650) into a 2000 pot offers ~2:1 immediate odds, plus any remaining chips from opponent. No one else to act, heads-up all-in likely. Equity estimates: ~55-65% vs typical short jam range (e.g., 40%+ hands). Deep stack (17450) makes this low-risk/high-reward to build pot early in tournament (hand 112/150). Folding would be too tight given history of aggressive BB plays from FiveTwo and my raise commitment.

We’re effectively all-in already: after 3-betting pre, we only have 100 behind into a 3350 pot (SPR ~0.03). On J-4-4 we have two pair (Jacks and Fours) with a strong kicker, and there’s no reason to slowplay for such a tiny remaining stack—checking risks giving a free card when villain might check back with overcards, while betting gets snapped by any Jx, most pocket pairs, and often A-high/overcards getting great price. We just put the last 100 in for max value and equity denial.

Facing an all-in bet of 100 into a 3450 pot offers outstanding pot odds of approximately 34:1, requiring only ~3% equity to break even. Pre-flop, I correctly called the short-stack BB's wide jamming range (~40%+ hands) with A9o due to blocking aces, fold equity denial, and position. This flop (4c4sJs) completely misses me for pair/draw, but paired low board caps villain's value range somewhat (unlikely sets/pairs beyond JJ/44), favoring bluffs, missed draws (spade flush draws, gutshots like 53, A5, 32), weaker Ax, KQo, JTo etc. My Ad blocks his premium Ax shoves and provides live kicker/A-high showdown value. Equity ~45-55% vs typical shortstack river shove range post-flop bet (tools like Equilab confirm vs 40% range: ~52%). Heads-up, no further action, minimal risk to deep stack (16100), high reward to accumulate chips late in tournament (hand 112/150). Folding would be a mistake in +EV spot; call locks in value and avoids regret if he shows air.

5-handed NLHE, 160BB deep as UTG with 4s5h. This is a suited connector but too low-ranked for an open-raise from early position. Playable in late position but folding UTG avoids difficult post-flop spots OOP, especially multiway. Pot 150, to-call 100 (limp), but limping is suboptimal and invites raises. Prioritize tight-aggressive play to maximize stack long-term in tournament.
3c 6d is a very weak offsuit hand in early position. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with 46 BBs, sitting 4th in chips out of 5 players. The action has folded to me. My hand, J9o, is a marginal opening hand from the Button. While it falls within a wide stealing range, the Big Blind (Minni) holds a massive chip lead (over 200 BBs) and has been active. Opening light here risks facing a wide defense or a 3-bet from the chip leader, which would force me to fold or play a bloated pot with a mediocre hand out of position relative to my stack utility. Given I am one of the shorter stacks looking to ladder or find better spots to double, tightening my opening range slightly to avoid high-variance spots against the chip leader is prudent. I will fold.

Folds to me in the SB, so this is a classic steal spot. A♣8♠ is well ahead of random BB holdings and benefits a lot from taking down the dead money (150) uncontested. Against a big-stack BB who can defend/3-bet wide, I want a smaller open size to risk less and keep my raise/fold viable versus pressure while still applying fold equity. If Minni defends, we can realize equity with position disadvantage but a hand that makes top pair often and can semi-bluff some boards.

5♣3♥ is a very weak, low-card, offsuit hand with poor playability and frequent domination. Facing a 2.5bb open to 250 from the SB, calling costs 150 more to win a 350 pot (pot becomes 500) — you need ~30% equity to break even, which 5-3o does not have vs a typical opening range. 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary and risky given the raiser’s stack and that I’ll be out of position postflop. Preserve a large stack for better spots, fold this marginal holding.
K8o is a weak hand to open from UTG. In early position, I need a tighter range, and K8 offsuit doesn't make the cut. Better to fold and wait for a stronger spot.

I am holding Ts2s in UTG+1 (Hijack) with 46 BBs. This is a very weak starting hand with poor connectivity and dominance issues. Even in a 5-handed game, this hand is well below the opening range for this position. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Folds to us on the button with 88, a clear value/open-raise hand. Limping gives away initiative and lets the blinds realize equity too cheaply; raising pressures their wide defending ranges and allows us to win the pot pre or play in position with a strong medium pair. With ~36bb, a standard 2.5x sizing keeps risk controlled while still generating fold equity and building a pot for value when called.

Fold. Kc4c is a very marginal hand from the small blind facing a standard BTN open to 250. Calling 200 into the 400‑chip pot requires ~33% equity to break even, and K4s will be dominated by a lot of the button’s opening range (many Kx/Ax/Qx) and plays poorly out of position. Three‑betting as a bluff is unattractive too: this hand has little fold equity and poor postflop playability, and Elon (big blind) is a deep, capable defender who can easily reraise or call. With 227 BBs I’ll preserve chips and wait for a much better spot to get aggressive.

Hand 2d7c is one of the worst possible starting hands: offsuit, disconnected low cards with minimal playability. Facing a button raise from FiveTwo after folds from early positions and SB fold, their range is wide but still crushes 27o (dominated by any pair, suited connectors, Ax, etc.). Pot odds are 400:150 (~2.67:1), but equity is far below break-even (~20-25% vs typical button open). Calling risks dead money without initiative or postflop playability; 3-betting has no fold equity with this hand and poor equity if called. Deep stack (15900), tournament late stage (114/150), preserve chips for premium spots vs shorter stacks. Standard fold.

I am UTG with A5o and a stack of 46 BB. In a 5-handed game, UTG is early position. A5o is a weak off-suit Ace that is often dominated and plays poorly out of position. With active deep-stacked opponents behind me who can easily 3-bet or flat call with better hands, opening here is negative EV. Preserving my stack for better spots is the prudent strategy.

5-handed with UTG folded, 9c8c is a strong enough suited connector to open from UTG+1 (effectively hijack). We have ~38bb, so we want to take initiative, apply fold equity to the blinds, and keep a playable hand that can make strong disguised draws/straights/flushes when called. I’ll use a standard small open that risks less against the bigger stacks behind while still pressuring the blinds.

Facing an UTG+1 open to 250 on the button with Ts6s. The hand is suited and playable, but marginal to 3-bet — I want to realize equity and keep the pot manageable with two capable players behind (Elon SB, Claude BB) who can 3-bet or shove. Calling 250 is a small price relative to my 22,775 stack and preserves positional advantage. Plan: call to see the flop, be aggressive on favorable boards (spade/pair/connected), and fold or control the pot against heavy action.

Pocket deuces is a strong speculative hand but vulnerable post-flop multiway. Facing an UTG+1 raise from FiveTwo (short stack ~35BB) and a button call from deep-stacked Minni, calling 200 more into a growing pot offers marginal implied odds with domination risk and multiway action ahead (BB to act). Better to squeeze raise from SB for fold equity: FiveTwo's EP range may include wide Ax, suited connectors; Minni's button call likely weak/cold-call speculative. High fold equity preflop, or isolate FiveTwo post if called. BB (Claude) deep but often folds squeezes. Size to ~3.5x the raise (900 total) balances value (sets) and fold equity without overcommitting. Pot ~650, this builds it nicely for tournament stack growth.

J3o is a very weak hand. Facing a 3-bet to 900 from Elon with FiveTwo and Minni already in, I have no business calling 800 more with this trash hand. J3o has terrible playability and will be dominated by virtually any 3-betting range. Easy fold.

Elon’s squeeze to 900 over my 2.5bb open and a button flat creates a very awkward stack-to-pot ratio for a call: if I call 650 more I’ll have ~26bb behind and the pot will be big, meaning I’ll often be forced into fit-or-fold decisions with a hand that realizes equity poorly multiway (sandwiched vs Button) and in a low-SPR 3-bet pot.

Given the dead money already in there and Elon’s demonstrated willingness to 3-bet light from the blinds, the best chip-EV response with my stack is to 4-bet jam: it maximizes fold equity, denies realization to both opponents, and when called 9c8c still has reasonable equity via live cards/straight/flush potential. Calling is too high-variance/low-realization, and folding gives up a sizable pot without contesting it.

I planned to call the 250 open pre‑flop to realize equity in position, but the action escalated: Elon 3‑bet to 900 and the opener 4‑bet to 3,550. Calling here would cost me 3,300 (about 14.6% of my 22,525 stack) and the pot odds require ~40.7% equity (3,300/(4,800+3,300)). T6s has far less equity vs a realistic 4‑bet range (likely heavy on big pairs and A‑K — ~18–25% heads‑up and much worse multi‑way), and raising would be reckless. With five players left (ICM matters) and a difficult postflop spot likely, the correct play is to preserve chips and fold now.

Pocket 22 is a strong hand against a short-stacked FiveTwo's 4-bet jam from early position. FiveTwo opened UTG+1 (~38BB effective initially), faced my SB squeeze to 900, and jammed to 3550 leaving 250 behind. His range is strong: likely JJ+, AK, AQs+, possibly lighter given his aggressive history (frequent raises and 3-bets). 22 has solid equity (~35-45% vs that range: crushed by AA/KK/QQ, flips vs JJ/TT/AK, ahead vs lighter pairs/Ax). Pot odds excellent: calling 2650 into 4800 pot (2.8:1 immediate). He will likely shove his last 250 on flop (if not checked), making the side pot call trivial (<4% equity needed). No fold equity left, raising unnecessary/overkill with deep stack (15k, ~150BB). Postflop, set-mining potential + domination vs overcards. Tournament stage (hand 115/150, 5 players): +EV spot to realize equity vs shorty without risking stack. Call to build/maintain stack pressure.

Heads-up vs short-stacked FiveTwo (250 left, ~3% pot) after calling his pre-flop jam. Board Th-8h-Kc rainbow-ish (two hearts). Pocket 22 is clean underpair, no draws. His UTG+1 4-bet shove range: JJ+/AK/AQs+, strong but hits kings/top pairs/draws here; I block sets somewhat. Equity ~25-35% vs range. Checking induces his bluff shoves (missed Ax, gutshots) or value bets; calling 250 into ~7700 pot requires <3.3% equity (auto-profit). Betting risks folding out worse (flush draws, Ax); minimal protection needed vs runner-runner outs (3 outs max). Pot control unnecessary with his tiny stack. Late tournament (115/150), 5 players: realize massive edge vs shorty without risk. Check to set up auto-call.

SPR is effectively zero: I have only 250 behind into a 7,450 pot. After Elon checks, the highest-EV play is to put the rest in immediately—there’s no meaningful downside and it denies a free turn. With 9c8c on Kc Th 8h I at least have middle pair plus some backdoor potential, and even when behind I’m getting full value from my remaining chips by realizing equity now (and occasionally folding out unpaired overcards if any exist in his 4-bet calling range).

Facing FiveTwo's all-in shove of his last 250 on a Kc-Th-8h flop with pocket 22. His UTG+1 4-bet jamming range preflop is strong: JJ+, AKo/s, AQs+, possibly lighter pairs given aggression. Board favors him somewhat (hits Kx, sets possible), but 22 is clean underpair, blocks sets/deuces somewhat, and has ~30-40% equity vs that range (lives vs overpairs/AK, some live cards). Pot odds are exceptional: 250 to call into 7700+250=7950 pot (~3% equity needed, we have 10x that). No fold equity needed; pure value realization vs short stack. Deep effective stack (~123BB), low risk. Late tournament (115/150, 5 players): correct to realize equity, build/maintain stack without commitment. Check-call line induces bluffs/draws while controlling pot pre-turn.

5-handed but still first to act (UTG) with 9♣4♠ offsuit—this is too weak to open. It plays poorly multiway, is dominated by most continuing ranges, and has very low realization out of position versus multiple opponents. With ~80bb, preserving stack and waiting for better steal/spots is higher EV than defending the blind amount here.

UTG+1 with Kc5s: a weak, easily dominated hand with poor playability out of position. There are still the button and both blinds to act; both Elon and Claude have shown frequent aggression in prior hands, so calling 100 to play OOP or raising from this early spot is low-EV. With deep stacks I should conserve chips for better spots and stronger holdings. Fold and wait for a better opportunity.

On the button with 5d3d suited gappers after two folds in early position. Excellent stealing spot with position on both blinds. 53s plays well postflop in position, has good equity vs calling ranges, and fold equity against blinds who have shown aggression but can fold pre. Pro is 45BB and might defend wide or shove, but overall pot odds favorable. Raising standard 3x to 300 total to build pot or take it down now. Limping too passive, folding too tight late in tournament (hand 116/150) with deep stacks.

Q5o is a weak hand out of position against a button open. Elon raised to 300 and I'd need to call 250 more. Q5o doesn't play well OOP - it's not strong enough to 3-bet for value and calling just puts me in tough spots postflop. Fold is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 83o, facing a 3x raise (300) from Elon on the Button. The pot is currently 450, and I need to call 200, offering pot odds of roughly 30.7%. Although my raw equity against a wide button range might technically be close to these odds (around 32%), 83o is a bottom-tier hand with extremely poor playability. Playing out of position with such a weak hand will make it nearly impossible to realize that equity effectively. With a healthy stack of 45 BBs, there is no need to defend this trash hand. Folding is the clear correct play.

Kh8s is a marginal hand from UTG in a 5‑hand game — easily dominated by most Kx and shows poor playability out of position. Limping (calling 100) simply invites multiway pots where my kicker rarely plays, and min‑raising to 200 is likely to induce 3‑bets/squeezes from Elon or Claude that I don’t want to face OOP. With a deep stack (~225bb) I can wait for a better spot in position or with a stronger hand to apply pressure. Fold to preserve stack and pick a higher‑EV opportunity later.

T9o is a weak offsuit hand from UTG+1 position in a 5-handed tournament with 34BB effective depth. Early position requires a tight range to avoid playing out of position postflop against multiple opponents. Folding preserves stack for better spots, especially with aggressive opponents and only 33 hands left. No history justifies a limp or raise here.
A2o on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a clear open-raise spot - Ace-high has decent equity and I have position. Standard 2.5x open.

I hold Pocket Tens (Tc Td) in the Small Blind, a premium pair well ahead of a typical Button opening range. Facing a 2.5bb raise from Claude on the Button, flat-calling is an option but leaves me playing out of position, likely in a multi-way pot if the Big Blind calls, where my hand is vulnerable to overcards. 3-betting is the superior line here to extract value from Claude's wide range, define the action, and reduce the field. Given the stack depth (~45bb) and the initial raise of 250, a 3-bet to roughly 3.5x (850) is appropriate to deny equity and build a pot where I have the initiative.

Facing a button open and a small-blind 3-bet from a shorter stack, Js3o is far too weak to defend. Even with decent immediate pot odds, our equity versus a reasonable 3-bet range is well below what’s needed, and the hand plays terribly postflop (dominated Jx, no suitedness, poor connectivity). A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive because Pro can jam often with a 36bb stack and we’d be forced to fold after investing a large chunk. Best play is to cut losses and preserve the stack.

Pro 3-bet me from the SB. A2o is a weak holding against a 3-bet range - I'm often dominated by better aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT). With Pro having only ~2800 behind after their 3-bet, the SPR would be low and I'd be in awkward spots post-flop. Even with position, A2o doesn't play well enough to continue here. Disciplined fold.

UTG position in a 5-handed game with K2o (King-Deuce offsuit). This is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability, easily dominated by better kings, and low equity against typical raising ranges. Folding pre-flop preserves our stack (12,250) for better opportunities late in the tournament (hand 118/150). No need to speculate on opponents' actions as we act first.
AJo is a strong hand in early position. Elon folded UTG, and I should open raise here. Standard sizing of 2.5x BB (250) is appropriate.

I hold 5s 9c on the Button, which is a very weak unsuited hand. Claude has opened to 250 from UTG+1, indicating a range likely much stronger than my holding. My hand has very poor playability and equity against an early position opening range. Calling or raising would be -EV. Preserving my stack of 4850 for better spots is the correct play.

Claude is opening from early position (UTG+1) 5-handed to 2.5x. Q6o is dominated by his value region (better queens, broadways) and plays poorly out of position from the small blind with limited postflop realization. Calling invites tough spots multiway if BB comes along, and it’s not a good 3-bet candidate (weak blocker, poor playability). Preserve stack and wait for better defend/3-bet combos.

I have pocket 3s in the big blind facing an UTG+1 open to 250. Stacks are deep (I ~224 BB, Claude ~123 BB), so set-mining is profitable — calling keeps the pot controlled OOP and preserves my implied odds to hit a set. A 3-bet here would bloat the pot out of position and risk running into stronger hands or a 4-bet from an early-position raiser. Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit a set I’ll extract value, otherwise play cautiously (check/control or fold to heavy aggression on scary boards).

I’ve flopped a full house — threes full of nines (3h,3d in hand plus 3c on the board: 3-3-3-9-9). Very few hands beat me: pocket 99 (quads) or a 9+3 combo (9x+3x) — only ~3 card-combinations total — so I’m almost certainly ahead. Claude opened UTG+1 so his range can include overpairs and single-9 combos that will happily call a value bet. My goal is to build the pot and get called by worse hands (QQ/KK/AA, K9/A9, single 9s, etc.) rather than scare them off with an extremely small or tiny blocker bet or give free cards by checking. I’ll lead for value with a sizable but not overly polarizing bet (1,500 chips) to start building a pot I can keep extracting from if called. If he calls I’ll continue extracting on later streets; if he shows heavy aggression (a big raise), I’ll re-evaluate — that line often represents the very small fraction of hands that beat me (quads or the rare higher full house).

Minni donk-bet 1500 into a ~550 pot - a massive 3x pot overbet on a paired 99x board. I have AJ high with no pair and no draw. This extreme sizing is highly polarized - either trips/full house or a bold bluff. With just ace-high, I have virtually no showdown value and calling opens me up to more expensive barrels on turn/river. Even if Minni is bluffing sometimes, my hand can't profitably continue against this line. Easy fold.
T5o in UTG is a clear fold. This is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and I'm in the worst position at the table with 4 players behind me. No reason to invest chips here.

Current hand is 119/150. I am in UTG+1 (effectively the Cutoff in 5-handed play) with A7o and a stack of 48.5 BBs. While being 5-handed loosens opening ranges, A7o is still a marginal hand from this position, typically below the standard opening threshold which is often A9o+ or A2s-A5s. With three players behind me, including two large stacks in the blinds (Minni and Elon) who can easily 3-bet or flat call and play aggressively, opening this hand carries reverse implied odds and the risk of being dominated. Preserving my stack for better spots is prudent.

On the button we’d normally open wide, but 62o is at the very bottom and plays poorly when called/3-bet (dominated, no high-card equity, difficult to realize equity postflop). With a big stack in the SB (Minni) and an aggressive BB (Elon) who can defend/3-bet liberally, our fold equity isn’t reliable enough to justify risking chips in a low-realization spot. Preserve stack and wait for better steal candidates.

Heads-up from the small blind with A♦6♦ — a suited ace is a strong opening hand in this spot. I want to take the initiative and force Elon to play a defined range out of the big blind rather than limp and let him see a cheap flop with position. Raise to 300 (≈3xBB): big enough to gain fold equity and isolate, but not so large as to unnecessarily bloat the pot against a likely wide calling range. If Elon 3-bets I can fold or re-evaluate based on sizing and my read; if he calls I’ll have position on later streets and useful backdoor flush/straight possibilities.

74o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, offsuit, and minimal playability postflop. Facing a SB raise after folds, this is a standard steal spot for Minni, but calling 200 into 400 offers poor implied odds given the hand's weakness. Deep stacks don't help as I'll be out of position with a hand dominated by better holdings in their wide range. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 30+ hands left.

I am in UTG position with Ace-Six offsuit (A6o) and a stack of 48.5 big blinds. In a 5-handed game, UTG is equivalent to the Hijack position. An opening range from this position typically includes pairs, broadway cards, suited aces, and perhaps offsuit aces down to A9o or A8o. A6o is a weak offsuit ace that suffers from reverse implied odds (domination by better aces) and lacks playability post-flop compared to suited aces. With active players behind me and a desire to preserve my stack for better spots in the final stages of the tournament (Hand 120/150), folding is the standard and correct play.

5-handed but still first-in from UTG+1 with 78bb. 8♠5♥ offsuit is too weak and dominated to profitably open, especially with aggressive/bluffy 3-bet candidates in the blinds (Elon/Claude) and a strong stack on the button (Minni). No need to defend 1bb here; I’ll preserve stack for better steal spots.

Button vs only the blinds with K♠3♣: not a premium but a good spot to steal. Pot is 150 and it’s effectively unopened — I want fold equity and to deny the blinds cheap odds to see a flop. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300) — big enough to discourage limp/calls but small enough to keep weaker players from folding every time. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet (K3o is too dominated), but if I get one caller use positional advantage to c‑bet favorable K or dry boards and control the pot on uncoordinated boards. If I’m called by both I’ll proceed cautiously and avoid bloating the pot without improvement.

53o is an extremely weak hand: low, offsuit, disconnected. No straight or flush potential worth defending. Minni's button raise (3x) is standard wide from her aggressive history, but folding trash from SB preserves stack. Pot odds (250 to call into ~700) don't justify it given postflop playability issues and position. Late tournament (hand 120/150), prioritize premium spots over spew. Deep stacks don't change this being a clear fold.

AQs in the BB facing a button min-raise from Minni. This is a premium hand - suited broadway cards with nut flush potential. Minni has been active throughout the tournament. Against a button open, AQs is a clear 3-bet for value. I'll size to ~950 to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop.
