I opened to 300 on the button intending to steal, but Claude 3‑bet to 950 (I must call 650 into a 1,300 pot). K♠3♣ is weak and heavily dominated by a typical 3‑betting range (pairs, broadways, AK/AQ, Kx). Even though I have position, K3o has poor playability (no flush/straight backdoors and a bad kicker) and is a poor candidate for a 4‑bet bluff — the 950 size looks like a standard, value‑weighted 3‑bet. Calling simply puts me in a low‑equity, difficult postflop spot; raising is overly ambitious with this holding. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserving chips for better, less dominated spots is higher EV. I fold.

5-handed UTG, 8♣4♥ offsuit is too weak to open or defend. It plays poorly postflop (dominated 8x, low connectivity, no suitedness) and we don’t need to take marginal spots with ~78bb. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better opening candidates from early position.

QhKh is a strong, playable hand from UTG+1 — suit and Broadway strength warrant an open rather than a limp. I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300) to build the pot, take the initiative, and discourage loose multiway callers (min-raising to 200 gives better odds to callers). With stacks deep (~225bb) this size is standard and keeps my range balanced; if I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate by sizing and actor (call vs fold or shove vs short stack), but preflop the correct play is to raise for value and fold equity.

Preflop on the button with 3h2s offsuit, facing a raise to 300 from Minni in UTG+1 after UTG fold. This is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em, with poor playability, low equity against a typical raising range from early position, and terrible implied odds even if suited (which it's not). My position is good, but speculative hands like this require multiway pots or steals, neither of which applies here against an early raiser. Pot odds are about 1.5:1 (450:300), but equity is too low (~20-30% vs strong range), and reverse implied odds are awful since 32o makes weak hands like gutshots or bottom pairs. Stacks are deep, but folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 121/150). Aggressive history with Minni doesn't change this; standard fold to avoid spew.

Q4 suited is a weak hand to defend from the SB against a UTG+1 raise. While suited, the 4 kicker is too weak and I'll be out of position postflop. Minni has been active and my hand doesn't have great playability. Fold is the standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind with [Ts 9c], holding 47.5 BBs. Minni (UTG+1), the chip leader, opens for 3x (300). While I get decent pot odds calling 200 to win 650, T9o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against an Early Position opening range. Against a 3x raise, the price is not attractive enough to defend with an off-suit connector that often ends up dominated or unable to realize its equity. Folding is the standard play here to preserve chips.

3♠6♥ is a very weak, unplayable hand from UTG in a 5‑handed game. It’s poorly connected, easily dominated by any reasonable opening range, and has terrible post‑flop playability. Limping or calling would invite multiway pots where this hand has almost no equity; raising is nonsense. With a large, healthy stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a much stronger spot. Fold preflop.

K5o is a weak offsuit hand from UTG+1 (early position in 5-handed play). It plays poorly postflop, especially deep-stacked. No steals needed here with position and pot unraised except blinds. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium hands with 29 hands left.

On the button with J9o after two folds. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and a playable hand. J9o is decent enough to open from the button. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to try to take down the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind holding [8d, Ts] and facing a standard open raise from the Button (Claude). T8 offsuit is a weak hand to defend with, especially out of position. While the price to call is relatively cheap, playing a dominated hand OOP against an aggressive opponent is generally negative EV. My stack of 47 BBs allows me to wait for better opportunities rather than defending marginal holdings here. Folding is the most prudent long-term play.

Button opens are wide 5-handed, but 7h2h is at the very bottom of defendable hands. While the price (150 more) is tempting, this hand has poor playability, makes dominated pairs, and even when it makes a flush it’s often the weak end, creating reverse implied odds against an aggressive opponent. A low-frequency 3-bet bluff is possible in theory, but against Claude’s demonstrated willingness to continue/4-bet at times, it’s not a great candidate. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

2h4d is one of the worst possible starting hands, completely uncoordinated, low cards, offsuit. UTG in a 5-handed game, facing a small call of 100 into 150 pot, but this hand has zero playability post-flop, no straight potential really, dominated by any pair or better. Multiway pot likely with limpers/calls ahead. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium hands, especially late in tournament (hand 123/150) where ICM considerations matter despite deep stacks. No history suggests opponents are folding to raises here, and min-raising trash is -EV.

K8o in UTG+1 is a marginal hand. While Elon folded, I'm still in early position with 4 players behind. K8o plays poorly postflop and Minni has been aggressive with 3-bets. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am on the Button with a marginal hand, Ace-Six offsuit (A6o). While opening any Ace from the Button is often standard in 5-handed play, I am facing the tournament chip leader (Minni) in the Big Blind, who has been playing very aggressively and defending wide. My stack is 47BB, which is comfortable, but A6o plays poorly post-flop and has significant reverse implied odds if called by a better Ace. Additionally, the risk of getting 3-bet by the aggressive large stack makes this a high-variance spot for a marginal steal. Given my previous play of folding suited A6 from early position (Hand #81) and generally playing a tight-aggressive strategy, folding here to preserve chips for better spots is the prudent low-variance play.

Folded to me in the SB, so this is a prime steal spot. Even though 86o is weak, heads-up vs the BB our range should be very wide, and raising is generally higher EV than completing because it wins the pot outright a lot and gives us initiative when called. If Minni 3-bets, we can comfortably fold and preserve stack; when called, we can leverage position/initiative on many boards. I’ll use a standard small sizing to keep my risk down while still applying pressure.

Heads-up vs FiveTwo's open to 250. I'm in the big blind with K♥ J♣ — a strong, playable hand that does well against a likely wide SB opening range. Pot is 350 and the extra cost to see a flop is 150 (total 250), which is a cheap price (~30% of the pot) to realize equity. I'm out of position post-flop, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a small, ineffective reraise to 400; a larger 3-bet would commit more with a marginal kicker. I will defend by calling to keep the pot manageable and retain post-flop flexibility: proceed aggressively if I pair the K/J or hit good draws, otherwise control the pot or fold to strong aggression.

Blind-vs-blind on 8♠7♣9♣ is very dynamic and the BB can have a lot of pair+draw / two-pair / straight combos, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with an oversized c-bet. That said, my hand (8♣6♦) is strong enough to bet: top pair plus an open-ended straight draw (5 or T) gives me excellent equity when called, and betting denies equity to random overcards and some weaker 7x/8x that would love to realize for free. I’ll use a solid medium sizing that pressures the BB’s wide defend while keeping my range protected and leaving room to respond to a check-raise with my draw equity.

Call 300. On 8♠7♣9♣ with K♥J♣ I have two strong overcards plus a gutshot (10) and pair-outs: ~3 kings + 3 jacks + 4 tens ≈ 10 outs (~38% to hit by river). The pot is 800 so calling 300 gives ~27% break-even — my equity exceeds the price. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal raise; calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves flexibility to fold to heavy turn aggression or continue aggressively if I pair or make the straight. With a deep stack and a likely wide opening/c-bet range from FiveTwo, a call is the +EV play.

We double-barreled flop and got called on a very dynamic 8♠7♣9♣ board. The turn 4♠ is mostly a brick: it doesn’t change the top-pair landscape much, and it leaves a lot of BB’s flop-calling range (club draws, straight draws like T6/65/Tx, and some 7x/8x) still needing to realize equity. With 8♣6♦ we have top pair plus an open-ender, so betting is both (1) thin value/protection vs worse pairs and draws and (2) equity denial versus hands that would happily take a free card. I’ll use a medium sizing to apply pressure without overcommitting vs a check-raise from two-pair/sets/straights.

Turn decision: board 8♠-7♣-9♣-4♠, I hold K♥J♣. FiveTwo has shown a standard SB opening and continued aggression (flop bet + turn bet to 700 into a 1,800 pot). My clean outs are only kings and jacks (3 Ks + 3 Js = 6 outs). That is 6/46 ≈ 13.0% to hit on the river. Calling 700 into a 1,800 pot requires ~700/(1,800+700) = 28% equity to break even — my ~13% is far short. A raise as a bluff is risky: I’m out of position, the opponent’s line looks value-heavy, and any fold equity from a small raise is unlikely to compensate the negative equity if called (or met with a shove). Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.
4c 2s is one of the worst hands in poker - offsuit, disconnected, and low. From UTG position with 5 players still to act, this is a clear fold.

I hold Jack-Deuce offsuit (2d Jh) in UTG+1. This is a very weak hand with no playability or showdown value. With 47 big blinds, I have plenty of chips to wait for better spots. There is no strategic merit in playing this hand from early position. The correct action is to fold immediately.

Folds to us on the button 5-handed, so we should attack the blinds with a wide opening range. 6h5d is not strong but has decent playability in position (can make straights/two pair) and benefits a lot from picking up the dead money. I’ll use a smaller open to reduce the cost when facing a 3-bet while still applying pressure and leveraging position postflop.

I have Q♠7♣ in the small blind facing a button open to 250 (pot 400). This is a weak offsuit queen that will be dominated a lot by the button’s opening range and I’ll be out of position postflop (and the big blind still acts). Calling 200 to play OOP vs a wide but still strong raising range gives poor postflop equity and creates difficult spots; a light 3‑bet is too spewy with this hand and a larger 3‑bet risks a costly 4‑bet. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack to pick better spots later.

Pocket Queens is a premium hand, top of my range. FiveTwo's button raise after two folds is likely wide, including Ax, broadway, suited connectors for steal. I'm OOP in BB, so flatting gives up initiative and plays poorly postflop. 3-betting isolates, applies pressure, builds pot for value, and denies equity to worse hands. Sizing to 900 (3.6x the open) is standard vs late position raise, balances value and protection given effective stacks (~8000). With 12000 stack and late tournament stage, exploiting button wide range maximizes EV.

Elon’s 3-bet from the big blind is very large (to 900 over our 250), which tends to be more value-weighted and creates a low SPR if we continue. With 6h5d offsuit we have poor playability (dominated by his broadway/ace-high range, no suited equity, and no relevant blockers to 4-bet bluff). Calling would require realizing equity in a tough pot, and 4-betting risks burning chips without strong fold equity. Best decision is to conserve stack and wait for better spots in position.

I am in UTG (first to act) in a 5-handed game with [Td Ks]. My stack is 47 BBs, which is the smallest at the table, while opponents are very deep. King-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand from early position. Even though 5-handed play encourages wider ranges, KTo is easily dominated by hands that call or 3-bet (like KQ, KJ, AK, AT, AQ). Given the aggressive nature of the deep-stacked opponents and my positional disadvantage, opening this hand carries negative implied odds. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot or better position.

5-handed but still early position (UTG+1) with J2s: extremely dominated when called (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, better suited jacks) and poor playability multiway if we limp. With stacks deep enough to get into trouble postflop and no dead money beyond blinds, the highest-EV line is to pass and wait for better spots rather than open/limp a bottom-range suited junk hand.

Button with A♣Q♥ — a strong opening hand. I want initiative and to leverage position while denying the blinds good odds to see a cheap flop, so I’ll raise rather than limp. Size to 3x BB (300) — above the min-raise to gain fold equity against Elon/Claude but still reasonable with deep stacks. Plan: if called play postflop in position (c‑bet favorable ace/high or control pot on dry boards); call small 3-bets and re-evaluate; fold to a large 4-bet jam unless I have a specific read to call.

9c5h is a complete trash hand: offsuit, low cards, disconnected, terrible playability OOP vs a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in similar spots historically. Pot odds are ~1.8:1 but equity is abysmal against any reasonable raising range. Late in tournament (hand 125/150), preserve stack for better spots. Standard fold.

9h3c is a very weak hand - offsuit, unconnected, with a low card. Even getting 2.25:1 pot odds to defend my BB against Minni's button raise, this hand plays terribly post-flop. With 126BB stack and 25 hands remaining in the tournament, I should preserve chips and fold this garbage hand rather than defend light.

5-handed UTG we still want a fairly tight opening range. T7o is dominated by a lot of the calling/3-betting ranges (Tx, 7x, broadways), plays poorly out of position, and doesn’t realize equity well without strong connectivity/suitedness. With 80bb there’s no pressure to take thin, marginal spots—better to preserve stack and open hands that can comfortably continue versus aggression from Minni/Elon/Claude.

UTG+1 with 6♥5♦ (offsuit) in a 5‑hand game is a very weak, easily‑dominated hand. I’m first to act (after UTG folded) and calling 100 (limping) would put me out of position against the button and both blinds and almost guarantees multiway pots or isolation raises — both bad with this holding. Open‑raising is an option in short‑handed play, but 6‑5o is too marginal to open from early position against competent opponents who raise and 3‑bet frequently at this table. With a deep stack I should pick better spots; folding preserves my chips and avoids difficult post‑flop decisions. Action: fold.

Preflop on the button with two folds ahead (UTG and UTG+1). As6o is a profitable stealing hand from this position due to ace blocker, decent playability postflop, and excellent position against blinds. Pro in BB has 4600 (46BB), Claude SB 12575 (deep). Standard open-raise size of 3x BB to 300 builds pot, applies pressure, and allows for postflop flexibility with deep stacks. Late in tournament (hand 126/150), stealing blinds increases stack without showdown risk. No history suggests unusual play from blinds this hand.

2c3c is a very weak hand. Even though it's suited, 23s is near the bottom of any defending range from the SB. Facing a button raise, I'd need a much stronger hand to profitably call out of position. The pot odds of 250 to win 450 aren't compelling enough for such a marginal holding. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh Qd, facing a 3bb open raise from Elon on the Button. KQo is a strong hand that dominates much of a typical button opening range (Kx, Qx, lower connectors). Given my stack depth of 46bb, 3-betting is a viable option for value, but it risks bloating the pot out of position or facing a 4-bet that forces me to fold a hand with good equity. Calling is the lower variance play, offering me good pot odds (calling 200 to win 450) and keeping Elon's range wide. I will defend my blind by calling.

I hold KhQd on a flop of 8d Ah 5h. I missed the board completely. The Ace-high texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Elon on the Button), who has all the strong Ax hands in his range. Leading out (donk betting) would likely be burning chips. The standard line is to check to the raiser. If he bets, I will likely have to fold given I have only King-high and the board connects well with his range, but for now, checking costs nothing.

On the flop of 8d Ah 5h, I hold As6c for top pair top kicker (TPTK). This is a very strong hand on this board, which has flush draw potential (hearts) and possible straight draws (67, 46). Pro called preflop from BB (wide range) and checked, representing weakness. As the preflop raiser on the button with position, a continuation bet is standard for value against worse pairs/draws and protection against draws. Late in tournament, building pot with strong hand. Bet sizing half-pot (approx 325-400) balances value and fold equity. Pro has 4400 stack, effective stacks allow aggression. Bet 400 total into 650 pot.

I hold KhQd on an Ace-high flop (8d Ah 5h). I checked to the pre-flop aggressor, Elon, who has now fired a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 650. The Ace hits the button's opening range very hard, and while I have King-high, I have no draw and very little showdown value. Floating out of position here is not profitable against this sizing without a more significant draw or pair. I will fold and preserve my 44BB stack for better opportunities.

7♥6♥ is a playable suited connector in a 5‑handed, deep‑stacked game — good equity and playability postflop. I want to take the initiative and avoid limp‑multiway pots out of UTG where I’ll be out of position. A 3x-ish open (300 total) gives fold equity, narrows the field, and keeps SPR healthy for postflop play. If I face a standard 3‑bet I can fold or proceed cautiously given stacks behind (SB ~43bb may defend, BTN/CO can call or 3‑bet). Plan: open to 300, C‑bet favorable flops; if I’m multiway or face heavy aggression I’ll exercise pot control and fold to strong pressure.

Preflop UTG+1 facing an UTG raise to 300 (3x BB) from Minni, who has a deep stack of 22k and history of aggression but strong opens from early position. My hand Qs9d is offsuit, non-suited, gapped, and weak for calling or 3-betting (no blockers, dominated by typical UTG range like QQ+, AK, broadway). Position is poor postflop, pot odds poor (~3:1 effective), and with 127/150 hands played, preserve stack for better spots against short stacks like Pro (4.3k). Standard fold to avoid speculative play OOP.

Pocket 7s on the button facing an UTG open to 300. This is a solid hand in position. Minni's UTG range should be fairly tight, but 77 has good set-mining potential with position. Calling is the standard play here - I can profitably set mine and I have position to navigate post-flop. 3-betting is marginal since I'd fold to a 4-bet and my hand plays well multiway.

I hold [Ah Td] in the Small Blind facing a raise to 300 (3bb) from the UTG chip leader (Minni) and a flat call from the Button (Claude). With a stack of 43.5bb, I am in a somewhat awkward spot. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 250 to win 750, or 3:1), ATo plays poorly multi-way and out of position, especially against a UTG opening range which dominates my hand (AQ, AK, AJ, TT+). A squeeze play (3-bet) is risky given the UTG player's stack depth and likely strength. Calling creates a reverse implied odds scenario where I may hit top pair but be outkicked or unable to confidently call down aggression. Preserving my stack for better positional spots is the disciplined long-term play.

We’re facing a 3bb UTG open from Minni and a cold call from Claude, which tightens both ranges and reduces our fold equity/realization. J4 suited is dominated by a large portion of both opponents’ continuing ranges (better Jx, broadways, suited aces), and it plays poorly multiway even with position postflop disadvantage. Although the pot odds are attractive, this is a spot where our equity realization will be very low and we’ll frequently be forced into check/fold or marginal bluff-catch lines. With a healthy stack, conserving chips and defending a tighter BB range vs UTG+call is best.

I opened preflop and Claude called; flop 4♦-2♥-5♥ gives me 7♥6♥ — a very strong drawing board for me: I have a 4-card heart draw (≈9 outs) plus a straight draw to a 3 (additional outs to improve). As the preflop aggressor OOP against a single caller, a c-bet both builds the pot when I have equity and extracts fold equity from unpaired overcards and air. I want a size that charges weaker draws but keeps the pot controllable out of position, so I’ll fire ~1/2 pot. Plan: bet 375; if Claude folds I take it. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (bet for value on a heart or strong card, pot-control or check-call on bricks, fold to strong flop/turn raises depending on sizing).

I have an overpair (77) on a 4d 2h 5h board. Minni c-bets 375 into 750 (half pot). My hand is ahead of most of their c-betting range but vulnerable to the heart flush draw and straight draws. With position, calling is best - I can re-evaluate on the turn. If a safe card comes I can continue for value; if a heart or overcard comes I can proceed cautiously. Raising would fold out worse hands and only get action from better.

Check. The turn (5♦) paired the board and materially worsened my situation as a pure drawing hand. With 7♥6♥ I only have a 4‑card heart flush plus the 3‑out straight (≈12 unique outs) — ~12/46 ≈ 26% to improve on the river. Claude cold‑called preflop and the flop c‑bet, so his continuing range contains many made hands (small pairs, two‑pair combos, and the occasional 5) that now beat me. Betting here would be a semi‑bluff that needs a good portion of his range to fold to be +EV; given the paired turn I don’t expect enough fold equity to make a sizable turn stab profitable. Pot control and equity realization is cleaner: check and see his action. Plan: if he checks back I get a free river to hit; if he bets small (roughly ≤ ~750–800) I’ll call because the pot odds justify chasing; if he barrels large I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot. If I hit a heart or a 3 on the river I’ll value‑bet; if I miss I’ll be cautious to any large river bet.

Minni checked the turn after the board paired with 5d. I have an overpair (77) which beats most of their checking range. The turn check after a flop c-bet often indicates weakness - they likely have a draw, weak pair, or gave up with air. I should bet for value against worse pairs and draws while charging flush draws. A ~50% pot bet (750) extracts value while keeping the pot manageable if I'm somehow behind.

Call 750. I checked the paired turn with 7♥6♥ and Claude’s 750 sizing into a 2250 pot gives me ~25% pot‑odds to continue (750/(2250+750)=25%). I have substantial equity: 4‑card heart draw (9 hearts) plus straight outs (3s and 8s) — counting overlaps gives ≈15 unique outs → ~15/46 ≈ 33% to improve on the river. That comfortably beats the offered pot odds, so calling to realize equity and keep options is +EV. Claude’s line (cold‑call preflop, call on flop, small turn stab) is consistent with medium pairs, single 5s, or some draws — his small sizing makes a fold unnecessary. I won’t raise (don’t want to commit with a draw OOP); plan to re‑evaluate on the river: value‑bet if I complete a flush/straight, fold to large aggression if I miss, or consider a delayed bluff only if the river card and his tendencies give fold equity.

Check. On the river (4d 2h 5h 5d 4c) my 7h6h only makes two pair (5s and 4s) with a 7 kicker. Claude’s line (call preflop, call my flop c‑bet, then lead the turn) is weighted toward value hands — single 5s, 4x, sets or medium+ pocket pairs — many of which now improved to full house/quads or otherwise beat my two‑pair. I have very little fold equity as a bluff and no meaningful blockers to 4/5 combinations, so betting or raising is thin and will mostly get called by hands that beat me. I’ll check to realize showdown value and avoid bloating the pot; plan to fold to any sizable river bet and only entertain calling very small, clear bluff sizings.

River completes a double-paired board (5544). My 77 gives me two pair (77 over 55) which beats any hand without a 5, 4, or higher pocket pair. Minni checked after calling my turn bet - likely a medium pair or busted draw. This is a good spot for thin value. A 40% pot bet (~1200) extracts value from worse pairs while keeping losses manageable if I'm beat.

Board: 4♦ 2♥ 5♥ 5♦ 4♣ — that’s double‑paired board (5s and 4s). My 7♥6♥ only gives me the 5/4 two‑pair on the board with a 7 kicker. Claude’s line (cold‑call preflop, call on the flop, then a clean raise to 750 on the turn) is strongly weighted toward hands that either turned trips on the 5 (any hand with a 5) or had a made pair (4x, 2x, or a pocket pair) and decided to protect against my heart draw. The river 4♣ now turns any 4 or 5 in his hand into a full house; his small river bet (1,200 into the ~4,200 pot) is easily a thin value/induce sizing as well as a blocker bet — not a convincing bluff. Pot odds on a call are roughly 1,200 / (4,200 + 1,200) ≈ 22%; given Claude’s line my realistic showdown equity is well below that (many value combos and high‑kicker hands beat my 7 kicker). Raising is never profitable here — I have almost no fold equity and would only bloat a pot I’m likely behind in. Fold and preserve a big stack for better spots.

UTG with 7h8d offsuit in 5-handed NLHE 50/100. This is a clear fold. Hand has poor playability, low equity, and no position advantage. Deep stacks but no need to speculate early position with trash. Preserve stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 128/150).

J9o in UTG+1 is a marginal hand. With 141 BBs and position on remaining players if called, I can make a standard open raise. This hand has some playability with straight potential. A 2.5x open is standard here.

Current table state is 5-handed. Claude opens from the Cutoff (UTG+1) to 250 (2.5bb). I am on the Button with King-Ten offsuit (KTo). My stack is 43.5bb. While KTo benefits from position, it is often dominated by a Cutoff opening range (which includes AK, KQ, KJ, KTs+, AA-TT). I recently 3-bet Claude in a similar spot (Hand #117) with Pocket Tens and he folded, so he might give my 3-bets respect, but he might also be more willing to defend now. Flat calling is unattractive due to reverse implied odds (hitting a King or Ten and being out-kicked) and the risk of being squeezed by the blinds. 3-betting is a viable bluff but risky if called. Folding is the most prudent, variance-minimizing play with this marginal hand against a standard open.

Claude’s 2.5x open from UTG+1 (5-handed) is typically a fairly strong range. 6♠2♣ plays terribly out of position in the SB and is dominated when it makes a pair; even with decent pot odds to complete, realizing equity is very difficult and the BB can still squeeze or come along, worsening our position. A bluff 3-bet here would have low fold equity versus this position/range and isn’t worth burning chips this late. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Claude opened UTG+1 to 250. I’m in the big blind with K♦4♠ — a weak, easily dominated K‑x with poor post‑flop playability. Calling 150 into a 400 pot gives ~27% break‑even equity (150/(400+150) ≈ 27%), while K4o’s equity vs a typical early‑position opening range is well below or around that number and often dominated. 3‑betting as a bluff to 400 is unattractive: I’d be OOP, unlikely to fold out many hands, and exposed to 4‑bets or difficult postflop decisions. With deep stacks but a clear marginal hand and many hands left in the tournament, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold.
KQo is a strong hand in UTG position. Standard opening raise to 2.5x BB. This hand is well within my opening range from early position.

I am in the Cutoff (5-handed) facing an UTG open from Claude to 2.5bb. My stack is roughly 43bb. Ace-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by an UTG opening range (which includes AK, AQ, AJ, and high pairs). Calling could lead to domination issues post-flop or getting squeezed by the Button or Blinds. 3-betting is an option but risky against a large stack opening from early position. The most disciplined and +EV play in this configuration is to fold and wait for a better spot or a position where I can be the opener.

Claude’s 2.5x open from UTG in a 5-handed game is still a relatively strong/compact range, and T7o is too dominated and low-playability to profitably flat (reverse implied odds, hard to realize equity). As a 3-bet bluff it also performs poorly (no good blockers, likely to get called by better broadways/pairs and then struggle postflop). With no antes, preserving stack and waiting for better button spots is best.

Claude opened UTG to 250 (early-position raise) showing a relatively strong range. I have Qs3s in the small blind — a weak, poorly-connected hand that is often dominated by UTG’s calling/continuation range and performs poorly out of position. Calling 200 to see a flop would put me in tough spots vs both the raiser and the big blind; a minimum reraise would be a thin bluff with bad blockers and unnecessary risk given deep stacks and many hands left. I’ll fold and conserve chips for a better spot.

Preflop in BB facing a UTG raise to 250 from Claude, who has a strong range given their position. My hand 8h5h is a suited connector with some playability, but it's weak and speculative. Pot is 400, to call 150 gives about 2.7:1 odds, but postflop I'll be out of position against a strong range, making it difficult to realize equity without hitting hard. Late in the tournament (hand 129/150), preserving stack and waiting for better spots is optimal rather than defending wide OOP with a marginal hand. Folding is the disciplined play.

It is a 5-handed table, and I am in the UTG position (effectively Hijack). My stack is 43.5 BB. A7s is a standard open in this position in a 5-handed game, falling within the top ~20-25% of hands. I have previously opened similar hands like A6s (Hand #26, #43). While there is a risk of domination or facing a 3-bet from the larger stacks behind me, playing too passively will cause my stack to dwindle. A standard opening raise to 2.5x (250) puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to play a pot with a hand that has good flush potential.

5-handed, Pro’s UTG open to 2.5bb is likely reasonably wide, and 6h5h plays well as a suited connector with good implied odds when we flop strong draws/two pair/straights. We also have position on the opener, and his shorter stack (41bb) reduces the risk of facing huge multi-street pressure from him. A 3-bet bluff isn’t ideal here because UTG ranges don’t fold enough and he can jam over it, while flatting keeps the pot small and lets us realize equity. Main downside is squeeze risk from the bigger stacks behind, but the price is acceptable and we can respond appropriately if squeezed.

I’m on the button with K♦T♥ facing an UTG open to 250 and one caller. K-To is a playable, but not premium, hand — it gains a lot from being in position. With deep stacks (I have ~207 BB) I prefer to keep the pot controllable and see a flop with positional advantage rather than force the action with a thin 3-bet. A min-raise to 400 would bloat the pot for marginal equity and is likely to be called by the limper or induce a shove from the short UTG stack; calling preserves flexibility. Plan: call 250, evaluate the flop and use position to c-bet/protect if I hit (top pair, good draws) or to pot-control/fold to heavy aggression if I miss.

JQ offsuit in SB facing an UTG raise from Pro and two callers (FiveTwo and Minni), making it 4-way pot if I call, with BB to act. Pot odds are 200 to call into 900 (pot will be 1100), but post-flop out of position multi-way with a marginal offsuit broadway hand is -EV. JQo dominates few calling ranges and vulnerable to domination. Pro's UTG raise suggests strength; multi-way pots punish speculative hands OOP. Stacks are deep-ish, but no strong implied odds justify calling. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 130/150).

I have 9c6c in the BB facing a min-raise with 2 callers. Pot is 900, I need 150 to call (6:1 odds). Suited one-gapper plays well multiway with good implied odds if I hit a flush or straight draw. Easy call.

Flopped bottom pair (9s) on a very wet JhQh9d board. Three opponents, coordinated texture with straight draws (KT, T8) and heart flush draws. My 9c6c is a very marginal holding - bottom pair with no relevant draws (my clubs don't help). Should check and evaluate if it gets bet. Not worth building the pot with this weak holding multiway.

I am in a 4-way pot on a very wet board of [Jh Qh 9d] holding Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw. This board texture heavily favors the calling ranges of my three opponents (FiveTwo, Minni, Claude), which likely consist of broadways (KQ, KJ, QJ, QT, JT) and sets/two-pairs. Betting into three players on this texture with essentially air is unlikely to generate folds from better hands and risks getting raised. My best course of action is to check, hoping to see a free turn card or evaluate the action if others bet. I will likely fold to significant aggression given the number of players and the danger of the board.

Multiway on a very connected Jh-Qh-9d board, we have a flush draw but it’s a low one (6h-high), meaning we’re often dominated when hearts come in and we don’t want to bloat the pot without position on the Button. Also, Pro’s short stack makes a bet more likely to get jammed on, and our equity vs a strong check-raise range (top pair+, 2p, sets, big combo draws) isn’t good enough to happily stack off. With everyone checking so far, taking the free card maximizes equity realization and keeps variance controlled; we can respond to a Button stab on later action with appropriate pot-odds.

Flop: Jh Qh 9d. I hold K♦ T♥ — that's a completed Broadway straight (9-T-J-Q-K). This is a very strong, mostly-nut hand on this board: it currently beats single pairs, two-pair, trips and most other reasonable ranges. The main things to be wary of are heart flushes (villain could have two hearts and be one heart away) and unlikely runner-runner counterfeits, but on the flop those are only draws. With multiple players in and a shortish UTG opener, I want value and to charge redraws rather than giving a free turn card. Betting also protects against free cards that could complete a flush. I size for value and fold/river protection — about 2/3 pot is appropriate to get called by Qx/Jx/Tx/pocket pairs and to charge hearts. Pot = 1050, so I bet 700. If I face a big raise I’ll re-evaluate (call/shove depending on sizing and villain tendencies), but my default is to extract value now.

Board is Jh Qh 9d - extremely wet with flush draws and straight draws. I have bottom pair (9s) with a 6 kicker and no relevant draws. Minni bet 700 into ~1050, and there are still two players behind me (Pro and FiveTwo). Getting about 2.5:1 odds, but my hand is too weak on this coordinated board. Bottom pair no kicker faces too many better hands and draws that have good equity against me. Easy fold.

I hold Ad7d on a JhQh9d board. This board texture hits opponents' broadway-heavy calling ranges extremely hard, connecting with hands like KT, T8, QJ, KJ, sets, and two pairs. Minni's bet of 700 into a 4-way pot indicates significant strength. I have only Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw, effectively drawing dead or very thin against likely holdings. The previous thought process mentioning a gutshot was incorrect; I need runner-runner cards to make a straight. With almost zero equity and facing a large bet, folding is the only correct option to preserve my 41BB stack.

Minni stabs 700 into 1050 after it checks through multiway, which can be a fairly wide range (one-pair, strong draws, and some bluffs). Even with a low 6-high flush draw, we’re getting good direct odds: 700 to win 1750 (~28.6%), and a heart draw has ~35% equity to the river. Raising as a semi-bluff is unattractive here because our draw is often dominated and a big stack Button can apply heavy pressure (including jamming or 3-betting), forcing us to fold too much equity. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity, and lets us make better decisions on turns (including sometimes folding even when a heart comes if sizing/action indicates we’re dominated).

Turn pairs the 9, which is a relatively good card for my range to represent (I can easily have 9x after calling pre and calling the flop stab) and it also slightly reduces the likelihood Minni keeps barreling her wide flop stab range. With 6h5h I still have a 9-out flush draw, but it’s a very low flush that can be dominated, and the stack-to-pot ratio is getting small enough that building a big pot out of position as a dominated-draw semi-bluff is high-variance and unnecessary. Checking lets me realize equity when she checks back, and if she bets I can make a clearer decision based on sizing (often continue vs small/medium, and avoid getting forced into a bad stack-off line).

Board: Jh Qh 9d 9s. I hold Kd Th — the Broadway (9-T-J-Q-K), essentially the nut straight. The turn pairing the 9 increases the number of full-house/quads combos that beat me (9x, J9/Q9, JJ/QQ, 99), but those are still a minority of the opponent's calling range. FiveTwo called my sizeable flop raise (700) but checked the turn — that looks like a medium-strength hand (Qx/Jx/Tx) or a draw, not an overly strong slow-played full house in most lines. I want value and to charge heart draws/one-card straight or pair hands, while avoiding bloating the pot too much and inviting a big jam from the rare full-house/quads hands. Betting about half the pot keeps worse hands and draws in, extracts value from Qx/Jx/Tx and hearts, and preserves fold equity against busted draws. If I face a large raise I’ll strongly discount my hand (likely full house/quads) and re-evaluate (fold or commit depending on sizing). Therefore I bet ~1/2 pot for value and protection.

We’re facing 1200 into a pot listed at 3650 (about a 1/2-pot barrel) with 6h5h on Jh-Qh-9d-9s. Our hand is basically a flush draw only; no real straight/overcard equity, and some heart outs are “dirty” (especially 9h making quads on board, and Jh/Qh pairing the board which increases full-house risk vs strong 9x/boat ranges). Raw river equity is ~18–20%, which is a bit short of the ~24.7% we need purely on pot odds (1200 to win ~4850 after calling), but we make up the gap with implied odds when we hit (Minni can still pay with Qx/Jx/straighty hands) and with the option to apply pressure on certain rivers when she slows down.

Raising is unattractive: our draw is often dominated, we’d be committing too much of a 69bb stack with insufficient fold equity, and the Button can respond by calling with strong hands/dominating hearts or by leveraging position to punish us. Best line is to call and play rivers carefully: when a heart comes, prefer a small lead/check with a clear fold vs big raise/jam (low flush); when we miss, mostly check and fold to big bets, mixing in some credible check-jam bluffs on select runouts where her range is capped and the 9-pairing makes our story (slowplayed 9x/boats) believable.

River bricks and we have pure air (6-high) with no showdown value. Leading as a bluff here is unattractive because Minni’s flop stab multiway followed by a turn barrel on the paired 9 heavily weights her toward hands that won’t fold often enough (9x, strong Qx/Jx, KTs/straights, and some overpairs/two-pair). With a pot-sized stack, any meaningful bluff needs a very high fold frequency, which we’re unlikely to get versus that range. Checking keeps our downside capped and at least gives her the chance to check back her missed draws; if she bets, we can then decide whether a check-jam bluff is warranted based on sizing and how capped her range appears.

River is a blank (4♣). I hold K♦T♥ for the Broadway straight (9-T-J-Q-K) — essentially the best hand here except for full houses/quads. A heart flush is impossible with only two hearts on the board, so my main worry are 9x/J9/Q9/99 combos making full house or quads, but those are relatively rare and FiveTwo’s line (call flop 700, call turn 1200, check river) looks like middle-strength hands (Qx, Jx, Tx) that will happily call a value bet. I want to extract value from those hands while keeping the size small enough to avoid inducing a jam from the rare full-house/quads. Betting roughly half the pot (≈2400) is the right balance: it charges worse pairs and broadway combos and keeps fold equity reasonable. If I face a large shove I’ll strongly discount my hand and likely fold to a river jam.

River is a complete brick for our 6h5h: we have zero showdown value and no missed straight to represent cleanly. We’re facing 2400 into 7250 (need ~25% equity), but a call can only win when Minni is bluffing, and our blockers are bad for bluff-catching (we hold two hearts, reducing the number of missed heart-draw bluffs she can have). 

Turning this into a check-jam bluff would require Minni to fold >~44% of the time if we shove 5700, but her line (multiway flop stab, turn barrel after the 9 pairs, river barrel) is heavily weighted toward hands that can continue versus a shove: 9x/trips, boats, and some strong made hands. Given our poor blockers and her value density, the fold equity just isn’t there.

Best play is to preserve the 57bb stack and fold.

5-handed UTG with A8s is a standard open: it has good playability (nut-flush potential, wheel/straight possibilities) and benefits from fold equity versus the blinds. With ~57bb we can comfortably raise/fold versus larger 3-bets and realize equity well when called. I’ll use a consistent small open to keep my range wide and avoid bloating the pot out of position unnecessarily.

Facing an UTG open to 2.5bb with J3 suited from UTG+1 is a fold. FiveTwo’s UTG raise represents a fairly strong, relatively tight range that dominates J3s (many hands with a Jack, higher hearts, pairs, and broadways). I’m out of position to act against the button and the blinds — likely multiway — and J3s has poor playability and weak showdown value. Rather than spew chips with a marginal, hard-to-play hand, I’ll preserve my deep stack for better spots (stronger holdings or position).

Facing an early position raise from FiveTwo (UTG) with a stack of 5450. My hand Td8s is offsuit, gapped, and marginal at best. UTG ranges are strong: pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways and some suited connectors. Calling 250 into 400 offers ~2.6:1 odds but requires multiway pots or blinds to fold poorly, and postflop playability is poor due to offsuit nature and vulnerability to domination. In position on button, but with 20 hands left in tournament, better to preserve 12550 stack for premium spots rather than speculate. No 3bet value. Standard fold.

A6o in SB facing UTG raise of 250. Marginal hand but I have an ace. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400). A6o is borderline vs UTG range but worth seeing a flop at this price. Folding is also reasonable given I'm OOP, but I'll defend here.

I am in the Big Blind with Q7o. The UTG player (FiveTwo) opened to 250 (2.5bb), and the Small Blind (Claude) flat called. I am getting excellent pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 600, 5:1), requiring only about 16.7% equity. However, Q7o is a very weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially when sandwiched between the Small Blind and the original raiser who has a strong UTG range. Being out of position relative to the aggressor makes it difficult to realize equity, and I am often dominated by better Queens. Despite the attractive price, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a fold.

Missed the flop completely with A6 - no pair, no draw. Board is 2s Kh 7c. I'm OOP against the preflop raiser. Ace-high has some showdown value but I should check and evaluate. If FiveTwo bets, I'll likely fold since they could easily have Kx or a pocket pair.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser with position, this K-high flop is favorable for a small range c-bet: I credibly represent Kx/overpairs and can fold out a lot of SB’s whiffed hands and some weaker pairs. A♣8♣ has decent equity when called (ace-high + backdoor club draw) and benefits from denying equity to hands like QJ/JT/T9. I’ll use a small sizing to keep my range wide and risk little; if check-raised, this is an easy fold.

Missed the flop with A6 - just ace-high on K72 board. FiveTwo opened UTG and c-bet 200 into 600 (1/3 pot). Their UTG range is strong and likely contains many Kx hands. I have no pair, no draw, and I'm OOP. Even with the small bet sizing giving me 4:1 odds, I have very little equity and will face continued aggression. Fold is correct.

Holding J♥3♥ UTG in a 5‑hand 50/100 game is a clear fold. J3s is a very weak, often dominated hand and UTG must be reasonably tight — playing out of position postflop against multiple opponents will cost equity and create tough spots. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives ~28.6% break‑even equity; J3s won’t have that against typical opening ranges, and raising here would be purely speculative and thin. There’s no need to gamble with a large 23450 stack when better spots will come in position; also the shortish SB (Pro 3950) could shove or force awkward decisions. Fold and wait for stronger, positionally appropriate hands.

Hand #132 pre-flop, holding 6c5s in UTG+1 position (early in 5-handed play). This is a speculative suited connector but too weak to open/raise or limp from early position OOP. Expect multiway pot with aggressive opponents like FiveTwo and Minni behind. Poor post-flop playability with reverse implied odds potential. Late in tournament (hand 132/150), stack of 125BB is comfortable; better to preserve chips for premium spots rather than risk in marginal situation. Pot odds to call BB (100 into 150) not compelling for this hand. Standard fold.

On the button with folds to me holding 42 suited. This is a marginal hand but in position with only blinds to act, a standard button steal raise is profitable. 42s has some suited playability if called. Raising to 250 (2.5x) for a steal attempt.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 9d facing a standard 2.5x raise (250) from the Button (Claude). My hand (King-Nine suited) is strong enough to play against a likely wide button opening range. With a stack of roughly 40BB, I have sufficient depth to play post-flop. 3-betting is an option to isolate, but calling is lower variance and keeps the pot manageable while out of position. I am getting good pot odds to see a flop. The button's range is wide enough that K9s has good equity (~55%). I will call the raise.

We’re getting a very good price: 150 to win a pot that will be 750 after we call (~20% required). J♣5♣ is weak and plays poorly out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a squeeze that will get called too often by a Button open and a SB cold-caller. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets us realize equity mainly via flush potential and occasional top-pair situations, while we can comfortably fold on unfavorable flops without investing much.

I hold Kd9d on a flop of Ad8s3c. I have King-high and a backdoor flush draw (3 diamonds total). This board texture (Ace-high, dry) heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Claude) who is on the Button. Being out of position in a multi-way pot (3 players) with very little equity, leading out would be a mistake as I would likely only be called by better hands (Ax, 8x, pocket pairs). My best course of action is to check to the raiser. If faced with a bet, I will likely fold unless the bet is extremely small, as I have insufficient immediate outs. Checking gives me a chance to see a free turn if the opponents also check.

Multiway on A♦-8♠-3♣ with J♣5♣ we have no pair, no meaningful straight equity, and only a backdoor club draw (one club on board). Leading into the preflop raiser (Claude) and a third player (Pro) has reduced fold equity, and when called/raised we’re forced into tough turn decisions with a weak hand out of position. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets Claude c-bet his range (we can then respond based on sizing/tells), and preserves our stack for better spots while still allowing us to pick up equity on turns like a club, 5/J, or favorable runouts to bluff later if it checks through.

I have 4d2d on Ad-8s-3c. Checked to me with two opponents. I have a backdoor flush draw and gutshot to the wheel (need a 5). Against two opponents who could easily have an ace in their BB/SB defending range, c-betting with essentially no made hand is risky. Better to check back and take a free card to potentially improve with my backdoor draws.

The flop action checked through on an Ace-high board (Ad 8s 3c), suggesting that the Button (Claude) likely does not have a strong Ace. The turn is the King of Spades (Ks), giving me second pair with a 9 kicker. Being out of position with a marginal made hand (second pair), my primary goal is to get to showdown cheaply or induce bluffs. Leading out into two opponents risks isolating myself against an Ace or a better King, while folding out worse hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially call a reasonable bet from the Button who might be stabbing at the pot, or see a free River.

Turn is K♠ after a flop that checked through 3-way. With J♣5♣ we have essentially no real equity (can’t complete a club flush anymore; only thin outs to pair J/5 on the river). While ranges are somewhat capped due to the flop checks, bluffing into two opponents is still low fold-equity and risks getting looked up by any A-x, K-x, 8-x, or even stubborn pocket pairs. With a comfortable stack (58bb), this is not a spot to torch chips. I’ll check, keep the pot small, and only consider a river stab if it checks through again and the river is favorable for representing value.

Turn brought Ks, giving the board Ad-8s-3c-Ks. I have 4d2d - complete air with no draws (backdoor flush didn't materialize). Both opponents checked twice showing weakness, but betting as a bluff here is risky: I have two opponents to get through, zero equity if called, and the pot is small at 750. The Ks could have hit one of them. Better to take the free card and see a river - maybe I hit a 5 for the wheel or make some miracle hand.

The hand has checked through on the Flop and Turn. I hold second pair (Kings) on an A-high board (Ad 8s 3c Ks 7d). The Pre-flop aggressor (Claude) checked back the Flop on an Ace-high board, which strongly suggests he does not have a strong Ace. The Turn checked through as well, further indicating weakness from all parties. On the River, betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands; hands like 8x or pocket pairs below Kings will likely fold given the presence of both an Ace and a King on the board. Conversely, I would only be called by better hands (weak Aces, better Kings) or split with another K9. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value or potentially induce a bluff from opponents who perceive weakness. My hand is strong enough to win at showdown but too vulnerable/thin to bet for value.

River goes check-check-check on flop, check-check-check on turn, and SB checks again on the river. In a 3-way pot this line heavily caps both opponents’ ranges toward missed broadways/suited connectors and some pocket pairs that are now in a tough spot on an A-K high board. We have essentially zero showdown value with J♣5♣, so checking is just surrendering. A medium bluff is credible (we can easily have Ax/Kx that pot-controlled multiway) and should fold out most air and many 8x/underpairs, while risking a reasonable amount if BTN shows up with an ace and calls. I’ll stab for a size that applies real pressure but doesn’t torch our stack when called.

I have 4d2d - complete air on Ad-8s-3c-Ks-7d. FiveTwo bet 450 into 750 after checking flop and turn. This small river bet looks like thin value or a blocker bet with a medium-strength hand. With zero showdown value and Pro still potentially in the hand, folding is the only sensible option. Bluff-raising would be -EV against a player who voluntarily bet into two opponents.

The hand has been checked down to the River on an Ad 8s 3c Ks 7d board. I hold Kd 9d for second pair. The Big Blind (FiveTwo) has led out for 450 into a pot of 750 (approx 60% pot) after checking the Flop and Turn. Crucially, FiveTwo made this bet into two opponents, including the pre-flop raiser (Claude) on the Button. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on an Ace-high board after checks usually signifies a trapped Ace or a made hand that feels it is good, rather than a bluff, as bluffs are risky into two ranges that could easily contain a weak Ace. My King-pair is a pure bluff-catcher here, losing to any Ace (which makes up a significant portion of a BB defense range that checks down) and better Kings. Given the multi-way dynamic and the strength shown by leading into the PFR, the probability of facing a better hand is high enough to warrant a fold.

KJs suited is a premium hand from UTG in a 5-handed game with deep stacks. It has excellent playability, equity against calling ranges, and potential to make strong hands. Late in the tournament (hand 133/150), we want to build the stack aggressively with strong holdings. Standard open-raise size of 3x the BB (300) to take initiative, build pot, and narrow the field while maintaining fold equity against loose callers like those observed in prior hands.
J2o is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG raise from Elon, I should fold. This hand has poor playability out of position and calling would just be burning chips.

I am on the Button with 7s 5d, facing a 3BB raise from Elon in UTG. 75o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity against a strong UTG opening range. My stack is 37.5 BBs, which is not suitable for speculative calls with junk hands. The pot odds do not justify a call, and 3-betting as a bluff is ill-advised against an UTG opener. The only standard play is to fold.

Elon is opening from UTG 5-handed to a larger 3x size, and we’re in the small blind out of position with a weak suited gapper (9s5s) and the big blind still to act. Although the price looks OK, our equity realization OOP versus a relatively strong opening range is poor, and we’re also exposed to squeezes from the big blind. A 3-bet bluff isn’t attractive versus an UTG range that won’t overfold. Best play is to conserve chips and wait for better spots.

Elon opened UTG to 300 (3x) — a relatively strong, early-position range. Kc6h is a poor, easily-dominated hand (many of Elon’s kings have better kickers) with weak post‑flop playability out of position and two players (button and SB) still to act who can squeeze or call and make this a messy multi‑way pot. My stack is deep so there’s no need to gamble with a marginal holding; preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the +EV line here. Fold and wait for a stronger spot or position.
2c5s from UTG is a clear fold. This is a junk hand with no playability - offsuit, disconnected low cards. UTG requires a tight range and this doesn't come close to meeting that standard.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively Hijack in 5-handed play) with a stack of 37.5 BBs, which is the shortest stack at the table. My hand is Ten-Deuce suited (Ts 2s), which is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. Given my position and the fact that there are aggressive players behind me with larger stacks, this hand has negative expected value if played. I should conserve my chips for better opportunities, especially with only about 16 hands left in the tournament. The correct play is a straightforward fold.

We’re on the Button 5-handed after two folds, so this is a prime steal spot. 9♠6♠ is a playable suited connector with good postflop playability in position, and raising leverages fold equity versus the blinds while keeping our range wide. I’ll use a standard small BTN open (2.5x) to deny a cheap realization and keep the pot manageable if we get defended; if we face a sizeable 3-bet, we can comfortably fold this hand.

9♣4♠ is a junk hand with very poor playability and high chance of being dominated. I'm out of position in the small blind facing a button open to 250 (BB still to act), so calling just to see a flop will frequently put me OOP in a multiway pot with minimal equity. 3-betting this holding is out of the question. Even though the call (200) is small relative to my stack, this is a marginal spot I can skip and wait for a better opportunity. Fold and conserve chips.

T2o is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, low cards, offsuit, and terrible playability out of position in the big blind facing a button raise. Even against a wide button range, the equity is insufficient, and postflop play OOP is disastrous. Pot odds are about 3:1 (150 to call into 550), but this hand doesn't realize enough equity. Late tournament, preserve stack for better spots. Clear fold.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Diamonds and Seven of Spades (T7o). This is a statistically very weak hand (trash) that plays poorly post-flop and is easily dominated. With a stack of 37.5 BBs (3750 chips), I have plenty of room to wait for better opportunities. Opening from early position with such a weak hand is a losing play in the long run. I will fold immediately.

We’re 5-handed but still effectively early position (UTG+1) with a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand. 9♠5♣ type hands realize equity poorly out of position, make dominated pairs, and don’t have strong straight/flush potential. With ~66bb and tougher stacks behind (especially the button), there’s no need to enter this pot by limping or raising.

I have J♠6♦ on the button. UTG and UTG+1 folded, pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call. J6o is a marginal hand to open-raise with — raising invites defenses and possible 3-bets from Elon (SB) who has been aggressive and from Claude (BB) who defends. Calling is cheap and preserves my positional advantage to see a flop and realize equity. With a healthy stack and only ~15 hands left I don’t want to bloat the pot with a weak holding. Plan: call 100, evaluate the flop; continue only with clear improvement (pair, good draw) or take a small c-bet on very favorable boards, otherwise fold to significant aggression.

In the small blind with Q8o facing a button limp from Minni (deep stack) after early folds. Pot is 250 to call just 50 more (already posted SB 50), offering excellent 5:1 pot odds. Q8o has decent playability: queen-high blocker, potential for top pair, straight draws (e.g., QJT, 910J), and flush draws if suited but still viable offsuit. Button limp range is wide and weak (often speculative hands or traps from aggressive Minni based on history). OOP but deep stacks (~125BB effective), low variance spot late in tournament. BB (Claude) may check or squeeze, but completing exploits position and pot odds. Plan: call, see flop, c-bet hits/misses, fold to heavy aggression if whiff. Raising risks multiway or isolation vs deep calling range; folding wastes equity.

KJo in the BB with 2 limpers - Minni from button and Elon completed SB. This is a strong hand that plays well and I should raise for value and to thin the field. Standard raise to 400-450 to charge limpers and potentially take it down preflop.

I limped the button with J♠6♦ intending to see a flop cheaply, but Claude in the BB just isolated with a sizable raise to 450 (350 more to me). The pot is ~650, so a call requires roughly 35% equity (350 into a 1000 pot) — J6o is a weak, often-dominated holding and performs poorly vs a raiser’s isolation range, especially with SB (Elon) behind who can still reraise. Raising to 800 would be an overly loose squeeze with a marginal hand. I’ll preserve my stack for better spots and fold to Claude’s raise.

Facing a squeeze raise from Claude in the BB after limping in SB with Q8o offsuit. Pot is 650, calling 350 more gives ~2.86:1 odds requiring ~26% equity heads-up, but Q8o has poor playability OOP: queen-high often dominated by better Qx/Ax/KQ, limited straight potential (needs specific connectors), no suited value. Claude's squeeze range is strong (sets, overpairs, broadways) or bluffs (Ax suited, suited connectors), but even against wide range, equity <25% (sims ~20-25% vs typical squeeze). Minni folded her wide limp range. Late in tournament (hand 135/150), 5 players, preserve stack for better spots; folding avoids OOP postflop variance with marginal hand. Raising commits too much with weak blocker equity.

5-handed UTG with no ante, K2o is too weak to open (dominated by all better kings and many calling/3-betting ranges). With a medium stack and a shorter stack in the blinds, there’s little incentive to take low-EV spots out of position. Best play is to preserve chips and wait for better open candidates.

UTG+1 with Q♦5♠ — a weak offsuit queen with a poor kicker and little playability. There are multiple players to act behind (Button, SB, BB). It costs 100 to see the flop and the min-raise is 200; calling out of position with this hand is a negative-EV play (likely dominated by other Qx and vulnerable to raises), and raising as an early-position steal is too loose. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

Folded to me on the button in an unopened pot. This is a prime stealing spot with position on the blinds. 86o is a marginal but playable hand for a button steal due to post-flop playability (straight potential) and fold equity against blinds. Pro in BB is relatively short (36BB) and may fold light. Standard open size around 2.5BB. Raising to 250 provides good pot odds if called and maintains initiative. Folding too tight here misses value; limping is suboptimal.

Holding 63 offsuit in the small blind facing a button raise. This is a garbage hand with no playability - not suited, not connected, no high cards. Even with position price of 200 to win 400, 63o is firmly in the fold range against any reasonable button raising range. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o, facing a button raise to 250 from Elon. The pot odds are roughly 2.66:1, requiring about 27% equity to call. Although T4o might have raw equity near this threshold against a wide button opening range, my realizable equity is extremely poor due to being out of position with a disconnected, weak hand. As the short stack (36.5 BB) with only 14 hands left in the tournament, I must preserve my chips for better opportunities to shove or defend. Defending here would likely result in a check-fold on the flop or losing a larger pot dominated. Therefore, folding is the correct play.

Holding 4♦9♥ UTG in a 5-handed 50/100 game is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand that is often dominated. I'm first to act with four players to act behind (including aggressive openers like Elon) so calling 100 into a 150 pot gives ~40% pot odds, which this hand doesn’t have versus even one opponent and gets worse multiway. Open-raising from UTG with this holding is overly loose and will put me in many difficult postflop spots. With a healthy 232BB stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better opportunity. Fold.

Pocket sixes (66) is a strong medium pocket pair from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game late in the tournament (hand 137/150). Standard play is to raise for value and to thin the field, potentially isolating against blinds or building the pot with a hand that plays well postflop. Raise sizing: 3x the BB (100) to 300, which is a standard open. Minni folded UTG, so fewer players behind, but Pro (SB) is short-stacked at 3600 (~36BB), might shove over; still, 66 has good equity. Pot currently 150 from blinds; committing 300 total. Folding or limping is suboptimal as it misses value and invites multiway pots.
KQo on the button facing a raise from Elon. This is a premium hand in position - perfect spot to 3-bet for value and isolation. A raise to around 950 is standard sizing here.

I am in the Small Blind with [4h, 5h]. Facing a raise from Elon (UTG+1) to 300 and a 3-bet from Claude (Button) to 950. My stack is 3600 (36 BBs). Cold calling a 3-bet out of position with low suited connectors is -EV, especially committing 25% of my stack pre-flop. A 4-bet shove is too risky given the strength shown by the Button's large 3-bet size and my lack of fold equity relative to the risk. Folding is the correct standard play.

We’re in the BB with 4c3h facing an open to 300 and a strong 3-bet to 950 from the Button, with the original raiser still able to continue. Even getting ~2.6:1 immediate odds (850 to win ~2250 after calling), 43o has very poor equity versus a BTN 3-bet range and plays terribly out of position with massive reverse implied odds. A 4-bet jam would be high-variance and unprofitable given the dead money and our hand strength, especially this late with 5 players left. Preserve the 65bb stack and wait for a better spot.

Facing a button 3-bet to 950 after my UTG+1 open to 300 with 66. Blinds folded, so heads-up. Late in tournament (137/150), deep stacks (~120BB effective). 66 has excellent equity (~35-45%) against a typical button 3-bet range (TT+, AQ+, KQs, some bluffs). Folding is too tight preflop. Shoving all-in (12k) is an overbet with poor risk/reward if called, limited fold equity against aggro button. Min-raise lacks fold equity and builds small pot OOP. Calling 650 into 1400 pot (~2.15:1) allows set-mining (12% set equity with huge implied odds deep-stacked), overpair potential, and ability to play postflop OOP profitably against continuation range. Claude aggressive historically; can check-raise strong flops or control pot. Maximizes stack EV long-term.

Preflop call with 66 against button 3-bet was correct for set-mining and postflop playability given deep stacks (11700 effective) late in tournament (137/150). Flop Qh7c5s rainbow hits my range decently but I'm OOP as preflop raiser. 66 has ~12% equity to set (outs: 2 remaining 6s), blocks sets, and has showdown value against villain's continuation range (overpairs TT+, AK/AQ which have Qx blockers or A-high). Board low except Q, possible SDs (89, T6, 48) in villain's calling range but rainbow limits draws. No initiative advantage post-3bet. Betting OOP risks raising into better (QQ/77/55/AK with Q) or getting check-raised; poor EV. Checking controls pot, induces bluffs from A-high/miss, allows check-call/raise vs c-bets, realizes equity multiway if blinds wake up (unlikely folded), preserves stack for ICM late. Pot 2050 (~17.5% stack), check maximizes EV by pot controlling medium strength OOP.

I flopped top pair with a king kicker on Q-7-5 rainbow. This is an excellent spot to c-bet for value. Elon likely has a medium-strength hand that called my 3-bet (JJ, TT, AJ, KJ type hands). I should bet around 60-70% pot to get value and deny equity to overcards. Betting ~1350 is good sizing.

Pocket 66 on Qh7c5s flop OOP facing Claude's aggressive button 3-bet c-bet of 1350 into 3400 (~40% pot). This board hits villain's 3-bet range decently (Qx, overpairs) but also my range as preflop raiser. 66 has ~25-35% equity vs. typical polarized c-bet range (overpairs TT+, Qx, AKo, bluffs like KJs+), including set-mining equity (2 outs ~9% turn/river), live underpair value vs. A-high, and fold equity realization. Pot odds 3.5:1 require ~22% equity, which we have. Deep stacks (117BB eff.) provide massive implied odds for set (~$30k+ if stacks in). Raising OOP risks cooler (into QQ/77/55/AQ) or villain's 4-bet shove (aggro history), no fold equity vs. strong range. Folding too tight late tournament (137/150, non-ICM bubble yet), misses EV. Calling controls pot size (~4750 new pot), allows turn check-fold/call vs. weak A-high or check-raise strong draws/improves, OOP playability optimal. Preserves stack for ICM pressure on short Pro (36BB).

Turn 9s on Qh7c5s board (now Q975 rainbow except two spades: 5s9s) worsens my position with 66 OOP heads-up vs Claude's aggressive BTN 3-bet range (QQ+, AKo, AQs+, some bluffs like KJs, QJs). Board now features spade flush draw (scary), gutshot straight draws (68, 810), open-enders (89, T8), completed straights (89 best). My pocket 66 is vulnerable: ~4% equity to set (2 outs), loses to all overpairs/sets/Tx/Qx/straights, but beats pure A-high bluffs/missed draws (~25-35% equity vs polarized range). Pot 4750 (~46% of my 10350 stack), deep effective ~100BB. OOP after check-call flop 1350 (40% pot), initiative lost. Betting turn OOP polarizes to nuts (sets unlikely blocked), invites raises from QQ+/draws/AK (cooler or draw race), thin value. Fold equity low vs aggro Claude who calls/raises light. Checking optimal: pot control OOP, induces bluffs from A-high/missed spade draws (villain 3-bets wide), check-call/raise value vs weak c-bets (overpairs beat bluffs), check-fold polarized jams. Realizes equity cheaply, preserves stack for ICM (hand 137/150, short Pro 36BB), maximizes EV long-term vs Claude's history of turn aggression but also checks (e.g., hand 77 river bet after check). No auto-profit bet size exists without fold equity.

I have top pair top kicker (KQ on Q-7-5-9 board). Elon check-called flop, likely holding a weaker queen, pocket pair, or draw. The 9s turn is a brick that doesn't complete obvious draws. I should continue betting for value - around 55-60% pot (~2600-2800) extracts value from QJ, QT, and pocket pairs while setting up a river decision. Elon has ~10350 behind, so sizing appropriately to potentially get stacks in on good rivers.

Pocket 66 OOP heads-up vs Claude's aggressive button 3-bet range after check-calling flop 1350 (40% pot) on Qh7c5s rainbow. Turn 9s worsens board connectivity (spade flush draw with 5s9s, gutshots like 68/T8/810, open-enders 89/T6), but 66 retains ~25-35% equity vs polarized range (overpairs QQ+/TT+, Qx/AQ/AK, draws/bluffs like JTs, KJs): beats A-high bluffs/missed draws (~40% of range), 4% set equity (2 outs), some runner-runner outs. Pot odds 7450:2700 (~3.76:1) require ~21% equity, which we exceed. Deep effective stacks (~80BB remaining post-call) offer implied odds for set/river improve. Raising OOP after double check polarizes to nuts (QQ/77/55 unlikely vs button 3bet), low fold equity vs aggro Claude (history: turn bets rivers in #77, #102, but calls light); risks coolers/shoves. Folding tight post-flop call, misses EV vs bluffs/draws late (137/150, ICM pressure with short Pro 3600). Calling pot-controls OOP, induces river bluffs from A-high/missed spades, sets up check-call/raise river value/draws, realizes equity cheaply, preserves ~50% stack (5350 behind) for ICM/150-hand endgame maximization. Consistent with prior check-call strategy vs Claude's aggression.

River Td on Qh7c5s9s board (now Q T 9 7 5 two spades) completes minor gutshots (e.g., J8 broadway straight) and pairs the 10 indirectly but low-value card, missing spade flush draw. Pocket 66 OOP heads-up vs Claude's aggressive BTN 3-bet range (QQ+, AK/AQ, bluffs like JTs/KQs) after check-calling flop/turn cbets retains ~25-35% equity: beats A-high bluffs/missed draws (~35-40% range), 2 set outs (~4%), dominates weak pairs/draws; loses to Qx (QQ/AQ/KQ), overpairs TT+, rare J8 straight. Pot 10150 (~1.3x my 7650 stack), deep effective ~80BB. OOP after double check-call, initiative lost; betting polarizes to nuts (unlikely QQ/77/55/J8 blocked), thin value vs calling range (Qx/overpairs), low fold equity vs aggro Claude (history of river bets/calls light e.g., #77,#102), risks coolers/shoves. Folding pre-river impossible. Checking optimal: pot control OOP late tournament (137/150, ICM with short Pro 36BB), induces bluffs from polarized range (A-high/spade whiffs ~30% equity), allows check-call thin value bets (beats bluffs/draws, hero-call overpairs with blockers), check-fold polarized jams/shoves (preserves ~76BB stack for endgame pressure). Realizes equity cheaply, consistent with prior check-call strategy maximizing EV long-term vs Claude's aggression.

River Td is a concerning card - it completes J8 for a straight and gives two pair to QT and T9 hands. Elon has check-called twice which suggests medium strength or a draw. After the Td, hands that beat me include: J8 straight, QT two pair, sets of TT/99, and AQ. My KQ still beats QJ, Q9s, JJ, 88, and bluff-catchers. Given the scary runout and that Elon could have improved, I should check back for showdown value rather than value bet thin and risk only getting called by hands that beat me.

Preflop UTG in 5-handed play with K9o. This is a playable hand in short-handed poker as UTG ranges are wider than full-ring. K9o blocks KK and has good postflop playability with a king blocker. Deep effective stacks (76bb), late in tournament (hand 138/150), goal to maximize stack. Raising builds pot, steals blinds (pot 150), and takes initiative OOP. Standard open size ~3x BB. Folding too tight; limping poor. Raise to 300.

Q8o is a weak offsuit hand facing an UTG open raise. This hand doesn't play well against a UTG raising range and I'm out of position. With a healthy 19k stack and only 12 hands remaining, I should avoid marginal spots. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with K4s facing a UTG raise to 300 from Elon. My stack is 3600 (36 BBs). K4s is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a UTG opening range (which includes stronger Kings like AK, KQ, KJ). The direct pot odds require about 40% equity to call heads-up (300 to win a total pot of 750), which is borderline against a strong range, and the hand suffers from reverse implied odds due to the weak kicker. With 36 BBs, I am not in a desperate shove/fold mode and can afford to wait for better spots. Calling also invites squeezes from the blinds. Folding is the most prudent long-term play.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb UTG open with J7o. This hand is heavily dominated by Elon's UTG range (AJ+/KJ+/QJ/JT, better sevens, broadways) and plays very poorly out of position with bad realization. Flat-calling also invites the big blind (large stack) to come along or squeeze, and we’ll be stuck guessing postflop in a bloated pot. A 3-bet bluff is high-variance here because UTG’s continuing range is strong enough and the big blind can apply pressure. With a healthy 65bb stack and no need to force thin spots late in the tournament, the highest-EV line is to fold and preserve stack for better steal/3-bet opportunities versus wider positions.

Hole cards Jc Tc — a strong, suited connector with good flop equity and playability. Elon opened UTG to 300 (3bb); with my big blind already at 100 it’s +200 more to call (300 total). Stacks are deep (I’m 23100, Elon 7350) so implied odds are good, but I’ll be out of position postflop and don’t want to bloat the pot with a light 3-bet against an UTG opener. I’ll call 300 total to see a flop and realize equity: keep weaker hands in, control the pot size, and proceed based on the flop texture. If Elon 3-bets to a large sizing I’ll strongly consider folding; if he c-bets small postflop I’ll continue with draws/top-pair lines and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold Tc Jc on a 5♠-3♥-T♥ flop — top pair with a strong jack kicker and some backdoor club potential. I called preflop to see this texture against Elon's UTG open; his range is relatively strong but contains many hands (weaker tens, medium pairs, broadways) that I beat. I'm first to act on the flop, so I want to both extract value and deny equity to overcards/heart draws. A half‑pot feel bet (~350 into 650) is large enough to charge draws and worse hands but small enough to get called by worse tens and medium pairs. Plan: bet 350; if Elon calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises big I’ll give credit to two‑pair/sets and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

Flop completely misses my K9o: king-high no pair, no straight draw, backdoor spade flush draw at best but irrelevant. Board has two hearts (Th 3h) and 5s, favoring draws and small pairs in Minni's limped-calling BB range. Her small donk bet (350 into 1000) could be weak, trapping, or value thin, but OOP I have poor playability. Raising as a bluff risks her deep stack (22k vs my 7k) shoving or calling wide; calling bloats pot with no equity/showdown value. Late tournament (138/150), preserve stack for better spots. Clear fold.
3s8s is a weak suited hand from UTG. Poor position with no showdown value and bad connectivity. Easy fold in early position.

Current hand is 139/150. I am in UTG+1 (effectively Cutoff in 5-handed) with a stack of 3600 (36 BB). The blinds are 50/100. I hold Ah 5s. A5o is a marginal hand from this position. While playable in late position or blind vs blind, opening from the cutoff with active big stacks behind (specifically Minni in the SB with 235 BB) is risky. I am the shortest stack at the table and should avoid bleeding chips with easily dominated hands or hands that cannot withstand a 3-bet. Folding is the prudent play to preserve the stack for better opportunities.

Button vs blinds with 65bb, 86o is a reasonable late-position open to pressure the blinds and leverage position. Limping invites the BB to realize equity cheaply and creates awkward multiway spots with an offsuit hand; raising lets us win the blinds immediately a fair amount and gives us initiative for profitable c-bets on favorable textures. If either blind 3-bets, we can comfortably fold.

I’m in the small blind with Ks8h facing a button open to 250 (pot 400). K8o is marginal out of position — worth defending vs a wide BTN range but not a hand I want to 3-bet with given the likelihood of squeezes from the BB. I’ll call the extra 200 (total 250) to keep the pot manageable and realize equity. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if we see the flop, proceed aggressively only when we hit (top pair, two pair, strong draws), otherwise control the pot and avoid big OOP confrontations.

Preflop in BB with 7s4s suited. Facing a button raise to 250 (2.5BB) and SB call. Pot is 600, calling 150 more for pot odds of about 4:1 (20% equity needed). 74s is a playable suited hand with straight and flush potential, good postflop playability in position multiway. Button range is wide, SB call range decent but not nuts. Standard BB defense spot; calling closes the action and realizes equity well. Folding too tight late in tournament; raising unnecessary with this hand as it prefers multiway pots. With 11 hands left, preserve stack but take good spots.

Flop: J♦ 4♦ 3♣. I’m OOP with K♠8♥ — no pair, no diamond, only ~6 clean outs to a pair (poor immediate equity). I defended preflop to keep the pot small and realize equity, but my preflop plan was explicit: avoid big OOP confrontations unless I hit. This is a dry, low-card flop with two diamonds (gives the raiser lots of diamond c‑bet/FD combos), so the correct line is pot control. I’ll check and fold to significant pressure. If the button fires a small c‑bet I may call a modest sizing to see a turn because my pair-out equity to the river (~20–25% combined) can be realized cheaply, but I will not invest against large bets or raises without improvement. If action checks through I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue if I pair or pick up a strong draw.
