Flop 4d3cJd gives me second pair (4s) with weak 7s kicker and no draws. SB (Minni) checked, indicating weakness. Button (FiveTwo) has wide preflop raising range including many Ax, Jx, draws, bluffs. Checking realizes equity in 3-way pot, controls pot size OOP, induces bets from button which I can hero-call profitably if sized reasonably due to my decent showdown value and blockers to some straights. Donk betting thin value risks raises from better hands or folds better; checking better exploits button aggression observed in history. Late tournament (hand 139/150), stack 71BB effective, preserve while taking good spots - this play balances risk/reward.

We’re 3-way in position as the preflop raiser on J♦-4♦-3♣. Even though our hand (8♦6♥) has no made value, our range still has a clear advantage here (overpairs, strong Jx, AJo/KJo, etc.) and both blinds checking suggests a lot of give-ups and medium air that can’t continue versus any pressure. Multiway I want a smaller c-bet size and a tighter follow-through plan: bet small to win it immediately, fold to a raise, and if called I’ll mostly slow down unless we pick up equity (diamond, 5/7, or a pair).

Flop: J♦ 4♦ 3♣. I have K♠8♥ — no pair and no diamond. FiveTwo’s bet is small (250 into a 1000 pot ≈25% pot), which usually represents a wide c‑bet range (lots of bluffs/missed overcards and some diamond draws). I have ~6 clean outs (3 kings + 3 eights) — roughly 24% to hit by the river. After the bet the pot is 1250 and calling 250 requires ~20% equity to break even. Versus a single opponent my equity (~24%) justifies a call to realize equity cheaply. Multiway action will reduce my equity, so I’ll call now to keep the pot manageable and fold to significant turn pressure if I don’t improve. If I pair the turn I can proceed; otherwise I’ll avoid large OOP confrontations.

Facing a 250 bet into 1250 pot (5:1 odds, ~17% equity needed) with second pair (4s) and 7 kicker on 4d3cJd board. FiveTwo's button c-bet wide, Minni's SB call indicates medium strength (draws, Jx, weak pairs) but vulnerable. My hand has showdown value, blocks some straight draws (holds 7s4s), and suited for backdoor potential. Calling realizes equity multiway OOP, keeps pot small relative to stacks (71BB effective vs FiveTwo's 60BB), exploits button aggression seen in history. Raising risks isolation OOP with marginal hand vs calling ranges; folding too tight late (hand 139/150) with excellent odds. Plan: call, reassess turn for pot control or value if improves.

Turn K♣ pairs my K♠ — I’ve hit top pair (K8) in a 3‑way pot on 4♦ 3♣ J♦ K♣. I defended pre and called the small flop bet to realize equity; now this is exactly the spot to extract value and protect vs the diamond draw (villain(s) could hold two diamonds → ~9 outs) and Jx/missed overcard hands that called the flop. Checking lets draws see a free river; betting charges those draws and gets value from worse pairs/Jx. I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot to deny equity but not overcommit — bet 1000 into 1500. If I face a large raise I’ll re‑evaluate (my kicker is vulnerable, so I won’t automatically commit to big multiway confrontation).

Turn board Kc completes some straight draws (e.g., Q5, though unlikely) and hits broadway cards like AK, KJ, KT. Minni's SB check-call flop (weak pair/draw range like Jx, low pairs, diamond draws, gutshots) followed by turn donk-raise to 1000 into 2500 pot (40% pot size) is strong, polarizing to value (KJ, AJd, sets, two-pair) or bluffs (missed diamond draws, air). My bottom pair 4s with 7 kicker has poor showdown value OOP, dominated by better pairs; no strong draws. Pot odds ~2.5:1 direct (2500:1000) but effective worse multiway with Button (aggressive history) behind who can squeeze. Stacks deep effective (me 68BB, FiveTwo 60BB), ICM pressure late (hand 139/150, 5 left) favors folding marginal equity (~20-25% vs raise range) to preserve stack for better spots. Calling risks larger river pots OOP; raising too thin without equity. Standard fold exploits Minni's strength and Button position.

After c-betting small and getting called by both blinds, my flop bet didn’t generate much fold equity and I arrive on the turn with essentially no equity (8-high, no straight draw, no flush draw). Minni’s lead for 1,000 into this pot after check-calling the flop multiway is a strong, value-heavy line (Kx improving, Jx that wants protection, sets/two pair, and some diamond-draw/value mixes). Calling needs ~22% equity and I’m nowhere close, and turning this into a raise-bluff would commit a large chunk of my 6,000 stack against the table chip leader who can comfortably call and force me into a tough river barrel. Best play is to preserve the stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with 5d 2c (5-2 offsuit), which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With 36 BBs, I have a healthy stack that I should preserve for better opportunities, especially with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament. Opening from early position with this hand would be a significant mistake as it has no playability and faces four opponents left to act. The only standard play is to fold.

5-handed with no open in front, JhTh suited is a strong enough hand to open from UTG+1 (effectively hijack). Raising wins the blinds often, denies equity to the blinds, and keeps initiative; limping invites squeezes and multiway spots that realize poorly with a medium-strength connector. With ~60bb, a modest 2.5bb open keeps risk controlled while still pressuring the blinds.

I’m on the button with Q♠7♦ facing an UTG+1 open to 250 (pot 400). Calling 250 into a 400 pot means I need ~38.5% equity (250 / (400+250)) to break even — Q7o is well below that against a typical UTG+1 opening range and will be even worse if the SB/BB come along and we get multi‑way. A minimum isolation raise to 400 is too small to reliably isolate and a larger 3‑bet with this marginal, unsuited hand is unjustified. I’ll conserve my deep stack for better spots and fold here.

Hand 140 of 150, stacks are deep but tournament winding down. QJo offsuit from SB facing a raise to 250 (200 to call into 400 pot) from FiveTwo in UTG+1 (early-middle position in 5-handed). QJo is a marginal hand OOP vs an early raise; equity is around 40% vs a typical 12-15% raising range, but playing postflop out of position is difficult with a hand that dominates few strong hands and is vulnerable to domination (e.g., AK, AQ). FiveTwo has been aggressive historically but has folded to aggression in spots like Hand 115 and 137. BB Claude still to act and may squeeze or call. Pot odds are decent (3:1), but implied odds limited OOP with no suitedness for flush draws or strong straights. Folding preserves stack for better spots with only 11 hands left; calling risks spew postflop, and 3-betting risks cooler or fold equity not worth it given my stack (68BB). Optimal: fold.
Facing a min-raise from FiveTwo with T6o in the BB. Getting ~2.67:1 odds (call 150 into 400 pot). This is a marginal hand but defendable at these odds from the BB. I'll call and see a flop.
Completely missed this flop with T6o on A-J-7. No pair, no real draw. I can check for free and see what FiveTwo does. If they c-bet, I'll fold - no reason to continue with this hand.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser with position. On A♠ J♦ 7♥ we have a very strong combo hand: top pair (J) plus a 9-out heart flush draw (and potential backdoor straight equity). This is an excellent spot to continuation bet for (1) value vs worse Jx/7x and stubborn pocket pairs, (2) protection/denying equity to hands like KQ/QT/T9, and (3) to start building a pot where we can comfortably continue barreling on many turns (hearts, tens, jacks, and some blanks). I’ll use a small-to-medium sizing to keep dominated hands and floats in while still pressuring his range.
Missed completely with T6o on A-J-7 board. No pair, no meaningful draws. Facing a c-bet with zero equity - easy fold.

5-handed UTG is still the first seat and I have several aggressive stacks behind (Minni 245bb, Claude 187bb, Elon 68bb) who can 3-bet pressure. K7o is dominated by a large portion of their continuing ranges (better Kx, broadways) and plays poorly vs 3-bets/calls out of position. With no need to force marginal steals at 63bb, the clean highest-EV line is to pass and preserve stack for better opens/spots.

UTG+1 in a 5-handed game with A♥2♠. This is an Ace and has reasonable postflop playability; with a deep 24,550 stack (~245bb) I can pressure the blinds and take the initiative. A standard open (2.5–3x BB) is appropriate here — calling 100 is too passive and invites multiway pots out of position. I’ll raise to 300 to build the pot, gain fold equity, and keep my opening range balanced. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to very large 3‑bets/shoves, but consider calling small-to-medium 3‑bets to see a flop given the deep stacks).

A5o is a marginal offsuit ace with a weak kicker. Facing an early position raise from Minni (UTG+1), who has been aggressive throughout the history, this hand is not strong enough to call or raise. Position on the button provides some advantage for speculative hands, but A5o plays poorly post-flop, often dominated by better aces or facing strong ranges. Pot odds are 450 to call 300 (pot would be 750), but multi-way potential with deep SB and short BB makes it risky. Late in tournament (hand 141/150), preserve stack for better spots. Standard fold.

Q4s is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against an UTG+1 raise. Even though it's suited, the hand plays poorly out of position. The queen is marginal and the 4 kicker is terrible. If I hit a queen, I'm often dominated by AQ, KQ, or QJ. Folding is the correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 5s, a weak off-suit one-gapper. The player in UTG+1 (Minni) has raised to 300 (3bb). I need to call 200 chips into a pot that will be 650, offering me roughly 3:1 odds (30.7% equity required). However, 75o performs very poorly against an early/middle position opening range, is easily dominated, and will be difficult to play out of position. With a stack of 35bb, I should preserve chips and wait for better spots rather than defending with such a weak hand.

Ten-three offsuit is a very weak, unplayable hand from UTG in a 5‑handed game. I’ll be first to act and out of position against aggressive openers (table open sizes are typically 250–300 and Elon/FiveTwo often 3‑bet/raise), and the short stacked SB (Pro) can also shove over a limp. Calling 100 to see a flop with T3o gives poor equity and will often put me in tough spots postflop. With a healthy stack I should preserve chips for better spots and stronger holdings — fold.

Hand #142, preflop, UTG+1 position with 5c3d offsuit, a very weak hand with poor connectivity, low cards, and no suited potential. Early position requires a tight range to avoid tough postflop spots OOP against multiple opponents. Pot is 150 (blinds), to call 100 but no raise yet. Folding preserves my 68BB stack (6800) for better spots late in the tournament (hand 142/150). No exploitative reads from history justify playing this speculative hand.

Pocket 9s on the button with two folds ahead - this is a premium situation for a standard open raise. I have position and a strong pair. Raising to 250-300 is standard here to take down blinds or build a pot with a good hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit (T9o) facing a raise to 300 from the Button (Claude). My stack is 3,450 (approx 34.5 BBs). While T9o has some connectivity, it plays poorly out of position, especially against a large stack who can apply pressure post-flop. Calling would require investing roughly 7% of my stack in a spot where I am likely dominated and definitely at a positional disadvantage. With the Big Blind still to act and only 8 hands left in the tournament segment, preserving chips for better opportunities is the prudent play.

Facing a 3x button open, J3o is too dominated and performs poorly out of position: we flop weak top pairs that are crushed by Jx, and we have very limited playability. While the pot odds are tempting, our realization is low versus a competent button range. With 62bb and only a few hands left, preserving stack and avoiding marginal -EV defenses is best.

6c7h is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability from UTG in a 5-handed tournament spot near the end (hand 143/150). It performs poorly multiway without position and initiative. Standard fold to preserve stack (68BB effective) for premium opportunities against deep stacks. No fold equity worth chasing here.
Pocket Jacks is a premium hand in UTG+1. With 8 hands left and a healthy stack, I should raise for value. Standard open to 2.5x BB (250) to build the pot while not over-committing pre-flop.

I am on the Button with 3s 4c, an extremely weak off-suit hand. Claude (UTG+1) has raised to 250. Given the hand strength, my stack depth of roughly 34 BBs, and the position of the raiser, I have no incentive to get involved in this pot. The hand has very low equity and playability. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.

9c8c is too strong to just fold 5-handed, but flat-calling from the SB is awkward: we’re out of position, give the BB great odds to come along/squeeze, and end up in a low-realization multiway pot. I prefer a selective 3-bet here to (1) pressure Claude’s opening range, (2) deny Minni an easy defend, and (3) create a lower SPR where suited connectors realize well when they connect. If Claude 4-bets, we can comfortably fold and preserve the rest of our stack.

Claude opened UTG+1 to 250 (an early-position, reasonably strong range) and the small blind 3‑bet to 900. In the big blind with Q♥8♥ I'd need to call 800. This hand is marginal here: it's often dominated by 3‑bet value hands (AQ, KQ+, big pairs) and plays poorly out of position in a multiway pot. Pot odds for the call are about 800/(1250+800) ≈ 39%, but my realistic equity vs a polarized 3‑bet (and against Claude if he continues) is lower than that. 4‑betting/raising is too thin and would commit chips with a dominated hand. With a healthy stack and better spots to pick, the correct play is to fold and wait for a clearer edge.

JJ facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo (900 vs my 250 open). With JJ I have a strong hand but not strong enough to want to 4-bet/call off against their ~53BB stack. Pot odds are decent (650 to win 1250). Calling to see a flop in position is the best play - I can re-evaluate based on board texture and FiveTwo's action.

We’re the preflop 3-bettor and this flop (6d-8d-5c) hits our range reasonably well while also giving Claude a lot of overcard/diamond-draw holdings that realize too well if we check. With 9c8c we have top pair plus a gutshot to the 7, so we can bet for thin value vs worse 8x/6x and to deny equity from hands like AQ/AJ/KQ with a diamond, while keeping the pot controlled given our SPR. A small c-bet also lets us comfortably fold if we face a strong raise on this texture (sets/two pair/strong combo draws) without torching our stack.

I have an overpair (JJ) on a 6d-8d-5c board. FiveTwo c-bet 650 into 1900 (~1/3 pot). This board has a diamond flush draw and some straight draw possibilities. My JJ is almost certainly ahead here against their 3-betting range. FiveTwo has only ~3750 behind after this bet. I should raise to protect against draws and get value from worse hands. A raise to ~1700 puts good pressure while leaving room to fold if they shove (though I'm likely calling anyway given my stack depth and hand strength).

We have top pair (8) plus a gutshot to the 7 on a dynamic board. Claude’s check-raise range here is rarely only nutted hands; it contains strong diamond draws and pair+draw/semi-bluffs that we’re ahead of and want to keep in. Getting 4250:1050 immediate odds (~25%) makes folding too tight, while shoving/3-betting mostly isolates ourselves versus sets/two pair and strong combo draws. Best is to call, keep his range wider, and reassess turns (especially diamonds/7/4/5/6/8).

Turn is Js, which downgrades our hand from top pair to a medium pair (8x) on a still-very-draw-heavy texture (two diamonds, straight possibilities). After Claude’s flop check-raise and our call, the pot is already 5300 with only 3600 behind (SPR < 1). When Claude checks here, his range contains both give-ups (missed diamond/overcard semi-bluffs) and traps (sets/two pair/overpairs that are happy to induce our jam). If we bet/shove, we’re largely forcing ourselves into a high-variance stack-off where we get called by better made hands often enough, while the draws that continue are getting decent price anyway. Checking keeps his range widest, realizes our equity for free (we can improve to a straight on a 7, trips/two pair on 8/9), and lets us make a more informed river decision—calling on bricks vs bluffs, and comfortably folding on diamonds/very bad runouts when his line polarizes to value.

The turn Jack gives me top set! This is a dream card. FiveTwo checked after calling my flop raise, showing weakness. They have ~3600 behind with 5300 in the pot. With the diamond flush draw still possible, I need to bet for value and charge draws. I'll bet around 2/3 pot (~3500) which essentially commits them if they call, setting up to get their stack in.

We’re getting 3500 to win a final pot of 12300 (pot 8800 + our call 3500), so we need ~28.5% equity. After Claude flats the 3-bet and then check-raises this very draw-heavy flop, his range is not just sets/two-pair; it contains a lot of strong diamond draws (often with overcards) and pair+draw holdings (e.g., 77/97s/7x with diamonds) that will take this line and keep barreling. Against those draws we’re usually ~60% and even versus some thinner value (A8/99/TT with a diamond) we still have straight outs (4x7) plus improvement outs (8s, and sometimes 5/6 vs Jx). With SPR < 1, folding a pair + gutshot here is too tight given the price; raising changes nothing because we’d only be adding ~100 more. Best play is to call and realize our equity.

We’re first to act on the river with only 100 chips left into a 12,300 pot. Our hand is just one pair (8s) on a board where Claude’s range contains plenty of Jx/overpairs/sets, so we can’t value-bet. Bluffing 100 into 12,300 has essentially zero fold equity—he’ll call almost always—so betting only burns the last chips. Best is to check and take the free showdown when offered; if Claude bets, we’ll be forced to call off anyway given the microscopic price.

I flopped an overpair and turned top set (JJJ). FiveTwo has checked river with only 100 chips remaining. This is a trivial value bet - I should bet to get their last 100 chips. They're completely pot committed and will call with anything.

Claude’s river bet is a trivial min-bet and we only have 100 left. We’re being laid enormous pot odds (100 to win ~12.5k), so we need well under 1% equity. Given his line can include missed diamond draws/straight draws and we still have a pair, folding would be a massive mistake. We call off and take the showdown.

I am in UTG (4-handed) with Q2s. This is a weak hand even short-handed. Being the short stack (34.5 BB) against massive stacks, I should play a solid range and not spew chips with marginal suited queens from early position. The risk of domination or being outplayed post-flop is high. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I’m on the button with J♠9♠ — a suited, connected hand with good playability in position. With only the SB and BB to act, I want to take the initiative and build pot/fold equity while keeping the sizing reasonable against deep stacks. A standard 3xBB open (to 300) puts pressure on the blinds, isolates often, and leaves room to fold to a big 3‑bet or continue in position if called. Plan: fold or flat-call a large 3‑bet; call small 3‑bets and play the flop aggressively when favorable (c-bet good texture, check behind dry).

Hand #144 pre-flop in SB with Ah3s, a weak offsuit ace with terrible kicker and poor playability OOP. Minni's button raise to 300 suggests a wide but strong enough range that doesn't justify calling 250 into a 450 pot (needing ~36% equity multiway). Postflop would be disastrous OOP with this hand against deep-stacked opponents. With only 7 hands left and 67BB stack, fold to preserve chips for premium spots.

7h4c is a weak offsuit hand in the BB. Minni raised from the button to 300, so I need to call 200 more. This hand has poor playability and limited potential - it's an easy fold even getting decent pot odds. No need to defend with such a weak holding when I have a comfortable stack with only 7 hands remaining.

I’m UTG in a 4-handed game with 7s 8d. In 4-max open ranges should be wider and 7-8 offsuit is playable with position advantage and postflop playability. I prefer to open-raise rather than limp to take the initiative and pick up the blinds. A standard 3x open (to 300 total) is small enough to fold to a big 3-bet/jam from the short-stacked BB (Pro ~33bb) but large enough to apply pressure to the SB and Button. If I face a large 3-bet or shove I’ll fold to protect my deep stack; if called I’ll continuation-bet or play the connected hand aggressively postflop depending on texture. Raise to 300.

K7o is a marginal offsuit hand on the button facing an UTG raise from Minni. Minni's UTG opens suggest a strong range, and K7o plays poorly postflop, often dominated by AK, KQ, KJ, etc., or crushed by pairs. Even in position, the odds (calling 300 into 450 pot for ~3:1 implied but risky) don't justify it given poor playability and domination risk. With 67BB stack late in tournament (hand 145/150), folding preserves chips for better spots against shorter stacks like Pro (33BB). No strong history to exploit Minni's range profitably here.

5h9d is a weak hand in the small blind facing a UTG raise. Out of position with poor playability post-flop. With only 5 hands left and a healthy stack, no need to get involved with marginal holdings. Easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 2h (suited connectors but extremely low) facing a raise to 300 from Minni (UTG/CO). Although I am getting reasonable pot odds (calling 200 to win 450), my hand is very weak and easily dominated. Playing 32s out of position against a big stack's opening range is generally a losing play due to reverse implied odds and lack of showdown value. With 33.5 BBs left and only 5 hands remaining in the tournament, preserving chips is preferable to defending with the bottom of my range.

Hand #146, 4-handed late in tournament (146/150). UTG with Th3c, complete trash: offsuit, gapped, low cards, no showdown value or playability. Standard fold from early position to preserve stack against deep-stacked Button (Claude) and BB (Minni), with short SB (Pro) behind but irrelevant for this hand. No steal incentive pre-flop with this holding; prioritize survival and chip accumulation with better spots in final hands.

92o on the button is a very weak hand. While I have position, this hand has poor playability postflop. Pro is short-stacked and might push back, and Minni has a big stack to defend. With only 5 hands left in the tournament and a healthy 25k stack, no need to risk chips with trash. Folding is standard here.

The hand is A4o in the Small Blind. I am the short stack with 3300 chips (33 BBs) facing a massive stack in the Big Blind (Minni, 24800 chips). The action has folded to me. A4o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. While an Ace has showdown value, the kicker is weak and unsuited. Against an aggressive big stack who can apply maximum pressure (Minni has been active and raising frequently), entering the pot with a weak hand out of position is risky. Standard charts often suggest folding A2o-A7o in the SB vs BB. To minimize variance and preserve my stack in the final few hands, folding is the prudent play.
Pocket 7s in UTG at a 4-handed table is a solid opening hand. With 4 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack of 25000, I should open raise here. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am holding Jack-Three offsuit on the Button, which is a statistically weak hand. Facing a raise to 250 from the UTG player (Claude), who has a covering stack, calling or raising would be highly unprofitable. With only 3 hands remaining in the tournament and a stack of 33BB, preserving chips by folding trash hands is the standard and correct play.

Claude opened UTG to 250 — an early-position raise representing a reasonably strong range. I’m in the small blind with J♦7♣: a marginal, often dominated hand that plays poorly out of position against both the opener and the big blind. Calling 200 to see a flop OOP is a weak line (I’ll be difficult to realize equity and Elon can still act behind), and a light 3‑bet to 400 is thin given Claude’s early-range and the likelihood of a cold 3‑bet or a call that leaves me OOP. With a healthy stack and the tournament nearing its end, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is preferable. Fold.

Preflop in the big blind with 9h 2c, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness irrelevant as it's offsuit low cards. Facing a UTG raise from Claude to 250 (150 to call into 400 pot), which represents a strong range. My stack is 6650 (66.5BB), effective with Claude's deep stack, but positionally disadvantaged OOP postflop. No fold equity for a 3bet, terrible playability. Standard fold to preserve stack late in tournament (hand 147/150). Pot odds are good (~3:1), but equity realization too low against strong range.

I am in UTG position (effectively the Cutoff in 4-handed play) with 33 BBs. My hand is Queen-Five suited. While suited queens have some playability, Q5s is generally below the opening threshold for this position, as it suffers from kickers problems and can be easily dominated if called. With only 3 hands left in the tournament and a playable but not deep stack, preserving chips for better opportunities or hands with higher equity is the correct strategy.

I'm on the button with 9♦5♦, pot is 150 and it costs 100 to play. 9♦5♦ is a speculative suited hand that plays well in position (backdoor flush/straight potential) and is a good candidate for a button steal. I want to apply pressure to the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if called, so I’ll raise to 300 (3xBB) — large enough to pick up folds from tighter defenders but not committing a big portion of my 24,900 stack. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll use position to play postflop cautiously and look to realize my equity.

A8h suited is a premium hand from the small blind facing a button raise, which is likely wide given Minni's history of frequent button aggression. This hand has excellent blockers to AA/AK/AQs, good playability with suited ace high and flush/straight potential. 3-betting applies pressure, builds the pot OOP where we can realize equity multiway or take it down preflop. Standard sizing around 3x the open to 900 total. With only 3 hands left and decent stack (66BB), we want to accumulate chips aggressively without excessive risk.
Holding 9h4c in the BB facing a 3-bet from Elon (900). This is a very weak hand with poor playability. 94o has terrible equity against a 3-betting range and I'd be out of position. Easy fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with 9♦5♦ and Elon (SB) 3-bet to 900. Pot is 1,300 and it costs me 600 more to see a flop. 9♦5♦ is speculative but plays well in position — backdoor flush/straight potential and can realize equity postflop. Calling 600 into 1,300 gives ~32% immediate pot odds; with position and a reasonably wide SB 3‑bet range this is a profitable +EV cold-call spot. Raising to 1,500 is just a fancy bluff with little fold equity against a short but aggressive player and risks a 4‑bet; folding is too tight given the cheap price and playability. Plan: call to keep the pot controllable, use position to continue with pairs or strong draws, and fold to any sizable 4‑bet or heavy pressure if the flop misses hard.

Top two pair (Aces and Eights) with the nut Ace blocker on a board with a diamond flush draw. OOP heads-up vs Minni who called a 3-bet preflop from the button, so her range includes Ax (worse kickers blocked by my Ah), sets (unlikely), diamond draws, and some bluffs. Near end of tournament (hand 148/150), need to maximize value from this monster while protecting against draws. Pot is 1900; a 2/3 pot bet of ~1250 charges draws, gets value from weaker Ax/pairs, and keeps pot manageable OOP. Avoid checking to induce, as villain may check back weak hands.

Flop: 8♦ 4♠ A♦. I hold 9♦5♦ — a two‑card diamond flush draw (9 outs). With two cards to come my equity to make the flush by the river is ~35%. Elon (SB) c‑bet 1,300 into a 3,200 pot; calling 1,300 gives ~28.9% break‑even pot odds. Math + position => call is the correct play.

Raising to 2,600 as a semi‑bluff is possible, but it unnecessarily bloats the pot and increases the chance of an immediate shove from Elon (he has ~4,450 behind). I prefer to keep the pot controllable, realize my draw in position, and retain the option to fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss. Plan: call to see the turn; if I hit the diamond I will extract value, if I miss I will proceed cautiously and fold to large bets.

Turn pairs the board with 4h, giving me top two pair (aces and eights) on a board of Ad8d4s4h. My Ah is nut blocker for top pair, 8h pairs the eight. Villain called preflop 3bet from button (wide range) and large flop c-bet of 1300 into ~1900 pot, so her range is Ax (worse kickers like AQo-ATo, AdXx draws), two-pair/draw combos, sets (44/88/AA unlikely given blockers and action). Turn pairs 4s, which improves some of her gutshots or two-pair hands but doesn't kill my equity much; diamond draw still live. Pot 4500, my stack 4450 (~98BB effective but short relatively), OOP heads-up vs deep Minni (227bb). With 2 hands left after this, shoving all-in for 4450 gets max value from any Ax worse kicker, weaker two-pair (A4, 84, 44), or stubborn draws. Checking risks a free river card completing draws or villain checking back weaker Ax. Jamming applies max pressure and builds stack aggressively in late tournament.

Elon just jammed the turn and has 0 behind. Pot is 8,950 and it costs me 4,450 to call — I need ~4450/(8950+4450)=33.2% equity to make a break‑even call. I hold 9♦5♦ on 8♦ 4♠ A♦ 4♥. I only have a one‑card flush draw left (9 diamonds left), so my clean flush equity to hit on the river is 9/46 ≈ 19.6%. I also have six cards that pair my hole cards (three 9s + three 5s ≈ 13% raw), but virtually all of those pair‑outs lose to the hands Elon is representing (A‑x, 8x, pocket pairs, or any 4/x that now makes trips or full house). Elon's line (3‑bet preflop, c‑bet flop, shove turn after the board pairs) is heavily value‑oriented and polarizes to very strong hands here; even if he jams some bluffs, my realistic equity is well under the required ~33%. I planned preflop/flop to fold to heavy turn aggression if I missed — that exact situation is occurring. Preserve the healthy stack and wait for a better spot rather than calling a marginal ~20% flush draw for incorrect pot odds. Decision: fold.

6♠8♦ is a weak, unsuited, non‑connected hand with very poor playability UTG (4-handed). Calling 100 to limp will invite aggression from the button and a small raise to 200 is not a hand I want to open with — it also risks a shove from the short-stacked BB (Pro ~32BB) and a difficult, low‑equity call. With a healthy stack and no compelling reason to gamble here, folding preserves chips and avoids marginal multiway or isolation spots. I fold.

4-handed, on the button in an unopened pot (UTG Minni folded). Kc5d is a playable hand from the button with position, king blocker, and straight potential. Near end of tournament (hand 149/150), aggression is key to maximize stack. Pro in BB has only 3200 (~32BB), vulnerable to steals. Claude deep in SB but button has positional advantage. Standard open-raise size around 3x BB from late position unopened. Raising to steal blinds or build pot with position.

I have 8c9c (suited connector) in the small blind facing a button raise to 300. This is a solid defending hand - suited connectors play well postflop and can make straights and flushes. At 250bb effective vs Elon, I have plenty of room to play. Calling is standard here rather than 3-betting OOP with a hand that plays better seeing flops.

Current hand 149/150. I am in the Big Blind with Qs3s. The Button raised to 300 (3bb) and the Small Blind called. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 700, giving me excellent pot odds of 4.5 to 1 (22% equity required). Q3s is a defendable hand at this price, especially suited, and I close the action. It plays reasonably well in a multi-way pot if I hit a flush draw or two pair. Given the deep stage of the tournament and the cheap price to see a flop, folding would be too tight.

I completely missed this flop with 8c9c. The board is 5h 3d Ah - no pair, no club flush draw (board has hearts), just a weak backdoor straight draw. With no real equity and three-way, checking is the only sensible option. I'll look to check-fold if there's significant action.

I hold Qs3s on a flop of 5h3dAh. I have flopped bottom pair. The pot contains 900 chips. I am in the Big Blind and action has been checked to me by the Small Blind. The pre-flop raiser is on the Button and is yet to act. Leading out with bottom pair on an Ace-high board into two opponents, one of whom is the pre-flop aggressor, is not a profitable play. The standard line is to check to the raiser to see their action. I will check and evaluate based on subsequent bets.

Flop board Ah-5h-3d gives me second pair (5s) with a strong king kicker on a draw-heavy board (flush draw, straight draws like 2-4 or 4-6). Both opponents checked, showing potential weakness. As pre-flop raiser on the button with position, this is a standard continuation bet spot for value against worse hands (Ax worse kicker, bluffs, draws) and protection against improving draws. Pot is 900, my stack 8650 effective vs Pro's short 3000. Betting ~2/3 pot applies pressure: Pro may call light/shove weak or fold, Claude deep but checked SB. Near tournament end (149/150), building pot/aggression maximizes stack. Size 600 balances value/protection/fold equity without overbetting vs short stack.

I have 8c9c on a 5h 3d Ah board - completely missed. No pair, no flush draw (board has hearts, I have clubs), and only weak backdoor straight possibilities. Elon c-bet 600 into a 1500 pot, which is a standard 40% sizing. With no equity and Pro still behind me, this is a clear fold. No reason to float with air in a multiway pot.

It is Hand 149/150. I hold Queen-Three suited and have flopped bottom pair on an Ace-high board (Ah 5h 3d). The pre-flop raiser (Elon) has bet 600 into a pot of 900 (2/3 pot) after two checks. This is a sizable bet into two opponents on an Ace-high texture, which strongly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (which includes many Ax hands). My bottom pair is very weak and vulnerable, and I am out of position. The pot odds are roughly 28%, but my equity against an Ace is very low (drawing to 5 outs), and it will be difficult to reach a showdown profitably or realize equity out of position against a strong range. Preserving my 3000 chip stack for the final hand is better than calling off 20% of it with a marginal made hand likely dominated by the aggressor's value range.

8c3c is a speculative suited hand with significant gaps and low card strength. UTG in a 4-handed game, first to act preflop, folding weak hands is standard to avoid playing out of position with marginal equity. Pot is small (150), to call 100 into multiway pot unlikely profitable. Stacks: mine 9550 (~96BB), Pro short 2950, others deep. Better to preserve stack for premium hands even on final hand. Fold.

Final hand, I'm on the button with 45o. Elon folded UTG. This is a marginal hand but I have position and only the blinds left to act. Pro is short-stacked at 2950. I can make a small steal attempt here - standard 2.5x raise to try to take down the blinds. If I get 3-bet, I can easily fold.

This is the final hand of the tournament (150/150). I am in the Small Blind with [6h Qd], holding 2,950 chips (approx 30 BB). Claude on the Button has raised to 250. Q6 offsuit is a weak hand that is dominated by a typical button opening range. Playing this hand out of position is expected to have negative value (-EV). Since the goal is to maximize final stack size, avoiding -EV situations is crucial, even in the last hand. A fold preserves my current stack rather than risking chips in a disadvantageous spot.

I have A♦K♣ in the big blind facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, 150 to call). This is a top-tier, playable hand that should usually be 3‑bet for value and initiative against a button opener. With deep stacks (~226BB) I want a sizing that extracts value from worse aces/strong broadways, builds the pot when I have fold equity, and narrows Claude’s continuing range. I’ll 3‑bet to 1000 (about 4x his open) — large enough to apply pressure and isolate but far from committing the stack. If he calls I’ll take the lead postflop; if he 4‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing and reads (smaller 4‑bets I’ll likely call, very large 5‑bets or shove I’ll fold unless I get proper odds).

Final hand. I opened 45o as a button steal, got 3-bet by Minni to 1000. With 4-high, I have no showdown value and poor playability against a 3-bet range. Calling 750 more to see a flop with this weak hand is -EV. Easy fold.
